title: Semi-Automatic Correction of 3D Tubular Structure Skeletons via Component-Wise MST and Filtered Delaunay Triangulation
summary:
Skeletonization of tubular structures from 3D imaging is essential for tasks such as morphometric analysis, transport or flow simulation, and procedural planning in domains including vascular networks, plant root systems, and neural connectomes. However, automatic skeleton extraction often introduces topological artifacts, such as erroneous connections between nearby branches and fragmented centerlines caused by noise or missing data. Correcting these artifacts manually can be time-consuming and error-prone, especially when precise interaction is required.
  We present a semi-automatic correction method that reconstructs a plausible centerline connection from minimal user input. Given a user-selected source and target point, our method traces a path by combining (i) component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation and (ii) a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph for bridging gaps and handling ambiguous junctions. Candidate steps are ranked using a score that accounts for direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and target-directed progress. The output is an ordered polyline (or edge sequence) that can be used as a suggested correction and integrated into downstream skeleton post-processing workflows.
  We implement the system in C++ with an interactive viewer based on Libigl and demonstrate representative qualitative results on brain vessel datasets, including correction of typical "crossing" and "dotted" artifacts. While our current validation is qualitative, the method is lightweight and serves as a practical building block toward more comprehensive interactive correction pipelines in biomedical imaging and related domains.

AI.tldr: Semi-automatic skeleton correction for 3D tubular structures using user-specified endpoints, combining component-wise MST for local propagation and a filtered 3D Delaunay graph to bridge gaps. Produces an ordered centerline as a corrective suggestion, with qualitative brain-vasculature results implemented in C++/Libigl.
AI.motivation: Automatic skeleton extraction often creates topological artifacts (false connections, fragmentation) in tubular networks. Manual correction is time-consuming; need an interactive, lightweight tool that can propose plausible corrections from minimal user input.
AI.method: Given a source and target point, trace a path by (i) component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation and (ii) a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph to bridge gaps and handle ambiguous junctions. Candidate steps are ranked by a score accounting for direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and progress toward the target. Output is an ordered polyline (edge sequence) that serves as a suggested correction integrated into downstream skeleton workflows.
AI.result: Demonstrated qualitatively on brain vessel datasets, showing correction of typical 'crossing' and 'dotted' artifacts. Implemented in C++ with a Libigl-based interactive viewer; outputs a correction suitable for integration into subsequent skeleton post-processing. Validation is qualitative and the method is lightweight.
AI.conclusion: A practical, interactive building block for correcting topology artifacts in 3D tubular skeletons. It requires minimal user input and emphasizes stability and sensible bridging of gaps, though results are currently validated qualitatively and not as a fully automated solution.

title: Quadratic Forms for Measuring Geometric Trees in 3-dimensional Space
summary:
Tree-like structures appear in many areas of science, and their shapes can help understand the underlying processes they drive or that give rise to them.
  By thinking of these structures as geometric graphs in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we gain access to tools from computational geometry and topology to study them.
  In this paper, we adopt the theory of quadratic forms to measure the directional spread of geometric graphs, and we introduce the hexplot model -- equipped with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle -- to visualize, measure, and collect statistics.

AI.tldr: Introduces the hexplot model and a quadratic-form based approach to analyze the directional spread of tree-like geometric graphs in R^3, using a Fisher-metric-inspired hexplot for visualization and statistics.
AI.motivation: To enable quantitative analysis of directional dispersion in tree-like geometric graphs by applying tools from quadratic forms, with an emphasis on visualization and statistical collection.
AI.method: Adopts the theory of quadratic forms to measure directional spread and defines the hexplot model, equipped with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle, to visualize, quantify, and collect statistics on the geometry.
AI.result: Proposes a visualization and measurement framework (the hexplot) based on quadratic-form metrics and a Fisher-metric-derived distance, enabling quantitative analysis of directional spread in 3D geometric graphs.
AI.conclusion: The hexplot framework provides a novel geometric-tool for analyzing the directional dispersion of tree-like structures in 3D, integrating visualization with statistical analysis.

title: Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima
summary: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

AI.tldr: A reparametrization-invariant flatness measure based on the Fisher Information Matrix defines Riemannian sharpness; invariance under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; links SGD noise to a stationary distribution concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima; provides PAC-Bayes bound; empirical validation shows SR tracks generalization better than Euclidean sharpness on MNIST/CIFAR-10, with scaling in η/B.
AI.motivation: Resolve the non-invariance of Euclidean sharpness measures and address theoretical critiques (e.g., Dinh et al.) by grounding flatness in information geometry; provide a robust, reparametrization-invariant account of how SGD biases toward generalizable minima.
AI.method: Define Riemannian sharpness using the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM); prove invariance under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; discuss limitations of the diagonal empirical FIM and advocate structured estimators (e.g., K-FAC) for exact invariance; model mini-batch SGD gradient noise with covariance proportional to the FIM and derive the resulting SDE; obtain a stationary distribution concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima; derive a PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled by SR; perform experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 to compare SR against Euclidean sharpness and test scaling with learning rate and batch size.
AI.result: Invariance holds for the true FIM; the diagonal empirical FIM provides approximate invariance in practice; SR correlates with generalization better than Euclidean sharpness; the theoretical scaling of SR with η/B matches observations; K-FAC can restore exact invariance in practice; experiments support the proposed link between geometry (SR) and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant specification of why flat minima generalize, tying SGD dynamics to Fisher-information geometry and offering a robust diagnostic (SR) that aligns with generalization performance beyond traditional Hessian-based measures.

title: Scaling Generative Foundation Models for Chest Radiography with Rectified Flow Transformers
summary: We introduce the first generative foundation model for chest radiograph synthesis trained from scratch at the billion-parameter scale. Existing radiographic AI models often suffer from poor generalisation across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings, resulting in limited real-world clinical utility. Controlled, high-fidelity synthesis of chest radiographs is a promising path toward diversifying clinical datasets and evaluating the robustness of diagnostic models. Therefore, we present the largest specialist generative foundation model for chest radiographs to date, with over 1.3B parameters, trained for 1.6T tokens on a curated, heterogeneous dataset comprising 1.2M radiographs and clinical expert-guided metadata. Our model supports controllable radiograph generation and editing across multiple demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and a dozen pathologies. Moreover, we significantly advance the state of the art in radiograph synthesis fidelity, producing images that are indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts.

AI.tldr: First billion-parameter generative foundation model for chest radiographs, trained from scratch, enabling controllable, high-fidelity synthesis across demographics, views, and pathologies.
AI.motivation: Overcome poor cross-population generalization in radiograph AI and enable diversification and robust evaluation via synthetic data.
AI.method: Train a large-scale generative model (≈1.3B parameters) from scratch on 1.6T tokens derived from a curated dataset of 1.2M radiographs with expert-guided metadata; implement controllable generation/editing across demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and about a dozen pathologies.
AI.result: State-of-the-art radiograph synthesis fidelity; clinical experts cannot reliably distinguish generated from real radiographs; supports controllable generation across demographics, views, and pathologies; largest specialist chest radiograph model to date.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of high-fidelity, controllable chest radiograph synthesis at scale, with potential to diversify datasets and stress-test diagnostic models, while raising considerations regarding data provenance and misuse risk.

title: Computational Identifiability
summary: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself. Through several experiments, we demonstrate how this framework allows us to answer fine-grained, practical identification questions, such as identification with small finite samples, with ambiguous graphical criteria, with mixed observational-interventional data, and across counterfactual data and estimands. Code is available at https://github.com/lbynum/metadentify.

AI.tldr: Introduces computational identifiability: a finite, search-based framework to empirically identify causal effects, enabling practical identifiability with finite data under defined search procedures and priors.
AI.motivation: Identifiability in expectation is theoretical and often relies on asymptotics; there is a need for operational, finite-data criteria that reflect real-world data constraints and ambiguous or mixed data scenarios.
AI.method: Propose a finite computational search procedure that, given data and a prior over parameters, seeks an estimator achieving a specified error tolerance. If such an estimator is found, identifiability is declared under the search assumptions. Validate via experiments across small samples, ambiguous graphical criteria, mixed observational-interventional data, and counterfactuals; provide open-source code.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the framework yields practical identifiability results in settings where traditional criteria are inconclusive or inapplicable, including finite samples and ambiguous DAGs; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Computational identifiability offers a practical complement to theoretical identifiability, enabling finite-sample, data-driven identification decisions under explicit search assumptions; invites further exploration of search design and robustness across scenarios.

title: Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems
comment: 10 pages, 1 figure. To be published in the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Agentic Services which is part of the IEEE Conference on Web Services
summary: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

AI.tldr: AgenticRei introduces a deontic governance framework for LLM-driven agents, adding obligations, dispensations, and conflict resolution via an OWL-expressed Rei-based policy language evaluated by an external logic engine, addressing governance gaps beyond XACML/Rego/Cedar and aligning with A2AS.
AI.motivation: Current policy engines cover only permits/prohibits; there is a need for lifecycle management of obligations, dispensations, meta-policy conflict handling, and domain-ontology reasoning to govern autonomous agents that can access tools and coordinate across organizational boundaries.
AI.method: Develop a deontic policy language on the Rei framework, encode it in OWL, and evaluate it at runtime with a high-performance logic engine external to the LLM. Apply the same governance pipeline to tool invocations and agent-to-agent messages; demonstrate interoperability with industry frameworks like A2AS.
AI.result: Illustrative examples show that deontic policies capture governance constraints (obligations, dispensations, conflicts, domain reasoning) that current production engines cannot express; the approach demonstrates feasibility and runtime viability outside the LLM.
AI.conclusion: AgenticRei extends enterprise governance for autonomous agents by providing full deontic governance (obligations and dispensations) alongside traditional permits/prohibits, with OWL-based representation and external logic evaluation, and with compatibility to existing frameworks such as A2AS.

title: Physical Atari: A Robust and Accessible Platform for Real-time Reinforcement Learning on Robots
summary: We built a robot called the Robotroller that actuates an Atari CX40+ controller and a device called the Atari Devbox that renders the game frame and the reward signal from the Arcade Learning Environment on a screen. The Robotroller and the Atari Devbox, together with an off-the-shelf camera and a desktop computer, constitute a system that can be used to study reinforcement learning algorithms in the physical world. We call the full system Physical Atari. In this paper, we detail the key decisions that make Physical Atari a robust and accessible platform. To make the system robust, we designed the Robotroller so that all movement is done through bearings, which reduces wear. Additionally, we wrote software that monitors the state of the servos at a high frequency and intervenes to limit stress. To make the system accessible, we used affordable off-the-shelf components and parts that can be manufactured using consumer 3D printers. Physical Atari can be built for under $1,000 and has been used for weeks of non-stop reinforcement learning experiments without any mechanical failures. We used it to validate that reinforcement learning algorithms can learn directly on robots and show that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade the performance of policies. Our results underscore the importance of on-device adaptation for strong performance on robots.

AI.tldr: A low-cost, robust hardware platform (Physical Atari) enabling real-world reinforcement learning by combining a robotized Atari controller with an AI-deployable display/reward system; emphasizes accessibility, durability, and on-device adaptation to address distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: To enable reproducible, real-world RL experiments using inexpensive, manufacturable hardware, bridging the gap between simulation and deployment and highlighting how small domain shifts can impact policy performance.
AI.method: Hardware-and-software stack: a Robotroller actuating an Atari CX40+ controller, an Atari Devbox rendering frames/reward from the Arcade Learning Environment, a camera and a desktop PC; components are off-the-shelf and 3D-printed, with bearings to reduce wear and high-frequency servo monitoring to limit stress; system built for under $1,000 and tested through weeks of continuous RL experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates that reinforcement learning can be learned directly on robots; shows that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade policy performance; claims robustness and repeated mechanical reliability over extended experiments.
AI.conclusion: On-device adaptation is crucial for strong performance of robot policies; accessible, low-cost hardware platforms like Physical Atari are valuable for advancing physical RL research and enabling systematic studies of distribution shifts and system robustness.

title: LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression for Vision Transformer Distillation
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones, such as DINOv2, have become essential for downstream tasks like object recognition and semantic segmentation. The immense computational requirements of backbones often necessitate distillation into smaller architectures for edge deployment. Feature-based knowledge distillation (KD) often suffers from the teacher-student gap; the student struggles to imitate teacher's complex feature map due to its limited capacity. To mitigate this bottleneck, we propose LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression, a training curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation. By utilizing the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets, our curriculum allows the student to build a foundational representation before tackling higher-level abstractions. Our results demonstrate that this paradigm significantly accelerates convergence through adaptive difficulty selection across various student model sizes and dataset scales. With our curriculum, the LEAP-distilled ViT-S achieves 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100, a +12.24% improvement compared with baseline. On ImageNet-1K, LEAP achieves +3.84% and +7.75% improvement for the instance retrieval task on the Oxford and Paris datasets, respectively. Furthermore, the curriculum enables 25.1% savings in training FLOPs and 21% savings in training time on ImageNet-100 by implementing early-stopping for teacher inference during the initial stages of training. Code is available at https://github.com/KevinZ0217/LEAP

AI.tldr: LEAP introduces a layer-skipping curriculum for ViT feature-based KD that uses progressively harder teacher features to guide the student, achieving faster convergence and better accuracy with notable training cost reductions.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the teacher–student gap in feature-based KD for Vision Transformers and reduce training cost for large backbones by enabling adaptive, curriculum-based distillation.
AI.method: Use the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets; adaptive difficulty selection; include early-stopping of teacher inference to save FLOPs/time; evaluate across various student sizes and data scales.
AI.result: LEAP yields 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100 with ViT-S (+12.24% vs baseline); on ImageNet-1K instance retrieval: +3.84% (Oxford) and +7.75% (Paris); 25.1% training FLOPs savings and 21% training time savings on ImageNet-100.
AI.conclusion: The curriculum effectively bridges the teacher–student gap, accelerates convergence, and delivers substantial efficiency gains across scales; code available.

title: When to Trust, How to Distill: Multi-Foundation Model Guidance for Lightweight, Robust Scientific Time Series Forecasting
comment: KDD 2026, paper decision: Accepted, track: AI for Science. total 12 pages including references and appendix
summary: The deployment of Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) in physical sciences is hindered by a critical trade-off: while these models encode rich, universal temporal dynamics, they suffer from severe distributional misalignment when applied zero-shot to specific scientific domains, and their computational cost prohibits deployment in edge-computing sensor networks. We address a fundamental challenge: How can we extract latent structural knowledge from misaligned foundation models (FM) to train lightweight, specialized forecasters? We propose Gated Uncertainty-Aware Routing for Distillation (Guard), a novel framework that reframes multiteacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics, exploiting complementarity across diverse foundation models; and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that acts as a "circuit-breaker," automatically attenuating distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality. We evaluate our proposed lightweight framework on four climate-critical domains: meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, and energy grids. Our method significantly reduces RMSE relative to a fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline, successfully distilling knowledge from pretrained FMs (teachers) even when they exhibit suboptimal zero-shot accuracy due to distribution shift between the original and target data domains. We demonstrate that these domain-misaligned teachers can still serve as critical correctives, outperforming the globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances. Ultimately, this enables high-precision scientific forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment. Code is available at https://github.com/RupasreeDey/GUARD-KDD2026.

AI.tldr: Guard is a two-component distillation framework that adaptively selects and attenuates knowledge transfer from multiple misaligned Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) to produce lightweight, domain-specialized forecasters for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: TSFMs exhibit strong universal temporal dynamics but suffer from distributional misalignment in specific scientific domains and are costly for edge devices. There is a need to extract latent structural knowledge from misaligned FMs to train compact, accurate domain-specific forecasters.
AI.method: reformulates multi-teacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics to exploit complementarity among diverse FMs; (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that attenuates distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality, acting as a circuit-breaker. The framework distills knowledge from multiple teachers into a lightweight student forecaster evaluated on resource-constrained settings.
AI.result: Applied to four climate-critical domains (meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, energy grids), Guard achieves significant RMSE reductions compared with a fixed-weight multi-teacher baseline. It can distill knowledge from pretrained, domain-misaligned FMs even when zero-shot accuracy is suboptimal, and the misaligned teachers can outperform globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances. The approach enables high-precision forecasting suitable for edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: Guard provides a practical, resource-efficient pathway to leverage diverse, misaligned foundation models for accurate domain-specific forecasting by adaptively routing knowledge and gating distillation uncertainty, with code available at the cited repository.

title: Measuring Curriculum Alignment across Topical Coverage, Competency, and Cognitive Depth: A Longitudinal Framework Applied to CS2013 and CS2023
summary: Undergraduate computer science is governed by international curricular guidelines revised about once a decade, yet programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover the current guidelines and how that coverage shifts when the guidelines are restructured. We address this with a human-in-the-loop pipeline that measures a program's coverage of an external body of knowledge, applied longitudinally to one accredited BSc in Computer Science against Computer Science Curricula 2013 (CS2013) and 2023 (CS2023). The pipeline represents the program and each guideline as structured corpora, generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches by semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Of seven benchmarked retrievers, a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble was strongest, and a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model, so retriever choice must be measured. Both maps were validated by an independent second rater (Cohen's kappa 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, near-constant across a decade. Extending the same retrieve-then-confirm design to competency articulation and cognitive depth shows that the program articulates the competency for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, yet delivers it at the recommended depth for 76% of present units under CS2023 against 95% under CS2013, a gap reflecting the newer guideline's raised expectations, not the program. The longitudinal comparison separates persistent structural gaps (parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), uncovered against both guidelines and ABET, from differences that reflect the standard's evolution. The instrument is reusable and available from the authors on request.

AI.tldr: A human-in-the-loop pipeline measures how well a CS program covers external knowledge guidelines (CS2013/CS2023) and how coverage shifts with guideline restructuring. Using a single accredited BSc program, structured corpora, semantic retrieval, and human confirmation, it finds ~50% coverage in both guidelines over a decade, with updated depth expectations in CS2023 and persistent gaps in certain domains. The method is reusable and demonstrates how to compare guidelines over time, though generalizability is limited by sample size.
AI.motivation: To provide a reliable, reproducible method for quantifying and tracking how a computer science program covers evolving external curricular guidelines, enabling longitudinal assessment and gap identification.
AI.method: Represent program and guidelines as structured corpora; generate candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches via semantic retrieval; confirm matches with human judgment under a formal coverage definition. Evaluate seven retrievers; adopt a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble as best; compare a long-context model to a small sentence model. Use independent second raters to assess validity (Cohen's kappa). Compute coverage of knowledge units and, for competency articulation and cognitive depth, determine attainment depth relative to guideline expectations.
AI.result: The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, with near-constant coverage across a decade. About 88% of covered units are articulated as competencies, but only 76% meet the recommended cognitive depth for CS2023 (versus 95% for CS2013). Persistent gaps (e.g., parallel/distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals) exist and reflect guideline evolution more than program shortcomings. The retrieval approach (reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble) outperforms individual retrievers; independent raters achieve Cohen’s kappa of 0.64–0.69. The instrument is reusable and available on request.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a practical, reproducible framework to quantify and compare curriculum coverage across evolving guidelines, revealing both stable coverage and depth-related gaps. It supports targeted curricular alignment and can be extended to broader programs and other external guidelines; the instrument’s reuse potential is a key strength.

title: WorkBenchMark: A LEGO-Based Assembly Benchmark with an Assembly-by-Disassembly Baseline for the Smart Manufacturing League
summary: We introduceWorkBenchMark, a LEGO Duplo-based robotic assembly benchmark motivated by the RoboCup Smart Manufacturing League. Robotic assembly couples low-level manipulation with task-level symbolic reasoning under physical constraints, a combination that current end-to-end learning methods do not yet solve reliably. The benchmark provides 400 tasks across four complexity tiers. We provide an open-vocabulary perception, Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline solution. Our planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action approach across all tiers. The benchmark, simulation environment, and baseline implementation will be released openly to support the broader robotic assembly community.

AI.tldr: Introduces WorkBenchMark, a LEGO Duplo-based robotic assembly benchmark with 400 tasks across four complexity tiers; presents open-vocabulary perception and an Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline; a planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action baseline across all tiers; benchmark, simulator, and baseline will be released openly.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where end-to-end learning methods struggle with joint low-level manipulation and high-level symbolic planning under physical constraints in robotic assembly, and to provide a standardized, scalable benchmark for evaluating planning-informed perception and manipulation.
AI.method: Proposes an open-vocabulary perception framework and an Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline; implements a planning-based pipeline for robotic assembly; compares against a state-of-the-art vision-language-action approach across four task tiers using 400 tasks in the benchmark.
AI.result: The planning-based pipeline outperforms the modern vision-language-action approach across all tiers, indicating the value of planning and structured reasoning in robotic assembly tasks.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark, simulation environment, and baseline implementation will be released openly to support the broader robotic assembly community.

title: LooseControlVideo: Directorial Video Control using Spatial Blocking
summary: Precise 3D spatial orchestration in text-to-video generation remains a significant challenge, particularly for multi-object scenes where semantic layout and temporal dynamics are often entangled. While existing depth-conditioned models achieve good structural fidelity, they necessitate dense, frame-accurate guidance that is labor-intensive to author for dynamic events involving deformable objects. We present LooseControlVideo, a framework that enables intuitive and expressive control by using sparse, oriented 3D boxes as a "blocking" proxy. This allows users to author high-level layout and trajectory while leveraging a video generative model to generate realistic occlusions, dynamics and interactions. We achieve this by fine-tuning a Wan 2.2 backbone on a video dataset annotated with DNOCS, a novel encoding for 3D size, orientation and depth-ordered occlusions. Furthermore, our method allows for localized refinement, such as adjusting a jump trajectory or adding an interaction, with minimal disruption to the global scene context. Extensive evaluations on the nuScenes, HO-3D, and BEHAVE benchmarks demonstrate that LooseControlVideo significantly outperforms existing 2D-box and flow-based baselines. Our findings indicate a 1.2x to 3x improvement in Trajectory Error; 2x improvement in Rigid Motion Consistency; and a 1.5x to 2x increase in Occlusion Accuracy over current state-of-the-art layout-conditioned models, demonstrating that oriented 3D primitives provide good geometric prior for complex, multi-agent video authoring.

AI.tldr: Introduces LooseControlVideo, a framework enabling intuitive 3D-layout-based video generation using sparse oriented 3D boxes as blocking proxies, fine-tuned on DNOCS-annotated video data, achieving strong improvements over 2D-box/flow baselines.
AI.motivation: Precise 3D spatial control in text-to-video is hard due to entangled layout and dynamics; dense frame-accurate guidance is labor-intensive for dynamic/deformable scenes; there is a need for intuitive high-level control that preserves global context while enabling robust multi-object interactions.
AI.method: Use sparse, oriented 3D boxes as a blocking proxy to specify layout and trajectories; introduce DNOCS encoding for 3D size, orientation, and depth-ordered occlusions; fine-tune Wan 2.2 on a DNOCS-annotated video dataset; support localized refinements with minimal global disruption.
AI.result: On nuScenes, HO-3D, and BEHAVE, LooseControlVideo achieves 1.2x–3x lower Trajectory Error, ~2x higher Rigid Motion Consistency, and ~1.5x–2x higher Occlusion Accuracy compared with 2D-box and flow-based baselines, outperforming existing layout-conditioned models.
AI.conclusion: Oriented 3D primitives provide a strong geometric prior for multi-agent video authoring, enabling intuitive control and localized edits with minimal disruption to the global scene context.

title: Closing the Social-Semantic Gap: SPSD for Edge-Based Prompt Compression in Cloud LLM Inference
summary: The prefill stage of Large Language Model (LLM) inference is a growing contributor to cloud-scale energy cost. Many consumer-support and conversational prompts contain social scaffolding: politeness markers, apologetic preamble, repetition, and rapport-building language that is important for human communication but carries low marginal information for machine reasoning. We call this discrepancy the Social-Semantic Gap. We present SPSD (Sentiment Preserving Semantic Distillation), an edge-based pipeline that compresses user prompts using a 4-bit quantised Small Language Model before transmission to a cloud-deployed LLM. Evaluation on a 248-prompt corpus using Gemma-2-2B-Instruct (Q4_K_M) as the SLM and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the cloud evaluation model yields a mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call, with all 146 distilled calls yielding positive savings. Response quality, assessed by blind LLM-as-judge scoring across 121 pairs, is non-inferior to the raw path within a pre-specified 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; the judge awarded 43 percent ties, 28 percent distilled wins, and 29 percent raw wins. Cosine similarity is mixed: mean 0.682, median 0.712, with 54.1 percent of pairs above the 0.70 reference threshold. Safety-critical domains are conservatively routed to passthrough via rule-based gates. Per-call net energy saving is estimated at 70-270 uWh under stated assumptions. SPSD shows that on-device prompt distillation can reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin.

AI.tldr: On-device 4-bit distillation (SPSD) compresses prompts before cloud LLM, yielding substantial input-token savings (~100 tokens/call) with non-inferior responses; energy savings estimated at 70–270 μWh per call; safety gates route to passthrough for critical domains; validated on Gemma-2-2B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct.
AI.motivation: Reduce cloud inference energy and latency by addressing the social-semantic gap in prompts while preserving response quality.
AI.method: Edge-based pipeline using 4-bit quantised Small Language Model to distill prompts before transmission; evaluation on 248-prompt corpus; cloud model Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; blind LLM-as-judge scoring; cosine similarity analysis; safety-routing via rule-based gates; per-call energy estimates.
AI.result: Mean input token savings of 99.9 tokens per distilled call; all 146 distilled calls saved tokens; non-inferior response quality within a 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; judge results: 43% ties, 28% distilled wins, 29% raw wins; cosine similarity mean 0.682, median 0.712; 54.1% of pairs above 0.70; safety gating for critical domains; net energy savings 70–270 μWh per call under stated assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of on-device prompt distillation to reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin; supports potential energy efficiency gains in cloud-based LLM inference.

title: Diffusion Language Models: An Experimental Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized language modeling through autoregressive generation, enabling strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Recently, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have emerged as an alternative paradigm that generates text through iterative denoising rather than next-token prediction, allowing parallel refinement of entire sequences. While numerous diffusion-based architectures have been proposed, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and generation hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. In this work, we present a systematic experimental analysis of modern DLMs. Specifically, we evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving, while explicitly considering both generation quality and computational efficiency. Beyond downstream evaluation, we analyze the impact of key inference-time factors, including denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies, and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets. We show that the behavior of DLMs is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation of eight diffusion language models (DLMs) across eight benchmarks, examining both quality and efficiency and elucidating how inference-time choices shape trade-offs.
AI.motivation: DLMs enable parallel sequence refinement through denoising, offering potential benefits but with inconsistent evaluation protocols; need standardized comparisons to understand capabilities and deployment implications.
AI.method: Evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs on eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving; measure generation quality and computational efficiency; analyze inference-time factors such as denoising steps, context length, block size, parallel unmasking; include controlled smaller-model comparisons trained under identical conditions.
AI.result: Findings show that DLM behavior is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, producing distinct trade-offs between performance and efficiency that vary by task and budget; provides practical insights into deployment characteristics.
AI.conclusion: The study clarifies strengths and limitations of diffusion-based LMs, offering guidance for practitioners and advancing understanding of DLMs across architectures and inference budgets.

title: 3D Scene Graphs: Open Challenges and Future Directions
comment: Invited article for the Annual Review of Control, Robotics, and Autonomous Systems Volume 10
summary: 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) have emerged as a powerful representation for spatial AI by combining geometric grounding with semantic and relational abstractions of the environment. Their expressiveness has made them relevant to a broad range of problems in robotics and computer vision, including manipulation, navigation, task planning, scene understanding, and many others. However, the field remains fragmented: different communities adopt distinct formulations, construction pipelines, and evaluation protocols, making it difficult to compare methods, identify common assumptions, and assess remaining challenges for robust real-world deployment. This survey provides a unified and critical review of 3DSGs, with particular emphasis on open challenges and future directions. We first formalize 3DSGs under a common definition and analyze the principal modeling choices that characterize existing formulations, including node and edge attributes, hierarchical structure, dynamic scene representations, and affordance-aware extensions. We then review how 3DSGs are built from raw sensory observations, discussing the most common terminologies, conventions, and techniques. Finally, we examine downstream applications and evaluation strategies, from intrinsic graph quality to task-level performance. To support the community, we also provide a dedicated website that organizes and extends the surveyed content, accessible at https://3dscenegraphs.com/.

AI.tldr: Unified formalization and critical review of 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs): taxonomy, modeling choices, construction pipelines, and evaluation; identifies open challenges and provides a community resource for standardization and future work.
AI.motivation: 3DSGs integrate geometry, semantics, and relations for spatial AI, but the field is fragmented across communities, hindering comparability and deployment. A unified definition, taxonomy, and evaluation framework are needed to accelerate progress and real-world robustness.
AI.method: Formalize 3DSGs under a common definition; analyze modeling choices (node/edge attributes, hierarchy, dynamics, affordances); review construction pipelines and conventions; examine downstream applications and evaluation strategies; provide a dedicated community website with organized content.
AI.result: A cohesive framework and taxonomy for 3DSGs; synthesis of design choices and pipelines; guidance for evaluation and deployment; a central resource website to support community collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Highlights persistent open challenges and directions toward standardization, robust evaluation, and cross-domain collaboration to enable reliable real-world deployment of 3DSGs.

title: ImageWAM: Do World Action Models Really Need Video Generation, or Just Image Editing?
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) commonly rely on video generation to bridge visual world modeling and robot control. However, video-based WAMs face three coupled limitations: dense multi-frame future tokens make inference costly, full video prediction spends capacity on action-irrelevant temporal and appearance details, and long-horizon future imagination may introduce errors that mislead action prediction. These issues raise a simple question: Does world action model really need video generation? We propose ImageWAM, a simple WAM framework that repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. In contrast to video generation, image editing provides a better-matched prior: it only needs to model a target-frame transformation, focuses on action-relevant current-to-target visual differences, and grounds task instructions to localized visual changes through edit pretraining. In practice, ImageWAM does not decode the target frame at inference time; instead, it conditions a flow-matching action expert on the KV caches produced by image-editing denoising, using them as a compact world-action context. ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and matching competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across different simulator and real-world experiments. It also reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis further shows that editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions, supporting image editing as an effective alternative to video-based world-action modeling.

AI.tldr: ImageWAM replaces video generation with image-editing caches to guide action prediction, achieving competitive performance with far lower compute and latency.
AI.motivation: Video-based World Action Models (WAMs) are computationally intensive and prone to modeling irrelevant temporal/appearance details and long-horizon errors; a more task-focused prior could improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: Utilize pretrained image-editing models to generate KV caches from denoising edits; condition a flow-matching action expert on these caches as compact world-action context; avoid decoding a target frame during inference; leverage edit pretraining to ground changes to task instructions.
AI.result: Outperforms standard VLA baselines and matches competitive WAMs without extra policy pretraining across simulator and real-world tests; FLOPs reduced to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs; attention analysis shows caches focus on task-relevant regions.
AI.conclusion: Image editing-based WAM is an effective and efficient alternative to video-based WAMs for robot action prediction, with empirical support across diverse settings.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures
summary: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive performance analysis of Med-DDPM diffusion model for 3D MRI across NVIDIA architectures; two architecture-aware optimizations (TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last) yield huge reductions in compute and memory overhead while preserving synthesis quality.
AI.motivation: High GPU resource demands and heterogeneous kernel behavior impede practical deployment of diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis; need architecture-aware optimizations to improve efficiency.
AI.method: Empirical performance analysis across three NVIDIA generations; kernel-level breakdowns; evaluation of TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout; measuring SM cycles, dynamic instructions, Tensor Core utilization, IPC, and synthesis quality.
AI.result: Optimizations reduce SM cycles by up to 100x; dynamic instructions by 100x; Tensor Core utilization increases from 1.45x to 9.98x; IPC up by ~7%; synthesis quality unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations targeting kernel and memory patterns can dramatically improve diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis performance on modern GPUs without sacrificing output quality.

title: Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

AI.tldr: Deliberation among multiple agents is modeled as a closed-loop system with internal anchors; anchors can be recovered from deliberation data and explain why agents can exceed initial convex-hull bounds in confidence. The work shows a spectrum of anchor positions across three open-weight models and provides a test for when closed-loop dynamics are needed.
AI.motivation: To understand why multi-agent LLM deliberation improves reasoning, by reconciling social influence models with individual internal beliefs (anchors) and explaining when and how deliberation can move beyond initial opinion bounds.
AI.method: Model deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden anchor pulling its belief. Recover the anchor from observed deliberation, test generalization on held-out runs, and compare three open-weight model families to assess the role and location of anchors.
AI.result: Anchors can be recovered from deliberation data; an agent’s confidence in the correct answer can exceed the initial convex hull, consistent with anchor-driven dynamics. Across three model families, anchors are present with similar influence but different locations; only when an anchor sits far from initial opinions does the system escape the hull and require the full closed-loop description.
AI.conclusion: Deliberation dynamics are driven by internal anchors rather than purely social influence, yielding a spectrum of anchor effects. A simple generalization test can diagnose anchor-driven dynamics, and the necessity of a closed-loop model depends on anchor position.

title: DiffusionVS: A Generative Framework for Robust Visual Servoing Based on Diffusion Policy
summary:
Visual servoing is a fundamental technique in robotic manipulation and navigation. Regression-based visual servoing frequently experiences trajectory jitter as a result of noise-sensitive single-step mappings and the accumulation of errors during distribution shifts. In contrast, Diffusion Policy maintains temporal consistency by predicting action sequences and improves robustness through implicit data augmentation.
  This paper presents a novel diffusion-based servoing method. Based on Diffusion Policy, the proposed approach uses normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. To overcome the generalization limitations of models trained on static datasets, an online training paradigm is adopted, continuously expanding the diversity of training data through interactive experience collection. This strategy substantially enhances both the performance and generalization capability of the model. Comprehensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving success rates of nearly 100\% in simulation and 93\% in physical experiments. Beyond the specific pipeline, we further validate the generality of the diffusion mechanism. Experiments show that existing visual servoing networks consistently achieve improved performance when integrated with our diffusion-based module. These results indicate that the proposed strategy possesses broad applicability and can enhance various visual servoing systems beyond the specific architecture presented here.

AI.tldr: Diffusion Policy-based visual servoing with online experience collection improves temporal consistency and robustness, achieving near-perfect performance in simulation and strong real-world results, with broad applicability to other visual servoing networks.
AI.motivation: Mitigate trajectory jitter and error accumulation in regression-based visual servoing under noise and distribution shift by leveraging temporally-consistent action sequences and implicit data augmentation via diffusion models.
AI.method: Input consists of normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners. A conditional denoising diffusion model generates camera velocity. An online training paradigm regularly gathers interactive experiences to expand data diversity and improve generalization; the diffusion module is shown to be transferable to existing visual servoing architectures.
AI.result: In simulation, success rates approach 100%. In physical experiments, success around 93%. The diffusion-based module improves performance of existing visual servoing networks and demonstrates generality across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based servoing provides robust, generalizable performance enhancements for visual servoing, with practical online data collection enabling rapid adaptation; potential extensions include broader adoption across architectures and further exploration of efficiency and real-time feasibility.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: PerceptionDLM enables parallel region captioning with diffusion LLMs, delivering speedups over sequential region processing while maintaining quality; also introduces ParaDLC-Bench for evaluating parallel perception.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive MLLMs are inefficient for tasks requiring captions for multiple regions; diffusion-based decoders offer parallelism, but need architectures and benchmarks to exploit it.
AI.method: Propose PerceptionDLM (based on PerceptionDLM-Base) that uses efficient prompting and structured attention masking to perceive multiple masked regions in parallel, generating region descriptions at both sequence and token levels.
AI.result: Maintains competitive region-captioning performance while achieving substantial inference speedups for multi-region perception; introduces ParaDLC-Bench to evaluate parallel perception; code/models/datasets released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that diffusion language models can enable efficient parallel visual perception and parallel region captioning, marking a first in this capability and enabling future research and applications.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: ILL learns interpretable rules via a partition lattice; for PMFs, rules map to marginal constraints; lifting yields joint distributions; entropy lifting yields log-linear models; lattice edges reflect refinement/coarsening, not conditional dependence; viewed as structure learning for constraint-based factor graphs.
AI.motivation: Clarify connections between information-lattice learning, probabilistic graphical models, and maximum-entropy methods; enable inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.
AI.method: Alternate projection of the signal onto a partition lattice encoding abstractions and lifting selected rules to the signal domain; interpret rules as marginal laws of quotient variables; use feasible lifting (max-ignorance) with L2 uniformity; under Shannon-entropy lifting, derive a log-linear factor graph with factors indexed by abstractions.
AI.result: Rules correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables; a partition induces a deterministic quotient variable; special lifting implements max-ignorance reconstruction; entropy lifting yields log-linear factors; the information lattice is not a Bayesian network (edges denote refinement/coarsening, not conditional dependencies); frames ILL as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables.
AI.conclusion: ILL provides a principled bridge to graphical models and maximum-entropy frameworks, clarifying its relation to PGMs and offering directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: Forecast-grounded, regulator-aligned supervision for DeFi risk using DeXposure-Claw.
AI.motivation: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks; general-purpose LLMs over-read weak evidence and regulators lack an aligned evaluation metric for false alarms.
AI.method: DeXposure-FM (graph time-series foundation model) forecasts exposure networks; deterministic monitors and stress scenarios convert forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation; emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales; DeXposure-Bench provides a six-axis evaluation harness comparing tickets to regulator-aligned ground truth with explicit false-intervention rate.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: A modular, auditable, regulator-aligned supervision pipeline for DeFi risk that routes forecasted signals through structured evidence to produce auditable tickets with rationales and controlled false alarms.

title: Playful Agentic Robot Learning
summary: Current agentic robot systems can write executable Code-as-Policy programs, observe feedback, and revise behavior across multiple attempts, but they remain largely task-driven: reusable skills are acquired only after explicit instructions. We study Playful Agentic Robot Learning, where an embodied coding agent uses self-directed play as a continual skill-learning stage before downstream tasks arrive. We introduce RATs, Robotics Agent Teams designed for play-time skill acquisition. During play, RATs proposes novel yet learnable exploratory tasks, plans and executes robot-code policies, verifies intermediate progress, diagnoses failures, retries with dense, step-level feedback, and distills successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, the agent reuses relevant skills from this frozen library to help solve new tasks. Experiments in LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces show that play-learned skills improve held-out downstream tasks over no-play and random-play baselines, with 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces, respectively. Moreover, the learned skills can be plugged into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents by simply retrieving them into the context, improving RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without finetuning the underlying model.

AI.tldr: Playful Agentic Robot Learning introduces RATs to cultivate reusable, code-policy skills through self-directed play, yielding substantial improvements on downstream tasks and cross-domain transfer without finetuning.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation that current agentic robots acquire reusable skills only after explicit instructions; propose a continual skill-learning stage driven by play to build a persistent skill library.
AI.method: Robotics Agent Teams (RATs) conduct a play phase where agents propose novel but learnable exploratory tasks, plan/execute robot-code policies, verify progress, diagnose failures, retry with dense step-level feedback, and distill successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, relevant skills are retrieved to aid new tasks. Evaluations conducted on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces; assessed transfer to RoboSuite and real-world settings without fine-tuning.
AI.result: Play-learned skills yield 20.6 percentage-point gains on LIBERO-PRO and 17.0 points on MolmoSpaces over CaP-Agent0. Retrieved skills improve RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without any fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Playful, play-driven continual skill learning via RATs robustly enhances downstream task performance and cross-domain transfer, demonstrating the viability of a play-phase for skill acquisition before downstream deployments.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: A mixed-precision PTQ framework (Mix-QVLA) for VLA models that uses task-evidence-aware metrics to allocate precision while preserving evidence and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Improve the accuracy–efficiency trade-off in low-bit VLA deployment by ensuring quantization preserves task-relevant evidence and its temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Quantize-VLA variants are anchored to full-precision action-token references. Compute normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations; compare full-precision and quantized maps via evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion. Use a soft-bottleneck objective to aggregate boundary degradation into per-layer sensitivity, and model phase-dependent sensitivity for time-varying importance. Allocate mixed-precision bits under model-size and BitOps budgets.
AI.result: On OpenVLA-style policies, Mix-QVLA improves accuracy–efficiency trade-offs for low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, memory reduces from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB; average success 96.3 vs 97.1 for BF16; 1.52× inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: Evidence- and time-aware scoring enables effective mixed-precision quantization for VLA models, yielding substantial memory savings and speedups with minimal loss in task performance; the approach highlights the role of task-relevant evidence and phase dynamics in quantization.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: Three-force Decomposition explains Weibull weight-scale dynamics (λ) under AdamW: alignment drives early weight-norm growth, decay balances near saturation; introduces a spline-based method to recover alignment from sparse data and reveals a data-coherence effect on peak λ; code and data released.
AI.motivation: Explain observed dynamics of the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ during AdamW training by formulating a tractable force-budget decomposition of the weight update and validating it on transformer-scale models with known optimizer moments.
AI.method: Derive a leading-order three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update within a two-parameter Weibull framework. Validate on self-trained Pythia-70M with ground-truth optimizer moments across seeds, decomposing the RMS-to-Weibull offset into bridge and integration components. Develop a spline displacement method to recover the alignment force from sparse checkpoints for real models lacking optimizer moments, and assess dependence on training-data coherence.
AI.result: Alignment force dominates the rise phase (88-94% of the absolute force budget) and remains robust to removal of super-weights; near saturation, alignment and decay balance to explain weight-scale relaxation. The RMS offset into the Weibull reconstruction is ~5-6% in dense regions, decomposed into bridge and integration components. Spline displacement yields ~92-94% accuracy in recovering alignment from sparse data (≈2× improvement over two-point baseline). peak λ(t) varies with training-data coherence, indicating a data-dependent component. Code and data are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The observed λ(t) growth-and-relaxation dynamics are governed by a dominant alignment force in the early phase and a balancing decay as training saturates. The proposed three-force decomposition provides a mechanistic view of weight-norm evolution under AdamW and, with the spline method, extends to real models with sparse optimizer moments. Data coherence influences peak weight-scale growth, suggesting a targeted follow-up study.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: Cross-model attribution divergence reveals LLM epistemic limits on structured data; combining few-shot prompts and SHAP evidence reduces disagreement and improves calibration, suggesting a path to epistemic self-awareness.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLMs can recognize their knowledge limits on structured clinical data and reduce epistemic uncertainty via cross-model attribution divergence across models (Qwen 2.5 7B vs XGBoost).
AI.method: Compare LLM and XGBoost on a structured prediction task; analyze LLM verbalized confidence; examine inverse difficulty effect; apply few-shot prompts and SHAP-derived feature evidence; employ a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence to estimate reliability without training internals; evaluate ADS and calibration error.
AI.result: Four findings: (1) LLM confidence is largely constant and insensitive to actual accuracy; (2) inverse difficulty effect observed as LLM accuracy drops when XGBoost is highly accurate but aligns when task is moderately uncertain; (3) few-shot and SHAP evidence are orthogonal, synergistic in reducing ADS and boosting accuracy; (4) a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, without internal access or repeated inference.
AI.conclusion: Framed as a cold-start problem for LLMs on structured data; proposes a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness via attribution-based reliability signals and external calibrators.

title: Simulating Robotic Locomotion in Sand: Resistive Force Theory in an Open-Source Physics Engine
summary: Recent advancements in Resistive Force Theory (RFT) enable approximation of ground reaction forces for locomotion in sand without the computational expense of modeling interactions with individual grains. However, these tools have been absent in 3D physics engines commonly used for robot simulation. We explore if resistive force approximations are sufficient, when integrated with standard dynamics calculations, to provide a stable substrate for a freely walking robot. To determine this, we implement 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (3D RFT) in a physics simulation engine, MuJoCo.
