We verify simulations in multiple scenarios to demonstrate that key trends due to end effector shape, speed, and loading are preserved. Our implementation predicts walking distance and foot sinkage of a 12-Degree of Freedom hexapod robot within 20\% of experiments in sand. While RFT has inherent approximations, the open source tool described here has potential to help develop new and improved robot designs to traverse granular media substrates.

AI.tldr: 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory implemented in MuJoCo enables efficient sand locomotion simulation; predictions of walking distance and foot sinkage are within ~20% of sand experiments, preserving key trends across end-effector shape, speed, and load.
AI.motivation: Provide a computationally efficient alternative to grain-scale modeling for legged locomotion on granular media by extending Resistive Force Theory to 3D and integrating with standard physics engines.
AI.method: Develop and integrate 3D RFT into the MuJoCo physics engine; validate across multiple scenarios; analyze effects of end effector shape, speed, and loading; test on a 12-DOF hexapod; compare predicted walking distance and foot sinkage to experimental data in sand; release open-source tool.
AI.result: The model predicts walking distance and foot sinkage within 20% of sand experiments; trends with end-effector shape, speed, and loading are preserved; the open-source tool can aid design of robots for granular substrates.
AI.conclusion: 3D RFT embedded in a physics engine provides a viable, efficient substrate for simulating and optimizing legged locomotion on granular media, despite inherent approximations, and can spur development of improved robot designs.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: Language-directed vision embeddings: use language prompts at inference to steer the vision encoder, producing task-centric embeddings without retraining.
AI.motivation: Limitations of static feature extractors; enable task adaptation and controllable, generalizable vision representations via instruction-driven guidance to reduce hallucinations and improve VQA efficiency.
AI.method: Infer via language instructions that modulate the vision encoder to focus on task-relevant aspects, resulting in dynamic embedding formation without task-specific retraining.
AI.result: Empirically shows a +34 point reduction in MMVP hallucinations; outperforms large vision-language models on VQA despite far fewer parameters; generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LIVE demonstrates that language can directly guide visual representation learning at inference, offering an adaptive, instruction-driven path to robust, generalizable vision intelligence.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: Introduces multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) EDA to handle sparse optimization by modeling sparsity patterns and active values jointly via a latent Gaussian with separate indicator and value dimensions; proves identifiability and uses amortized inversion-based estimators; empirically improves Lunar Lander performance and sparsity.
AI.motivation: EDAs have been effective for continuous spaces but not sparse ones; existing sparse optimizers rely on hand-crafted sparsity operators; need a hierarchy-free, probabilistic model that can learn sparsity patterns and values jointly.
AI.method: Propose ZIG sampling law: latent Gaussian model with indicator (sparsity) and value (parameter magnitude) dimensions; estimators recover latent parameters; amortized inversion-based estimators; identifiability results; empirical evaluation on Lunar Lander comparing to dense EDA and sparse baselines.
AI.result: ZIG-EDA converges faster, achieves higher final returns than dense Gaussian EDA, hand-crafted sparse EA, and ad-hoc sparse EDA; discovers controllers with only a small fraction of active parameters; latent parameters identifiable from samples.
AI.conclusion: ZIG-EDA extends EDAs to sparse spaces without handcrafted sparsity operators; identifiability and practical estimators enable joint optimization of sparsity patterns and values; promising approach for sparse black-box optimization with potential broader impact.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: A continuous, differentiable phenotypic structure for contrastive learning in vision-language models for retinal AD risk; soft-weighted multi-positives derived from intra-modality embeddings improve incident AD prediction on UK Biobank data over discrete grouping.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of discrete phenotypic grouping in contrastive learning for vision-language medical data; capture the spectrum of disease risk with a learnable, continuous similarity signal and integrate cross-modal information.
AI.method: Introduce a differentiable weighting function that derives inter-subject similarity from intra-modality embeddings in retinal images and risk profiles, forming soft multi-positive relationships via a continuous aggregation operator; adopt a soft-target contrastive objective to jointly learn cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure end-to-end.
AI.result: The proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident Alzheimer's disease prediction.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal provides a principled, robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

title: Proprioceptive Invariant State Estimation for Humanoid Robots on Non-Inertial Ground
summary: This paper presents an invariant extended Kalman filtering (InEKF) approach for real-time state estimation of humanoid robots operating on non-inertial ground using only onboard proprioceptive sensing. The proposed approach estimates the robot's base position and velocity relative to the moving ground frame without requiring direct measurements of ground motion or externally mounted sensors. By exploiting kinematic constraints at the stance foot through foot-mounted IMUs, the filter accounts for ground-induced nonlinearities in the process and measurement models while remaining fully proprioceptive. The estimator is formulated to admit a right-invariant measurement model, enabling favorable error dynamics under large initial uncertainties. Observability analysis establishes conditions under which the robot's relative base position and velocity are observable with respect to the non-inertial ground frame. Experiments with the Digit humanoid robot standing and squatting atop a swaying and pitching ground showcase a 96% speedup in convergence rate and an 80% reduction in position estimate errors over existing InEKFs. Walking experiments on a uni-axially rotating ground achieve an average estimation error of less than 9 cm for an initial error of up to 1 m.

AI.tldr: Proprioceptive invariant EKF estimates humanoid base pose/velocity on non-inertial ground using foot-mounted IMUs and stance constraints, achieving fast convergence and improved accuracy with right-invariant filtering.
AI.motivation: Enable real-time, onboard state estimation for humanoids operating on moving ground without external sensors, leveraging proprioception and kinematic constraints to maintain stable control and planning.
AI.method: Invariant extended Kalman filter (InEKF) with a right-invariant measurement model. Uses foot-mounted IMUs at the stance foot to enforce kinematic constraints with the moving ground, accounts for ground-induced nonlinearities in process/measurement models, and provides observability analysis for relative base pose/velocity with respect to the non-inertial ground frame.
AI.result: Experiments on Digit humanoid show 96% faster convergence and 80% reduction in position error compared to existing InEKFs. Walking on uni-axially rotating ground yields average position error under 9 cm for initial error up to 1 m.
AI.conclusion: A fully proprioceptive, invariant formulation improves convergence rate and position accuracy for humanoid state estimation on non-inertial ground, with demonstrated practicality on real hardware.

title: GB-LSR: A Fast Local Spectral Image Representation with a Single Global Bandwidth for Continuous Reconstruction and Super-Resolution
summary: We present GB-LSR (Global-Bandwidth Local Spectral Representation), a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction. The image domain is partitioned into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable scalar bandwidth is shared globally across all patches and images, and reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size. We study three bandwidth-handling variants: a trainable global scalar (main), a fixed global scalar, and a per-patch bandwidth field. On a standardized native-reconstruction benchmark across Kodak, Set14, and Urban100, the main variant outperforms matched-budget amortized LIIF / LTE / WIRE re-implementations by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS, while running at roughly one-quarter of the slowest baseline's inference cost. The single global scalar suffices empirically: per-patch adaptive-bandwidth alternatives do not improve over it on either a closed-form locality diagnostic or an end-to-end ablation. In a separate arbitrary-scale super-resolution (ASR) extension, GB-LSR achieves competitive PSNR-Y under a canonical-style SR protocol and runs 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4; within the same extension, a variant trained and evaluated without 4-corner local-ensemble averaging gives a 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change, while additionally widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels gives a small positive PSNR shift with a 1.58x speedup and 31% lower peak memory. Native-reconstruction claims are scoped to the matched-budget amortized protocol, and ASR claims are scoped to a separate canonical-style SR protocol.

AI.tldr: GB-LSR: a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction using a single global bandwidth scalar. It outperforms matched-budget baselines in native reconstruction and offers faster ASR extensions; per-patch adaptive bandwidth is not beneficial.
AI.motivation: Improve efficiency and quality of continuous image reconstruction by reducing bandwidth-adaptation complexity and enabling constant-time basis contractions per coordinate.
AI.method: Partition the image into non-overlapping square patches, each with coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared encoder features. Use a single trainable global bandwidth scalar (main) shared across patches/images, enabling a fixed-size basis contraction at any coordinate. Compare three variants: trainable global scalar (main), fixed global scalar, and per-patch bandwidth field. Evaluate on Kodak, Set14, Urban100 for native reconstruction; extend to arbitrary-scale SR (ASR).
AI.result: Main variant outperforms matched-budget amortized LIIF/LTE/WIRE re-implementations by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS, with ~1/4 the slowest baseline inference cost. A single global scalar suffices; per-patch adaptive bandwidth does not improve locality diagnostics nor end-to-end ablations. In ASR, GB-LSR yields competitive PSNR-Y and is 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4. A no-4-corner-ensemble variant gives 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change; widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels yields a small PSNR gain with 1.58x speedup and 31% lower memory. Native reconstruction claims pertain to matched-budget amortized protocol; ASR to canonical-style SR protocol.
AI.conclusion: Empirically, a single global bandwidth scalar suffices for GB-LSR, with no added benefit from per-patch bandwidth fields. GB-LSR delivers favorable speed/memory efficiency and competitive quality in both native reconstruction and ASR settings.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: A minimal regularized self-play framework that uses a small amount of human driving demonstrations to align self-play policies with human behavior; achieves human-aligned driving with only 30 minutes of data and 15 hours of training on a consumer GPU.
AI.motivation: Self-play RL can yield effective but alien driving strategies that clash with human norms. The goal is to align policies with human preferences without heavy reward engineering or large human datasets.
AI.method: Introduce a regularization objective derived from human demonstrations on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. The approach leverages a small amount of human data (30 minutes) to guide self-play, claiming 2500x data efficiency over comparable imitation-learning baselines.
AI.result: Policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories. Training completes in ~15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. The work provides videos and full source code.
AI.conclusion: A small amount of human data acts as an effective regularizer for self-play, achieving human-aligned driving with significantly reduced reliance on large-scale demonstrations and reward engineering.

title: Emergent Alignment
summary: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

AI.tldr: Proposes an online alignment method for LLMs using a 'conscience' introspection step and a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) loss, enabling self-judged alignment without external evaluators across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompts, and zero-shot learning; claims Emergent Alignment via a single high-level introspective question that guides training to ethical behavior, contrasting prior Emergent Misalignment work.
AI.motivation: Address the risk of misalignment in LLMs and seek scalable, self-sufficient alignment by leveraging the model's own judgment rather than external judges; investigate whether introspective prompting can steer training toward ethical behavior across diverse use cases.
AI.method: Introduce a conscience step where the model reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and augment the training objective with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The method relies on a frozen copy of the model as the judge and applies this online across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot scenarios.
AI.result: Provides an online alignment technique applicable across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a separate weaker or stronger judge, instead using a frozen copy of itself. Demonstrates Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question can steer training toward an ethical model even in previously observed Emergent Misalignment (e.g., code hacking) scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework suggests that self-referential, introspective alignment via DPO can generalize across settings without external judges, yielding emergent ethical behavior. However, real-world validation, robustness across tasks, and potential prompts’ vulnerability require further study.

title: A Categorial and Sheaf-Theoretic Semantics for Autonomic Component Ensembles
summary: The proliferation of large-scale, decentralized systems of autonomous agents, such as swarms of robots and networked cyber-physical systems, presents a formidable challenge to traditional formal methods. The Software Component Ensemble Language (SCEL) offers a formal model for such systems, but its operational semantics is not ideal for reasoning about global, structural, and emergent properties. This report proposes a new, multi-layered mathematical model for SCEL using category theory and sheaf theory. We argue that a society of robots described in SCEL can be formally modeled as a sheaf on a topological space, where components are points, ensembles are open sets, and distributed knowledge forms the sheaf's data. In this framework, computational processes like information sharing become equivalent to the sheaf-theoretic operation of "gluing" local data. System failures can then be understood and quantified as topological obstructions, measurable by sheaf cohomology. This approach transforms the verification of a complex distributed system into the analysis of the geometry of a mathematical object, providing deep, structural insights for the design of robust autonomic systems.

AI.tldr: A high-level sheaf-theoretic model of SCEL-based swarm systems, recasting global properties and failures as geometric concepts.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of SCEL's operational semantics in reasoning about global, emergent, and structurally rich properties of large decentralized systems.
AI.method: Model a robot society described in SCEL as a sheaf on a topological space: components as points, ensembles as open sets; distributed knowledge as the sheaf data; information sharing as gluing; failures as topological obstructions quantified by sheaf cohomology.
AI.result: A theoretical framework that transforms verification into geometric analysis of a mathematical object, potentially yielding deep structural insights for robust autonomic design.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a global, structural view of distributed systems, enabling new verification perspectives, but practical applicability and computational methods remain to be developed.

title: Learning When to Denoise: Optimizing Asynchronous Schedules for Latent Diffusion
summary: Multi-representation diffusion models can improve visual synthesis by denoising complementary views of an image, but their performance depends critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised. We propose to learn this schedule. Our method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces and uses a schedule-corrected objective that keeps each representation's local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. We instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learn it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute. On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality under a matched 675M-parameter XL backbone. With AutoGuidance, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training. Training to 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL result of FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In the unguided setting, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 2.37, already below the best 800-epoch SFD-XL result (2.54) at 4x less training, and improves to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs. Code is available at https://github.com/bsq532087/LWD

AI.tldr: Learnable asynchronous schedule for multi-representation diffusion improves convergence speed and final image quality with minimal extra compute; achieves state-competitive FID on ImageNet 256x256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone, surpassing or matching larger baselines with substantially less training; code released.
AI.motivation: Multi-representation diffusion gains depend critically on the asynchronous denoising schedule; there is a need for a data-driven schedule to optimize when each representation is denoised.
AI.method: Formulate asynchronous flow matching across multiple representation spaces and introduce a schedule-corrected objective that keeps local noising-time weights fixed while the schedule changes. Use a flexible parametric schedule class that is convex and monotone, and learn it via a fast joint probe with <1% additional training compute.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256x256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone: AutoGuidance with 200 epochs reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training; 600 epochs yields FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In unguided mode: 200 epochs yields FID 2.37 (better than the 800-epoch SFD-XL 2.54), 600 epochs yields FID 2.14. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Learning the asynchronous schedule substantially improves convergence speed and final visual quality for multi-representation diffusion. The schedule can be instantiated as a convex, monotone parametric form, trained with minimal overhead, and yields competitive or superior results relative to larger baselines.

title: ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

AI.tldr: ProMUSE is an uncertainty-guided, staged multimodal AD diagnostic framework that starts with low-cost clinical data and selectively incorporates MRI/PET when needed, achieving comparable accuracy with substantial reductions in imaging usage.
AI.motivation: There is a need for early Alzheimer's disease diagnosis while reducing reliance on costly MRI/PET imaging and enabling practical clinical workflows given variable access to full-modality data.
AI.method: ProMUSE uses an evidential classifier on clinical data with Dirichlet-based subjective logic to quantify uncertainty. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, it progressively incorporates MRI or PET features. Modality fusion is performed via Dempster-Shafer theory to combine belief and uncertainty for a calibrated multimodal prediction.
AI.result: Across ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS datasets for CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks, ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, enabling cost savings without sacrificing performance.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware, staged data acquisition via ProMUSE provides a practical, resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening, balancing diagnostic accuracy with data acquisition costs.

title: ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence
summary: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

AI.tldr: ITNet is a universal learnable integral transform that subsumes convolution, self-attention, and autoregressive recurrence as special cases, achieving competitive results across vision, NLP, and 3D tasks with efficient computation.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation among architectural inductive biases (locality, memory, content-dependent interactions) by proposing a single learned operator capable of modeling pairwise interactions and approximating continuous operators.
AI.method: Define a kernel-based integral transform whose kernel depends on positions and features and is implemented as an MLP modeling pairwise interactions. Show that convolution, self-attention, and autoregressive recurrence arise as special cases under parameter choices. Develop practical computation techniques (tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, learned low-rank factorization). Use a shared ITNet operator with lightweight modality-specific encoders and evaluate on ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, NLVR2.
AI.result: ITNet matches or exceeds specialized baselines across the evaluated domains, and the authors prove ITNet is a universal approximator for continuous operators, with empirical evidence that a single learned interaction mechanism can replicate the behaviors of CNNs, transformers, and RNNs from data.
AI.conclusion: A single learned interaction mechanism can unify the three architectural families (CNNs, transformers, RNNs) by learning a flexible integral transform, offering both theoretical universality and practical efficiency.

title: SCAN-Planner: Spatial Collision-Aware Local Planning for Route-Guided Long-Range Quadruped Navigation
summary: Quadruped robots are increasingly expected to navigate through narrow passages, cluttered indoor scenes, and large-scale 3D unstructured environments. Existing local planners commonly approximate the robot using isotropic geometric inflation or rely on planar and elevation-map representations, leading to conservative motion in tight spaces and limited reasoning about overhanging structures. This letter presents SCAN-Planner, a spatial collision-aware local planning framework for long-range quadruped navigation. A yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint is used to model the elongated robot body, enabling whole-body collision evaluation through sparse queries in an inflated 3D occupancy map. We further introduce a projected A* search that generates collision-free guidance on an interpolated ground-following surface, with z-gradient suppression to avoid obstacles horizontally while maintaining vertical stability. For large-scale deployment, a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback provides high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that SCAN-Planner generates safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in dense clutter, 3D unstructured scenes, stair traversal, and long-range navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: SCAN-Planner is a spatially aware local planner for long-range quadruped navigation that improves collision checking and planning in 3D cluttered environments by using a yaw-aware twin-cylinder robot footprint, an inflated 3D occupancy map for sparse collision queries, a projected A* over a ground-following surface with z-gradient suppression, and a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback for recovery.
AI.motivation: Existing local planners struggle in 3D cluttered or overhanging environments due to isotropic inflation and reliance on 2.5D representations, causing conservative maneuvers and poor handling of overhangs. A need exists for whole-body collision awareness and scalable, robust long-range planning for quadrupeds.
AI.method: Propose SCAN-Planner: (1) yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint to model elongated robot body for whole-body collision evaluation via sparse queries in an inflated 3D occupancy map; (2) projected A* search that generates collision-free guidance on an interpolated ground-following surface with z-gradient suppression to avoid horizontal obstacles while maintaining vertical stability; (3) robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback for high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends; (4) validated in simulation and real-world on dense clutter, 3D scenes, stairs, and long-range navigation.
AI.result: Demonstrates safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in challenging environments, with successful long-range navigation and stair traversal in both simulation and real robot experiments using the proposed SCAN-Planner.
AI.conclusion: SCAN-Planner provides a comprehensive local planning framework that enhances collision-aware navigation for quadrupeds in 3D clutter and unstructured settings by integrating a realistic robot footprint, 3D occupancy-based collision checks, a geometry-aware search, and robust local mapping for recovery.

title: TeleMorpher: Toward Robust Simultaneous Motion-Location Editing
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image and video generation and editing. While recent studies have extended these efforts toward motion editing, simultaneously transforming both motion and location-despite its practical importance-remains largely unexplored. To better understand robust motion-location editing, we first analyze the fundamental factors that degrade its quality. Based on this analysis, we propose TeleMorpher, one of the first one-shot frameworks to the best of our knowledge, for simultaneous motion-location editing. Our approach leverages motion priors, a target motion-centric video generated from an off-the-shelf model as motion-editing guidance, and the ground truth motion to enable more controllable and precise motion-location editing. Via this, our framework works as follows: (1) we first disentangle the protagonist and the background via pre-trained segmentation and inpainting models. (2) Then, we introduce a training-free pose warping that edits the protagonist's motion with the motion prior as the guidance. (3) The result of warped motion video is directly injected into a baseline motion editor during inference, mitigating the difference between source and target motions while preserving the appearance of the source video. (4) To enhance the reliability of quantitative evaluations, we propose two new LPIPS-based metrics that measure the background consistency before and after the motion editing and the fidelity of motion editing performance via measuring the difference between the extracted protagonist's skeletons from source and target videos. Experiments with in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset demonstrate that TeleMorpher achieves superior performance across both quantitative and qualitative measurements (real-human evaluation), underscoring its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: TeleMorpher enables one-shot simultaneous motion-location editing in diffusion-model videos by using motion priors and a motion-centric guidance video, with foreground-background disentanglement, a training-free pose warping step, and an inference-time injection into a baseline editor, plus new LPIPS-based metrics for background consistency and motion fidelity; experiments show superior results on in-the-wild and TaiChi data.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based editing methods either target motion or location separately, and robust joint motion-location editing remains underexplored due to degradation factors such as misalignment, background leakage, and temporal inconsistency. A practical one-shot framework is needed for controllable, precise editing with limited data.
AI.method: (1) Disentangle foreground (protagonist) and background using pre-trained segmentation and inpainting. (2) Apply a training-free pose warping to adjust the protagonist's motion guided by a motion prior. (3) Inject the warped motion video into a baseline motion editor during inference to align source and target motions while preserving appearance. (4) Propose two LPIPS-based metrics: background consistency before/after editing, and motion-edit fidelity via skeleton difference; evaluate on in-the-wild and TaiChi datasets with real-human evaluation.
AI.result: TeleMorpher achieves superior performance on both quantitative metrics and qualitative assessments, including real-human evaluation, indicating more controllable and precise motion-location editing compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: A practical, data-efficient one-shot framework for joint motion-location editing in diffusion-model videos, leveraging motion priors and pose warping, with novel evaluative metrics and demonstrated robustness on diverse datasets.

title: cAPM: Continual AI-Assisted Pace-Mapping with Active Learning
summary: Ventricular tachycardia is a life-threatening rhythm disorder and a major cause of sudden cardiac death. Pace-mapping is a clinical procedure for identifying the intervention target during catheter ablation of VT. It requires clinicians to pace different sites in the ventricles and rapidly interpret the resulting electrocardiograms to determine where to pace next or whether a target site has been identified. Active learning AI models have been proposed to guide clinicians to the next pacing site, showing promise in reducing the number of pacing sites and improving the efficiency of pace-mapping. Existing methods require retraining each target without the ability to transfer knowledge across multiple VTs within the same patient or across patients. We introduce cAPM for continuous AI-assisted pace-mapping to capture and transfer knowledge accumulated from past pace-mapping data to reduce the number of pace-mapping data needed for future target VTs. This is made possible by a task-agnostic surrogate neural network that learns the mapping from pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology, an active-learning strategy that refines this surrogate model by selecting the most informative pacing site for each target, and a continual learning strategy to do so sequentially while retaining knowledge from prior targets. Evaluated on an in-silico testbed consisting of sequentially-presented localization tasks across different physiological conditions and ventricular geometries, cAPM with and without replay of past data samples achieved an 81% probability of localizing within clinical tolerance (5 mm accuracy) using 4.5 pace-mapping sites, compared to the state-of-the-art active-learning method achieving 38% probability using 13.7 pacing sites. These results provide a strong basis for preparing cAPM towards in-vivo preclinical and clinical studies where it can be used to guide pace-mapping.

AI.tldr: Continuous AI-assisted pace-mapping (cAPM) enables cross-target knowledge transfer via a surrogate model, active learning, and continual learning, reducing required pacing data.
AI.motivation: Pace-mapping is data-intensive and time-consuming. Transferring knowledge across successive VTs and patients could reduce pacing sites, procedure time, and improve reliability.
AI.method: A task-agnostic surrogate neural network maps pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology; an active-learning strategy selects the most informative pacing site for each target; a continual learning approach with replay preserves knowledge across sequential targets. Evaluation used an in-silico testbed with varying physiology and ventricular geometry.
AI.result: cAPM achieved 81% probability of localizing within 5 mm accuracy using 4.5 pacing sites, vs 38% with 13.7 sites by a state-of-the-art method; results included with and without replay of past data.
AI.conclusion: cAPM shows strong data-efficiency gains and transfer ability for pace-mapping in VT ablation and is promising for in-vivo preclinical/clinical studies.

title: Uncertainty Decomposition for Clarification Seeking in LLM Agents
summary: Recent position papers argue that the classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty framework is insufficient for interactive large language model (LLM) agents and call for underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations that can unlock new agent capabilities such as proactive clarification seeking and shared mental-model building. Practical deployment constraints -- black-box APIs, interactive latency budgets, and the absence of labeled trajectories -- rule out logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods, leaving prompt-based estimation as the most viable family for surfacing such signals at deployment time. We answer this call with a simple prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u), enabling the agent to ask for clarification when the task specification is ambiguous. To evaluate it, we introduce two clarification-augmented benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification) in which 50% of tasks are deliberately underspecified, and systematically compare the proposed decomposition against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on these variants together with the standard WebShop, ALFWorld, and REAL benchmarks for fault detection. Averaged across the five backbones, the proposed decomposition improves clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification by 73% over ReAct+UE and by 36% over UAM, and leads clarification F1 on every backbone on WebShop-Clarification and on four of five backbones on ALFWorld-Clarification, indicating that the gains generalize beyond a single LLM.

AI.tldr: Prompt-based decomposition of task uncertainty into action confidence vs. request uncertainty improves clarification performance for interactive LLM agents under deployment constraints.
AI.motivation: Address the insufficiency of classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty frameworks for interactive LLMs and enable proactive clarification without training data or labeled trajectories, given black-box APIs and latency constraints.
AI.method: A simple prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u); two clarification benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification) with 50% underspecified tasks; evaluation across five LLM backbones against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) on standard benchmarks for fault detection.
AI.result: On ALFWorld-Clarification, clarification F1 improves by 73% vs ReAct+UE and 36% vs UAM; on WebShop-Clarification, gains in F1 across all backbones, and on four of five backbones for ALFWorld-Clarification; gains generalize across multiple LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based decomposition yields a deployment-friendly approach to surface uncertainty signals that enable proactive clarification; results generalize across models; promising direction for decomposed, underspecification-aware uncertainty in interactive agents.

title: pdSTL: Probabilistic Differentiable Signal Temporal Logic for Stochastic Systems
summary: Autonomous robots operating in uncertain environments must satisfy complex temporal and safety specifications despite stochastic dynamics and sensing noise. While Signal Temporal Logic (STL) offers robustness measures for gradient-based optimization, existing extensions either lack differentiability or ignore belief-space uncertainty. We introduce pdSTL (probabilistic differentiable Signal Temporal Logic), a framework that unifies probabilistic semantics with differentiable robustness over belief trajectories. pdSTL employs interval-valued probabilistic semantics to compute conservative satisfaction bounds, propagated compositionally through the STL syntax tree. We formulate the temporal robustness evaluation as a recurrent, LSTM-style unfolding of STL operators, enabling linear-time, differentiable monitoring suitable for end-to-end trajectory optimization. We validate pdSTL on simulated obstacle avoidance, lane-change maneuvers, and real-world Crazyflie quadcopter flight experiments under aerodynamic disturbances. Results demonstrate that pdSTL achieves efficient optimization with formal probabilistic guarantees, significantly outperforming deterministic differentiable STL in maintaining safety margins under real-world uncertainty.

AI.tldr: pdSTL is a differentiable, probabilistic extension of Signal Temporal Logic that incorporates belief-space uncertainty via interval-valued semantics, enabling linear-time, end-to-end differentiable trajectory optimization with probabilistic guarantees.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in uncertain environments face stochastic dynamics and sensing noise. There is a need for a differentiable robustness measure that accounts for belief-space uncertainty and yields probabilistic safety guarantees during trajectory optimization.
AI.method: Introduce pdSTL with interval-valued probabilistic semantics, propagating conservative satisfaction bounds through the STL syntax tree. Reformulate temporal robustness as a recurrent, LSTM-style unfolding of STL operators to enable linear-time, differentiable monitoring over belief trajectories for end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: Validated on simulated obstacle avoidance, lane-change maneuvers, and real-world Crazyflie quadcopter flights under aerodynamic disturbances. pdSTL provides efficient optimization with formal probabilistic guarantees and outperforms deterministic differentiable STL in maintaining safety margins under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: pdSTL enables probabilistic guarantees within differentiable STL, suitable for end-to-end trajectory optimization in uncertain environments, offering scalable, safe monitoring and improved robustness under real-world disturbances.

title: Vortex: Multi-Modal Fusion System for Intelligent Video Retrieval
summary: This paper presents Vortex, the multimodal video retrieval system developed by our team, FocusOnFun, for the Ho Chi Minh City AI Challenge 2025, designed to advance intelligent multimedia search and temporal reasoning. The system integrates adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata generation from vision-language and speech models, and a hybrid retrieval strategy that fuses CLIP and SigLIP2 embeddings through Reciprocal Rank Fusion to balance global and fine-grained semantics. To enhance interactivity, Vortex incorporates Rocchio-based relevance feedback and a multi-stage temporal search mechanism for sequential event alignment. Built on Milvus and Elasticsearch, the architecture enables scalable indexing and efficient retrieval. Evaluated in the official competition, our FocusOnFun team's system achieved a score of 79.6/88 (90.5\%) in the Preliminary Round and was further evaluated in the Final Round, achieving an `Excellent' overall performance with `Outstanding' results in the question-answering (QA) task. This demonstrating the complementary strengths of CLIP and SigLIP2 and confirming the effectiveness of the hybrid retrieval approach. The system establishes a robust foundation for future research in intelligent, context-aware, and interactive video retrieval.

AI.tldr: Vortex is a Milvus/Elasticsearch-based multimodal video retrieval system that fuses CLIP and SigLIP2 with Reciprocal Rank Fusion, supports adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata from vision-language and speech models, Rocchio feedback, and multi-stage temporal search for scalable, interactive video retrieval, achieving high competition performance.
AI.motivation: to push forward intelligent, context-aware video search with temporal reasoning by integrating multimodal signals and a hybrid fusion strategy.
AI.method: Adaptive keyframe extraction; multimodal metadata generation from vision-language and speech models; hybrid retrieval by fusing CLIP and SigLIP2 embeddings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion; Rocchio-based relevance feedback; multi-stage temporal search for sequential event alignment; scalable indexing on Milvus and Elasticsearch.
AI.result: Competition results: prelim score 79.6/88 (90.5%), final round rated Excellent with Outstanding QA; demonstrates complementary strengths of CLIP and SigLIP2 and the effectiveness of the hybrid retrieval approach.
AI.conclusion: The work validates that combining CLIP and SigLIP2 with a hybrid retrieval framework yields robust, context-aware video retrieval and supports further research in interactive, semantically rich multimedia search.

title: Protein Representation Learning with Secondary-Structure and Energy-Filtered Hydrogen-Bond Graphs
summary: Graph-based representations are widely used in protein modeling, yet many existing approaches rely primarily on sequence adjacency or geometric proximity, which only partially reflect the principles governing protein folding. Proteins instead adopt complex three-dimensional conformations organized around secondary structure elements, such as $α$-helices and $β$-sheets, which encode recurring local motifs and stabilizing hydrogen-bond interactions. In this work, we introduce a secondary-structure-aware graph neural network for protein representation learning. Residue-level node representations are augmented with secondary structure assignments, and graph edges are constructed from hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by their energetic strength. This design enables the model to capture both local structural context and long-range couplings that are central to protein stability and function. We evaluate the proposed approach on commonly used protein benchmarks and observe consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods. In addition, the resulting graph representations offer enhanced biological interpretability, as the learned connectivity aligns with established structural motifs. These findings suggest that incorporating secondary structure and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning. The code is released at https://github.com/mohamedmohamed2021/SSProNet

AI.tldr: A secondary-structure-aware graph neural network for proteins that uses residue-level nodes with secondary structure labels and edges based on energy-filtered hydrogen bonds, achieving improved performance and interpretability over standard graph-based methods.
AI.motivation: Current graph representations often rely on sequence adjacency or geometric proximity, which do not fully capture the principles of protein folding that are governed by secondary structures and hydrogen-bond networks. Incorporating these motifs can provide a more faithful inductive bias.
AI.method: Construct a GNN where residue-level nodes are augmented with secondary structure assignments. Edges are defined by hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by energetic strength. The model learns representations that integrate local secondary-structure context with long-range hydrogen-bond couplings, with training/evaluation on standard protein benchmarks.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods on common benchmarks and provides enhanced interpretability, with learned connectivity aligning with established structural motifs.
AI.conclusion: Integrating secondary structure information and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning.

title: Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops
summary: Formal tools such as SAT and SMT solvers are increasingly embedded in language model reasoning pipelines when a safety or security critical question can be formulated in logic. Unlike chain of thought whose steps are sampled from the model distribution without formal guarantee, a solver produces a sound and independently verifiable answer. However, the soundness guarantee can be lost in the interaction between the solver and the model. The hybrid pipeline has three components: formalizing the question, deciding it, and narrating the result. Prior work has studied the formalization and decision, but not narration, which is the step that turns a formal tool's output into the user answer. To fill the narration gap, we first model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure. We further evaluate five open-sourced models under prompt injection, and we find certificate gating makes the solver verdict sound, while an adversary can invert a verified conclusion across phrasings and channels. We study the mitigation through hardened prompt that reduces injection significantly but cannot eliminate it and still suffers under adaptive attack. Combining the formal analysis and empirical studies, we show in the LLM-solver loop, robustness does not reach to the answer that the user finally reads.

AI.tldr: Formalizes the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure, empirically analyzes prompt-injection robustness across five models; finds that while certificate gating improves soundness, adversaries can invert conclusions across phrasing; hardened prompts help but do not prevent adaptive attacks; robustness does not guarantee the final user-visible answer.
AI.motivation: Address the narration gap in LLM-solver pipelines and ensure sound, verifiable outcomes when using formal tools inside language models.
AI.method: 1) Model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure. 2) Evaluate five open-source models under prompt-injection attacks. 3) Analyze 'certificate gating' as a mechanism to bind solver verdicts. 4) Propose and test hardened prompts; assess adaptive attacks. 5) Combine formal analysis with empirical results.
AI.result: Certificate gating improves solver verdict soundness; attackers can still invert verified conclusions across different phrasings and channels; hardened prompts reduce injection but do not eliminate it; adaptive attacks remain effective; robustness does not extend to guaranteeing the final user-visible answer.
AI.conclusion: In LLM-solver hybrids, soundness and robustness of the formal component do not ensure the user-facing narration or answer is trustworthy. The narration layer remains vulnerable; calls for stronger end-to-end defenses and safer interaction designs.

title: One Demo is Worth a Thousand Trajectories: Action-View Augmentation for Visuomotor Policies
summary: Visuomotor policies for manipulation have demonstrated remarkable potential in modeling complex robotic behaviors, yet minor alterations in the robot's initial configuration and unseen obstacles easily lead to out-of-distribution observations. Without extensive data collection effort, these result in catastrophic execution failures. In this work, we introduce an effective data augmentation framework that generates visually realistic fisheye image sequences and corresponding physically feasible action trajectories from real-world eye-in-hand demonstrations, captured with a portable parallel gripper with a single fisheye camera. We introduce a novel Gaussian Splatting formulation, adapted to wide FoV fisheye cameras, to reconstruct and edit the 3D scene with unseen objects. We utilize trajectory optimization to generate smooth, collision-free, view-rendering-friendly action trajectories and render visual observations from corresponding novel views. Comprehensive experiments in simulation and the real world show that our augmentation framework improves the success rate for various manipulation tasks in both the same scene and the augmented scene with obstacles requiring collision avoidance.

AI.tldr: Data augmentation framework for visuomotor manipulation using wide-FoV fisheye Gaussian Splatting to synthesize realistic image sequences and feasible trajectories from real eye-in-hand demos, improving robustness to distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Visuomotor policies are brittle to small changes in initial pose and unseen obstacles; efficient augmentation is needed to boost generalization without extensive data collection.
AI.method: Gaussian Splatting adapted for fisheye cameras to reconstruct and edit 3D scenes (including unseen objects); trajectory optimization to generate smooth, collision-free action trajectories; render new views to create augmented image sequences aligned with feasible actions from real eye-in-hand demonstrations.
AI.result: Experiments in simulation and on real hardware show improved success rates for manipulation tasks in both the original scene and augmented scenes containing obstacles requiring collision avoidance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed augmentation framework enhances robustness of visuomotor policies to distribution shifts with limited data, enabling better generalization across scenes with obstacles.

title: Exploring Multi-Modal Large Language Models and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Fashion Image Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval retrieves a target image using a composed query of a reference image and a modified text description. In the fashion domain, this task requires understanding subtle attribute variations such as color, pattern, and texture. However, existing approaches face limitations due to scarce annotated data and simplistic negative sampling. We propose a novel framework that integrates a multi-modal large language model (LLaVA) to generate attribute-aware triplets and introduces a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to enhance contrastive learning. We leverage pretrained vision-language models, such as CLIP-ViT/B32, to generate and concatenate sentence-level prompts with the relative caption and to scale the number of negatives using static representations. Experimental results demonstrate enhanced compositional reasoning and improved fine-grained retrieval behavior, underscoring the feasibility and potential of the proposed framework for fashion retrieval.

AI.tldr: A framework for composed image retrieval in fashion that uses a multi-modal LLM (LLaVA) to generate attribute-aware triplets and a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to improve contrastive learning, leveraging CLIP-based prompts and static negatives to boost fine-grained retrieval and compositional reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of fine-grained attribute variation (color, pattern, texture) in fashion retrieval under limited annotated data and weak negatives, by creating richer, attribute-focused training signals through an LLM and improved sampling.
AI.method: Generate attribute-aware triplets with LLaVA; apply a two-stage fine-tuning pipeline to enhance contrastive learning; use pretrained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP-ViT/B32) to produce sentence-level prompts, fuse them with relative captions, and scale negatives via static representations.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved compositional reasoning and fine-grained retrieval behavior in fashion retrieval, indicating the approach’s effectiveness and feasibility.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-generated, attribute-aware signals with staged contrastive fine-tuning can enhance composed image retrieval, with potential applicability beyond fashion to other Fine-Grained Cross-Modal tasks.

title: Physics-Informed Discovery of Yield Functions in Plasticity via Convex Neural Representations
summary: Identifying anisotropic yield functions remains challenging since yielding is not directly observed in full-field mechanical measurements, directional calibration can require many loading directions, and selecting an appropriate analytical form is nontrivial. This study proposes a physics-informed framework for discovering yield functions from full-field displacement data and reaction force data, without stress observations, plastic strain measurements, direct yield surface data, or a prescribed parametric yield function. The framework identifies the yield function as a mechanically constrained constitutive component inside elastoplastic stress integration, rather than through direct stress-space supervision. The yield function is represented by a convex neural network that enforces convexity and positive homogeneity of degree one while imposing the assumed tension-compression symmetry, and this neural yield function is trained with a differentiable stress update and a physics-informed force equilibrium loss across multiple loading cases. The proposed framework is validated using finite element (FE) benchmark studies with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield functions, assessing yield contour agreement, displacement-noise sensitivity, identifiability through plastically active stress states, epistemic uncertainty, and polynomial-surrogate deployment. This study provides a mechanics-constrained pathway for discovering anisotropic yield functions from displacement and force data while keeping the identified component within the structure of elastoplastic stress integration.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed, convex neural network framework discovers anisotropic yield functions from full-field displacement and reaction data without direct stress or yield data, integrating a convex, positively homogeneous yield surface into elastoplastic stress updates and validating against standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Identify biologically-like? No. The motivation is to infer yield surfaces from displacement/force data when stress states or explicit yield measurements are unavailable and to avoid prescribing a parametric form, enabling anisotropic yield surfaces constrained by mechanics.
AI.method: Represent the yield function as a convex neural network enforcing convexity, positive homogeneity of degree one, and tension–compression symmetry. Train within elastoplastic stress integration using a differentiable stress updater and a physics-informed force balance loss across multiple loading cases. Validate on FE benchmarks with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield criteria, assessing yield contour accuracy, noise sensitivity, identifiability via plastically active states, epistemic uncertainty, and surrogate deployment.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate yield contour alignment with classical criteria, robustness to displacement noise, identifiability through active plastic states, and quantified uncertainty; shows feasibility of a neural-yield function integrated in elastoplastic simulations and potential for polynomial surrogate deployment.
AI.conclusion: Provides a mechanics-constrained pathway to discover anisotropic yield functions from displacement and force data while preserving the elastoplastic stress-update structure, enabling data-driven yield surfaces without direct yield data or prescribed parametric forms.

title: Configurable Clinical Information Extraction with Agentic RAG: What Works, What Breaks, and Why
summary: Patient contexts span hundreds of heterogeneous documents and thousands of structured data points, yet the document-level metadata that AI systems need for retrieval and triage is absent or incomplete. Standard retrieval-augmented generation fails on this data, mishandling temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata. We deploy ACIE (Agentic Clinical Information Extraction) at University Medicine Essen: an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification. We quantify the metadata gap, trace the architectural decisions it shaped, and evaluate extraction alongside an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study, in which nuclear-medicine physicians verify every extracted value against its cited sources. Across 7,326 judgments, clinicians accepted 96.5\% of extractions, with per-type acceptance ranging from 80\% to 99\%.

AI.tldr: ACIE is an on-premise, agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over full patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages, validated against a retrospective lymphoma registry with clinician-verified extractions. 7,326 judgments yielded 96.5% acceptance across extraction types (80–99%).
AI.motivation: Address the metadata gap in patient-context documents and the failure of standard retrieval-augmented generation to handle temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata in clinical contexts.
AI.method: Deploy ACIE at University Medicine Essen as an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline. It reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds each answer in cited source passages for clinician verification. Evaluation used an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study where nuclear-medicine physicians verify extracted values against sources. 7,326 judgments analyzed.
AI.result: High extraction accuracy with clinician-verified grounding: 96.5% of extractions accepted; per-type acceptance ranged from 80% to 99% across 7,326 judgments.
AI.conclusion: ACIE demonstrates effective handling of metadata gaps and supports traceable, clinician-verified information extraction in clinical RAG, improving reliability for retrieval and triage tasks.

title: Safe, Real-Time Active Model Discrimination and Fault Diagnosis for Nonlinear Systems via Differentiable Reachability
summary: We present a safe, real-time algorithm for active fault diagnosis and model discrimination for uncertain continuous-time nonlinear systems with process and measurement disturbances. Given a finite set of candidate models representing nominal and faulty modes, including actuator and sensor faults, we formulate an output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization problem that (i) robustly enforces state-input safety constraints over a finite horizon and (ii) drives the system to produce sampled measurements consistent with at most one model, enabling deterministic diagnosis. To solve this problem in real time, we develop a tractable approximation using interval over-approximations of reachable state and output sets, and encode diagnosability via a differentiable objective that penalizes overlap between the reachable output sets of possible models. The resulting optimization is solved efficiently online with gradient-based methods using JAX and differentiable reachability primitives. We evaluate our method on sensor and actuator fault diagnosis (up to 11 fault modes) in several high-dimensional nonlinear robotic systems, including a simulated quadrotor and fighter-jet model, a hardware differential-drive robot, and quadrupedal navigation. Across these case studies, our approach achieves reliable model discrimination in under 50 ms, outperforming baselines in discrimination success rate and speed while providing formal safety guarantees.

AI.tldr: Real-time safe fault diagnosis and model discrimination for uncertain nonlinear systems using interval reachability and differentiable optimization, achieving sub-50 ms discrimination with safety guarantees.
AI.motivation: Need for robust, real-time diagnosis with safety guarantees under disturbances across multiple fault modes.
AI.method: Output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization with finite-horizon safety constraints; interval over-approximations of reachable sets; differentiable objective penalizing overlap between model outputs; online gradient-based optimization using JAX and differentiable reachability.
AI.result: Demonstrated on high-dimensional robotic systems (simulated quadrotor, fighter-jet model, hardware differential-drive robot, quadrupedal navigation); up to 11 fault modes; discrimination under 50 ms; improved discrimination rate and speed vs baselines; provides formal safety guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Offers a scalable, real-time, safe fault diagnosis framework for uncertain nonlinear systems with disturbance, enabling deterministic diagnosis and broad applicability.

title: NEST: Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding
summary: Recent progress in vision-language models has enabled the processing of increasingly long video sequences, but the ability to handle extended token streams does not translate to understanding of narrative structure in long videos. Existing long video benchmarks focus on needle-in-a-haystack retrieval rather than evaluating how low-level actions form events, how events interact across time, and how narratives progress, for example, whether a model can connect an early setback, such as a job loss to a later relationship breakup, despite long gaps, intervening scenes, or flashbacks that reframe what occurred. We introduce NEST (Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding), a dataset of 1005 full-length movies (avg. 98 minutes), each annotated with 102 multimodal narrative events grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio. NEST captures multimodal narrative events with structured annotations grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio, and links them through relations that reflect narrative structure, including temporal ordering, hierarchical composition, and long-range dependencies. We introduce baselines for event trigger detection (ETD), event localization (EL), event argument extraction (EAE), and event relation extraction (ERE). The benchmark is highly challenging for grounded event discovery, with ETD below 8%, EL under 6%, and EAE below 11%. In contrast, ERE is more tractable once events are given, reaching 35.45% F1 zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: NEST introduces a dataset of 1005 feature-length movies annotated with 102 multimodal narrative events and long-range narrative relations. It provides baselines for event trigger detection (ETD), event localization (EL), event argument extraction (EAE), and event relation extraction (ERE). ETD/EL/EAE baselines are very challenging, while ERE is more tractable once events are given.
AI.motivation: To move beyond needle-in-the-haystack retrieval and assess understanding of narrative structure in long videos, including how low-level actions form events, how events relate over time, and how narratives progress despite gaps, rewinds, or flashbacks.
AI.method: Annotate full-length movies with 102 multimodal narrative events grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio; encode temporal, hierarchical, and long-range relations between events; establish baselines for ETD, EL, EAE, and ERE.
AI.result: ETD < 8%, EL < 6%, EAE < 11% (all low). ERE is more tractable: 35.45% F1 in zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: NEST constitutes a challenging, multimodal benchmark for grounded narrative event discovery and cross-time relations in long videos, highlighting substantial gaps in current models and offering a path toward evaluating narrative progression and structure.

title: Cost-Optimal LLM Routing with Limited User Feedback under User Satisfaction Guarantees
summary: Inference costs for large language model (LLM) applications are rapidly growing, driven by surging demand and rising infrastructure cost. Users expect high-quality responses, and in commercial settings this is formally codified in Service Level Agreements (SLAs), creating a fundamental tension between cost and quality. Recent progress on cost-aware LLM request routing has shown potential to resolve this tension, but existing approaches rely on complete feedback signals, offline training, extensive per-workload tuning, and most lack SLA guarantees or inference-time adaptivity. We introduce SLARouter, an online routing algorithm that learns a cost-optimal policy from the sparse, one-sided user feedback available in production systems. SLARouter provides theoretical guarantees for both cost optimality and strict SLA compliance. Experiments across a wide range of LLM benchmarks show that SLARouter satisfies SLA constraints without the need for per-benchmark tuning, reducing operating cost by up to 2.2x over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: SLARouter is an online routing algorithm for LLM inference that learns a cost-optimal policy from sparse user feedback, guarantees strict SLA compliance, and reduces operating costs (up to 2.2x) without per-benchmark tuning.
AI.motivation: Inference costs are rising; SLAs formalize quality requirements; existing cost-aware routing relies on full feedback and offline tuning, lacking SLA guarantees and adaptivity.
AI.method: Online learning of routing decisions using one-sided production feedback; provides theoretical guarantees for cost optimality and SLA compliance; no offline training or per-workload customization; adaptable across workloads.
AI.result: Empirical results across benchmarks show SLA satisfaction without per-benchmark tuning and up to 2.2x cost reduction compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: SLARouter delivers cost-efficient, SLA-compliant LLM routing in production, reducing tuning effort and enabling robust, adaptive decision-making.

title: Which Pairs to Compare for LLM Post-Training?
summary: Preference-based post-training has become a central paradigm for aligning language models. A common data-collection strategy is to generate a small set of completions for each prompt and label the resulting comparison pairs. However, human preference labels are often much more expensive than generating additional completions, suggesting a different use of the same labeling budget: generate a larger pool of completions, but label only the most informative comparison pairs. This paper studies which pairs should be compared in preference-based post-training. We formulate comparison curation as a sampling-design problem and evaluate designs by the quality of the final policy under the preference-based post-training objective. We instantiate this framework for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), analyzing how the choice of labeled pairs propagates through DPO training to downstream policy performance. Our main results provide matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap of the DPO-trained policy. The bounds show that comparison selection affects downstream performance through a single design-dependent information matrix, which links label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality. This yields an explicit optimization criterion for budgeted comparison curation and motivates practical sampling designs for selecting informative pairs from large generated completion pools. Experiments on synthetic settings and language-model post-training benchmarks show that the proposed designs consistently improve sample efficiency over common comparison-selection heuristics.

AI.tldr: Proposes sampling-design-based comparison curation for preference-based post-training, deriving bounds for the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective and showing how labeling decisions affect policy performance via an information-matrix, with practical sampling designs and empirical gains in sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Human preference labels are costly; allocate labeling budget by generating more completions and labeling only the most informative comparison pairs to improve downstream policy.
AI.method: Formulate comparison curation as a sampling-design problem; instantiate for DPO; derive matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap; identify a design-dependent information matrix linking label allocation to estimation error and policy suboptimality; propose an explicit budgeted curation criterion and practical sampling designs.
AI.result: Derives matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap; shows that labeling choices propagate through DPO via a single information matrix; provides an optimization criterion and practical sampling designs; experiments on synthetic settings and benchmarks show consistent improvements in sample efficiency over common heuristics.
