AI.conclusion: Comparison selection in preference-based post-training significantly impacts downstream policy performance; the proposed design framework enables cost-effective labeling and improved efficiency, with practical sampling designs applicable to large completion pools.

title: Fail-RAG : A Retrieval Augmented Generation Informed Framework for Robot Failure Identification
summary: Industry automation is witnessing an evolution in robotics driven by both technological breakthroughs and societal changes: progress towards generalist robots, embodied and physical artificial intelligence (AI), and increasing labor shortage in manufacturing.An intelligent autonomous robot needs to not only act according to planned motions but also react to any unexpected events. In this study, we focus on such unexpected events in warehouses where robots are used for material handling. Specifically, we refer to any unexpected events as failures and develop methods to detect robot operations related failures. Rule-based detection methods may break since the form of failures could change due to the dynamic nature of both environments and tasks. We propose 'Fail-RAG', a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)-based failure detection framework where failure images and context information are embedded and queried against a failure database by calculating their similarities. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are further used to analyze failures and provide details by following our instruction template. We evaluated the performance of Fail-RAG by conducting both simulation and physical experiments using fixed robot arms and a mobile manipulator for multiple tasks that are common in warehouse automation. Fail-RAG achieved 25 percentage point higher failure detection accuracy on average across five types of robot operations compared to using off-the-shelf VLMs, indicating its effectiveness for real-world failure detection.

AI.tldr: Fail-RAG: a Retrieval Augmented Generation framework for detecting unexpected robot-operation failures in warehouses, achieving +25pp accuracy vs off-the-shelf VLMs.
AI.motivation: Overcome brittleness of rule-based detectors due to dynamic environments and tasks; need robust, scalable failure detection using multimodal data and retrieval-augmented reasoning.
AI.method: Construct a failure image/context database; embed and retrieve similar failure instances; use Vision-Language Models with an instruction template to analyze failures; validate in simulation and on real hardware (fixed arms, mobile manipulator) across five tasks.
AI.result: Avg improvement of 25 percentage points in failure detection accuracy across five operation types compared to baseline off-the-shelf VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of Fail-RAG for real-world failure detection in warehouse automation; leverages retrieval-augmented reasoning to handle diverse, changing failure modes; potential for broader applicability and further improvement with larger-scale datasets and real-time integration.

title: One-Shot Novel View and Pose Human Image Synthesis via 3D Prior Guided Diffusion Model
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of one-shot novel view and pose human image synthesis. The existing methods transfer the reference human image to a target pose using a set of 2D pose keypoints or synthesize human images based on generalizable human NeRF which uses human model priors to extract point-wise features. However, pose transfer based methods can not handle complex human pose using ambiguous 2D pose as the condition, while generalizable human NeRFs may be inaccurate to recover occluded/invisiable human parts without extracted reliable features. To solve these problems, we propose a novel approach for novel view and pose synthesis from a singe human image via conditional denoising diffusion model. Our diffusion model divides the novel view and pose synthesis problem into a sequence of conditional denoising steps. Specifically, to generate humans with complex and arbitrary poses, we introduce 3D human priors, i.e., 3D normal map and color prompt, as geometry and color conditions into the generation process. By transferring the reference human into the target human with a series of diffusion steps, our diffusion model enables high-quality synthesis including the occluded/invisible parts. Further, we propose a self-reconstruction based customized refinement to enhance fine details when tested on novel persons.Experimental results on different public datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms previous methods and also shows better generalization ability across datasets. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Yankeegsj/3DPGDM.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based one-shot framework for novel-view/pose human synthesis from a single image, using 3D priors (3D normal map and color prompt) as conditioning, plus self-reconstruction refinement to recover occluded parts and improve generalization across datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing pose-transfer methods rely on 2D pose cues that struggle with complex poses and ambiguous keypoints, and generalizable NeRFs often fail to recover occluded/invisible parts due to weak features. A robust single-image approach is needed to handle diverse poses and occlusions.
AI.method: A conditional denoising diffusion model that decomposes synthesis into sequential denoising steps. It incorporates 3D priors (3D normal map and color prompt) as geometry and color conditioning to support complex/arbitrary poses and occluded regions. The approach starts from a reference image and diffuses toward the target pose/view; includes a self-reconstruction based customized refinement during testing to improve fine details for novel subjects.
AI.result: Experimental results on public datasets show significant performance improvements over prior methods and better generalization across datasets. The authors also provide the code publicly.
AI.conclusion: The method enables high-quality, generalizable novel-view and pose synthesis for a single human image by leveraging 3D priors and diffusion-based generation, effectively handling occlusions and complex poses; demonstrated superior results and broader applicability across datasets.

title: Emyx: Fast and efficient all-atom protein generation
summary: Computational enzyme design requires generating proteins that scaffold catalytic residues and ligands, a task that demands both geometric accuracy and structural diversity from the underlying generative model. Current all-atom generators inherit expensive architectures from structure prediction, leading to high training costs and limited sample diversity. We argue that much of this complexity is unnecessary for generators, which condition on sparse geometric constraints rather than rich co-evolutionary signals. Emyx is a 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model that concentrates capacity within standard transformer blocks, replacing heavy embedding stacks with lightweight conditional representations and sparse connectivity. We additionally derive an exact reparametrisation of the flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework, bridging flow matching training efficiency with state-of-the-art sampling methods designed for diffusion models without retraining. Despite being the smallest model, Emyx outperforms both Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 against the AME enzyme design benchmark across success rate under strict evaluation requiring both global fold recovery and catalytic geometry accuracy, structural novelty, scaffold diversity, and geometric validity, while training in just $682$ GPU-hours, roughly $4\times$ less than RFdiffusion3.

AI.tldr: A lightweight 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model (Emyx) achieves high efficiency and strong performance for enzyme design by focusing on sparse geometric constraints, outperforming larger models on the AME benchmark with ~682 GPU-hours training.
AI.motivation: Current all-atom protein generators are expensive and have limited sample diversity due to reliance on heavy architectures and rich co-evolutionary signals. The authors argue that much of this complexity is unnecessary for generators that condition on sparse geometric constraints.
AI.method: Emyx is a 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model built with standard transformer blocks and lightweight conditional representations and sparse connectivity, avoiding heavy embedding stacks. It includes an exact reparametrisation of the flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework to align flow matching training with diffusion-model sampling without retraining.
AI.result: Emyx outperforms Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 on the AME enzyme design benchmark across multiple metrics: success rate with global fold recovery, catalytic geometry accuracy, structural novelty, scaffold diversity, and geometric validity. It trains in 682 GPU-hours, roughly 4× less than RFdiffusion3.
AI.conclusion: Even as the smallest model, Emyx achieves competitive to superior performance while dramatically reducing training cost, supporting the thesis that conditioning on sparse geometric constraints suffices for effective enzyme design and that flow-matching with EDM-inspired sampling can yield practical, efficient generators.

authors: Antonio de Sousa Leitão Filho; Allan Kardec Duailibe Barros Filho; Fabrício Saul Lima; Selby Mykael Lima dos Santos; Rejani Bandeira Vieira Sousa
title: Toten: Knowledge-Based Ontological Tokenization Of Physical Quantities And Technical Notation In Brazilian Portuguese
summary: Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization is statistically efficient for vocabulary compression, but semantically blind to structured technical entities, fragmenting physical quantities, numbers, units, and symbolic expressions into lexically arbitrary subwords. We present TOTEN, a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework that replaces statistical derivation with declarative classification grounded in a formal ontology of engineering entities (OEE). We formalize TOTEN as the triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>: the ontology gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants; the classification function maps raw text into typed regions; and the instantiator family yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness derives from deterministic coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Intrinsic evaluation covers four properties verifiable by construction -- ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction -- over an internal, physically validated benchmark (EngQuant, N=800) and four Brazilian Portuguese external corpora (N=1771 eligible cases). We also report detection recall, distinguishing coverage from conditional atomicity. Against eight state-of-the-art baselines, TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts and numerical reconstruction of 0.775-0.904 on external corpora, vs. 0.627-0.703 for the best baseline (Quantulum3); on EngQuant, 0.780 vs. 0.340. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation between internal and external rankings confirms concurrent validity of the control benchmark. Dimensional equivalence shows statistical parity with Pint, the oracle from which the system inherits dimensional authority.

AI.tldr: TOTEN is a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework for engineering texts that replaces statistical subword tokenization with declarative ontological classification, yielding higher ontological atomicity and numerical reconstruction accuracy across EngQuant and Brazilian Portuguese corpora, outperforming baselines (e.g., Quantulum3); robustness via three external oracles; improvements are statistically significant.
AI.motivation: Byte-Pair Encoding and similar statistical tokenizers are semantically blind to structured technical entities (quantities, units, symbols). There is a need for ontological, semantically aware tokenization grounded in a formal ontology of engineering entities to preserve dimensionality, units, and relationships.
AI.method: TOTEN is formalized as the triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>. O collects types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants. classify maps raw text into typed regions. inst_tau yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness is achieved by coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Evaluation uses intrinsic metrics across ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction. Internal benchmark EngQuant (N=800) and external Brazilian Portuguese corpora (N=1771 eligible). Detection recall reported. Compared against eight baselines, including Quantulum3, TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts; numerical reconstruction external: 0.775-0.904 vs baseline 0.627-0.703; EngQuant: 0.780 vs 0.340. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm). Spearman correlation validates concurrent validity; dimensional equivalence shows parity with Pint.
AI.result: TOTEN demonstrates superior ontological atomicity and numerical reconstruction across internal and external corpora, with statistically significant gains over eight baselines. It maintains dimensional authority comparable to Pint and yields a self-descriptive, structured representation suitable for semantically aware tokenization.
AI.conclusion: Ontology-grounded tokenization (TOTEN) provides robust, semantically informed tokenization for engineering text, outperforming statistical baselines in both atomicity and numerical fidelity, with external robustness via three linguistic/typographic/or ontological oracles and validated by internal/external benchmarks and correlations.

title: Formal Verification of Learned Multi-Agent Communication Policies via Decision Tree Distillation
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026), Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, USA, September 27-October 1, 2026
summary: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables agents to develop coordination strategies through emergent communication, but neural policies lack the formal safety guarantees required for safety-critical robotic deployment in drone swarms and autonomous vehicle fleets. We present the first end-to-end framework for safety verification of learned multi-agent communication policies through policy abstraction: neural policies are distilled into interpretable decision trees, then formally verified, with empirical validation confirming that verified safety properties transfer to original networks. Our four-stage pipeline consists of domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, decision tree distillation achieving 97.9% +/- 1.2% fidelity to neural policies, automated translation to PRISM probabilistic model checker specifications with complete feature-to-state-variable correspondence, and compositional verification of Probabilistic Computation Tree Logic (PCTL) properties via pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling. Evaluating Vector-Quantized Variational Information Bottleneck (VQ-VIB) policies for multi-drone coordination with 5-7 agents, we verify 18 temporal logic properties across safety, liveness, and cooperation, achieving 88.9% property satisfaction with all five safety thresholds satisfied (0.3% collision probability vs. 1% threshold). Monte Carlo validation of original neural policies confirms that verified safety properties transfer with <=0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI). Discrete VQ-VIB messages provide +11.6 to +13.6 percentage-point fidelity advantages over continuous methods, enabling 3-4x faster verification. Our framework provides empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, serving as a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for multi-robot deployment.

AI.tldr: End-to-end safety verification framework for learned multi-agent communication policies via distillation into interpretable decision trees and formal verification in PRISM; demonstrates high fidelity, safe transfer to neural policies, and faster verification for multi-robot systems.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical robotics require formal safety guarantees for learned MARL policies; raw neural controllers lack verifiability, hindering deployment in drone swarms and autonomous fleets.
AI.method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, (2) decision-tree distillation achieving 97.9% ±1.2% fidelity to neural policies, (3) automated translation to PRISM probabilistic model checker specifications with complete feature-to-state-variable correspondence, (4) compositional verification of PCTL properties via pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling.
AI.result: Applied to Vector-Quantized Variational Information Bottleneck (VQ-VIB) policies for multi-drone coordination with 5–7 agents; verified 18 temporal logic properties across safety, liveness, cooperation; 88.9% property satisfaction; all five safety thresholds satisfied (0.3% collision probability vs. 1% threshold). Fidelity transfer to original neural policies with ≤0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI) via Monte Carlo validation. Discrete VQ-VIB messages provide +11.6 to +13.6 percentage-point fidelity advantages over continuous methods, enabling 3–4× faster verification.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, creating a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for multi-robot deployment.

title: QueryGaussian: Scalable and Training-Free Open-Vocabulary 3D Instance Retrieval
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2026)
summary: Efficiently retrieving specific 3D instances from large-scale scenes via natural language prompts remains a formidable challenge in multimedia analysis. Existing approaches predominantly follow a "scene-level embedding" paradigm, which requires distilling high-dimensional semantic features into every 3D primitive. This strategy suffers from a fundamental architectural bottleneck: memory and computational costs scale linearly with scene complexity, inevitably triggering out-of-memory (OOM) failures in city-scale environments. To address this barrier, we propose QueryGaussian, a training-free framework for expeditious and scalable open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval. Unlike holistic semantic distillation, QueryGaussian employs an instance-level query mechanism that decouples semantic understanding from geometric representation. Specifically, we leverage pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret user prompts and lift segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy, ensuring semantic-visual consistency. To mitigate projection ambiguity, we introduce a temporal fusion module with multi-stage adaptive density clustering. Experimental results demonstrate that QueryGaussian not only matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art methods but also delivers a decisive efficiency leap, reducing GPU memory usage by over 70% and accelerating inference by 180x. Crucially, QueryGaussian enables expeditious instance retrieval on city-scale scenes containing tens of millions of Gaussians using consumer-grade hardware.

AI.tldr: A training-free, instance-level 3D open-vocabulary retrieval framework that decouples semantics from geometry, dramatically reducing memory usage and boosting inference speed for city-scale scenes.
AI.motivation: Scene-level embedding approaches incur prohibitive memory and compute costs that scale with scene complexity, leading to inefficiency and OOM in large environments. A scalable, open-vocabulary 3D retrieval method is needed.
AI.method: Propose QueryGaussian with an instance-level query mechanism that uses pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret prompts and lift segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy. Address projection ambiguity with a temporal fusion module and multi-stage adaptive density clustering.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing GPU memory usage by >70% and increasing inference speed by ~180x. Enables fast retrieval on city-scale scenes with tens of millions of Gaussians on consumer hardware.
AI.conclusion: QueryGaussian provides a training-free, scalable solution for open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval, overcoming memory bottlenecks of scene-level embeddings and delivering substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy.

title: A Hybrid GNN-FEM Framework for Phase-Field Fracture Simulation. Physics-Preserving Hybridization for Generalizable Surrogate Modeling
summary: Scientific machine learning (SciML) has emerged as a promising approach for accelerating simulations of complex physical systems, yet achieving physically consistent and generalizable predictions for nonlinear, history-dependent problems remains a central challenge. In this study, we propose a hybrid GNN--FEM framework for efficient and generalizable phase-field fracture modeling. While phase-field approaches provide a robust variational framework for simulating complex crack evolution, their high computational cost limits practical applications because they require solving coupled, nonlinear, and history-dependent systems within an incremental finite element procedure. To address this challenge, a graph neural network surrogate is integrated into the conventional staggered scheme, replacing the phase-field update at each load increment while retaining the FEM-based displacement solver to enforce mechanical equilibrium and boundary conditions. By preserving the incremental solution structure, the framework remains consistent with history-dependent fracture evolution without requiring the surrogate to approximate the full solution trajectory. This selective surrogate strategy emphasizes the identification of a physically meaningful and incrementally structured learning target, rather than relying on brute-force data generation to learn the full fracture process. The proposed framework achieves strong generalization across varying geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations through dimensionless feature design, a graph-based formulation on mesh-based domains, and a physics-informed loss derived from the governing phase-field equation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the hybrid approach reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy compared with conventional FEM, and exhibits robust predictive performance across diverse problem settings.

AI.tldr: Hybrid GNN–FEM surrogate replaces the phase-field update in a staggered FEM scheme to accelerate phase-field fracture simulations while preserving mechanical equilibrium, enabling generalization across geometry, loading, materials, and discretization with physics-informed learning.
AI.motivation: Phase-field fracture provides a robust variational framework but is computationally expensive due to solving coupled, nonlinear, history-dependent systems in incremental FEM. A fast, generalizable surrogate that respects history dependence is highly desirable.
AI.method: Integrate a graph neural network as the phase-field updater within the existing staggered solution procedure, keeping the FEM displacement solve for equilibrium. Learn incremental phase-field updates using dimensionless, mesh-aware features; employ a physics-informed loss derived from the phase-field governing equation; target incremental updates rather than the full trajectory to respect history dependence.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show reduced computational cost with maintained accuracy compared to conventional FEM, and strong generalization across diverse geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid GNN–FEM framework delivers efficient, physically consistent, and generalizable phase-field fracture simulations, demonstrating a viable approach for integrating SciML surrogates into history-dependent PDE problems.

title: AI4SE and SE4AI Exploration: A Decade Looking Back and Forward
summary: The March 2020 INCOSE INSIGHT special issue on AI and Systems Engineering (SE) became the most downloaded issue in the publication's history and launched a research community that now draws over 250 registrants to its annual workshop. In this article, we trace the progress in AI and SE across three phases (labeled here foundational, applied, and LLM inflection) based on the authors' reading of the field's core papers, and describe our opinions of where the community has converged and where critical gaps remain. Separately, a human-AI agreement literature review leveraging both human expertise and six AI models was performed to assess the relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. The results identify five critical research gaps and offer guidance for practitioners navigating AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE. We share the agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application so readers can compare their own relevance judgments with the human and AI raters.

AI.tldr: A survey of AI and SE progress across foundational, applied, and LLM-inflection phases; plus a human–AI agreement study across numerous SE articles, identifying five gaps and offering practical guidance, with data and an interactive web app for comparison.
AI.motivation: Map the AI-and-SE landscape, identify critical gaps, and provide practitioner guidance for AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation; and to explore the reliability of human vs. AI relevance judgments in SE literature.
AI.method: 1) Qualitative synthesis of core SE/AI papers to delineate three phases of progress (foundational, applied, LLM inflection). 2) A combined human–AI agreement study using human expertise and six AI models to judge relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. 3) Public dissemination of agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application for comparison of judgments.
AI.result: The study traces progress in AI and SE, noting significant engagement (e.g., a special issue becoming the most downloaded) and a community with ~250 workshop registrants. It identifies five critical research gaps and offers practitioner guidance on AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE. The authors publish agreement data and provide an interactive Explorer app to enable readers to compare their judgments with human and AI raters.
AI.conclusion: AI and SE show convergences in certain areas but retain critical, addressable gaps. The work supplies data, tooling, and recommendations to advance AI adoption and governance in SE and invites ongoing community engagement.

title: CTS-MoE: Implicit Terrain Adaptation via Mixture-of-Experts for Perceptive Locomotion
summary: Perceptive legged locomotion over discontinuous terrain (e.g., stairs, gaps, and obstacles) requires adaptive behavior, as a single conservative gait cannot produce the anticipatory maneuvers needed for abrupt topology changes. Cast as multi-task reinforcement learning, this problem introduces a tension between sharing and separation. Tasks use a common locomotion base but have conflicting rewards, so a policy must share behavior while avoiding value interference. Prior work addresses only one side, with monolithic policies sacrificing specialization and hierarchical sub-policies sacrificing generalization across transitions and unseen terrain. We propose CTS-MoE, which combines a dense mixture-of-experts actor with perception-based gating to compose shared behaviors and a multi-critic with task-specific value heads to prevent interference. The model is trained end-to-end in a single-stage concurrent teacher-student setup that handles partial observability and avoids sequential distillation, with task labels used only during training. At deployment, routing depends solely on perception, allowing terrain adaptation without a high-level selector or terrain classifier. Experiments on a Unitree Go1 in simulation and on hardware across seen and unseen terrains show task-aware specialization, with lower tracking error and higher success rates than monolithic baselines. Project Website: https://cts-moe.github.io/ .

AI.tldr: CTS-MoE implements a perception-guided mixture-of-experts policy with multi-critic heads for multi-task legged locomotion over discontinuous terrain, trained end-to-end in a teacher-student setup, achieving better specialization and generalization than monolithic baselines.
AI.motivation: Perceptive legged locomotion over discontinuous terrain requires anticipatory maneuvers; a single conservative gait is insufficient. Multi-task RL introduces a tension between sharing base behaviors and task-specific value signals, and prior approaches fail to balance specialization with generalization across terrain transitions.
AI.method: Proposes CTS-MoE: a dense mixture-of-experts actor with perception-based gating, plus a multi-critic with task-specific value heads. Trained end-to-end in a single-stage concurrent teacher-student framework, addressing partial observability and avoiding sequential distillation; task labels are only used during training. Deployment routing relies solely on perception, with no terrain classifier or high-level selector.
AI.result: Experiments on a Unitree Go1 in simulation and hardware across seen and unseen terrains show task-aware specialization, with lower tracking error and higher success rates than monolithic baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively balances shared locomotion behavior with task-specific specialization; perception-driven routing enables terrain adaptation without a high-level terrain classifier, improving generalization to unseen terrains.

title: VFACamou: View-Fused Adversarial Camouflage for Environment-Adaptive Physical Evasion
summary: Adversarial camouflage in the physical world remains highly challenging, particularly under UAV reconnaissance where targets undergo continuous geometric changes and extreme illumination variations. Existing methods either optimize 2D digital perturbations that fail to generalize to dynamic viewpoints or produce visually unnatural textures that cannot be deployed in real scenarios. Therefore, we propose an end-to-end framework for adversarial camouflage generation that automatically produces wearable adversarial patterns and maintains stable attack performance in real physical environments with changing viewpoints, poses, and lighting conditions. Our method integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator, enabling consistent appearance under varying scales, poses, and lighting conditions. To ensure environmental realism, we propose an illumination color consistency estimator that extracts dominant background attributes and guides a natural texture loss to align the generated UV texture with the surrounding environment. A multi-scale dynamic training strategy further enhances robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation. Extensive experiments across multiple mainstream detectors demonstrate that our method achieves strong and stable physical attack performance while maintaining high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.

AI.tldr: Proposes an end-to-end adversarial camouflage generation framework for physical UAV scenarios, combining UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator, plus illumination consistency and multi-scale dynamics to maintain robust performance under changing viewpoints, lighting, and poses; claims improved naturalness and attack success across detectors.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between 2D digital perturbations and real-world robustness; create wearable adversarial textures that remain effective under varying viewpoints, poses, and illumination while appearing natural.
AI.method: Integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator; uses an illumination color consistency estimator to align generated texture with background attributes; applies a natural texture loss; employs multi-scale dynamic training to handle viewpoint shifts and body deformations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on mainstream detectors show strong, stable physical attack performance and high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables robust, realistic physical adversarial camouflage under dynamic conditions and enhances practicality for real-world deployment.

title: How Linear Is a Transformer Feed-Forward Block? Per-Block Linear Recoverability Is Learned, Not Architectural
summary:
Transformer feed-forward networks (FFNs) are often treated as nonlinear stores of computation, yet how nonlinear a trained FFN block actually is has rarely been measured. We treat each FFN as a position-wise input-to-output map and split it into the exact least-squares linear approximation plus a residual. The held-out variance the closed-form linear map explains defines a block's linear recoverability (R^2_lin), an optimiser-free measure of its linearity.
  Across all twelve blocks of GPT-2, Pythia-160m, and llama-160m, R^2_lin is highly heterogeneous and non-monotone with depth, ranging from near-linear (>0.99) to strongly nonlinear (<0.3) between adjacent blocks, and is not set by the activation function: same-width GELU models GPT-2 and Pythia-160m have sharply different profiles, so recoverability is a learned property of individual trained blocks, not an architectural one. A low-rank bilinear probe of the residual recovers only a few points of R^2, with gain uncorrelated with residual nonlinearity: the unrecovered computation is not a single position-wise product but higher-order or distributed structure.
  The measurement also serves as a targeted compression signal: recoverable blocks admit large single-layer replacements (GPT-2's early FFN at 8x fewer parameters for +0.77 perplexity), while low-recoverability blocks flag where this is unsafe. It further exposes a methodological pitfall: trained linear baselines can badly under-converge on ill-conditioned transformer activations, so we report the exact closed-form least-squares ceiling throughout.

AI.tldr: A study of the nonlinearity of transformer FFN blocks via a closed-form least-squares linear decomposition, defining R^2_lin as the extent the block's input-output map is captured by a linear model. Across GPT-2, Pythia-160m, and llama-160m, R^2_lin is highly heterogeneous, not monotone with depth, and is learned rather than architectural; a low-rank bilinear probe recovers only a few points of R^2. The measurement serves as a compression signal, enabling large single-layer FFN replacements for highly recoverable blocks, while flagging unsafe reductions for low-recoverability blocks. It also highlights a pitfall: trained linear baselines can under-converge on ill-conditioned activations, so the authors report the exact closed-form ceiling.
AI.motivation: Quantify how nonlinear a trained FFN block is by isolating the linear component and quantifying recoverability; explore whether linear approximations can substitute parts of FFN computation, and what this implies for model design, compression, and interpretability.
AI.method: Treat each FFN block as a position-wise map; decompose into exact least-squares linear approximation plus residual; compute R^2_lin as the fraction of held-out variance explained by the linear map; evaluate across 12 blocks from GPT-2, Pythia-160m, llama-160m; use a low-rank bilinear probe to assess potential unrecovered structure; perform targeted compression experiments with large single-layer replacements; report the closed-form least-squares ceiling to avoid ill-conditioned baselines.
AI.result: R^2_lin ranges from >0.99 (near-linear) to <0.3 (strongly nonlinear) across adjacent blocks; heterogeneity and non-monotonicity with depth; independence from activation function (same-width GELU models can differ sharply); linear recoverability is a learned property, not architectural; low-rank bilinear probes capture only a fraction of R^2 with gains uncorrelated with residual nonlinearity; recoverable blocks allow substantial parameter reductions (e.g., GPT-2 early FFN at 8x fewer parameters with +0.77 perplexity) while low-recoverability blocks resist such compression; the measurement reveals a methodological pitfall: trained linear baselines can under-converge on ill-conditioned activations, hence the exact closed-form ceiling is reported.
AI.conclusion: R^2_lin is a useful diagnostic of FFN nonlinearity and compressibility; nonlinearity is block-specific and learned; targeted compression is feasible for highly recoverable blocks; cautions on evaluation baselines are necessary to avoid underestimation of linear recoverability.

title: BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) brain MRI is central to clinical neurology and neuro-oncology, where generative models could augment under-represented cohorts, simulate disease trajectories, and support privacy-preserving data sharing. Latent diffusion has been the go-to solution for modeling imaging data, but it places two competing demands on the tokenizer: encoder embeddings must retain the clinical information that downstream tasks act on, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful volumes. Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers achieve the second at the expense of the first. To address this, we introduce a fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, decoupling encoder and decoder: a frozen 3D MAE encoder produces clinically informative embeddings, while a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. We pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites, and demonstrate its dual utility in two settings. First, on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA models (i.e., BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet) on 21 of 23 tasks. Second, a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) trained on these clinically informative embeddings supports both conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. Together these results establish a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space capable of both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a fully volumetric MAE-based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI in latent diffusion, freezing a 3D MAE encoder and using a CNN decoder. Pretrains on a large, diverse multi-site dataset, achieving strong task performance and enabling controllable generation and longitudinal forecasting within a unified embedding space.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tension between preserving clinically informative embeddings (needed for downstream tasks) and reconstructing anatomically faithful volumes (needed for realistic images) in latent diffusion models of 3D brain MRI.
AI.method: A fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) tokenizer with a frozen 3D MAE encoder to generate embeddings, and a dedicated CNN decoder that reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. Pretraining on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts across four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites. A conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) is trained on these embeddings for conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.
AI.result: The encoder achieves 21/23 tasks on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, outperforming or matching SOTA models (BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, MedicalNet). The DiT model enables controllable generation across six variables and supports patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. This establishes a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space useful for both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.
AI.conclusion: A unified 3D brain-MRI embedding space is feasible: decoupled encoder/decoder MAE tokenizer preserves clinical information while enabling realistic voxel reconstruction, with a pretrained embedding space supporting both predictive analyses and controllable, longitudinal generation.

title: Scaling Self-Play for End-to-End Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models are typically trained on offline human-demonstration datasets that provide limited state coverage and often no closed-loop feedback, making them prone to compounding errors when deployed in closed-loop and brittle to long-tail agent interactions. To overcome these limitations, we propose an alternative strategy for training end-to-end driving models: large-scale self-play directly from pixels in simulation. While prior self-play approaches have shown promising transfer to real-world driving, they typically assume vectorized Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) observations that are incompatible with end-to-end policies operating directly on sensor observations. To this end, we introduce Gigapixel, a high-throughput batched driving simulator with perspective rendering, enabling scalable self-play directly from pixel observations. Rather than targeting compute-costly photorealistic sensor simulation, Gigapixel renders a simplified bounding-box world that preserves essential scene structure while achieving throughput at 50k agent steps per second. Since direct pixel-space self-play RL is prohibitively sample-inefficient at end-to-end model scale, we propose self-play DAgger training: we train pixel-based policies in self-play via on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher. To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we subsequently transfer the self-play trained policies to real-world sensor data through lightweight perception adaptation. Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real-world sensor data achieve competitive performance on the HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Moreover, scaling self-play training yields proportional gains in policy performance, establishing self-play as a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end models.

AI.tldr: Self-play from pixel observations in a high-throughput driving simulator (Gigapixel) with on-policy distillation from a privileged teacher enables scalable end-to-end driving policies that transfer to real sensor data and perform competitively on HUGSIM/NAVSIM-v2 without human trajectories.
AI.motivation: Offline human-demonstration data provide limited state coverage and lack closed-loop feedback, causing compounding errors in end-to-end driving; a scalable, end-to-end training paradigm is needed that can operate directly on sensor observations.
AI.method: Introduce Gigapixel, a high-throughput batched driving simulator with perspective rendering that outputs a simplified bounding-box world; achieve 50k agent steps per second. Develop self-play DAgger: on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher to train pixel-based policies in self-play. Transfer policies to real-world sensor data via lightweight perception adaptation.
AI.result: Self-play trained policies, when adapted to real sensor data, achieve competitive performance on HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Scaling self-play yields proportional gains in policy performance, demonstrating practicality and scalability of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Self-play is a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end driving models, with effective sim-to-real transfer through perception adaptation and without reliance on human demonstrations.

title: Occ-VLM: Occupancy Grounded Vision Language Model for Indoor Scene Understanding
summary: Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have made significant progress in 3D scene understanding, driving advances in applications such as embodied intelligence and robotic vision. However, existing approaches typically either rely directly on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds or RGB-D sequences), or introduce an additional 3D geometry encoder to derive 3D-aware visual tokens from 2D images. Such designs structurally decouple 3D geometric perception from the rich 2D semantics learned via vision-language pre-training, hindering the development of a unified 3D vision-language representation. In this work, we propose Occ-VLM, a novel framework for 3D scene understanding that operates purely on posed RGB images and employs a single 2D vision encoder. Specifically, Occ-VLM reconstructs 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior, which is utilized to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space. These tokens are then decoded by a Large Language Model (LLM) for unified scene understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Occ-VLM achieves both accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning: it attains state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction, while performing on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D Visual Question Answering (VQA) and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Occ-VLM introduces a 3D scene understanding framework that uses only posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder. It reconstructs a 3D occupancy prior to link 2D tokens to 3D space, and decodes them with an LLM for unified vision-language reasoning. It achieves state-of-the-art multi-view occupancy prediction and competitive results on 3D VQA/dense captioning compared to 3D-input VLMs.
AI.motivation: Eliminate the dependency on explicit 3D inputs or separate 3D geometry encoders in VLMs, enabling a unified 3D vision-language representation trained through 2D semantics and minimal 3D priors.
AI.method: Pose-based RGB images are processed by a single 2D vision encoder. A 3D occupancy prior is reconstructed as an auxiliary geometric cue to spatially associate 2D tokens with 3D space. The associated tokens are then decoded by a Large Language Model (LLM) to perform unified scene understanding.
AI.result: Occ-VLM achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction and performs on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D VQA and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A unified, 3D-aware VLM can be built without explicit 3D inputs or a dedicated 3D encoder by leveraging a geometric occupancy prior to connect 2D vision tokens with 3D space, enabling robust 3D scene reasoning through an LLM.

title: VERITAS: Verifier-Guided Proof Search for Zero-Shot Formal Theorem Proving
summary: LLM-based formal provers often collapse rich verifier signals (syntax errors, type mismatches, partial goal progress) into a binary pass/fail bit. We present VERITAS, a zero-shot framework that routes every verifier signal back into proof search through a two-phase protocol: Best-of-N sampling first, then a critic-guided MCTS pass that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples. The protocol preserves every theorem solved by its own Phase 1 sweep, so Phase 2's additional solves are attributable to feedback-driven exploration. VERITAS reaches 40.6% on miniF2F (vs. an independently run Best-of-5 at 36.9%, Portfolio 26.2%) and 7.3% on VERITAS-CombiBench, a 55-theorem combinatorics benchmark we release on which Best-of-5 (1.8%) falls below Portfolio (3.6%), exposing that unguided sampling hurts when correct lemma names must be recovered iteratively from verifier feedback. Artifacts are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: VERITAS integrates verifier signals into proof search via a two-phase zero-shot framework: Best-of-N sampling followed by critic-guided MCTS that uses Phase 1 failures as negative examples, preserving Phase 1 results.
AI.motivation: To address the tendency of LLM-based formal provers to collapse rich verifier feedback into a binary pass/fail, and to recover implicit signals (e.g., lemma names) by guided exploration.
AI.method: Phase 1: Best-of-N sampling to explore candidate proofs. Phase 2: Critic-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples and uses them to steer search; the protocol preserves Phase 1-solvable theorems.
AI.result: VERITAS achieves 40.6% on miniF2F (vs. Best-of-5 36.9% and Portfolio 26.2%) and 7.3% on VERITAS-CombiBench (55-theorem combinatorics benchmark; Best-of-5 1.8%, Portfolio 3.6%), indicating that guided feedback improves performance and unguided sampling hurts when correct lemma names must be recovered iteratively.
AI.conclusion: Feedback-driven exploration via a structured two-phase protocol yields measurable gains over unguided sampling; the Phase 2 search benefits from Phase 1 failures as negative guidance; artifacts are available on GitHub.

title: Denoising Implicit Feedback for Cold-start Recommendation
summary: Implicit feedback is widely used in recommender systems due to its accessibility and generality, yet it usually presents noisy samples (e.g., clickbait, position bias). Meanwhile, recommenders inevitably face the item cold-start problem due to the continuous influx of new items. We identify that cold items are more prone to noisy samples due to the aforementioned factors, and researchers often overlook the significance of denoising implicit feedback for cold items. Previous denoising studies usually identify noisy samples based on heuristic patterns, such as higher loss values, and mitigate noise through sample selection or re-weighting. However, these methods have limited adaptability and are ineffective in cold-start scenarios. To achieve denoising implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation, we propose a model-agnostic denoising method called DIF. First, user preferences for content remain stable, which allows us to infer pseudo-labels indicating whether a user is interested in a cold item through content-similar warm items. Furthermore, to improve pseudo-label accuracy, we model the confidence of pseudo-labels based on the content similarity between the cold item and warm items, and then aggregate multiple pseudo-labels for each sample. Finally, we explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering its relative entropy and the cold-start status of the item, which adaptively guides the role of pseudo-labels to correct the noisy labels at the sample level. DIF's superiority is supported by both theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. The method has been deployed on a billion-user scale short video application Kuaishou and has significantly improved various commercial metrics within cold-start scenarios.

AI.tldr: DIF is a model-agnostic denoising framework for implicit feedback in cold-start recommendation. It builds pseudo-labels via content-similarity to warm items, weights them by confidence derived from content similarity, aggregates multiple pseudo-labels, and estimates sample uncertainty using relative entropy and cold-start status, enabling adaptive noise correction. The approach is theoretically justified, empirically validated on real data, and deployed at Kuaishou with notable cold-start gains.
AI.motivation: Implicit feedback is noisy and cold-start items are especially prone to noise due to continuous item influx and exposure biases. Existing denoising methods rely on heuristics and struggle in cold-start scenarios; there is a need for a model-agnostic, scalable denoising approach that leverages content information to address cold items.
AI.method: 1) Infer pseudo-labels for cold items by linking user interest inferred from content with warm items via content similarity; 2) model pseudo-label confidence based on similarity between cold item and warm items; 3) aggregate multiple pseudo-labels per sample to improve accuracy; 4) explicitly estimate uncertainty of the noisy label using relative entropy and the item’s cold-start status to adaptively weight pseudo-labels during training; 5) apply in a model-agnostic fashion to denoise implicit feedback.
AI.result: Theoretical justification supports the method; extensive experiments on real-world datasets show strong denoising performance, and deployment on a billion-user-scale short-video platform (Kuaishou) yields significant improvements in commercial metrics under cold-start conditions.
AI.conclusion: DIF provides an effective, scalable solution for denoising implicit feedback in cold-start recommendation, with solid theoretical grounding, empirical validation, and real-world deployment evidence across large-scale systems.

title: DF-ExpEnse: Diffusion Filtered Exploration for Sample Efficient Finetuning
summary: A natural recipe for intelligent robotic decision-making is initializing from pretrained generative control policies, which have summarized offline experience, and adapting them to self-collected online experience. We present DF-ExpEnse, an exploration technique that improves the quality of online experience collection, thus increasing finetuning sample-efficiency. DF-ExpEnse leverages the multimodal modeling capabilities of the generative control policy to create an expressive and tractably evaluatable candidate set. It then utilizes an ensemble of critics to identify the action that best balances quality with high exploration interest. In fleet settings, DF-ExpEnse further enables cross-agent communication to facilitate collaborative exploration as a group. DF-ExpEnse can be seamlessly integrated with existing strategies that finetune pretrained generative control policies via reinforcement learning. We experimentally validate consistent sample-efficiency benefits through DF-ExpEnse across a variety of manipulation and locomotion tasks, compared to default finetuning and alternative action selection schemes. Project can be found at https://df-expense.github.io.

AI.tldr: DF-ExpEnse is an exploration method that enhances online data collection for fine-tuning pretrained generative control policies by generating a multimodal candidate action set and using an ensemble of critics to select actions balancing quality and exploration; it supports fleet-level cross-agent communication and is compatible with standard RL fine-tuning, achieving consistent sample-efficiency gains across manipulation and locomotion tasks.
AI.motivation: Improve sample efficiency in online fine-tuning of pretrained generative control policies by enabling more informative data collection during online interaction.
AI.method: Construct a diverse candidate set via multimodal generative policy; evaluate actions with an ensemble of critics balancing action quality and exploration; optionally enable cross-agent communication in fleets; integrate with existing RL fine-tuning pipelines; empirically evaluate on manipulation and locomotion tasks.
AI.result: DF-ExpEnse yields consistent improvements in sample-efficiency over baselines (default fine-tuning and other action-selection schemes) across multiple tasks; demonstrated in both single-agent and fleet settings; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: DF-ExpEnse is a practical, scalable exploration technique that enhances fine-tuning efficiency for pretrained generative control policies and extends to multi-agent collaboration, compatible with current RL fine-tuning workflows.

title: HypOProto: Hyperbolic Ordinal Prototypes for Left Ventricular Filling Pressure Classification
summary: Echocardiography (echo) is a widely used imaging modality for assessing cardiac function, with Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) serving as a critical physiological marker for conditions such as heart failure. Standard LVFP classification into normal \emph{vs} elevated categories relies on the Doppler-derived $E/e'$ ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings, motivating methods that infer LVFP directly from B-mode echo. Existing deep learning approaches achieve high performance but remain largely black-box, limiting clinical interpretability. We propose HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable LVFP classification using a frozen, explainable foundation model backbone. HypOProto arranges prototypes along the physiological $E/e'$ scale, placing borderline cases near the hyperboloid root where small angular differences separate similar cases, while normal and elevated cases occupy outward positions reflecting increasing diagnostic certainty. This hyperbolic geometry encodes clinically meaningful ordinal relationships and improves interpretability. We also introduce a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss, enforcing inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. HypOProto achieves SOTA performance while maintaining transparency, and highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations. This work represents the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo. Our code can be found at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.

AI.tldr: Introduces HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for LVFP classification from B-mode echo, achieving SOTA with a HyperPAS loss and providing interpretable region-focused visualizations.
AI.motivation: LVFP classification traditionally relies on Doppler-derived E/e' but is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings; a transparent, image-based method is needed for LVFP estimation.
AI.method: A frozen, explainable foundation-model backbone with prototypes arranged along the physiological E/e' scale in hyperbolic space; Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss enforces inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space; prototypes reflect ordinal relationships; outputs include region highlight visualizations.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance and enhanced interpretability; first prototype-based LVFP classifier for echocardiography; provides clinically meaningful visualizations and a public code repository.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic, ordinal prototype frameworks can capture the ordered nature of LVFP categories, delivering accurate yet transparent classification and paving steps toward deployment in settings lacking Doppler data.

title: Thermodynamic Signatures of Reasoning: Free-Energy and Spectral-Form-Factor Diagnostics for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) is deployment-critical, and recent work shows that the spectrum of attention-derived graph Laplacians carries strong signal about reasoning quality. Prior spectral diagnostics, however, summarize the Laplacian spectrum by a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars, leaving most of its structure unused. We propose Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a spectral descriptor that treats each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts its thermodynamic potentials partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity together with the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. We prove three results: (i)~Lipschitz stability of Fes under attention perturbation; (ii)~an expressiveness result showing that Fes enriches finite spectral summaries and approximates moment-derived spectral functionals under explicit regularity and grid-resolution assumptions; and (iii)~a finite-sample PAC bound on the AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Empirically, across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, a lightweight probe on Fes descriptors achieves the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, improving over LapEig by $+6.5$ AUROC points and over GoR-4 by $+2.4$ points on average, while requiring no update to the underlying LLM. In the fully unsupervised setting, an RMT-deviation score achieves mean AUROC $0.71$, providing a label-free but weaker detector. A complementary RMT analysis shows that correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson like spectral statistics, whereas hallucinations exhibit more Poisson-like statistics. The anonymized code and config are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: Free-Energy Signatures (Fes) are a spectral descriptor for attention Laplacians in LLMs, using thermodynamic potentials and random-matrix-theory metrics to detect hallucinations; theory (stability, expressiveness, PAC) and strong empirical AUROC gains without updating the model; includes unsupervised RMT deviation and spectral-statistics findings; code released.
AI.motivation: Address deployment-critical hallucination detection; overcome limited information from few eigenvalues by exploiting full Laplacian spectrum via thermodynamic descriptors; leverage RMT to distinguish correct vs. hallucinated outputs.
AI.method: Model each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian; compute partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity, and spectral form factor; prove Lipschitz stability, expressiveness, and PAC bounds; evaluate on six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks; propose a training-free detector; perform RMT-based analysis.
AI.result: Fes-based descriptors achieve the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, outperforming LapEig and GoR-4; unsupervised RMT score yields AUROC 0.71; code provided; observation that correct generations have more Wigner-Dyson-like statistics while hallucinations are more Poisson-like.
AI.conclusion: Fes enriches spectral summaries beyond finite moments, enabling robust, model-agnostic detection; supports both supervised and unsupervised detection; aligns spectral statistics with reasoning quality; practical deployment without modifying LLMs.

title: Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation
summary: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

AI.tldr: Decentralized coalition formation via exit-and-join dynamics using Aumann-Dreze values; links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-responses; analyzes equilibria, Lyapunov/potential structure under switching costs; finite-time stabilization and a convex-game benchmark supported by simulations.
AI.motivation: Understand how local, unilateral coalition changes shape global coalition structures and stability, by using Aumann-Dreze payoff allocation within the current coalition and studying its interaction with dynamic best-response behavior.
AI.method: Model discrete-time, unilateral exit-and-join moves with payoffs computed from the Aumann-Dreze value within each agent's current coalition. Characterize terminal partitions as coalitions with no admissible profitable exit-and-join deviations. Derive conditions for equilibrium, Lyapunov function or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching/acceptance costs affect local stability. Complement with numerical experiments on finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a convex-game benchmark.
AI.result: Equilibrium characterizations are established; conditions identified under which the dynamics admit a scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representation; analysis shows how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability; numerical experiments confirm finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and benchmark performance for a convex-game case.
AI.conclusion: The decentralized dynamics induced by exit-and-join decisions with AD-payoffs produce tractable equilibrium descriptions and stability properties. When certain cost structures hold, the process admits potential-like representations, facilitating analysis and computation; the results illuminate how local coalition adjustments aggregate into globally stable partitions, with practical implications for coordination and cost design.

title: Safe Local Navigation for Ackermann-Steered Robots in Unmapped Environments
comment: Presented at the 23rd Conference on Robots and Vision (CRV 2026)
summary: A control framework is proposed for safe local navigation of mobile robots equipped with Ackermann steering in unmapped environments where a global goal is absent. Based on local obstacle detections, the safest heading angle is determined along the direction of the largest open space ahead of the vehicle. Guided by this direction, bounding lines are constructed on the left and right sides of the vehicle to achieve obstacle separation. These bounding lines are obtained by solving a convex quadratic optimization that maximizes vehicle-to-obstacle clearance. Optionally, conditions are imposed on the bounding lines to preserve parallelism and smooth abrupt changes from prior control steps. A feedback-linearizing controller is then used to regulate the vehicle's distance from one or both bounding lines, effectively enabling tracking of a local reference path that preserves safety through obstacle clearance maximization. Open-source code is included for the application of this control scheme. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces safer navigation paths with significantly shorter computation times, compared to some existing exploration-based planners.

AI.tldr: A local, map-free navigation framework for Ackermann-steered robots that selects the safest heading based on largest forward free space, computes left/right bounding lines via convex quadratic optimization to maximize obstacle clearance, optionally enforces parallelism/smoothness, and uses a feedback-linearizing controller to track a local, safe path; demonstrates safer paths with faster computation and provides open-source code.
AI.motivation: To enable safe, real-time local navigation in unmapped environments without relying on a global goal, addressing the need for rapid obstacle-avoidance decisions for Ackermann vehicles.
AI.method: 1) Identify the direction of the largest open space ahead to define a safe heading. 2) Construct left and right bounding lines around the vehicle representing obstacle-separation constraints. 3) Solve a convex quadratic program to maximize clearance from obstacles given these lines, with optional constraints to preserve line parallelism and smooth changes from the previous step. 4) Apply a feedback-linearizing controller to regulate the vehicle’s distance to the bounding lines, effectively following a local reference path that maintains safety via clearance maximization. 5) Provide open-source code for implementation.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate the proposed approach yields safer navigation paths and significantly faster computation times compared to some existing exploration-based planners.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a real-time, safe local navigation strategy for unmapped environments with Ackermann steering, leveraging convex optimization for obstacle separation and a feedback-linearizing controller for path tracking, supported by open-source code and favorable experimental performance.

title: ParaScale: Scale-Calibrated Camera-Motion Transfer via a Gauge-Invariant Parallax Number
summary: Transferring the camera motion of a reference video to a freshly generated one lets creators reuse cinematic moves. Yet reference and target often live at incompatible scales -- a sweep across a galaxy versus a nudge across a desk -- and naively reusing the recovered trajectory yields either imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion. We trace this to a geometric fact: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, so a monocular trajectory is meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. We distill this into the Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar, a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt, and prove that it -- not the raw trajectory -- is the quantity that scale-faithful transfer must preserve. ParaScale is a plug-and-play module that reads Pi off any reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own depth, per frame, leaving rotation untouched. Sitting between pose extraction and pose injection, it requires no retraining and drops into any pose-conditioned generator. We further introduce the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric that -- unlike the similarity-aligned TransErr -- exposes scene-scale mismatch. Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps the realized parallax on the identity line and cuts PCE by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: A scale-aware Parallax transfer framework (ParaScale) preserves perceptual camera parallax when transferring motion between videos with different depths by using a gauge-invariant Parallax Number Pi. It yields a plug-and-play module and a new scale-sensitive error metric (PCE) that improves transfer fidelity across scales and backbones.
AI.motivation: Transferring camera motion across reference and target videos fails when the scenes have different depth scales, because image motion from translation depends on depth. A scale-invariant descriptor is needed to faithfully transfer motion without exaggeration or suppression.
AI.method: Define Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar as a dimensionless gauge-invariant quantity capturing how strongly a camera move is felt. Develop ParaScale, a plug-and-play module that reads Pi from the reference video and re-realizes it for the target scene per frame using the target depth map, leaving rotation unchanged and requiring no retraining. Introduce Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric to reveal scale mismatches during transfer.
AI.result: Empirically, ParaScale maintains realized parallax on the identity line and reduces PCE by more than 3x across four orders of magnitude of scale and multiple backbones, with no loss of visual fidelity compared to uncalibrated transfers.
AI.conclusion: Parallax-based, scale-aware transfer provides robust, generalizable impersonation of camera motion between videos of disparate depths. PCE is an effective diagnostic for remaining scale mismatches, and ParaScale’s plug-and-play nature enables easy integration without retraining.

title: FlexLAM: Resolving the Bottleneck Trade-off in Latent Action Learning
summary: Latent actions provide a compact interface between action-free video and downstream decision-making, yet existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) force every transition through a fixed-capacity bottleneck. We identify a bottleneck trade-off: overly tight codes can discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while overly loose codes preserve additional transition variation that must be resolved when alignment labels are scarce or narrowly distributed. FlexLAM replaces this fixed capacity with variable-length latent actions trained by nested dropout, yielding prefix-valid codes that capture compact transition structure first and add detail only when needed, without new architectures or losses. A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test, indicating that FlexLAM is not merely adjustable at inference time but learns a better latent-action interface at the same token budgets. The same model supports inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining, and FlexLAM improves Ego4D transition reconstruction. These results suggest that variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to the fixed-capacity bottleneck in latent action models, latent-action world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

AI.tldr: FlexLAM introduces variable-length latent actions via nested dropout, replacing a fixed bottleneck to better balance compression and action alignment; it matches or beats fixed-capacity LAMs across budgets and enables inference-time budget control.
AI.motivation: Standard Latent Action Models (LAMs) suffer a bottleneck trade-off: too-tight codes discard transition cues needed for alignment, while too-loose codes preserve variation that is hard to resolve when alignment labels are scarce. A flexible, prefix-valid latent action interface could adapt to label scarcity and budget constraints.
AI.method: Train latent actions with nested dropout to produce variable-length, prefix-valid codes without changing architectures or losses; the model first captures compact transition structure and adds detail only as needed; supports inference-time token-budget adjustment.
AI.result: A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test; improves Ego4D transition reconstruction; acts as an architecture-free upgrade suitable for latent-action world models and video-pretrained interfaces.
AI.conclusion: Variable-length latent actions offer an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to fixed-capacity bottlenecks in latent action models, enabling better, budget-adaptive action interfaces for video understanding.

title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

AI.tldr: Aggregate-score benchmarks for deployed agents underspecify deployment-relevant performance; proposes predictive-validity-based rankings and a 12-tier framework to expose deployment dimensions; includes explicit out-of-distribution criteria and a preregistered pilot for next-gen benchmarks.
AI.motivation: There is rapid growth in agent benchmarks, but no single benchmark captures the deployment-relevant dimensions. Rank stability across in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings is questionable, necessitating a rethinking of evaluation metrics.
AI.method: Synthesis of fourteen parallel MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark studies (including new asset classes and multi-modal extensions) plus seven prior benchmarks; analysis of rank instability; proposal of a 12-tier measurement apparatus; development of three falsifiable OOD criteria with explicit thresholds; preregistered pilot design and field-level roadmap.
AI.result: Findings indicate that aggregate scores underspecify deployment performance and predictions across OOD settings. Rank instability is evidenced by retrospective public-to-hidden comparisons. The authors argue for predictive validity-based rankings and provide a 12-tier framework to reveal deployment-relevant dimensions; evidence is suggestive but too thin for confirmation.
AI.conclusion: Shift benchmarking toward predictive validity, report a richer set of deployment dimensions via a 12-tier apparatus, and adopt preregistered pilots and explicit OOD criteria to guide next-generation agent benchmarks.

title: ForEnt: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Characterizing Quadruped Robot Entrapments in Forest Environments
summary: Legged robots are increasingly deployed in forests for ecological surveying and monitoring, yet their autonomy is often interrupted consequent to the challenges posed in traversing forest environments. Forest entrapments, for example, when a robot's legs are ensnared in vines or other vegetation, result in loss of stability and toppling. Such events not only disrupt the mission and require manual intervention, but also risk damage to the robot hardware. To address the absence of a dedicated dataset to investigate these failure modes in forest environments, we present ForEnt, a multi-modal dataset collected with the low-cost Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites in the Southampton Common Woodlands, UK. For our dataset, over approximately 1.7 km of traversals in 11 sequences were conducted, yielding 69 recorded entrapment events. ForEnt includes time-synchronized RGB-D images, LiDAR scans, proprioceptive data, and third-person video, enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and providing labeled sensor streams for reproducible benchmarking. By supporting the evaluation of entrapment detection strategies, ForEnt lowers the barrier to developing robust quadruped robot deployments in challenging forest environments.

AI.tldr: ForEnt provides a multi-modal entrapment dataset for quadruped forest robotics, enabling study and benchmarking of entrapment detection.
AI.motivation: Address the absence of a dedicated forest entrapment dataset to improve autonomous performance and resilience of legged robots.
AI.method: Data collection with a low-cost Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites ( Southampton Common Woodlands, UK), covering ~1.7 km in 11 sequences, recording 69 entrapment events. Multi-modal sensors include synchronized RGB-D, LiDAR, proprioception, and third-person video; datasets labeled for reproducibility and benchmarking.
AI.result: Yields labeled sensor streams and multi-modal data enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and benchmarking of entrapment-detection strategies.
AI.conclusion: ForEnt lowers the barrier to deploying robust quadruped robots in challenging forest environments by providing a dedicated, reproducible dataset for entrapment analysis.
