title: Training-Free Metrics for Synthetic Object Detection Data: A Proxy for Detector Performance
summary: With the recent advent of image generative models, synthetic data are increasingly being used to supplement limited real datasets for training computer vision models. However, not all synthetic datasets improve performance equally, and their effectiveness can only be assessed by training a downstream model, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. This problem is pronounced in the task of object detection, where the required annotations are much more dense due to bounding boxes. In this paper, we propose a pre-computable metric family, dubbed Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), which serves as a proxy for the relative utility of candidate synthetic training sets for downstream detection. Experiments on the VisDrone-DET dataset show that the CCDM metric families achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.

AI.tldr: CCDM provides a pre-computable proxy to rank synthetic training sets for object detection; on VisDrone-DET with YOLOv8, it achieves a perfect Spearman correlation (1.0) with downstream performance, outperforming prior metrics.
AI.motivation: Evaluating synthetic data usefulness for training object detectors is costly since it requires training a detector on each candidate dataset. A pre-computable metric could guide data collection/selection without expensive training.
AI.method: Introduce Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM) metric family. Compute a set of domain-match scores that account for conditional distributions and spatial composition between synthetic and real data; validate by correlating with downstream detector performance (YOLOv8) on VisDrone-DET.
AI.result: CCDM metrics achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with downstream YOLOv8 performance on VisDrone-DET, outperforming existing synthetic data evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: CCD M offers an effective, computation-friendly proxy for selecting synthetic data for object detection. However, evidence is based on a single dataset and detector; broader validation across tasks, models, and data sources is needed.

title: Spectral DPPs via NEPv: A Scalable Continuous Relaxation of Determinantal MAP for Diversity-Aware Data Selection
summary: Selecting a small, diverse, high-quality subset from a massive pool of candidates is a recurring primitive in modern machine learning -- data curation and coreset selection for training and fine-tuning large models, active-learning batch acquisition, prompt and exemplar selection for in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. Determinantal Point Processes (\DPP s) give a principled, well-calibrated notion of diversity for this task, but their \emph{MAP} objective -- pick a size-$k$ subset $S$ maximizing $\logdet(L_S)$ -- is NP-hard, and the standard greedy and sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size $n$. This cost is prohibitive precisely in the data-centric regime where diversity matters most, where $n$ ranges over millions to billions of candidate examples, features, or embeddings. We recast \DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, and show that its first-order optimality conditions form a \emph{Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency} (\NEPv) of a previously unstudied form. This \NEPv\ admits a self-consistent field (\SCF) iteration with a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee, giving a principled iterative solver where the diversity objective drives an eigenvector-dependent operator. The resulting algorithm, \OurMethod, requires only matrix-vector products with the kernel and runs in time $O\!\big((ndk+nk^2)\,t\big)$ for a small number of iterations $t$, scaling near-linearly in $n$ and integrating directly with low-rank and feature-map kernels common in ML. This paper focuses on the relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis; full real-data benchmarking is left to a planned empirical study.

AI.tldr: Recasts DPP-MAP as a nonlinear eigenproblem on the Stiefel manifold and solves it with a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration, yielding OurMethod with near-linear scaling in dataset size.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, diverse subset selection from massive candidate pools where traditional DPP-MAP optimization is NP-hard and existing greedy/sampling methods scale poorly for n up to billions.
AI.method: Formulate DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization over the Stiefel manifold; derive first-order optimality as a nonlinear eigenvalue problem with eigenvector dependence (NEPv); solve via a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration that updates an eigenvector-dependent operator using kernel/mapping evaluations; analyze contraction via spectral gap; implement OurMethod that uses only matrix-vector products with the kernel; complexity O((n d k + n k^2) t) for t iterations, near-linear in n; integrates with low-rank and feature-map kernels.
AI.result: The paper centers on relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis rather than full real-data benchmarking; empirical validation and benchmarking are planned for a future study.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled, scalable solver for DPP-MAP leveraging NEPv/SCF on the Stiefel manifold, with near-linear scaling and compatibility with common ML kernels, laying groundwork for large-scale data-centr ic diversification; real-data experiments are deferred to an upcoming empirical study.

title: GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations
summary: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: An LLM-mediated interface enables natural-language querying of global explanations for black-box image classifiers by translating questions into SQL over local explanations, improving accessibility and usability of XAI.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between complex global explanations and practical user needs by offering targeted, natural-language access that does not require exposure to low-level representations.
AI.method: A large language model acts as a mediator, converting natural-language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data; the system returns statistics-augmented natural-language responses and intent-aligned visualizations, evaluating intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to new queries/datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors.
AI.result: Demonstrates that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI, with positive results on interpretation, mapping, generalization, and robustness.
AI.conclusion: LLM-mediated querying is a promising approach to make global explanations for vision models more accessible and usable in human-centered XAI, enabling flexible, queryable exploration without exposing low-level representations.

title: Route-Constrained Robust Fusion Estimation for MEMS/GNSS Integrated Navigation of Unmanned Ground Vehicles in GNSS Degraded Environments
comment: Accepted workshop paper, 1st Workshop on Robot Meets GNSS and Ranging for Seamless Autonomy, IEEE ICRA 2026
summary: To address cumulative localization drift of unmanned ground vehicles in structured road environments under severe Global Navigation Satellite System signal occlusion, this paper proposes a robust route-constrained state estimation method. During periods without satellite signals, the proposed method establishes the correspondence between the historical dead reckoning trajectory and local segments of the mission route extracted from a high-definition map, and estimates a route-referenced position via a two-dimensional rigid transformation. The estimated position is then formulated as a pseudo-position observation and incorporated into an Extended Kalman Filter update. In this way, route constraints at the road level can be continuously injected into a unified state estimation framework, thereby suppressing position deviation relative to the mission route while indirectly improving azimuth estimation. To enhance practical applicability, engineering strategies, such as trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single update correction limiting, are further introduced. Experiments in three representative scenarios, including a long tunnel, a multi-segment tunnel, and a curved tunnel, show that the proposed method effectively suppresses error accumulation during satellite outages, reduces the risk of large maximum deviation, and improves localization continuity and road-level usability.

AI.tldr: A route-constrained EKF localization method using map-aligned route segments to generate pseudo-observations during GNSS outages, effectively suppressing drift and improving road-level continuity in structured environments.
AI.motivation: GNSS outages cause accumulating drift in unconstrained dead-reckoning for UGVs; robust localization is needed in structured road networks (e.g., tunnels) to maintain accuracy and road-level usability.
AI.method: Establish correspondence between historical dead reckoning and local map route via a 2D rigid transformation; formulate the route-referenced position as a pseudo-position observation and fuse it into an Extended Kalman Filter. Enhance practicality with trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single-update correction limiting.
AI.result: Experiments in long tunnels and curved/multi-segment tunnels show suppressed error accumulation, reduced risk of large maximum deviation, and improved localization continuity and road-level usability.
AI.conclusion: Route-constrained state estimation effectively mitigates GNSS-denied drift by enforcing road-level constraints within a unified EKF framework, with practical applicability supported by targeted engineering strategies.

title: CSWinUNETR: Segmentation of Thin Anatomical Structures in Medical Images
summary: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures, such as retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, and facial wrinkles, remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance. Although recent convolutional and Transformer-based models have improved performance, they often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches. We propose CSWinUNETR, a general-purpose backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation. It employs cross-shaped stripe self-attention to model long-range principal-axis context and incorporates cyclic shifts to enhance information exchange across stripes. To better preserve fine-grained details, we further introduce a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module that aggregates contextual features from multi-resolution representations. In addition, we propose sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, which reconstructs reliable dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to better follow tortuous geometry. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology demonstrate that CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses. The code is available at https://github.com/labhai/CSWinUNETR.

AI.tldr: CSWinUNETR introduces a general-purpose backbone for 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation, leveraging cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts, detail-enhanced multi-scale attention, and sparse-control dynamic snake convolution to recover fine tortuous structures, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks without task-specific post-processing.
AI.motivation: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous structures (e.g., retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, facial wrinkles) is hampered by low contrast, discontinuities, and severe class imbalance, leading to fragmented predictions and missed fine branches. A model that captures long-range axis context and preserves fine details is needed.
AI.method: 1) Cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts to model long-range principal-axis context; 2) Detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention to fuse multi-resolution contextual features; 3) Sparse-control dynamic snake convolution to reconstruct dense curvilinear kernels from sparse control points for better tracking of tortuous geometry.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology show CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses.
AI.conclusion: CSWinUNETR is a robust, general-purpose backbone for 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation, effectively preserving fine details and improving segmentation of tortuous structures; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Spectral Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting leverages historical patterns to predict future values, but traditional methods face challenges when dealing with complex, non-stationary patterns that are difficult to memorize during training. Retrieval-augmented approaches have emerged as promising solutions by retrieving similar historical patterns to enhance predictions. However, existing retrieval methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: spectral blindness, which overlooks critical frequency-domain characteristics that capture underlying periodic structures, and temporal recency, which treats all historical data equally without emphasizing recent, more relevant patterns. In this paper, we propose SpecReTF, a novel retrieval method that addresses these issues by converting time series into windowed frequency representations, measuring similarity with a combined metric that captures both amplitude and phase information. To balance recency and historical context, we apply an exponential moving average weighting scheme that emphasizes recent windows. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series.

AI.tldr: SpecReTF introduces a retrieval-based augmentation for time series forecasting that uses windowed frequency-domain representations with amplitude–phase similarity and EMA-based recency weighting to improve non-stationary series forecasting.
AI.motivation: To overcome spectral blindness and lack of recency bias in existing time-series retrieval methods by leveraging frequency-domain structure and emphasizing recent patterns.
AI.method: Convert time series into windowed frequency representations; measure similarity using a combined amplitude-and-phase metric; apply exponential moving average weighting to emphasize recent windows before retrieval.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on benchmark datasets show SpecReTF achieves superior forecasting accuracy compared to time-domain retrieval methods across diverse, non-stationary time series.
AI.conclusion: SpecReTF effectively addresses spectral blindness and recency limitations in retrieval-based time-series forecasting, yielding improved performance on non-stationary data.

title: Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks
summary: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

AI.tldr: EPB introduces a task-appropriate interpretability framework for neural combinatorial optimization by evolving a bank of human-readable programs that distill per-step action distributions of black-box NCO models, with a hybrid gradient-based training and dynamic capacity management.
AI.motivation: NCO models are black-box, dynamic, and lack a stable concept vocabulary; CBMs are ill-suited for sequential decision-making; need interpretable, human-readable representations of policy behavior.
AI.method: EPB uses an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs; per-step action distributions from the NCO model act as bottlenecks; Block I couples numerical gradients (for student router updates) with textual gradients for program revision; Block II expands/shrinks bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning.
AI.result: Program portfolios distilled by EPB largely retain original performance; reveals stage-wise shifts in NCO behavior and that NCO can be approximated as compositions of classic heuristic variants; shows broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: EPB advances interpretable NCO and establishes it as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

title: Comparative Study on Agility, Efficiency, and Impact Absorption of Bipedal Robots with Active Toes
summary: Human legs exhibit high efficiency, agility, and impact absorption, with toes playing a crucial role in these capabilities. While many attempts have been made to implement human-like toes in robots, they have not fully replicated human characteristics nor rigorously validated their benefits. We propose a 14-DOF biped robot emulating human toes' lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. To quantitatively analyze the effectiveness of the active toes in terms of agility, efficiency, and impact absorption, we developed a high-fidelity simulation training environment that reflects actual actuators with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption. To ensure a fair comparison between configurations with and without active toes, we designed a minimal RL reward function and applied an identical training procedure to both. The simulation results indicate that, at 1.33 m/s walking, the toe-equipped robot reduced CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with the toe-ablation configuration. On the agility test, average and maximum path deviation decreased by 25.0% and 34.0%, respectively.

AI.tldr: A 14-DOF biped robot with active toes; high-fidelity simulation shows improvements in efficiency, agility, and impact absorption compared to toe-absent configuration.
AI.motivation: To quantify the benefits of human-inspired active toes and provide rigorous validation via fair, controlled comparison.
AI.method: Developed a 14-DOF biped with active toes; built a high-fidelity simulator with coupled transmissions and realistic power use; trained toe-enabled and toe-absent configurations with an identical RL procedure using a minimal reward; evaluated CoT, heel-strike GRF, and agility.
AI.result: Toe-equipped robotic configuration reduced CoT by 17.5% at 1.33 m/s; heel-strike GRF reduced by 5%; agility metrics showed average path deviation down 25% and maximum deviation down 34%.
AI.conclusion: Active toes yield measurable improvements in efficiency, impact absorption, and agility in simulation; results support further hardware validation and exploration of toe mechanics for legged robots.

title: 3D-PLOT-LLM: Part-Level Object Tokens for 3D Large Language Models
summary: 3D multimodal large language models (3D MLLMs) describe a 3D object as a whole but cannot address, name, or reason about its parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. We take a fundamentally different path: we reorganize the input token stream so that parts become directly addressable through the LLM's own vocabulary. Our model, 3D-PLOT-LLM, partitions the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions and inserts, before each region's patch tokens, a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>; a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module then conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. The model thus cites parts in its output and follows prompts that refer to parts by token, a capability absent from prior object-level 3D MLLMs. To probe this interface, we construct PartVerse-QA, a vocabulary-level part-QA benchmark adapted from PartVerse mesh annotations (77K training pairs and 588 held-out queries on disjoint object splits), on which 3D-PLOT-LLM reaches caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78%, with a slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge of 44.68. On the 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware grounded description benchmark, 3D-PLOT-LLM outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on every text-output metric, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. On Objaverse whole-object captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR) despite targeting a different (part-grounded) objective. All with under 1M new trainable parameters on a frozen point encoder, an order of magnitude below prior part-aware 3D MLLMs, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

AI.tldr: 3D-PLOT-LLM enables part-aware reasoning in 3D MLLMs by inserting per-region markers into the token stream, achieving robust part-level descriptions with under 1M trainable parameters and no segmentation decoder, and outperforming baselines on PartVerse-QA, 3DCoMPaT-GrIn, and Objaverse captioning.
AI.motivation: Current 3D multimodal LLMs describe objects holistically and struggle to name, reason about, or ground parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. A vocabulary-level approach that makes parts addressable by the LLM itself aims to provide direct part references with little extra trainable capacity.
AI.method: Leverage a frozen 3D point encoder. Partition its patch tokens into K locally coherent regions. Before each region’s patch tokens, insert a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>. A Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module conditions each marker on the region’s spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. This yields part-grounded outputs via prompts that refer to parts by token, without segmentation decoders or bounding-box heads and with ≤1M new trainable parameters.
AI.result: PartVerse-QA: caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459; Exact-match 13.78%; slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge 44.68. 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware: outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on all text-output metrics, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. Objaverse full-captioning: adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR). All results achieved with under 1M new trainable parameters and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.
AI.conclusion: A vocabulary-level, part-grounded interface for 3D MLLMs is viable and efficient, enabling explicit part references and improved part-aware generation across benchmarks without heavy architectural additions.

title: Does Text Actually Help? Uncovering and Resolving Text Collapse in Multimodal Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multimodal time series forecasting, which pairs numerical sequences with domain-relevant textual reports, promises to inject world knowledge into forecasting pipelines. However, we uncover a critical failure mode in existing frameworks that we term text collapse: the text branch converges to a content-independent transformation, contributing negligible discriminative signal regardless of the input description. We argue that text collapse is a consequence of a fundamental asymmetry in time series forecasting: the numerical input is strongly autocorrelated with the output, making the numerical backbone inherently dominant, while the text branch, despite carrying complementary and often critical information, is insufficiently utilized, leading to its systematic underexploitation. To address this, we propose \textbf{REST-TS} (\textbf{R}esidual-\textbf{E}xclusive \textbf{S}upervision for \textbf{T}ext in \textbf{T}ime \textbf{S}eries), which turns the asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent numerical forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual, the prediction gap that numbers cannot explain. Because no numerical pathway can reduce these losses, the text branch must extract genuine content from the input description. Evaluated across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently demonstrates greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, providing strong empirical evidence that supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input.

AI.tldr: REST-TS: a residual-exclusive supervision approach to mitigate text collapse in multimodal time-series forecasting, yielding state-of-the-art results and better text utilization.
AI.motivation: Text collapse occurs due to time-series autocorrelation overwhelming the text modality; the text branch learns a content-agnostic transformation, underutilizing input descriptions.
AI.method: Numerical backbone forecasts independently; text branch trained only to predict residual structure (the explainable portion) of the target; residual-exclusive supervision ensures text stores real content from description.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across varied domains and backbones shows state-of-the-art performance and consistently higher text-branch utilization than baselines.
AI.conclusion: Turning asymmetry into a design principle via residual-exclusive supervision enables better exploitation of textual information in time-series forecasting; the approach generalizes across domains and backbones.

title: A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, Submitted to International Journal of Speech Technology
summary: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: Domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained speech transformers (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on a Quranic corpus (~870+ hours) yields state-of-the-art ASR for Quranic recitation, with WER ~0.08–0.11, outperforming Citrinet and reducing training time. Diacritics-free Arabic and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 give strongest representations; future work includes dataset quality and phoneme-aware Tajweed models.
AI.motivation: Address high word error rates and limited corpus coverage of Quranic ASR by leveraging domain-adaptive fine-tuning of self-supervised Transformer models to improve transcription accuracy for Quranic recitation and Tajweed-sensitive applications.
AI.method: Fine-tune pretrained Transformer-based speech models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Conduct comprehensive ablations across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations. Evaluate on EveryAyah and combined EveryAyah+Tarteel subsets; compare against Citrinet baseline.
AI.result: Best configuration achieves WER 0.08 on EveryAyah and 0.11 on EveryAyah+Tarteel, about a five-percentage-point gain over Citrinet (WER 0.163). Training time for the combined model reduces from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation.
AI.conclusion: Domain-adaptive fine-tuning substantially improves Quranic ASR performance and efficiency. Future work should focus on improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to better capture Tajweed-sensitive features.

title: A Differentiable Composite Approximation Framework for Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Maneuvering Modeling from Sea-Trial Data
summary: Field-based modeling from onboard measurements can produce autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) maneuvering models that reflect real operating characteristics. From an approximation perspective, conventional maneuvering models use predefined constraint polynomial bases, whereas data-driven models use data-adaptive bases. Motivated by this basis-function view, this paper presents a differentiable composite-approximation formulation, in which the polynomial-basis component and the data-adaptive basis component are treated as differentiable parts of a single predictor and calibrated jointly. A gradient-based co-calibration method is developed for full-scale AUV maneuvering prediction, where a sensitivity-aware mechanism regulates bounded polynomial updates while the neural residual captures remaining nonlinear discrepancies under a shared prediction objective. To account for ocean-current effects in field data, a turning-motion-based current estimation and compensation procedure is incorporated to construct current-compensated learning targets for training and rollout. The framework is evaluated using sea-trial data collected from a 7-meter AUV under multiple maneuvering conditions. Results show that the proposed method improves recursive trajectory and velocity prediction compared with polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines, demonstrating its applicability to field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling.

AI.tldr: A differentiable composite-approximation method for AUV maneuvering that blends polynomial-basis and data-driven bases into a single optimizable predictor, with gradient-based co-calibration, a sensitivity-aware update bound on the polynomial part, and current estimation/compensation to produce current-adjusted learning targets. Evaluated on sea-trial data from a 7-meter AUV, it yields improved recursive trajectory and velocity predictions versus polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior baselines.
AI.motivation: Field measurements enable models that reflect real operating characteristics, but traditional approaches rely on fixed polynomial bases while purely data-driven models lack structured interpretability and may misrepresent known physics; a differentiable hybrid that jointly optimizes both bases can leverage the strengths of each while accommodating environmental disturbances like currents.
AI.method: Form a differentiable predictor comprising a polynomial-basis component and a data-adaptive (neural residual) component. Use gradient-based co-calibration to train jointly, with a sensitivity-aware mechanism that bounds updates to the polynomial part. The neural residual handles remaining nonlinear discrepancies. Introduce a current-estimation/compensation step to generate current-compensated targets for training/rollout, addressing ocean-current effects. Train and evaluate on sea-trial data from a 7 m AUV under multiple maneuvers, comparing against polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior baselines.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves improved recursive trajectory and velocity prediction over the baselines, validating its effectiveness for field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling.
AI.conclusion: A differentiable composite-approximation approach that couples polynomial and data-adaptive bases with current compensation yields superior field-data-based AUV maneuvering models and can be extended to similar field robotics scenarios.

title: Neural Events: Discrete Asynchronous Autoencoders for Event-Based Vision
summary: Event cameras capture dynamic scenes with exceptional temporal fidelity by representing them as a continuous stream of microsecond resolution \textit{events}. Each individual event, however, only carries minimal semantic value, merely signaling a localized brightness change. To derive meaningful signals, downstream algorithms need to quickly integrate cues from a potentially massive torrent of low-information events. Current architectures, however, are easily overwhelmed, struggling to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics and maintaining a manageable data throughput. This paper proposes a framework to re-tokenize event streams into a small set of highly informative \textit{neural events}, each representing a local spatio-temporal context window with a discrete learnable code. Every time this code flips, a neural event is triggered, yielding a highly compressed data stream. We demonstrate that, across object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events are on par or surpass the performance of state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.

AI.tldr: A neural-event tokenization framework converts raw event streams into a small set of discrete, learnable-context events that trigger on code flips, achieving similar or better object detection/classification performance while halving event rate.
AI.motivation: Event cameras produce大量 events with minimal semantic value; processing them efficiently is challenging. A compact, informative representation is needed to preserve temporal fidelity while reducing data throughput.
AI.method: Re-tokenize event streams into a small set of neural events defined by a local spatio-temporal context window encoded by a discrete learnable code; a neural event is triggered whenever the code flips, yielding a compressed data stream.
AI.result: For object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events match or exceed state-of-the-art performance while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Neural events enable an efficient, scalable representation of event-camera data, maintaining performance with substantially reduced data throughput, supporting real-time and energy-efficient perception.

title: MortarBench: Evaluating Mortgage Loan Origination Agents
summary: Loan origination is the process by which a lender creates a new loan, from application and underwriting through approval and funding. This process serves a critical role in evaluating the eligibility and level of risk posed by an applicant. Recently, firms have begun using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, despite a lack of any public benchmark. To fill this gap, we present MortarBench, a loan origination agent benchmark. MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate examples with broad edge case coverage that match real-world distributions and questions. We find that state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) perform poorly, with closed-source models achieving at most 77.1\% exact match accuracy. We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

AI.tldr: MortarBench: a loan origination benchmark using synthetic data to test LLMs on loan decisions; reveals LLMs struggle and exhibit non-English name bias; CRIT calibration improves accuracy and reduces bias.
AI.motivation: Fill the public benchmark gap for loan origination agents, enabling evaluation of LLMs in real-world underwriting tasks and risk management.
AI.method: Financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to create edge-case examples that match real-world distributions; evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs (closed/open) and analysis of bias; development of CRIT confidence calibration framework.
AI.result: LLMs achieve at most 77.1% exact-match accuracy; biases in perception of foreignness for non-English names detected; CRIT improves accuracy to 80.5% and enhances risk management control and reduces bias.
AI.conclusion: MortarBench exposes weaknesses in current LLMs for loan origination and risk assessment, while CRIT provides a practical mitigation by improving calibration and fairness; suggests priority on robust evaluation and bias mitigation in fin-ML deployment.

title: Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems
summary: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.

AI.tldr: AI review systems can correlate with human-quality signals and detect errors better than chance, but still miss substantial errors; OpenAIReview with GPT-5.5 performs best among tested configurations, and real-user deployment shows overall positive reception despite some false positives.
AI.motivation: Assess how AI-generated peer reviews could supplement or replace traditional peer review under AI-assisted research, and identify strengths and limitations of contemporary AI review systems.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple AI review configurations (OpenAIReview, coarse, Reviewer3, and a zero-shot baseline) across six LLMs; assess correlation with external quality signals (citations, acceptance), use a perturbation benchmark to test error detection across eight arXiv classes; observe a public deployment with real users to gauge reception.
AI.result: Best config (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) achieves 83.0% pairwise accuracy; perturbation recall 71.6% for strongest setup; union recall across models 83.3%; real-user deployment reports favorable votes (1.44 to 1) with common complaints about false positives and minor nitpicks.
AI.conclusion: AI reviews show potential to reflect human quality judgments and catch important errors, and enjoy positive user reception, but substantial room for improvement remains; better design and model ensembles could further improve performance.

title: Bidirectional Tutoring for Developmental Motor Learning in Robots: Co-Developed Interaction Dynamics Support Stable Learning
summary: Infants are well known to develop their motor skills through dense interaction with caregivers. Although such social interaction is crucial for human development, motor-skill learning in robots is often treated as a unidirectional process in which robots passively receive demonstrations from tutors. This overlooks a key property of social interaction: it is inherently bidirectional, with tutor and learner dynamically adapting to each other. In such interactions, the robot's past experiences may function as prior constraints that shape the dynamics of their co-developed trajectories. We hypothesize that bidirectional tutoring allows such constraints to guide the formation of consistent behavioral patterns that preserve behavioral coherence and support generalization, whereas unidirectional interaction lacks such constraints and leads to broader, less consistent behavioral patterns. To examine this hypothesis, we conducted two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: one involving human-robot interaction and another employing an AI tutor interacting with the real robot through an adaptive intervention mechanism designed to examine whether similar effects would emerge under more controlled conditions. We implement the developmental learning framework using a free-energy-principle-based neural network extended with generative replay, which supports stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization, while the robot gradually required less tutor guidance. These results suggest that bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots.

AI.tldr: Bidirectional tutoring in robot learning yields more coherent, generalizable motor behavior than unidirectional tutoring; a free-energy-principle neural network with generative replay enables stable sequence-by-sequence learning from limited tutoring episodes; tested in human-robot and AI-tutor setups, showing reduced need for guidance over time.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether bidirectional social interaction during motor learning constrains behavior via prior experiences, leading to coherent, generalizable trajectories, and whether this extends beyond human tutors to AI tutors under controlled conditions.
AI.method: Two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: (1) human-robot interaction and (2) AI tutor with an adaptive intervention mechanism. The developmental framework is implemented with a free-energy-principle-based neural network augmented with generative replay, enabling stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes.
AI.result: Bidirectional tutoring produced more consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization in both setups. Over time, the robot required less tutor guidance, indicating effective consolidation.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional tutoring, grounded in embodiment and social interaction, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots, promoting coherence and generalization while reducing dependence on tutoring.

title: OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification
summary: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

AI.tldr: OTCHA: a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment using optimal transport to refine patch tokens for multi-view medical image fusion, with token-conditional dustbins for partial matching and a transport-based alignment loss.
AI.motivation: Medical multi-view imaging often has unregistered views and view-specific artifacts that contaminate fused representations. Direct per-view fusion can propagate irrelevant cues, reducing robustness across view configurations. There is a need for view-agnostic, robust token alignment prior to fusion.
AI.method: Introduce latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, compute an optimal transport plan between patch tokens and hub tokens, incorporating feature similarity and geometry. Add token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. Use the transport plan to obtain token-wise matching confidence to gate hub-mediated message passing. Employ an OT-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement of tokens before fusion.
AI.result: Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: OTCHA improves multi-view medical image fusion by aligning per-view patch tokens to a common latent hub via OT with confidence-guided pruning, yielding robust performance across varying view setups and anatomies.

title: 3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning
summary: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

AI.tldr: 3D-DLP is a self-supervised, object-centric representation learning model that decomposes RGB-D/voxel scenes into a set of 3D latent particles with disentangled attributes (position, size, appearance). It yields interpretable per-particle segmentation, supports controllable scene generation by manipulating particles, and improves downstream robotic manipulation over non-object-centric or dense-3D baselines; code/videos available.
AI.motivation: To obtain compact, interpretable, and controllable 3D scene representations for manipulation tasks, addressing limitations of dense 3D inputs and lack of explicit object-centric structure in existing methods.
AI.method: Extends Deep Latent Particles (DLP) to 3D by representing each particle with 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features. Learns per-particle segmentation maps via an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective on RGB-D or voxel data, enabling scene decomposition into a set of disentangled latent particles.
AI.result: Empirical evidence on simulated and real datasets shows the latent space is interpretable and controllable. Manipulating particle positions and decoding yields novel scene configurations. Using these 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance versus baselines that lack explicit 3D information or rely on dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure.
AI.conclusion: 3D-DLP provides a compact, interpretable, and controllable 3D object-centric representation that benefits scene generation and robotic manipulation. The authors release code and videos at the provided URL.

title: Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models
summary: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

AI.tldr: Defines four AI-blended architectural primitives to deterministically encapsulate probabilistic generative models and two anti-patterns, forming a framework to de-risk AI integration into traditional systems and guide next-gen interfaces.
AI.motivation: Industry seeks reliable, interoperable, and safer integration of generative models with deterministic systems; current ad hoc approaches risk behavior, governance, and integration costs.
AI.method: Proposes four architectural primitives shaping how probabilistic models are encapsulated within AI-blended architectures; identifies two widespread anti-patterns; discusses design implications for interfaces and governance.
AI.result: A structured framework that enables deterministic composition of AI components within traditional systems and warns against common missteps; intended to guide practitioners and model provider interfaces.
AI.conclusion: Offers a concrete foundation for de-risked AI adoption in traditional environments and a basis for further tooling, standards, and empirical validation.

title: VOiLA: Vectorized Online Planning with Learned Diffusion Model for POMDP Agents
comment: Submitted to the 2026 International Symposium of Robotics Research (ISRR)
summary: Planning under uncertainty is an essential capability for autonomous robots. The Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) provides a powerful framework for such a capability. Although POMDP-based planning has advanced significantly, its application to real-world problems is often limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models. We present Vectorized Online planning wIth Learned diffusion model for POMDP Agents (VOiLA), a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty. VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models and learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. To enable efficient online planning, the diffusion samplers are distilled into compact feedforward generators and integrated with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP), an online POMDP planner designed to leverage GPU parallelization. Experimental results indicate the distillation strategy reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude, making learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning. Evaluation of VOiLA on three benchmark problems indicate that VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% training data, and generalizes much better to unseen environment configurations. Physical robot evaluation indicates VOiLA uses the models learned using only simulated data and generates a policy that successfully accomplish the task in 10 of 10 runs.

AI.tldr: VOiLA learns task-agnostic POMDP models via conditional diffusion models, distills diffusion samplers into fast feedforward generators, and uses GPU-accelerated online planning (VOPP). It achieves large sampling-time savings, strong data efficiency, good generalization, and successful sim-to-real transfer in robotic tasks.
AI.motivation: Faithful POMDP models for planning under uncertainty are hard to obtain; online planning requires data-efficient, scalable methods that work with limited real-world data and transfer from simulation to hardware.
AI.method: Train transition and observation samplers with conditional diffusion models; learn observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates; distill diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators; integrate with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP) to exploit GPU parallelization for online planning.
AI.result: Sampling cost reduced by up to ~1000x (three orders of magnitude). VOiLA matches or surpasses Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% of the training data; shows superior generalization to unseen configurations. Physical robot experiments succeed in 10/10 runs using models learned from simulated data.
AI.conclusion: VOiLA demonstrates practical, data-efficient, learned generative POMDP models compatible with online planning and real-world execution, enabling robust decision-making under uncertainty with limited data and computational efficiency.

title: ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference
summary: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

AI.tldr: ViCoStream offers a stage-wise streaming framework for Video LLMs that coordinates preprocessing, encoding, token dropping, and decoding to achieve high throughput and low latency with near full-history accuracy.
AI.motivation: Real-time streaming Video LLMs require end-to-end coordination to meet both ingestion throughput and query latency; existing work focuses on isolated modules and lacks end-to-end performance guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce ViCoStream, a stage-wise pipeline using chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval; analyze bottleneck migration; explore hyperparameters (chunk size, token retention, attention locality, retrieval scope).
AI.result: Achieves 134 FPS video throughput and <50 ms TTFT on a single A100 across Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct benchmarks, with accuracy close to full-history baselines.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end coordinated streaming can bound per-chunk costs and sustain real-time throughput while preserving accuracy; design choices and parameter regimes strongly affect throughput-accuracy trade-offs.

title: Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?
summary: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

AI.tldr: ICL-based intrinsic rewards can guide exploration without inner-loop updates in non-temporal settings, but fail to unbiasedly measure learning progress in general MDPs; theory yields a boundary: possible and convergent in active learning/Bayesian experimental design, with empirical demonstrations of near-optimal data-collection policies.
AI.motivation: To scale intrinsically motivated data collection by leveraging the in-context learning capabilities of large sequence models as update-free world models, avoiding costly inner-gradient updates.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze intrinsic rewards based on ICL predictions. Prove impossibility of unbiased learning-progress rewards in general MDPs; identify a broad non-temporal subclass (active learning, Bayesian experimental design) where ICL-derived rewards bound and converge to true learning progress. Validate with controlled experiments in continuous and symbolic environments.
AI.result: In general MDPs, intrinsic rewards derived from ICL suffer bias or cannot be implemented via an in-context learner. For non-temporal settings, ICL-derived rewards bound and asymptotically converge to true learning progress. Experiments show ICL-driven data-collection policies explore optimally across tested environments.
AI.conclusion: ICL can enable update-free intrinsic rewards in a restricted class of problems (non-temporal active learning/experimental design), yielding near-optimal exploration, while in general MDPs the approach is not unbiased or practical.

title: Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers
summary: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

AI.tldr: A queueing-theoretic framework for topic-aware expert forums where schedulers assign topic-specific requests to experts to maximize capacity while maintaining stability, with exploration of collaboration among experts to further boost capacity.
AI.motivation: Address scalability of QA forums by introducing knowledge workers (topic experts) and scheduling policies that assign requests efficiently, ensuring system stability and high throughput.
AI.method: Develop a multi-class queueing model with topic-specific servers (experts) and topic-varied processing rates; design and analyze schedulers that dispatch requests to appropriate experts; derive capacity (stability) results and investigate the impact of expert collaboration on throughput.
AI.result: Derives capacity bounds and constructs schedulers that achieve capacity; demonstrates that collaboration among experts can increase system capacity, offering guidelines for scheduler design to maximize throughput under stability constraints.
AI.conclusion: Topic-aware scheduling combined with expert collaboration can significantly enhance the capacity and stability of QA forums; provides theoretical foundations and design principles for future knowledge-work platforms.

title: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation policies trained with reward shaping can still exploit dense rewards through inefficient interaction, while rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten during training. We argue that temporal efficiency itself provides a powerful and underutilized source of self-supervision for reinforcement learning. We introduce Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL), a reinforcement learning framework that mines temporally efficient successful trajectories generated during learning and converts them into reusable supervision for future policy improvement. TSIL progressively refines learning using configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from fast successful trajectories, while preserving and replaying efficient behaviors through efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning. Across 15 distinct long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training conditions. More broadly, our results suggest that the temporal structure of successful behavior itself provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

AI.tldr: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL) leverages temporally efficient successful trajectories during learning to provide adaptive, reusable supervision for long-horizon robot manipulation RL, via adaptive temporal targets and efficiency-weighted self-imitation, improving learning efficiency, task performance, and robustness across tasks.
AI.motivation: Reward shaping often yields dense signals but can cause unnecessary exploration and forgetting of rare efficient behaviors; temporal efficiency offers an underutilized self-supervised signal to reinforce concise, effective policies.
AI.method: Mine fast, successful trajectories during learning; derive configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets from these trajectories; apply efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning to preserve and replay efficient behaviors; evaluate across long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.result: TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training across 15 long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting the temporal structure of successful behavior provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning, complementing reward shaping and enabling better generalization and efficiency.

title: PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

AI.tldr: PSCT-Net presents a geometry-aware reconstruction of 3D pediatric skull CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays, using differentiable back-projection, an Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D), and a Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module, plus a private PedSkull-CT dataset for internal validation.
AI.motivation: To enable low-dose 3D skull reconstruction by seeding a geometry-aware prior and non-linear voxel-wise 2D-3D correspondences, addressing depth ambiguity inherent in geometry-agnostic feature lifting.
AI.method: PSCT-Net integrates differentiable back-projection to establish a spatially faithful volumetric prior, AGP-3D to learn voxel-wise 2D-3D correspondences, and BiM-3D to capture long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. The study also introduces PedSkull-CT, a private pediatric skull CT cohort for internal evaluation.
AI.result: The abstract claims improved depth handling and boundary fidelity via geometry-aware modeling but provides no quantitative results; no external validation is reported, and dataset is private.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware, low-dose 3D reconstruction framework for pediatric skull CT is proposed, with potential clinical impact if validated externally; dataset privacy limits broader benchmarking.

title: Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

AI.tldr: Introduces Insulin4RL, a real-world irregular-sampling offline RL healthcare dataset derived from MIMIC-IV for insulin titration in ICU; enables evaluation under realistic clinical sampling with baseline model-free offline RL and fitted Q-evaluation.
AI.motivation: Discretization of EHR data in current offline RL studies yields non-realistic scenarios and can impair generalisability; a dataset capturing irregular clinical trajectories is needed to evaluate RL methods under realistic sampling conditions.
AI.method: Dataset derived from MIMIC-IV; includes 12,209 patients and over 375,000 labelled insulin titration decisions; features irregular inputs and actions; baseline model-free offline RL experiments conducted; evaluation standardized via fitted Q-evaluation; dataset structure and characteristics described.
AI.result: Provided baseline offline RL performance metrics and a standardised evaluation protocol; demonstrates feasibility and usefulness of Insulin4RL for research on offline RL under realistic sampling; no specific numerical results are stated in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The resource enables future research on offline RL in healthcare under realistic sampling assumptions; future work suggested includes broader benchmarking, algorithm development for irregular sampling, and robust evaluation protocols.

title: Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

AI.tldr: ICT framework uses Jensen-Shannon divergence on token logits to identify distinctive tokens and updates only the top 10% of unique tokens, stabilizing RL-based LLM reasoning and improving pass@4 on Qwen models across seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address optimization instability in RL with verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning, caused by entropy collapse from uniform updates and entropy explosion from excessive exploration; shift focus from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits.
AI.method: Compute token logit distributions; use Jensen-Shannon divergence to find tokens with distinctive distributional patterns; selectively update the top 10% most distinctive tokens; provide theoretical analysis using Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy to show reduced entropy and controlled concentration; empirical validation on Qwen2.5 models (0.5B/1.5B/7B).
AI.result: Updating only the top 10% of unique tokens yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% and a maximum gain of 14.9% over baselines (GRPO, 20-Entropy, STAPO) across seven benchmarks, spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.
AI.conclusion: ICT stabilizes RL-driven LLM reasoning by focusing updates on distributionally distinctive tokens, balancing Shannon and Rényi entropy to prevent over- or under-exploration and achieve robust performance gains.

title: Data Standards for Humanoid Robotics: The Missing Infrastructure for Physical AI
summary: The scalability of humanoid robots will depend not only on models and hardware, but also on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1, Humanoid robot datasets -- Part 1: General requirements, within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, this article argues that data standards are becoming foundational infrastructure for Physical AI. We develop three insights. First, humanoid robot data is embodied interaction data, not a collection of isolated digital samples; a useful dataset must preserve the relationship among robot body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome. Second, its value depends on physical coherence: multimodal streams are reusable only when timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization assumptions remain inspectable. Third, the main bottleneck is not only data scarcity, but non-cumulative data caused by high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. We argue that humanoid robot data standards address these bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable. A general standard should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts should define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, human-robot interaction, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities. As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

AI.tldr: Data standards for humanoid robot datasets are essential infrastructure for Physical AI, emphasizing embodied interaction, data coherence, and tackling non-cumulative data bottlenecks via general and capability-specific standards.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable humanoid robotics by sharing and reusing embodied data across robots, tasks, organizations, and time; support ISO/WD 26264-1 development and address data scarcity, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation.
AI.method: Argues, drawing on the authors’ work in ISO/WD 26264-1, that data are embodied interaction data requiring preservation of relationships among body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome; identifies coherence requirements; highlights bottlenecks; proposes a general standard with lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, traceability, plus domain-specific parts defining grammar for manipulation, locomotion, HRI, cognition, and future capabilities.
AI.result: Proposes that humanoid robot data standards can make embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable; outlines a horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle and metadata along with capability-specific domain grammar for key robot functions.
AI.conclusion: As AI transitions from screens to bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.

title: Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: Present at NeurIPS 2025 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
