summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

AI.tldr: MM-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models to a multimodal CLIP framework by using dual bottleneck layers to align image and text embeddings into shared interpretable concepts, enabling zero-shot classification and image retrieval with strong performance.
AI.motivation: Address generalization beyond a fixed concept set and prevent leakage of non-concept information, while improving interpretability in multimodal vision-language models.
AI.method: Introduce dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) that align image and text embeddings into interpretable concept features within the CLIP framework, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and retrieval.
AI.result: Achieves up to 51.26% average accuracy improvement across four benchmarks, and maintains performance within approximately 5% of black-box models while offering greater interpretability.
AI.conclusion: MM-CBM provides interpretable, multimodal extension of CBMs that generalizes to unseen tasks with strong accuracy, reducing leakage and enhancing interpretability in vision-language settings.

title: Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

AI.tldr: CFMs replace flat concept bottlenecks with a hierarchical decision tree to localize concept usage, achieving interpretability and competitive accuracy while mitigating information leakage.
AI.motivation: Flat concept bottlenecks in CBMs suffer from information leakage as the number of concepts nears the embedding dimension, allowing spurious correlations and undermining interpretability. A hierarchical, concept-driven structure can provide localized reasoning and auditability.
AI.method: Construct a hierarchical decision tree from visual embeddings, assign semantic concepts to tree levels, and train differentiable concept weights via probabilistic traversal. Internal nodes focus on localized discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope.
AI.result: CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs across diverse benchmarks while significantly reducing information leakage by limiting effective concept usage. They also produce stepwise, auditable decision flows with hierarchical class structures.
AI.conclusion: CFMs offer interpretability and auditability without sacrificing accuracy, by organizing concepts hierarchically to control information flow and enable transparent reasoning.

title: AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA
summary: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: AgentFinVQA is an auditable, on-premise multi-agent financial chart QA system with per-sample traceability (MEP) that achieves notable accuracy gains over backbones (including open-weight models) and provides a verifier-based confidence signal for human-in-the-loop routing.
AI.motivation: In regulated finance, data residency and auditability are mandatory. Existing chart-QA solutions are accuracy-centric, opaque, and typically rely on external APIs, hindering trust and compliance.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposes a query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. Evaluation on FinMME uses a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash) and open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8, with ablations showing the verifier’s usefulness for confidence signaling.
AI.result: AgentFinVQA improves +7.68 percentage points over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline (71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar p ≈ 1.1×10^-16) and +4.84 pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. Verifier verdict provides a confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers). Error analysis attributes two-thirds of failures to question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction errors, with these categories least detected by the verifier.
AI.conclusion: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical with substantial accuracy gains preserved by open-weight models, and the per-sample MEP trace enables better trust and human-in-the-loop routing; code release supports reproducible evaluation.

title: Start Right, Arrive Right: Asynchronous Execution via Initial Noise Selection
comment: First version 19 pages, project site: https://paint-action-chunking.github.io
summary: Action chunking enables robot policies to produce temporally coherent behavior, but generating multi-step action sequences with flow-based policies incurs latency that is incompatible with real-time control. Under asynchronous execution, the robot continues executing the current chunk while the next one is generated, causing even minor delays to create inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Existing methods address this problem by steering generation toward the already executed action prefix. We instead show that prefix consistency can be achieved by selecting an appropriate initial noise before generation begins, allowing the unmodified flow ODE to produce a coherent next chunk. This reframes asynchronous inference as a noise selection problem rather than a trajectory steering problem. We introduce \textbf{PAINT}, a training-free method that finds this noise via backward Euler inversion and constructs the final chunk through a repainting rule. In summary, \texttt{PAINT} requires no gradients, retraining, or policy modification; yet it improves execution consistency and task performance across \textit{12 simulated benchmarks} and \textit{6 real-world manipulation tasks} spanning single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments. Website: ~\href{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}{\texttt{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}}.

AI.tldr: PAINT is a training-free noise-selection method that achieves prefix-consistent, real-time chunking for flow-based action generation by backward Euler inversion, avoiding policy retraining or inference-time steering.
AI.motivation: Real-time control requires low-latency, temporally coherent multi-step action chunks. Asynchronous inference causes boundary inconsistencies; existing methods steer generation to the executed prefix.
AI.method: Backward Euler inversion to pick an appropriate initial noise before generation; a repainting rule constructs the final chunk; no gradients, retraining, or policy modification is needed.
AI.result: Improved execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world manipulation tasks, across single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments.
AI.conclusion: Reframes asynchronous inference as a noise-selection problem; PAINT is training-free and effective for coherent, real-time flow-based control.

title: SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics
summary: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

AI.tldr: SurgVista introduces two training recipes to fix long-horizon, data-driven surgical world models: Deformation Consistency Regularization to enforce cross-frame physical coherence in tissue dynamics, and Drift Adaptation Training to curb long-horizon visual drift. A new SurgWorld-Bench benchmark enables rigorous, decoupled evaluation of instrument motion and tissue response. Empirical results show improved visual quality, temporal stability, and interaction fidelity, especially at longer horizons.
AI.motivation: Autonomous surgical policy learning is constrained by scarce expert demonstrations and safety risks of in vivo exploration. Existing surgical world models produce action-conditioned future frames but suffer spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse, hindering long-horizon planning. A robust, evaluable world model is needed to enable scalable, safe autonomous surgery.
AI.method: Two training recipes. 1) Deformation Consistency Regularization (DCR): extract scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforce cross-frame coherence via latent contrastive learning to produce physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. 2) Drift Adaptation Training (DAT): reduce long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to drift statistics, preserving visual fidelity over extended rollouts. Additionally, SurgWorld-Bench provides diverse procedures, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity to enable rigorous evaluation.
AI.result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with larger gains at longer prediction horizons.
AI.conclusion: The pair of training recipes effectively mitigate the core failure modes of surgical world models, while the SurgWorld-Bench benchmark enables robust, decoupled evaluation. The approach advances scalable, safer autonomous surgical policy learning, though empirical validation across more procedures and real-world deployment remains for future work.

title: Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale
comment: 34 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

AI.tldr: Identifies a parameter-only dead-direction in LayerNorm transformers: the inverse-scale direction of the LayerNorm affine is an exact kernel of the post-final-norm activation covariance, enabling a cheap read of singular directions without forward/backward passes; validated across 14 pretrained models, distinguishing LayerNorm from RMSNorm and informing about Fisher geometry.
AI.motivation: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima where the Fisher information degenerates along dead directions. Existing approaches to locate such directions require forward passes and eigen decompositions. The work seeks a direction computable solely from network parameters, specific to LayerNorm, to cheaply probe the loss landscape.
AI.method: Derives that the LayerNorm inverse-scale direction γ^{-1}/||γ^{-1}|| is an algebraic kernel of the covariance of post-final-norm centered activations and thus induces a dead direction in parameter space. This direction is read directly from the LayerNorm scale parameter without any forward/backward pass or eigen decomposition. Empirically evaluated on 14 pretrained transformers (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M–35B), comparing with bottom singular direction via one forward pass and SVD, across random initialization and trained checkpoints.
AI.result: At random initialization, the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction to four decimal places for 9/9 LayerNorm models and is absent in 5/5 RMSNorm models. At trained checkpoints, the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ~10^3× and new dead directions emerge. The random-init to trained gap can be read with one forward pass per checkpoint. Consequences include: (1) the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block in 13/14 transformers on their own input distribution (Gemma4-31B is a genuine dead direction pinpointed by the read), and (2) the kernel direction’s presence classifies a transformer’s normalization type from parameters alone.
AI.conclusion: A cheap, parameter-derived dead-direction readout exists for LayerNorm transformers, revealing and tracking subtle Fisher-geometry features during training and across architectures. This direction serves as both a diagnostic of normalization type and a predictor of how the smallest singular value behaves across layers, offering a practical probe of transformer geometry without forward or backward computation.

title: ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end benchmark, ORAgentBench, evaluating autonomous OR agents on 107 realistic tasks with operational artifacts; frontier models struggle, highlighting gaps between optimization coding and reliable operational decision-making.
AI.motivation: To evaluate complete OR agent workflows in realistic settings and address limitations of prior studies that separate modeling from solving and rely on synthetic/text-only tasks.
AI.method: Introduce ORAgentBench: 107 tasks in isolated environments each with natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and explicit submission schema; agents must write/run solution code; submissions validated by hidden validators for schema compliance, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality; evaluate 14 frontier agent-model configurations.
AI.result: Best agent passes 35.51% of tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks; many feasible submissions fall below quality threshold; failures driven by strategic weaknesses (missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible solutions, lack of solution improvement); OR-specific procedural skills improve hard-task feasibility but do not reliably enhance quality or pass rate.
AI.conclusion: To advance OR agents, research should move from plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making, with emphasis on robust problem formulations and improvement loops.

title: EquiVLA: A General Framework for Rotationally Equivariant Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Comment: First version 22 pages, project site: https://equivla.github.io/
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generalist robot manipulation, yet they lack geometric inductive biases: policies trained at specific orientations require substantially more data to generalize across rotational configurations. We present \textsc{EquiVLA}, the first general framework for end-to-end $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant VLA models, applicable to any architecture coupling a frozen vision-language backbone with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. \textsc{EquiVLA} introduces \textsc{EquiPerceptor}, which produces approximately $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features; and \textsc{EquiActor}, an exactly $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. Together, they establish an approximate $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ equivariance chain from camera observations to predicted action sequences. Instantiated on GR00T~N1.5 and evaluated across four LIBERO suites, CALVIN ABCD$\to$D, and five real-robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA, \textsc{EquiVLA} achieves $92.6\%$ average success on LIBERO (vs. $78.1\%$ baseline), an average sequence length of $4.03$ on CALVIN (vs. $3.45$), and improves real-robot success from $54\%$ to $72\%$.

AI.tldr: EquiVLA introduces a general SO(2)-equivariant Vision-Language-Action framework with two components, EquiPerceptor and EquiActor, to establish an approximately and exactly equivariant chain from images to action sequences. It improves data efficiency and generalization to rotations, demonstrated on GR00T N1.5 across LIBERO CALVIN and real-robot tasks, yielding notable gains (LIBERO 92.6% vs 78.1%; CALVIN sequence length 4.03 vs 3.45; real-robot 72% vs 54%).
AI.motivation: VLA models lack geometric inductive biases for rotational generalization; policies trained at specific orientations need excessive data to generalize to varied rotations. Introducing SO(2) equivariance can improve data efficiency and robustness to orientation changes.
AI.method: Proposes EquiVLA, an end-to-end SO(2)-equivariant VLA framework that can wrap any architecture with a frozen vision-language backbone and a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. It includes EquiPerceptor, producing approximately SO(2)-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features, and EquiActor, an exactly SO(2)-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer for action heads. The framework creates an approximate SO(2) equivariance chain from camera to action sequences. Evaluated on GR00T N1.5 and across LIBERO suites (CALVIN ABCD->D) and five real-robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA.
AI.result: Quantitative gains across tasks: LIBERO average success 92.6% (vs 78.1% baseline); CALVIN average sequence length 4.03 (vs 3.45); real-robot success improved from 54% to 72%.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that incorporating explicit SO(2) equivariance in VLA pipelines yields substantial performance and data-efficiency benefits, establishing an equivariance chain from perception to action and enabling better generalization to rotational configurations. The framework is architecture-agnostic with respect to the backbone and compatible with diffusion-based action heads.

title: Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

AI.tldr: LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) for single-image super-resolution, balancing accuracy and efficiency by combining LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement via learned prototypes; achieves state-of-the-art-like performance with competitive compute; code released.
AI.motivation: LRU provides stable linear recurrence suitable for long-range dependencies but suffers from static parameterization and a single-scan approach, limiting 2D vision tasks. The goal is to adapt LRU for efficient, high-quality SR by introducing a modulatory mechanism and a prototype-driven enhancement to capture spatial context and 2D details.
AI.method: Introduce a semantic modulating unit (SMU) that modulates LRU, performs spatial categorization, and enhances features via learned prototypes. Build a restoration network for SR that leverages LRU dynamics with SMU for improved performance and efficiency. Compare against state-of-the-art on standard SR benchmarks and report FLOPs/parameters to demonstrate efficiency; release source code and models.
AI.result: Empirical results show quantitative and qualitative improvements over recent SOTA methods. The method achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods, indicating a favorable accuracy/efficiency trade-off. Ablation studies validate the contributions of LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and prototype-based feature enhancement.
AI.conclusion: LRU-based restoration network with SMU effectively leverages stable linear recurrence for 2D SR tasks, achieving high-quality results without sacrificing efficiency. The proposed SMU is a versatile component that enables modulation, spatially-aware categorization, and prototype-driven feature enhancement. Code available for reproducibility.

title: Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning
summary: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

AI.tldr: Kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM) calibrates generated samples to a target feature distribution by minimizing an unbiased MMD-based objective with KL regularization, preserving model proximity to the pretrained generator. It improves feature-alignment while maintaining validity, demonstrated on antibiotics, and extended to protein and DNA generation across autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Generative models trained on broad spaces can drift away from the target distribution of key features (e.g., antibiotic-like drug features). Direct finetuning on the target set risks overfitting and can degrade other aspects such as validity; a principled method to re-align distributions using feature-level supervision is needed.
AI.method: Introduce kCGM, which minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions. Uses an unbiased score-function estimator for gradient estimates and incorporates KL regularization to keep the model close to the pretrained generator. Demonstrates compatibility with autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision; evaluation on 174 antibiotics; code released.
AI.result: Compared with direct finetuning on the antibiotic subset, finetuning degrades chemical validity while matching feature distributions. kCGM achieves improved alignment to target features while preserving higher chemical validity. Beyond molecules, the approach is shown to adapt to protein and DNA generation tasks, indicating broad applicability across model classes.
AI.conclusion: kCGM provides a principled, versatile framework for distribution calibration of generative models via feature-level MMD optimization with KL regularization, offering improved target alignment without sacrificing validity. It shows promise across modalities (molecules, proteins, DNA) and model types, with potential for further exploration of kernel choices, estimator variance, and scalability to larger target sets.

title: CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models
summary: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

AI.tldr: CombEval is a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in LLMs using typed Cofola specifications, enabling controlled, solver-verified generation of counting problems; it reveals brittleness in models on ordered/indistinguishable objects and nested constraints, with code released for further study.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness of LLMs in combinatorial reasoning and counting tasks by introducing a dynamic, controllable benchmark that can systematically vary problem structure and size.
AI.method: Define problems as typed Cofola specifications over entities, objects, dependencies, and constraints; generate natural-language counting tasks with exact solver-verified answers; vary object type, scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth; evaluate 11 LLMs in direct and code-augmented settings; perform error analysis.
AI.result: Models show brittleness on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, positional constraints, and nested dependencies; constraint interpretation and counting principles are common failure modes; CombEval serves as a diagnostic testbed; code and benchmarks are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: CombEval provides a framework to study when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning and can be extended to analyze broader reasoning faults; the authors release the benchmark and code for community use.

title: TIDY: Thermal Infrared Image Denoising via Wavelet Domain Entropy and Directional Stripe Index
summary: Thermal infrared (TIR) imaging has been a popular choice for field robotics due to its robust perception capability under low light visual degradation, but it suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise that breaks downstream estimation. This noise is intensified indoors due to low thermal contrast and uniform temperature distributions, contributing to the relative lack of indoor TIR deployments. Existing TIR denoising methods exhibit a poor accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, either too slow for online deployment required in robotics or insufficiently robust to severe degradation, while typically being trained on synthetic noise. Addressing these problems, we propose TIDY, a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser trained on real clean-noisy TIR data. By reformulating TIR denoising in the wavelet domain, TIDY explicitly disentangles noise from structural content, enabling targeted suppression with reduced spatial complexity, significantly improving inference speed over prior methods (~34Hz). TIDY introduces two new metrics, Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index, as complementary loss terms to explicitly suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts. Across severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings, TIDY improves robustness and yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks including thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation. Code and dataset is available at: https://github.com/williamrheeth/TIDY

AI.tldr: Proposes TIDY, a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser for thermal infrared (TIR) images trained on real data, delivering real-time performance (~34 Hz) and improved robustness in indoor noise; introduces Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index as loss terms; demonstrates gains in robotics tasks; code/dataset released.
AI.motivation: Indoor TIR suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise, exacerbated by low thermal contrast; existing denoisers trade accuracy for speed and are often trained on synthetic noise, hindering real-world robotics performance. A fast, robust denoiser trained on real data is needed for online robotics tasks.
AI.method: Reformulates denoising in the wavelet domain to separate noise from structural content; uses a lightweight network trained on real clean–noisy TIR data; reduces spatial complexity to boost inference speed (~34 Hz); introduces two novel loss terms: Wavelet Entropy (to suppress stochastic noise) and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index (to suppress stripe artifacts).
AI.result: Improved robustness under severe indoor corruption; consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks (thermal inertial odometry, monocular depth estimation) compared with prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Wavelet-domain denoising with real-data training and wavelet-based losses yields fast, robust TIR denoising suitable for online robotics applications; code and dataset are publicly available.

title: Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos
summary:
Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

AI.tldr: A Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) for endoscopic video restoration uses a temporal GP prior instead of a factorized latent prior, enabling uncertainty-aware frame interpolation. It combines EndoVAE and Vision Transformer encoders with scalable GP approximations (HPA, SPA) and DUCKNet-based masking for specular regions. On C3VDv2 colonoscopy data, it yields substantial reconstruction RMSE gains (avg ~21.9%, up to 26.1%), improves downstream trajectory RMSE (~12.7%), at ~27.3% higher training time per epoch, and provides per-frame uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Endoscopic videos suffer transient corruptions (specular reflections, motion artifacts, missing frames) that degrade visual interpretability and downstream tasks. Temporal dependencies are essential for robust restoration and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Replace factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior in a VAE framework (GPVAE). Use endoscopy-specific encoders (EndoVAE backbone, GastroNet-5M Vision Transformers). Employ two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Mask corrupted pixels with a DUCKNet-based pipeline during reconstruction loss. Evaluate on C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset against matched VAE baselines.
AI.result: Best GPVAE variants reduced reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average (up to 26.1%). Downstream trajectory RMSE reduced by 12.7% on average across classical VO and PoseNet. Training time per epoch increased by 27.3% on average. GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty reflecting temporal support for restored frames.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a temporal GP prior into a VAE enables uncertainty-aware restoration for endoscopic videos and improves both image quality and downstream trajectory estimation, with a trade-off in training efficiency. The GP posterior yields actionable frame-level uncertainty signals.

title: Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning
summary: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference. To avoid explicit Hessian computations when characterizing the tangent space of the manifold, we employ a Krylov subspace method that relies solely on matrix-vector products. These products can be efficiently obtained via automatic differentiation, ensuring both efficiency and robustness throughout the optimization process. The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated using both toy problems and examples from deep learning.

AI.tldr: A predictor-corrector framework (PPE) for interactive Pareto-front exploration in multi-task learning, using Krylov-subspace methods to compute tangent directions without Hessians; validated on toy problems and deep learning tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses computational and decision-making challenges in multi-task learning with many objectives. Weighted sums fail to capture user preferences and can be expensive to adjust; requires efficient, geometry-aware exploration of the Pareto set.
AI.method: Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE) uses predictor steps tangent to the Pareto manifold according to user preferences, followed by corrector steps to obtain new trade-offs. Avoids explicit Hessian computations by employing a Krylov subspace method based on matrix-vector products, computed via automatic differentiation. Demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning examples.
AI.result: Demonstrates the framework's functionality and performance on toy problems and deep learning tasks, indicating efficiency, robustness, and the ability to reflect the decision maker's preferences in the resulting trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: PPE provides an interactive, geometry-aware approach to multi-objective optimization in deep learning, enabling efficient, preference-aligned exploration of Pareto-optimal solutions and scaling to DL applications.

title: Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning
summary: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

AI.tldr: Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation (SeVRA) is a deployment-layer controller that decides when to preserve a frozen solver’s initial answer or invoke active verification, aiming to balance accuracy and compute at serve time.
AI.motivation: Test-time reasoning can be variably valuable: it may fix failures, waste compute, or alter correct answers. A deployment-oriented solution is needed to allocate reasoning effort efficiently, rather than a new verifier.
AI.method: Freeze Qwen3-4B as the solver. Collect intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. Implement SeVRA to decide whether to verify after initial solve. Evaluate on multiple datasets/platforms (mathfive, gsm, CommonsenseQA). Compare selective verification to always verify, measure accuracy, post-generation tokens, and harmful flips; analyze different initial solve lengths (e.g., 8k/21k tokens).
AI.result: On mathfive, SeVRA achieves 76.3% accuracy vs. 75.5% with always-verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8% and harmful flips from 2.2% to 1.0%. An 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0% accuracy with 28% fewer total model tokens. In transfer to GSM, SeVRA verifies only 3.0% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4% to 94.5%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2% relative to always verifying; longer initial solves match accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at roughly five times the realized token cost. The deployment rule is to tune the initial budget first, then apply selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.
AI.conclusion: Selective recovery is a valuable addition for real-world deployment, providing accuracy gains with substantial token-cost savings, though it may not always reach the best possible cost-efficiency frontier across all tasks.

title: World Engine: Towards the Era of Post-Training for Autonomous Driving
summary: Autonomous vehicles must operate safely in the real world, where errors can have severe consequences. Although modern end-to-end driving policies excel in routine scenarios, their reliability is limited by the scarcity of safety-critical ``long-tail'' events in real driving datasets. These rare interactions define the practical safety boundary of the learned policy, yet they are difficult to collect at scale in the real world. Here we show that this fundamental limitation can be addressed by post-training pre-trained driving models on synthesized high-stakes interactions. We introduce World Engine, a generative framework that reconstructs high-fidelity interactive environments from real-world logs and systematically extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. This paradigm enables reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints, circumventing the physical risks inherent in real-world exploration. On a public benchmark built on nuPlan, World Engine substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and yields significantly larger gains than scaling pre-training data alone. Furthermore, when deployed on a production-scale autonomous driving system, the resulting policy reduces simulated collisions and demonstrates measurable improvements in on-road testing, showing that post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions offers a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving. The full codebase suite, including training, is released to the public.

AI.tldr: Post-training driving policies with World Engine by synthesizing high-stakes interactions from real logs to align with safety constraints; outperforms data-scale alone on rare events.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical long-tail events are underrepresented in real driving data, limiting policy reliability. Safe exploration in the real world is risky; synthetic, high-fidelity scenarios offer a scalable alternative.
AI.method: World Engine reconstructs interactive environments from real-world logs and extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. It enables reinforcement-based post-training to fine-tune pre-trained driving models against safety constraints, evaluated on nuPlan, and deployed in production-scale systems.
AI.result: Significant reduction in failures for rare safety-critical scenarios on nuPlan, with larger gains than simply scaling pre-training data. In production-scale deployment, policy reduces simulated collisions and improves on-road testing.
AI.conclusion: Post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions provides a scalable, effective path to safer autonomous driving, complementing data collection with synthetic exploration; codebase released.

title: SpatialSV: Internalizing Interpretable 3D Spatial Awareness in MLLMs via Task-Oriented Visual Supervision
summary: Unlocking the spatial intelligence of multimodal large language model (MLLMs) is crucial for understanding and interacting with the 3D world. Prevailing approaches typically inject spatial priors via external tools, which impose significant inference overhead, or rely on latent feature distillation, which remains uninterpretable and lacks fine-grained geometric constraints. To address these issues, we propose SpatialSV, a framework designed to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness within MLLMs while simultaneously offering inherent interpretability. Deviating from passive feature imitation, SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision, compelling the model to actively lift its 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, including depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds. Crucially, this 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into the model's representations: the resulting 3D reconstructions serve as an intuitive proxy for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of the model's intrinsic spatial knowledge. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SpatialSV in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. Furthermore, the framework exhibits strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, validating its potential to leverage unlabeled visual data for scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: SpatialSV internalizes 3D spatial reasoning in multimodal LLMs by supervising 2D-to-3D lifting (depth, pose, point clouds) for interpretable, scalable and semi-supervised spatial understanding.
AI.motivation: Current approaches rely on external tools (causing inference overhead) or latent distillation (uninterpretable, lacks geometric constraints). A principled internal 3D spatial representation with interpretability is desired.
AI.method: SpatialSV uses task-oriented visual supervision to force 2D visual features to be lifted into explicit 3D representations (depth maps, camera poses, point clouds). The 3D reconstructions provide a transparent diagnostic window into the model's spatial knowledge.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple models and benchmarks shows SpatialSV improves and clarifies the model's spatial intelligence; it generalizes well in semi-supervised settings, enabling learning from unlabeled visual data.
AI.conclusion: SpatialSV successfully internalizes robust 3D spatial awareness in MLLMs with interpretability and good generalization, addressing both performance and diagnostic needs while reducing reliance on external tools.

title: Techniques for Peak Memory Reduction for LoRA Fine-tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices
comment: Hassan Dbouk and Matthias Reisser contributed equally to this work
summary: Fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on an end-user's data offers personalized experiences while keeping data private, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware. Peak memory during fine-tuning often exceeds device limits, especially for models with billions of parameters and long-context training data. This paper introduces a suite of complementary techniques to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing model quality: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking. Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to $26\times$ and $28\times$ reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: Memory-efficient LoRA fine-tuning on end-user data via quantization, checkpointing, softmax subset approximation, and masking; achieves up to ~26–28x peak memory reductions on 3B models (Llama-3.2 3B, Qwen-2.5 3B).
AI.motivation: Enable privacy-preserving, personalized fine-tuning of LLMs on consumer hardware by dramatically reducing peak memory, addressing the memory bottleneck for large models and long-context training.
AI.method: Four complementary techniques: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B shows peak memory reductions of up to 26x and 28x, respectively, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices while preserving model quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed toolkit makes on-device fine-tuning of LoRA-enabled LLMs feasible, delivering substantial memory savings with negligible loss in performance, and broadening accessible, private personalization for end users.

title: Human-on-the-Loop Orchestration for AI-Assisted Legal Discovery
summary: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in electronic discovery (e-discovery), where compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains can constitute legal malpractice. Unlike single-turn retrieval, agentic workflows operating over privileged document corpora exhibit a class of failure we term "trajectory collapse": an early misclassification silently propagates, rendering an entire privilege review invalid. This paper makes three contributions. First, we propose a structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, organized by functional stage. Second, we introduce a four-layer verification architecture -- spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification -- designed to intercept these failures before they compound. Third, we present a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus that demonstrates how mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk relative to fully autonomous baselines. Our results suggest that calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.

AI.tldr: Proposes a taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, a four-layer verification architecture (planning, reasoning, execution, uncertainty quantification) to intercept failures, and a preliminary simulation showing HOTL escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk (up to 61%) while keeping document review burden below 25%.
AI.motivation: Address trajectory-collapse and malpractice risk in autonomous LLM agents performing privilege reviews in e-discovery by preventing early misclassifications from propagating through multi-step reasoning.
AI.method: Develops a functional-stage taxonomy of agentic failures; designs a four-layer verification architecture; conducts a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus with mandatory Human-on-the-Loop escalation thresholds.
AI.result: Simulation indicates calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% compared to fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than a quarter of documents to attorney review.
AI.conclusion: A calibrated, multi-layer verification approach paired with uncertainty quantification can substantially mitigate risks in autonomous e-discovery workflows; results motivate further validation on real-world corpora and deployment considerations.

title: MMD-SLAM: Structure-Enhanced Multi-Meta Gaussian Distribution-Guided Visual SLAM
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has significantly boosted novel view synthesis and high-fidelity scene reconstruction, expanding the potential of 3DGS-based Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) methods. However, most existing systems fail to fully exploit the underlying structural information, which limits rendering quality and often leads to inconsistent maps. To address these limitations, we propose MMD-SLAM, a structure-enhanced Visual SLAM framework that leverages the Atlanta World (AW) assumption to guide a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation for photorealistic mapping. First, we introduce a point-line fusion strategy for pose optimization, where 3D line segments are incorporated to improve tracking robustness and provide additional constraints for mapping. Second, we design a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions, explicitly encoding structural priors from the AW hypothesis. Finally, we propose a Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and incorporates structural cues into global optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that these innovations enable MMD-SLAM to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both tracking accuracy and mapping quality. e.g., our method achieves a 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and a 5.71% improvement in PSNR on Replica, compared with MonoGS.

AI.tldr: MMD-SLAM is a structure-aware SLAM that leverages Atlanta World priors with a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation for photorealistic mapping in conjunction with 3D Gaussian Splatting, via point-line fusion, dominant-direction Gaussians, and a Gaussian evolution strategy to improve tracking and mapping quality, achieving state-of-the-art gains vs MonoGS on ScanNet and Replica.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS-SLAM methods underutilize structural priors, leading to less robust tracking and inconsistent maps. Incorporating structural information (AW) and a probabilistic Gaussian representation can constrain reconstruction and improve both pose and map quality.
AI.method: Introduce: (1) a point-line fusion strategy for pose optimization, incorporating 3D line segments as constraints; (2) a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions inspired by AW to encode structural priors; (3) a Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and integrates structural cues into global optimization.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance in tracking accuracy and mapping quality. Quantitatively, 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and 5.71% PSNR improvement on Replica compared with MonoGS.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware SLAM with AW priors and a Gaussian-based representation significantly enhances photorealistic mapping and robustness of 3DGS-SLAM, demonstrated by substantial gains on benchmark datasets.

title: CARE: Competence-Aware Reward Shaping for Adaptive Reasoning Length in Video-MLLMs
summary: In multimodal video reasoning, reinforcement learning-based methods typically rely on simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence. This mismatch may suppress necessary exploration at early stages, while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding once the model becomes more competent. In this paper, we propose CARE, a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal reasoning. Specifically, CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates, and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift the reward preference from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning. To avoid conflating verbosity with intrinsic task complexity, CARE further normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics, and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples. The proposed mechanism is seamlessly integrated into the GRPO training pipeline and incurs no additional inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency. Moreover, CARE exhibits a characteristic inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, and yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget. We provide the source code for our proposed CARE framework and experiments at https://github.com/1Pansy/Video-CARE.

AI.tldr: CARE introduces a competence-aware reward shaping framework to adaptively optimize reasoning length in multimodal video reasoning, improving accuracy and token efficiency while stabilizing training, with an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length and shorter, informative traces at convergence.
AI.motivation: Fixed, simplistic reasoning-length controls in RL-based multimodal video reasoning hinder exploration early on and cause inefficiency later. Adaptive, competence-aware mechanisms can align reasoning depth with model capability.
AI.method: CARE maintains an EMA-based competence estimate of pass rates, uses it to stage-training that shifts reward preference from long-form exploration to concise reasoning, normalizes reasoning effort using batch-level statistics to separate verbosity from task complexity, and applies a posterior amplifier to boost rewards on unexpectedly strong performances on hard samples. Integrated into the GRPO training pipeline with no inference-time overhead.
AI.result: Across multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks, CARE yields improved reasoning accuracy and training stability, and significantly enhances token efficiency. It exhibits an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training and converges to shorter, more informative reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: CARE effectively allocates reasoning budget adaptively during RL-based multimodal reasoning, providing data-efficient training and robust performance gains without increasing inference costs.

title: Tracking Representation Dynamics in Large Language Models with Persistent Homology
summary: Large language models are commonly aligned through supervised fine-tuning, yet little is known about how their internal representations evolve during this process. We study alignment dynamics using persistent homology by tracking the topology of activation spaces throughout fine-tuning. Across four transformer language models ranging from 1B to 7B parameters and three alignment objectives corresponding to helpful, harmless, and mixed training data, we find that the majority of topological reorganization occurs during the earliest stages of training. A dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization. We further show that different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories, while instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different patterns of evolution. Our results suggest that persistent homology provides a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes that are not apparent from behavioral metrics alone.

AI.tldr: Persistent homology reveals early, transient topological reorganization in LLM alignment fine-tuning; distinct trajectories by objective; instruction-tuned vs pretrained differ; offers representation-level insight beyond behavioral metrics.
AI.motivation: To understand how internal representations evolve during supervised alignment of LLMs using a topology-based lens.
AI.method: Track activation-space topology via persistent homology across four models (1B–7B) and three alignment objectives (helpful, harmless, mixed); perform dense checkpoint analysis to identify peaks and stabilization; compare across objectives and pretraining/instruction-tuning.
AI.result: Most topological change occurs early in training; a transient peak in topology followed by rapid stabilization; alignment objectives yield distinguishable topological trajectories; instruction-tuned and pretrained models show qualitatively different evolution patterns.
AI.conclusion: Persistent homology provides a complementary view of alignment dynamics, capturing representation-level changes not evident in behavioral metrics.

authors: V V N K Someswara Rao Koduri
title: TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE GLOBECOM 2026
summary: Key Performance Measurement (KPM) forecasting is essential for proactive network management of 5G and next-generation telecom networks. However, existing machine learning (ML) approaches face significant limitations in scalability and explainability, restricting their effectiveness in real-world deployments. We propose TelcoAgent, a foundation model-based framework that enables accurate, scalable, and explainable forecasting of multiple KPMs across diverse network cells without the need for site-specific training. Specifically, the framework comprises three key components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline that constructs a 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) knowledge graph directly from specification documents, (ii) a scalable, time-series foundation model (TSFM)-based prediction pipeline to deliver accurate, zero-shot forecasting, and finally (iii) a reasoning and explanation pipeline that provides actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics. Evaluated using a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G KPM dataset from a U.S.-based network operator, TelcoAgent demonstrates high forecasting accuracy for all 7 considered KPMs per cell across 200 cells, while delivering explainable insights and actionable instructions to address network degradations.

AI.tldr: TelcoAgent introduces a foundation-model–based framework for scalable, explainable KPM forecasting in 5G/next-gen networks via a 3-agent knowledge-graph pipeline, TSFM zero-shot forecasting, and a reasoning/explanation module, validated on real 3-month city-scale data across 200 cells for 7 KPIs.
AI.motivation: To overcome scalability and explainability limitations of existing ML-based KPM forecasting methods and enable proactive, site-agnostic management of 5G networks by leveraging 3GPP specifications and foundation models.
AI.method: An automated three-agent pipeline constructs a 3GPP knowledge graph from specification documents; a scalable time-series foundation model (TSFM) performs zero-shot forecasting; a reasoning and explanation pipeline delivers actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics.
AI.result: Evaluation on a real-world 3-month city-scale 5G KPM dataset (200 cells, 7 KPIs per cell) shows high forecasting accuracy across all KPIs, with the added benefit of explainable insights and actionable instructions to mitigate network degradations.
AI.conclusion: TelcoAgent demonstrates accurate, scalable, and explainable KPM forecasting without site-specific training, indicating practical viability for deployment in 5G and future networks.

title: One-to-Two Acting: A Novel Framework for Single-arm Agent Action Expansion to Dual Arms
summary: Dual-arm manipulation can improve throughput via parallel execution, but collecting bimanual demonstrations for training is costly and difficult. We present ExS2D, a hierarchical action expansion framework that enables dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision. ExS2D first generates structured subtasks from textual instructions while explicitly capturing temporal precedence. It then grounds each subtask into executable actions through subtask-guided action mapping in observation. Finally, precedence-aware action allocation and synchronized planning are performed by a multimodal large language model driven coordinator to select collision-free dual-arm executions. Simulation experiments demonstrate that ExS2D reduces the average execution steps by 54.4% while maintaining a comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments on four tasks further demonstrate the reliability of ExS2D for dual-arm execution under few-shot single-arm samples, while using zero bimanual demonstrations.

AI.tldr: ExS2D enables dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision via hierarchical action expansion, dramatically reducing steps while preserving success rates, demonstrated in simulation and real robots.
AI.motivation: Dual-arm manipulation increases throughput but collecting bimanual demonstrations is costly; a single-arm supervised approach is desirable for scalable dual-arm control.
AI.method: Extract structured subtasks from textual instructions with explicit temporal precedence, ground subtasks into executable actions via subtask-guided action mapping in observation, and use a multimodal LLM-driven coordinator for precedence-aware action allocation and synchronized planning to ensure collision-free dual-arm execution.
AI.result: Simulation shows a 54.4% reduction in average execution steps with comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments on four tasks show reliable dual-arm execution under few-shot single-arm samples and zero bimanual demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: ExS2D enables efficient and scalable dual-arm manipulation using single-arm supervision, achieving faster execution without sacrificing reliability, validated in both simulation and real robots.

title: Spatial-Aware Reduction Framework: Towards Efficient and Faithful Visual State Space Models
summary: Mamba demonstrates strong efficiency in modeling long visual sequences. However, when token reduction is applied to structurally enhanced Mamba variants, these models exhibit a severe performance collapse. We attribute this degradation to the spatially agnostic nature of existing reduction methods, which violate the two-dimensional structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism. In this work, we propose STORM, a spatial-aware token reduction framework designed to maintain structural integrity throughout the compression process. STORM reformulates reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain both grid topology and neighborhood coherence. As a plug-and-play module, STORM equips existing reduction pipelines with explicit spatial awareness without any training. Empirical results demonstrate that STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings. Notably, STORM delivers a substantial accuracy recovery on VMamba, outperforming prior methods by up to 63.3\% in top-1 accuracy. Meanwhile, STORM incurs only a 1.0\% accuracy drop on PlainMamba, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

AI.tldr: STORM is a plug-and-play, training-free, spatial-aware token reduction framework that preserves 2D structural integrity during compression, achieving state-of-the-art pruning accuracy for Mamba backbones and notable improvements on VMamba with minimal accuracy loss on PlainMamba.
AI.motivation: Existing token reduction methods are spatially agnostic and disrupt the 2D grid structure required by selective scanning in Mamba variants, causing severe performance degradation when pruning.
AI.method: Reformulate reduction as a structured operation on spatial units with localized constraints to preserve grid topology and neighborhood coherence. STORM is plug-and-play and requires no training.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy in training-free pruning across diverse vision Mamba backbones. On VMamba, accuracy gains up to 63.3% in top-1; on PlainMamba, only 1.0% accuracy drop, comparable to ViT.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-aware token reduction effectively preserves structural integrity during compression, enabling robust, training-free pruning and broad applicability to Mamba variants, with high gains on VMamba and minimal losses on PlainMamba.

title: Predicting Mergeability of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Updates
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes it cheap to train many domain- and task-specific language model adapters, but whether two adapters can be merged is usually discovered only after both have been fully trained and evaluated. This late feedback is costly: adapters that are strong in isolation can interfere destructively once their updates are combined. We ask whether this outcome can be anticipated. We formalize adapter mergeability as the degree to which an adapter preserves its single-task utility after merging, and show that it can be forecast from signals measured in the first few percent of training -- chiefly how the low-rank updates and their gradients align across tasks and how much they disturb shared representations. We package these signals into MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that estimates pairwise and set-level retention and turns the estimate into a concrete decision: merge directly, reweight, prune, or route. On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark spanning math, code, science, instruction following, and safety, MergeProbe attains the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing. This turns LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem.

AI.tldr: Forecast adapter mergeability early in LoRA training using MergeProbe; predicts retention after merge from initial training signals to guide merging, reweighting, pruning, or routing.
AI.motivation: Reduce costly post-hoc evaluation when merging adapters by predicting their compatibility before full training/evaluation.
AI.method: Extract early-training signals: alignment of low-rank updates and their gradients across tasks and their disturbance to shared representations; integrate into MergeProbe to estimate pairwise and set-level retention and generate actionable decisions.
AI.result: On MERGE-PEFT, MergeProbe achieves best average and worst-case retention among strong baselines that account for interference, with lower deployment overhead than full routing.
AI.conclusion: Early, lightweight signals can predict adapter mergeability, turning LoRA merging from a post-hoc step into an anticipatory measurement problem and enabling proactive merging strategies.

title: A Systematic Evaluation of Black-Box Uncertainty Estimation Methods for Large Language Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities across a wide range of tasks, their outputs often remain unreliable and may contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation (UE) essential for building trustworthy LLMs. In practice, many mainstream LLMs are only accessible through restricted APIs, where internal signals such as logits and hidden states are unavailable, making black-box UE especially important. However, existing work on black-box UE for LLMs remains fragmented in methodology and lacks a unified empirical comparison. To address this gap, we present a systematic review of black-box UE methods and organize them into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. We further build a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings. Our results show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings. Nevertheless, methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective, and hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions. By releasing the benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework, we aim to facilitate reproducible comparisons and support future research, while our empirical findings provide practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods for LLMs.

AI.tldr: A systematic review and benchmark of black-box uncertainty estimation (UE) for LLMs, classifying methods into five categories, with a unified evaluation framework and benchmark across multiple models and datasets.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit hallucinations/unreliability; black-box UE is needed when API signals (logits/hidden states) are unavailable; fragmented methods lack unified comparison.
AI.method: Survey organizing black-box UE methods into verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid. Develop unified evaluation framework. Benchmark 24 methods across 4 models and 4 datasets.
AI.result: No single method dominates across all settings. Candidate-space reasoning and comparisons are generally effective; hybrid methods performing well across conditions. The benchmark and framework enable reproducible comparisons.
AI.conclusion: The work provides practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods and facilitates reproducible research by releasing benchmark data and framework.

title: Co-policy: Responsive Human-Robot Co-Creation for Musical Performances
summary: Art has long stood as a pivotal expression of human creativity. Embodied artificial intelligence offers a route for generative models to participate in that creativity through physical action rather than disembodied digital content. In robotic music co-creation, it is challenging to connect semantic musical understanding with real-time and physically executable performance.
