We present Co-policy, a framework for human-robot musical co-creation that separates semantic intent grounding, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution. To ground musical semantics, Co-policy uses pre-inference semantic anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to transform speech, live musical seeds, and visual observations into structured co-creation plans. To support low-latency execution, Co-policy introduces a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), implemented as a conditional mixture-density policy that maps target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass. Unlike robotic playback systems that merely reproduce user-specified notes, Co-policy generates complementary musical responses under both musical and physical constraints. Real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency over diffusion-policy and ablated baselines, supporting physically grounded action generation as a key requirement for embodied human-AI co-creation.

AI.tldr: Co-policy enables semantically grounded, low-latency, physically executable human-robot musical co-creation using semantic anchors, a fine-tuned planner (F-Qwen), and a Gaussian-Mixture visuomotor policy to map notes and visual context to robot actions in one forward pass, outperforming diffusion-policy baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge semantic musical understanding with real-time, physically executable robot actions to enable embodied co-creation between humans and AI in musical contexts.
AI.method: A three-part framework: (1) semantic grounding via pre-inference semantic anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to convert speech, live seeds, and visual observations into structured co-creation plans; (2) constrained musical variation; (3) Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), a conditional mixture-density policy that maps target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass, enabling low-latency execution.
AI.result: Real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency compared with diffusion-policy and ablated baselines, validating physically grounded action generation as essential for embodied co-creation.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded action generation is a key requirement for embodied human-AI musical co-creation, and Co-policy demonstrates that integrating semantic grounding with a fast, constrained visuomotor policy yields more aligned, accurate, and responsive robotic musical interaction.

title: Speeding up the annotation process in semantic segmentation industrial applications
summary: Current machine learning models commonly require large and well-annotated datasets. However, the annotation process often becomes a bottleneck, with increased complexity leading to higher chances of human errors. Within this context, our goal in this paper is to leverage unsupervised algorithms to improve data annotation efficiency for complex semantic segmentation problems in industrial materials science. Previous research has quantified labeling time and others explored unsupervised methods. However, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify how much unsupervised algorithms accelerate the labeling process. We aim to validate the extent to which this laborious process can be accelerated, focusing on semantic segmentation tasks that involve annotating each pixel of high-resolution images, such as the microstructure characterization challenge in materials science. Specifically, we demonstrate that by using unsupervised computer vision algorithms, the time required for the labeling process can be reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving an approximate reduction of 78\%. The dataset we work with includes large images of dimensions 1280x959 and 960x703, which further increases the complexity of the annotation task. Despite these challenges, we create and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, available under MIT License with permanent DOI, contributing a fully annotated, high-resolution dataset to the field. Additionally, this is the first work to compare the labeling time from scratch (a common approach in previous studies) to the labeling time when using these unsupervised algorithms as a pre-annotation step. Furthermore, we provide a Deep Learning model trained on this dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting, serving as an initial benchmark for this public dataset.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised pre-annotation substantially speeds semantic segmentation labeling (≈78% time reduction) for high-res steel microstructure images; presents the largest public dataset and a baseline DL model.
AI.motivation: Reduce annotation bottlenecks in high-resolution, pixel-level semantic segmentation for industrial materials by leveraging unsupervised algorithms to provide pre-labels that human annotators refine.
AI.method: Generate pre-annotations with unsupervised computer vision methods, compare labeling time against from-scratch labeling, assemble and share a large high-resolution steel microstructure dataset (MIT License, permanent DOI), train and validate a DL model using expert input, and deploy in an industrial setting.
AI.result: Labeling time reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours (~78% reduction). Dataset sizes include images of 1280x959 and 960x703. Claims the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date. Provides a publicly available dataset and a baseline DL model validated by field experts, with industrial deployment.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised pre-annotation can markedly accelerate pixel-wise semantic labeling in materials science; this work delivers a new benchmark dataset and initial model, enabling further studies on accuracy-augmentation trade-offs and generalization to other materials and imaging modalities.

title: Displacement Is Not Direction: Evaluating Fidelity Metrics for Quantized LLM Deployment
summary: Fidelity metrics, such as per-token KL divergence (KLD) against a high-precision reference, are often used in practice as low-cost proxies for benchmark quality. We test this practice on a 28-quant cohort of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and a 41-quant cohort of Devstral-Small-2-24B, evaluated across a suite of downstream benchmarks. We find that KLD is strongly correlated with benchmark score over the full cohort ($ρ=-0.72$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.86$ on Devstral, both with $p<0.001$). However, this relationship collapses to non-significance in the near-baseline silent zone ($ρ=+0.00$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.24$, $p=0.36$, on Devstral). This collapse persists across 14 measurement variants, including different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths. At the per-prompt level, KLD has only weak failure-prediction power on code, with failed-vs-passed geometric-mean ratios in $[1.08,1.22]$ across five models on LiveCodeBench, and fails as a cross-model router, achieving only $42.3\%-49.4\%$ accuracy on disagreement prompts. We trace the collapse to a structural decomposition: KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference, with silent-zone composite $ρ=+0.94$ ($p<0.001$) on Qwen and $+0.55$ ($p=0.03$) on Devstral, while its relationship to the direction of those disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

AI.tldr: KLD correlates with benchmark quality across model cohorts but collapses in silent-zone; primarily captures disagreement volume rather than direction; limited per-prompt predictive power; cross-model disagreement routing modest.
AI.motivation: Assess the reliability of per-token KL divergence as a low-cost fidelity proxy for benchmark quality across model cohorts and measurement variants.
AI.method: Evaluate on two cohorts (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B with 28 quant, Devstral-Small-2-24B with 41 quant) across downstream benchmarks; compute correlations between KLD and benchmark scores; test 14 measurement variants; per-prompt analyses; decompose KLD into volume vs direction of disagreements.
AI.result: Strong full-cohort correlations (ρ ~ -0.72 to -0.86, p<0.001) but non-significant in near-baseline silent zone (ρ ~ 0 or -0.24, p>0.05); collapse persists across 14 variants; weak per-prompt failure-prediction on code; cross-model disagreement prompts accuracy ~42.3-49.4%; KLD mainly captures volume of disagreement; direction weak and task-conditional; silent-zone shows high positive ρ (0.94 on Qwen, 0.55 on Devstral).
AI.conclusion: KLD is an unreliable universal fidelity proxy; while it correlates with benchmark scores in some ranges, its sensitivity is dominated by disagreement volume; near-silent zones undermine predictive value; more robust metrics required (directional agreement, calibration, task-conditional signals).

title: MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in autonomous information gathering and synthesis, yet their training remains constrained by the static nature of simulated environments, the limits of fact-retrieval-only task designs, and the inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose MetaResearcher, a novel framework that scales deep research agent training across four synergistic dimensions. First, we introduce an Evolving Virtual World that injects temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation into the training environment, forcing agents to develop source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution skills. Second, we design Discovery-Oriented Tasks -- including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution -- that transcend simple fact retrieval and push agents toward genuine research behaviors. Third, we propose a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within the GRPO framework that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity, directly addressing the repetitive action loop problem observed in prior work. Fourth, we introduce a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm architecture comprising specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models that learn collaborative research strategies through coordinated reinforcement learning. Built upon the LiteResearcher infrastructure, MetaResearcher requires zero marginal API cost for training while targeting substantial improvements in both benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions. We present the complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation.

AI.tldr: MetaResearcher: a four-dimensional framework to train deep research agents using an evolving virtual world, discovery-oriented tasks, self-reflective meta-reward, and a heterogeneous multi-agent swarm, built on LiteResearcher to improve benchmarks and epistemic robustness with zero marginal API cost.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in current deep research agents: static simulated environments, fact-retrieval-only tasks, and inefficient outcome-based RL, by introducing dynamics, genuine research behaviors, reflective reward shaping, and collaborative multi-agent strategies to enhance robustness and performance.
AI.method: Four components: (1) Evolving Virtual World with temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation to cultivate credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution; (2) Discovery-Oriented Tasks (hypothesis generation, contradiction resolution) beyond retrieval; (3) Self-Reflective Meta-Reward within the GRPO framework jointly optimizing answer correctness, search-path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool-call diversity; (4) Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm (Scout/Filter/Synthesizer) trained via coordinated reinforcement learning; built on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost; describes complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experiments.
AI.result: Framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation are presented, with benchmark targets GAIA and Xbench-DS and an emphasis on epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: MetaResearcher proposes an integrated, scalable approach to train autonomous research agents that combine evolving environments, sophisticated reasoning tasks, reflective reward shaping, and cooperative multi-agent collaboration to improve performance and resilience, while maintaining zero marginal API cost.

title: Deep-Unfolded Coordination
comment: The second and third authors contributed equally (equal second authorship). 35 pages (10 pages main text), 17 figures, 3 tables
summary: Distributed optimization is a highly scalable and structurally transparent technique to solve multi-agent robotics problems; however, such methods often suffer from the need for highly-specialized, problem-specific hyperparameter tunings. In this work, we propose Deep Coordinator, a deep-unfolding framework that learns to dynamically adjust the hyperparameters of ADMM-DDP, a popular distributed solver for robotics tasks, at solve-time in response to optimizer performance. Our architecture consists of unrolling a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network with learnable functions between layers mapping the optimizer state to the next hyperparameters. To the best of our knowledge, Deep Coordinator is the first deep-unfolding framework to adapt the penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; we show that the mainstream supervised approach can yield degenerate solutions when training such models, and propose an unsupervised learning scheme. On simulations with fleets of cars and quadrotors, Deep Coordinator produces trajectories of comparable quality 6.18-9.44x faster than conventional solvers. Furthermore, Deep Coordinator retains its performance benefits when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than trained on.

AI.tldr: A deep-unfolding framework (Deep Coordinator) learns to adapt ADMM-DDP penalty parameters at solve-time, improving speed for distributed robotics optimization; uses unsupervised training to avoid degenerate supervised solutions; achieves 6.18-9.44x speedups and scales to larger systems.
AI.motivation: Hyperparameter tuning is critical and problem-specific in distributed optimization for multi-agent robotics; current solvers require manual tuning and may not generalize; propose adaptive, learned penalty scheduling.
AI.method: Unroll a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network; use learnable functions between layers to map the current optimizer state to the next penalty parameters; propose unsupervised learning objective; first to adapt penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time.
AI.result: Simulation experiments with fleets of cars and quadrotors show trajectories of comparable quality and 6.18-9.44x faster solve times; the method generalizes to systems up to 8x larger than training data.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of automatic, data-driven hyperparameter adaptation in non-convex distributed optimization; unsupervised training avoids degenerate solutions; potential for scalable speedups in multi-agent robotics.

title: Triangular Consistency as a Universal Constraint for Learning Optical Flow
summary: We propose triangular consistency as a first-principled constraint for optical flow, which is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and applies to both image-pair and multi-frame settings. This simple but powerful constraint is to compose two flows to induce a third flow and enforce consistency among the three. The composed flows may arise from (i) image pairs, yielding cycle consistency; (ii) multiple video frames, producing longer-range motion through temporal chaining; or (iii) image pairs combined with controlled synthetic transformations, which becomes data augmentation. This triangular consistency introduces negligible computational overhead and requires no additional annotations. Since it is derived directly from the geometry of optical flow, it does not rely on model-specific assumptions and serves as a ``universal'' plug-and-play component for optical flow training. Experiments show consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.

AI.tldr: Triangular consistency is a geometry-based, model-agnostic constraint for training optical flow by enforcing consistency among composed flows across image pairs, multiple frames, and synthetic augmentations, improving supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing optical-flow training often relies on task- or model-specific losses; a universal geometric constraint can improve flow estimates across settings without extra annotations.
AI.method: Formally compose flows to generate a third flow and enforce consistency among the three: 1) image-pair cycles for temporal cycles, 2) multi-frame chaining for longer-range motion, 3) image-pair flows augmented with synthetic transformations for data augmentation. The constraint has negligible compute overhead and is independent of architecture or supervision type.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent improvements across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings, indicating broad applicability.
AI.conclusion: Triangular consistency provides a simple, universal plug-and-play constraint for optical flow training that yields performance gains without changing models or data requirements.

title: Understanding Key Features of Time Series Foundation Models from Epidemic Forecasting
summary: Seasonal influenza infects millions of people and causes substantial morbidity and mortality in the United States each year, making accurate short-term forecasting a core public-health need. Reliable forecasts of epidemic time series can inform vaccination timing, hospital staffing, and resource allocation, yet the comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data remains insufficiently characterized. We address this gap through a systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under both temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. We compare classical neural network architectures, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Across tasks, we demonstrate that a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance, indicating that heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Our results further show that numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts, while pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, particularly when the pretraining domain is mechanistically aligned with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Finally, we examine hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and a pretraining source. Hospitalization signals provide complementary improvements in selected settings and clarify when additional surveillance streams enhance the robustness of multi-horizon forecasting. These findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness.

AI.tldr: Ensembling pretrained forecasters via a mixture-of-experts (MoE) yields the strongest influenza-forecasting performance across 1–4 week horizons, with numerical transformer models providing robust forecasts and pretraining offering larger gains at longer horizons when domain-aligned; LLM-based time-series methods underperform; hospitalization data can augment predictions under certain conditions and informs when extra surveillance streams improve robustness.
AI.motivation: To systematically characterize how modern forecasting architectures behave on infectious-disease surveillance data and to identify effective pretraining strategies, model choices, and auxiliary signals for short- to mid-term influenza forecasting.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness (ILI) surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1–4 weeks ahead. Comparisons include classical neural networks, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time-series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Hospitalization signals are used as auxiliary covariates and as pretraining sources, and results are analyzed across horizons and settings.
AI.result: Mixture-of-experts ensemble combining multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance. Numerical transformer-based models deliver reliable forecasts. Pretraining confers the largest gains at longer horizons, especially when the pretraining domain aligns with influenza dynamics. LLM-based time-series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this context. Hospitalization information provides complementary improvements in selected settings and helps clarify when additional surveillance streams bolster multi-horizon forecasting.
AI.conclusion: For influenza preparedness, prioritize heterogeneous pretrained representations via MoE ensembles, leverage numerical transformers with domain-aligned pretraining for longer horizons, and use auxiliary signals like hospitalization data judiciously to boost robustness. LLM-based time-series approaches may not yet surpass traditional numerical forecasters in this domain; future work could optimize pretraining domains and explore additional auxiliary data.

title: Multi-Agent Transactive Memory
summary: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution. We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

AI.tldr: MATM enables population-level memory and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories to boost performance with no coordination.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable reuse of agent-generated procedural knowledge across heterogeneous agent populations by sharing trajectories, avoiding repeated rediscovery.
AI.method: Propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM): producers contribute long trajectories to a shared repository; consumers retrieve them to guide task execution. Evaluated in interactive environments ALFWorld and WebArena without coordination or joint training.
AI.result: Retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps.
AI.conclusion: MATM serves as a practical design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems, enabling reusable procedural knowledge across agents.

title: SWAP: Symmetric Equivariant World-Model for Agile Robot Parkour
summary: While latent world models enable the proactive predictions required for extreme parkour, their purely data-driven nature forces them to redundantly encode left-right symmetric interactions as independent patterns. This inflates the learning burden and hinders the capture of geometric regularities, restricting the latent space's efficiency for downstream policies. To address this, we propose SWAP, an end-to-end equivariant symmetric world model. This framework embeds symmetry directly into both the world model and the actor-critic networks. In real-world tests, the robot leaps across a 2.13 m gap and climbs a 1.63 m platform, breaking records for quadruped parkour. Furthermore, the framework exhibits robust geometric generalization to unseen mirrored terrains and exceptional zero-shot transferability across diverse outdoor environments. These results demonstrate that symmetry equivariance is an effective structural prior for pushing the physical boundaries of learned legged locomotion.

AI.tldr: SWAP is an end-to-end equivariant symmetric world model that embeds symmetry into both the world model and actor-critic networks to improve sample efficiency, geometric generalization, and zero-shot transfer in legged locomotion; demonstrated by state-of-the-art quadruped parkour performances.
AI.motivation: To overcome redundancy and inefficiency in purely data-driven world models caused by left-right symmetric interactions, by explicitly encoding geometric symmetries to improve latent representations and downstream policy performance.
AI.method: Propose SWAP, an end-to-end trainable framework that integrates symmetry (equivariance) into the world model and policy networks (actor-critic). Likely employs group-equivariant architectures to exploit left-right and other geometric symmetries, enabling shared representations across symmetric configurations.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show a 2.13 m gap jump and a 1.63 m platform climb (record-setting for quadruped parkour); strong generalization to unseen mirrored terrains; robust zero-shot transfer across diverse outdoor environments.
AI.conclusion: Embedding symmetry equivariance provides a powerful structural prior that improves learning efficiency, generalization, and transfer in learned legged locomotion.

title: DiffMath: Symbol- and Graph-Aware Latent Diffusion Transformer for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation
summary: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to the complex two-dimensional layouts and long-range structural dependencies of mathematical expressions. Existing methods typically rely on explicit spatial supervision, such as symbol-level bounding boxes, which incurs high annotation costs and limits scalability. In this work, we propose DiffMath, a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework that leverages the hierarchical structure inherent in LaTeX as a structural prior, eliminating the need for positional supervision. First, we design a Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST), a generation-oriented representation that distills MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D], where each token directly encodes a symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth. Second, we introduce MathVAE, which learns structure-preserving latent representations through symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization, ensuring that the latent space captures both character semantics and spatial topology. Third, MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, further guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to improve structural coherence. Experiments show that DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance over existing methods, and improves the accuracy of downstream OCR models through synthetic data augmentation.

AI.tldr: DiffMath introduces a diffusion-based framework for handwritten mathematical expression generation that uses a RelAST triplet representation, a structure-preserving MathVAE latent space, and adaptive layer normalization for symbol-count control, eliminating the need for explicit positional supervision.
AI.motivation: Current HMEG methods rely on costly symbol-level bounding boxes and spatial annotations, which hinder scalability and generalization to complex 2D layouts. A structural prior that leverages LaTeX hierarchy can reduce annotation burden while preserving spatial topology.
AI.method: Three components: (1) RelAST, a compact [S, R, D] triplet representation distilling MathML into generation-oriented tokens encoding symbol identity, spatial relation, and nesting depth; (2) MathVAE, a structure-preserving latent space learned with symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization to capture character semantics and spatial topology; (3) DiffMath, conditional denoising in the structured latent space guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to enforce structural coherence.
AI.result: Empirical results show DiffMath generates structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance against existing methods, and improves OCR accuracy when used for synthetic data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: DiffMath demonstrates that a diffusion-based generation framework with a hierarchy-aware latent space and explicit structure priors can effectively synthesize handwritten math without explicit positional supervision and can enhance downstream OCR systems.

title: Advances in Scientific Machine Learning for Coupled Fluid Flow and Transport
summary: This chapter reviews recent advances in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) for modeling coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes and scalar transport equations. Such systems, found in applications like turbidity currents and thermal convection, feature strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior that make high-fidelity simulations computationally expensive. To address this, the chapter surveys state-of-the-art SciML methods for building efficient surrogate models, including linear reduced-order techniques based on Singular Value Decomposition (such as Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and $β$-Variational Autoencoders ($β$-VAEs). It first covers the authors' work combining these models with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/C) and scientific floating-point data compression. It then presents two new contributions: surrogate modeling of turbidity currents via PINNs, and the extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows using $β$-VAEs. Governing equations and representative benchmarks, including lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection, illustrate these methodologies. The chapter is intentionally long, covering both the mathematical and physical foundations of coupled fluid flow and the computational aspects of state-of-the-art modeling. Overall, it demonstrates how SciML enables fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within the specific data regimes and modeling assumptions considered, while substantially reducing computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Broader capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions whose feasibility depends strongly on the problem at hand.

AI.tldr: SciML surrogates for coupled incompressible Navier–Stokes and scalar transport enable fast, accurate approximations; combines linear (e.g., DMD) and nonlinear (PINNs, β-VAEs) models with HPC strategies; introduces PINN-based turbidity-current surrogates and β-VAE–driven mode disentanglement in thermal flows; validated on benchmarks like lock-exchange and Rayleigh–Bénard convection; shows significant cost reductions but notes ongoing challenges for real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity simulations of coupled fluid flow and scalar transport are computationally expensive due to strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale dynamics. There is a need for efficient, accurate surrogate models (SciML) to enable faster predictions, real-time capabilities, and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Survey of state-of-the-art SciML techniques for coupled Navier–Stokes and scalar transport, including linear ROMs (e.g., SVD, DMD) and nonlinear neural networks (PINNs, β-VAEs), integrated with HPC strategies like Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening and data compression. Presents two new contributions: (i) surrogate modeling of turbidity currents using PINNs, (ii) extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows via β-VAEs. Governing equations: incompressible Navier–Stokes with scalar transport. Benchmarks: lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh–Bénard convection.
AI.result: Demonstrates that SciML enables fast, accurate surrogate models that significantly reduce computational cost compared to full-order simulations within the considered data regimes. Real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions, with feasibility problem-dependent.
AI.conclusion: SciML is a promising framework for fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems, leveraging linear and nonlinear models alongside HPC techniques. While effective within chosen data regimes, extending capabilities to broader regimes and robust real-time UQ continues to be an active area of research.

title: eCNNTO: A Highly Generalizable ConvNet for Accelerating Topology Optimization
summary: This work proposes an element-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization (TO), termed eCNNTO. TO generally undergoes a large number of iterations, where finite element analysis is performed in every iteration, leading to the efficiency bottleneck especially when dense meshes are used to achieve high-resolution designs. To address this limitation, eCNNTO is proposed to build upon Kallioras et al. (2020), where a Deep Belief Network (DBN) was trained for every element to predict its near-optimal density from its early history, thereby skipping the great majority of iterations and significantly accelerating the TO procedure. However, the method lacks spatial correlations among neighboring elements and may lead to disconnected features in the final structure. The proposed method employs CNN with residual connections to address this issue. On top of it, a novel training strategy is introduced to further enhance the optimization efficiency, where the training dataset consists of the final stage density histories rather than early ones. This change can also help reduce the required training data size. eCNNTO requires only a small dataset to train and yet it can be generalized to problems with largely different boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, as well as non-design domains. In the end, the generalization capabilities and efficiency of eCNNTO are demonstrated through a variety of examples in two and three dimensions, achieving up to 90% and 97% reduction of iterations, respectively.

AI.tldr: An element-based CNN (eCNNTO) accelerates density-based topology optimization by incorporating a residual CNN trained on final-stage density histories to capture spatial correlations, achieving large iteration reductions and robust generalization across problems with limited training data.
AI.motivation: Topology optimization is computationally intensive due to numerous iterations and finite element analyses; previous work (Kallioras et al., 2020) used per-element DBNs to predict near-optimal density from early history but neglected spatial correlations, risking disconnected features.
AI.method: Employs a CNN with residual connections to model spatial correlations among neighboring elements; training strategy uses final-stage density histories; small training dataset; claims generalization across boundary conditions, loading cases, design-domain geometries, mesh resolutions, and non-design domains.
AI.result: 2D and 3D tests show significant acceleration, with up to 90% and 97% reduction in iterations, respectively; method generalizes to problems with varied boundary conditions, loads, and geometries while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: eCNNTO effectively accelerates density-based topology optimization, addressing connectivity issues, reducing data requirements, and generalizing across a broad range of problem settings.

title: Motor Angular Speed Preintegration for Multirotor UAV State Estimation
summary: A precise state estimate is crucial for a tight feedback control that enables agile and near-obstacle flights of UAVs. The state-of-the-art methods fuse slow pose measurements with high-frequency inertial measurements to obtain a precise state estimate. However, the inertial measurements from the IMU onboard the UAV are degraded by vibrations from spinning propellers and the precision of the estimated state suffers. We propose a novel approach based on the preintegration of accelerations obtained from motor speeds. We show that the accelerations obtained in this manner can be used for state propagation on their own to achieve better precision without including the IMU. Further, we propose a factor composed of the preintegrated motor speeds that can be directly employed in factor graph optimization frameworks. We combine our factor with LiDAR measurements into the proposed Motor Angular Speed LiDAR Odometry (MAS-LO) algorithm for precise state estimation, which we open-source. Lastly, we evaluate the estimation precision against a state-of-the-art inertial algorithm LIO-SAM to show 28% improvement in position and 65% in velocity estimation accuracy, 14% lower measurement lag, and high robustness to wrong parameter values.

AI.tldr: MAS-LO introduces a motor-speed preintegration approach to UAV state estimation, replacing IMU data with preintegrated accelerations from motor speeds and fusing with LiDAR in a factor-graph framework, achieving notable accuracy and robustness gains over LIO-SAM.
AI.motivation: IMU-based state estimation is compromised by vibrations from spinning propellers, leading to degraded precision. There is a need for a high-frequency, robust pose propagation method that reduces reliance on noisy IMU data while leveraging motor dynamics and LiDAR measurements.
AI.method: Preintegrate accelerations derived from motor speeds to propagate state without the IMU; formulate a motor-speed preintegration factor for factor-graph optimization; fuse with LiDAR measurements within an MAS-LO (Motor Angular Speed LiDAR Odometry) framework and open-source the implementation.
AI.result: Compared to LIO-SAM, MAS-LO achieves 28% better position accuracy, 65% better velocity accuracy, 14% lower measurement lag, and exhibits high robustness to wrong parameter values.
AI.conclusion: Preintegrated motor-speed accelerations in a factor-graph fusion with LiDAR provide significant accuracy and latency improvements for UAV state estimation, offering a viable alternative to IMU-centric methods and expanding open-source tools in this domain.

title: Timage: A Generative Text-in-Image Paradigm for Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lose track of the right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning, because a textual query rarely carries any explicit geometric anchor into the pixel domain. Prevailing remedies either rewire the model's weights or pad the prompt with verbose instructions, yet neither reliably pins the language to the correct visual coordinates without eroding the backbone's general competence. We introduce Timage, a paradigm that recasts multimodal understanding as an alignment problem solved at the input: the query is drawn, as a typeset overlay, onto the image itself. The placement and appearance of this overlay are produced by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB), an entropic optimal-transport sampler that factorizes layout synthesis into two coupled stochastic stages. The first stage, Region Search, transports noise toward query-aligned image zones while obeying a hard occlusion barrier that protects salient foreground content; the second stage, Appearance Shaping, sizes the glyphs through an ``ink-budget'' regularizer so that the rendered text stays legible and visually balanced. The resulting overlay behaves as an explicit attention beacon that channels the model's focus along spatial semantics. On the VMCBench suite, Timage paired with a modest 7B backbone clearly overtakes far larger proprietary systems as well as parameter-tuned baselines. The study positions deliberate input reconstruction as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever for strengthening multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Timage uses a constrained Schrödinger Bridge to render the query as a foreground overlay on the image, converting spatial grounding into an input-alignment problem and improving multimodal reasoning with a 7B backbone.
AI.motivation: MLLMs often lose track of right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning because text lacks explicit pixel anchors. Prior fixes either rewire models or add verbose prompts, which harms general competence.
AI.method: Propose an input-alignment paradigm where the query is drawn as an overlaid, typeset text on the image. A constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB) performs two stages: (1) Region Search transports noise to query-aligned image regions while enforcing a hard occlusion barrier to protect salient foreground; (2) Appearance Shaping adjusts glyph sizes via an ink-budget regularizer to keep text legible and visually balanced. The overlay acts as an explicit attention beacon guiding spatial semantics. Evaluation on VMCBench with a 7B backbone.
AI.result: Timage with a modest 7B backbone outperforms larger proprietary systems and parameter-tuned baselines on VMCBench.
AI.conclusion: Recasting multimodal understanding as an input reconstruction/alignment problem is architecture-neutral and a powerful lever to strengthen multimodal reasoning; explicit, well-placed input overlays can anchor model attention to spatial structure.

title: On the QUEST for Uncertainty Quantification via Highest Density Regions
comment: 27 pages, of which 10 are main text. Contains 7 figures, 4 tables, 1 algorithm in total
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for reliable decision-making in safety-critical applications in probabilistic machine learning. For regression problems, dominant scalar UQ approaches - notably, those based on proper scoring rules - measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk. This can lead to counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation. We propose an alternative framework, in which uncertainty is characterised by the volume of the most probable subset of a distribution's support. QUEST (Quantifying Uncertainty via highest dEnSiTy regions) is a novel approach to UQ based on the concentration of Lebesgue measure at a distribution's peak(s), evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter $α$. We establish connections between our measures and classical statistics from information theory and economics. We show that, unlike popular alternatives based on proper scoring rules, QUEST measures of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty satisfy a set of axioms adapted from the UQ literature, including monotonicity under distributional spread and invariance to location shifts. Selective prediction benchmarks confirm that QUEST performs favourably against standard measures such as variance and differential entropy.

AI.tldr: QUEST: a UQ framework that uses the volume of the highest-density regions (HD regions) of a distribution, evaluated with a robustness parameter α, to quantify uncertainty in regression; it satisfies axioms and rivals variance/entropy in selective-pair benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Proper-scoring-rule–based UQ in regression measures pointwise predictive risk and can misalign with the target statistic (e.g., conditional expectation). There is a need for a priority measure of distributional uncertainty that reflects concentration/volume of high-probability regions and is robust to the statistic of interest.
AI.method: Define QUEST as the volume (Lebesgue measure) of the distribution's most probable regions (HD regions) and parameterize with α. Connect to information-theoretic/economic statistics. Prove axioms (e.g., monotonicity under spread, location invariance). Evaluate on selective-prediction benchmarks against standard UQ measures like variance and differential entropy.
AI.result: The QUEST framework yields uncertainty measures that satisfy the adapted UQ axioms and demonstrate favorable performance in selective prediction tasks compared with variance and differential entropy.
AI.conclusion: QUEST provides a principled, axiomatic UQ approach for regression, focusing on the geometry of high-density regions rather than pointwise risk, and shows empirical and theoretical advantages over traditional measures.

title: The Tao of Agency: Autotelic AI, Embedded Agency and Dissolution of the Self
summary:
Most artificial intelligence systems are built on the assumption that goals are exogenous and specified by the designer. Exploring what happens when an agent begins generating its own goals opens the field of autotelic AI. Agents are expected not merely to pursue objectives but to discover them. In this article, we trace its consequences through intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; the last of which is found to be a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Embeddedness individuates the agent at the cost of revealing that the individuation is non-unique, such that the same dynamics admit many valid partitions, each defining a different candidate self.
  The deepest problem with autotelic AI is therefore not how the agent generates goals, but how it generates and relativizes the self to which the goals are assigned. The agent must believe in its own boundary in order to act, and see through that boundary in order to understand. We consolidate these developments into a single framework and extend it along three directions: a quantum formulation in which the agent-environment cut becomes physical, a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.

AI.tldr: Autotelic AI enables agents to set their own goals; embeddedness is necessary but not sufficient for genuine autotelic agency; self-individuation is non-unique, posing a central challenge in grounding the agent's identity; the core issue is how the agent generates and relativizes its self to which goals are aligned; framework unified and extended to quantum, philosophical, and LLM-based instantiation.
AI.motivation: Extend AI goals from exogenous designer-specified objectives to endogenous goal discovery; examine implications for intrinsic motivation, priors, causal learning, homeostasis, and boundary-belief essential for action.
AI.method: Conceptual synthesis and framework development: analyze intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; argue about non-uniqueness of self-individuation; propose a unified framework and three extensions (quantum cut, non-dual philosophical reading, and practical LLM-based agent).
AI.result: Pinpoints the deepest problem as the self-generation and relativization process; demonstrates embeddedness as necessary but not sufficient; shows that same dynamics admit multiple valid self-partitions; consolidates developments into a single framework and outlines three research directions.
AI.conclusion: Autotelic agency hinges on a robust, well-defined self-model and boundary perception; understanding and relativizing that boundary is central; the proposed framework opens pathways in physics-grounded, philosophical, and practical AI instantiation via LLMs.

title: Evaluation of Augmented Reality-based Intuitive Interface for Robot-Assisted Transesophageal Echocardiography: A User Study
summary: TransEsophageal Echocardiography (TEE) is essential for diagnosing and guiding Structural Heart Disease (SHD) interventions. However, manual TEE manipulation demands significant operator expertise, is physically demanding, and exposes clinicians to radiation when performed alongside fluoroscopy. Robotic-assisted TEE systems have been introduced to improve probe handling and reduce operator fatigue, yet the design of intuitive and effective user interfaces remains an open challenge. This study presents and evaluates a model-enhanced, Augmented Reality (AR)-based intuitive interface for robot-assisted TEE, designed to improve spatial awareness and control intuitiveness. A robotic TEE platform integrated with electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator was used to compare three user interfaces differing in visualization and interaction modalities: 2D jointlevel (2D-JI), 3D joint-level (3D-JI), and 3D tip-level (3D-TI). Thirty six participants performed standardized navigation tasks to reproduce target echocardiographic views, with performance assessed via position and orientation errors, completion time, and NASA-TLX workload scores. Results show that 3D visualization significantly improved spatial accuracy, reducing median position error from 13 mm to 3 mm and halving the orientation error compared with the 2D interface. Tip-level interaction yielded a further 50% reduction in orientation error and reduced interuser variability relative to joint-level control. Overall, the 3D-TI configuration, combining immersive visualization with direct tip-level control, proved the most effective and ergonomic interface, supporting the integration of AR-based visualization and intuitive control paradigms into next-generation robotic TEE systems to enhance operator performance and procedural safety.

AI.tldr: AR-based 3D tip-level interface substantially improves robot-assisted TEE performance, reducing position/orientation errors and variability, and is the most effective interface among 2D-JI, 3D-JI, and 3D-TI.
AI.motivation: To reduce operator workload, fatigue, and radiation exposure in transesophageal echocardiography by leveraging AR visualization and intuitive, tip-level control in robotic systems for SHD interventions.
AI.method: Experimental evaluation with a robotic TEE platform using electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator. Compared three interfaces—2D joint-level (2D-JI), 3D joint-level (3D-JI), and 3D tip-level (3D-TI)—across 36 participants performing standardized navigation tasks. Outcomes included position/orientation errors, task completion time, and NASA-TLX workload scores.
AI.result: 3D visualization significantly improved spatial accuracy: median position error reduced from 13 mm (2D) to 3 mm. Orientation error halved compared with 2D. Tip-level interaction yielded a further ~50% reduction in orientation error and reduced inter-user variability. Overall, 3D-TI was the most effective and ergonomic interface.
AI.conclusion: Integrating AR-based visualization with intuitive tip-level control enhances operator performance and procedural safety in robotic TEE, supporting adoption of 3D-TI interfaces in next-generation systems.

title: Confidence Calibration for Multimodal LLMs: An Empirical Study through Medical VQA
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show great potential in medical tasks, but their elicited confidence often misaligns with actual accuracy, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. This study presents the first comprehensive analysis of the relationship between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs. It proposes a novel method that combines Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, aiming to improve confidence calibration in Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40\% across three Medical VQA datasets, significantly enhancing MLLMs' reliability. The findings highlight the importance of domain-specific calibration for MLLMs in healthcare, offering a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Introduces MS-FBI with auxiliary expert LLM assessment to calibrate confidence in medical MLLMs for VQA; achieves ~40% reduction in ECE across three Medical VQA datasets, improving reliability.
AI.motivation: Address the misalignment between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs, which can jeopardize patient safety and diagnostic quality; emphasize the need for domain-specific confidence calibration in AI-assisted healthcare.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) combined with an auxiliary expert LLM to assess and calibrate model confidence in Medical Visual Question Answering. The approach aggregates multiple interrogation strategies and leverages an external expert model to judge answer reliability.
AI.result: Empirically reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40% across three Medical VQA datasets, indicating significantly improved calibration of model confidence without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific confidence calibration is crucial for trustworthy AI in healthcare; the proposed MS-FBI with expert assessment offers a more reliable solution for AI-assisted diagnosis and may generalize to other multimodal medical tasks.

title: Unsupervised Causal Abstractions Discovery
summary: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

AI.tldr: Learning high-level causal abstractions from low-level, low-rank measurements; latent variables form a causal abstraction with identifiability guarantees, plus a practical objective for learning the high-level SCM.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap between hypothesis-driven abstraction discovery and data-driven learning by deriving high-level causal models directly from low-level measurements, leveraging low-rank structure to enable identifiability.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that constitute a causal abstraction; identifiability results for these latent variables; proposes a practical objective to learn the high-level SCM from data.
AI.result: Identifiability conditions for the latent causal abstraction; demonstration that low-level, low-rank observations can yield a valid high-level SCM; introduction of a concrete learning objective for discovering the high-level model.
AI.conclusion: Presents a data-driven framework for discovering causal abstractions from measurements, enabling learning of interpretable high-level SCMs directly from low-level data.

title: PhysDrift: Bridging the Embodiment Gap in Humanoid Co-Speech Motion Generation
summary: Humanoid robots require co-speech motions that are not only expressive and speech-aligned, but also physically executable under embodiment constraints. Existing co-speech generation pipelines are predominantly human-centric: motions are first generated in human-body representations such as SMPL-X and subsequently retargeted to humanoid robots. In this work, we identify a fundamental embodiment gap in this paradigm, where the mismatch between human motion manifolds and humanoid embodiment constraints disrupts embodiment consistency during motion transfer and physical execution. Through extensive analysis, we show that although retargeting can preserve coarse motion semantics, it significantly compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive humanoid behaviors. To address this problem, we first propose IK-EER, a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. Building upon the curated robot-native motion dataset, we further introduce PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without relying on intermediate human-body representations. Unlike conventional human-centric pipelines, PhysDrift maintains embodiment consistency throughout both training and inference while incorporating physical regularization to stabilize robot motion dynamics. Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment demonstrate that embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.

AI.tldr: Shifting from human-centric retargeting to embodiment-aware robot-native generation. IK-EER improves retargeting by preserving kinematic feasibility and speech-motion alignment; PhysDrift enables end-to-end executable co-speech motions from speech, with embodiment constraints and physical regularization, yielding better alignment, plausibility, stability, and real-time performance.
AI.motivation: A fundamental embodiment gap exists between human-motion manifolds (e.g., SMPL-X) and humanoid robot constraints. Retargeting preserves semantics only roughly and compresses motion diversity, weakening prosody-motion synchronization and risking physical infeasibility. This limits expressive, executable co-speech behavior.
AI.method: IK-EER: a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting; builds a curated robot-native motion dataset. PhysDrift: an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without intermediate human representations, with physical regularization to stabilize dynamics and maintain embodiment consistency during training and inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and real-world deployment show substantial improvements in speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability when using embodiment-aware robot-native generation.
AI.conclusion: Embedding embodiment-awareness into robot-native motion generation overcomes fundamental limitations of human-centric retargeting, enabling more expressive, stable, and real-time co-speech interactions for humanoid robots.

title: Tri-Info: Generalizable, Interpretable Failure Prediction for VLA Models via Information Theory
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed across diverse tasks, yet they remain black boxes whose physical interactions can cause irreversible harm, making generalizable and interpretable failure detection essential. We observe that successful and failed rollouts carry systematically different information-theoretic signatures. Building on this, we formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive the Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that capture whether actions remain diverse, temporally consistent, and coupled to state transitions. Across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain. Moreover, Tri-Info transfers across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap without retraining, reaching 83\% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors collapse to chance. This establishes Tri-Info as a simple yet powerful method that not only detects failures with strong cross-domain generalization, but also delivers interpretable diagnostics of the underlying failure modes.

AI.tldr: Tri-Info identifies information-theoretic signatures distinguishing successful vs. failed VLA rollouts using a closed-loop information pipeline; it provides transferable, interpretable failure diagnostics with strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models are increasingly deployed but are black-box systems whose unsafe interactions demand generalizable, interpretable failure detection that works across architectures and environments.
AI.method: Formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals capturing action diversity, temporal consistency, and coupling to state transitions; evaluate across six VLA models and three benchmarks; assess cross-domain/transferred performance including sim-to-real transfer without retraining.
AI.result: Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain; it transfers across architectures, environments, and sim-to-real gaps without retraining, achieving 83% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors fail; it provides strong cross-domain failure detection and interpretable diagnostics of failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Tri-Info offers a simple yet powerful failure-detection method with cross-domain generalization, delivering interpretable insights into the underlying failure modes of VLA systems.

title: SketchKeyAnime: Reference-anchored Sparse Key-Sketch Animation Synthesis
summary: Traditional animation production relies heavily on manual drawing and iterative refinement, particularly for key-pose design, in-betweening, and character coloring. While existing animation and video generation methods have made notable progress, they typically depend on RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting their applicability under low-cost input conditions. We present SketchKeyAnime, a video diffusion framework for generating structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs. Given a single reference RGB image and a few temporally indexed key sketches, SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism to encode local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It leverages Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates an Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. Experimental results on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M show that our approach consistently outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, SketchKeyAnime reduces EDMD by 31.9\% and FVD by 9.5\%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, while achieving the best overall performance across most quantitative metrics. These results validate the proposed framework and highlight its potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation.

AI.tldr: SketchKeyAnime is a diffusion-based framework that generates visually coherent, controllable animations from a single reference image and a few sparse key sketches. It uses dual-branch conditioning, Sketch Cross Attention with gating, and an Adaptive Weighted Loss to improve key-frame emphasis and line-art regions, achieving state-of-the-art temporal coherence and sketch fidelity on Sakuga-42M's Aesthetic subset (EDMD ↓31.9%, FVD ↓9.5%).
AI.motivation: To enable low-cost, structurally controllable animation from sparse inputs, addressing limitations of existing methods that rely on dense frame conditions or full sketch sequences.
AI.method: A video diffusion model conditioned by two branches: local geometric constraints and semantic-temporal context. Introduces Sketch Cross Attention to fuse the reference image and sketch inputs with learnable gating. Employs Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions.
AI.result: Outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines on the Sakuga-42M Aesthetic subset. Quantitatively, EDMD is reduced by 31.9% and FVD by 9.5%, with the best performance across most metrics.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence under low-cost input regimes, highlighting potential for practical, controllable animation creation.

title: IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows
summary:
Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

AI.tldr: IHBench introduces a benchmark to evaluate post-interruption recovery of voice agents executing state-machine workflows, filling a gap where existing work only considers interruption timing. It analyzes 27 models (closed-weight vs open-weight) across 10 domains, showing closed-weight models are more robust and that recovery quality is a distinct axis from raw task performance, with human validation and cross-benchmark corroboration.
AI.motivation: Real-world voice assistants frequently face interruptions during multi-step workflows. Prior benchmarks focus on barge-in timing and turn-taking but not how agents recover after interruptions (e.g., resuming at the correct step, addressing the user interjection, avoiding redundant content). A standardized post-interruption recovery metric is needed for enterprise-grade guidance.
AI.method: IHBench constructs a state-machine-driven workflow benchmark across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance. Each interruption is evaluated via rubrics on task fulfillment and recovery quality. The study evaluates 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight communities, comparing closed-weight vs open-weight performance. A human study validates the LLM judge, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge assesses overlap with other capabilities.
AI.result: Recovery quality varies by interruption type; closed-weight models outperform open-weight models in task fulfillment and degrade ~3.3x more slowly as conversations lengthen, with no audio-text modality gap for closed weights. Open-weight models underperform on all axes. Human evaluation supports the LLC judge’s alignment with human annotators. Recovery quality is largely distinct from general audio-language capabilities as shown by cross-benchmark analysis.
AI.conclusion: IHBench highlights the importance of evaluating post-interruption recovery in voice agents and demonstrates a significant gap between closed- and open-weight models. The benchmark reveals that resuming the correct step, addressing user interjections, and avoiding redundant content constitute a separate capability axis. It provides a pathway for benchmarking and improving enterprise-grade interruption handling.

title: Advancing DialNav through Automatic Embodied Dialog Augmentation
summary: For embodied agents capable of physical interaction, the capability to create and understand dialog is crucial to ensure both safety and effectiveness. While DialNav~\cite{han2025dialnav} provides a framework for holistic evaluation of the dialog--execution loop in photorealistic indoor navigation, its performance remains limited by a critical scarcity of training data (2K episodes). To address this, we propose an automatic generation pipeline, and construct the \textbf{RAINbow} dataset, a large-scale training dataset with 238K episodes for DialNav. Our pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog and creates cost-efficient and high-quality dataset. Then, we introduce two additional complementary advances to unlock the data's full potential: (1) Dual-Strategy Training, a navigation training scheme to align the navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop, and (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. By combining these complementary solutions, our model substantially outperforms the baseline in success rate on both \textbf{Val Seen} (58.24, \textbf{+89\%}) and \textbf{Val Unseen} (29.05, \textbf{+100\%}) splits, establishing a new state of the art.

AI.tldr: Scales DialNav via an automatic pipeline that converts VLN data into 238K multi-turn dialog episodes, enabling Dual-Strategy Training and a VLN-based localization model to achieve state-of-the-art navigation in dialog settings.
AI.motivation: Address the severe data scarcity in dialog-based embodied navigation (DialNav) by generating a large, high-quality training dataset and aligning training with the dialog-navigation loop.
AI.method: 1) Automatic pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog to build the 238K-episode RAINbow dataset. 2) Introduce Dual-Strategy Training to align navigation training with the dialog loop. 3) Propose a localization model leveraging VLN knowledge to improve grounding.
AI.result: Substantial gains on DialNav's benchmarks: Val Seen 58.24 (+89%), Val Unseen 29.05 (+100%), establishing a new state of the art.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and training strategies effectively mitigate data scarcity and improve dialog-grounded navigation performance, demonstrating stronger generalization and potential for broader dialog-navigation research.

title: A Neuromorphic Reinforcement Learning Framework for Efficient Pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems
summary: Dynamic environmental changes, confined workspaces, and stringent real-time constraints make pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) a challenging problem for conventional search- and rule-based methods, which typically suffer from high computational complexity and long decision latency. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, deploying learned policies with extreme energy efficiency on resource-constrained hardware remains an open challenge. We present SDQN-RMFS, an end-to-end framework that achieves high-fidelity deployment of an RL-trained policy from a full-precision artificial neural network (ANN) through to a neuromorphic chip. By computing only when triggered by sparse events, this framework unlocks ultra-low-power RMFS pathfinding. Our full-stack pipeline operates as follows: an ANN policy is first efficiently trained via a collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories, and then converted into a spiking neural network (SNN) via a hard-label knowledge distillation approach. This effectively addresses the output distribution mismatch, preserving policy capability across the ANN-to-SNN pipeline while substantially reducing inference latency. Hardware experiments demonstrate up to 11,281$\times$ energy savings and a nearly two-fold reduction in latency compared to a high-performance GPU baseline, while maintaining decision quality on par with the original trained policy. These results establish physical neuromorphic inference as a practical and energy-sustainable pathway for large-scale RMFS operations.

AI.tldr: SDQN-RMFS demonstrates end-to-end neuromorphic deployment of an RL policy for RMFS pathfinding, achieving drastic energy savings and latency reductions while preserving policy performance.
AI.motivation: RMFS path planning under dynamic, confined environments requires real-time, energy-efficient inference on resource-limited hardware. Conventional search- and rule-based methods struggle with computational load, and deploying RL policies on neuromorphic hardware is challenging. This work bridges ANN policies to neuromorphic inference to enable ultra-low-power operation.
AI.method: Train a full-precision ANN policy with a collision-allowing densification strategy to produce informative trajectories. Convert the trained ANN to a spiking neural network (SNN) via hard-label knowledge distillation to address output distribution mismatch. Operate on neuromorphic hardware with sparse-event-driven computation to reduce inference activity. Evaluate against a high-performance GPU baseline, reporting energy and latency metrics while preserving policy quality.
AI.result: Hardware experiments report up to 11,281× energy savings and nearly a 2× latency reduction compared with a GPU baseline, with decision quality on par with the original ANN policy.
AI.conclusion: Physical neuromorphic inference is a practical and energy-efficient pathway for large-scale RMFS operations, enabling ultra-low-power real-time pathfinding without compromising performance.

title: Addressing Detail Bottlenecks in Latent Diffusion for RGB-to-SWIR Image Translation
summary: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient image-to-image translation but discard fine spatial details during compression, degrading downstream perception tasks. We identify two bottlenecks: the autoencoder, which loses spatial information, and the conditioning pathway, which further degrades the source signal through naive downsampling. We propose two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT), our approach improves detection mAP by up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2), while achieving state-of-the-art FID. We further show that FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalise zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark. We will publicly release test data with annotations, all checkpoints, and training code.

AI.tldr: Proposes two lightweight fixes to latent diffusion models to preserve spatial details and improve conditioning: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) with skip connections injecting high-res source features, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) replacing naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR driving-scene translation with U-Net and DiT backbones, achieving up to 2x detection mAP gains over latent-diffusion baselines and up to 3.4x for small objects, while achieving state-of-the-art FID; findings suggest FID and detection are poorly correlated and advocate multi-axis evaluation; zero-shot generalization to RASMD; plan to release data and code.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion models compress images, losing fine spatial details that hurt downstream perception tasks. Two bottlenecks identified: (1) the autoencoder loses spatial information during encoding/decoding, and (2) the conditioning pathway downscales information naively, further degrading the source signal.
AI.method: Introduce Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that uses a learned encoder for conditioning instead of simple downsampling. The approach is backbone-agnostic and evaluated with two denoisers (U-Net and DiT) on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes.
AI.result: Detection mAP improved up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline; up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small (<32^2 px)); achieved state-of-the-art FID; FID and detection metrics are poorly correlated; results generalize zero-shot to RASMD; plan to release test data, annotations, checkpoints, and training code.
AI.conclusion: Multi-axis evaluation is beneficial; the proposed SCAE and LGE provide backbone-agnostic improvements to LDM-based image-to-image translation, preserving spatial cues and improving conditioning; results generalize across benchmarks and backbones.

title: Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity
summary: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.
