AI.result: On CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and MakeupSelfies datasets, MakeupMirror yields +60% relative improvement in facial recognition similarity, -50% relative skin tone difference versus Stable-Makeup, with 0.7s latency and a 94% expert acceptance rate for core facial identity preservation criteria.
AI.conclusion: MakeupMirror demonstrates notable progress toward production-level makeup transfer by improving identity fidelity and color consistency; further validation across broader demographics and varying conditions would strengthen claims.

title: An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling
summary: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

AI.tldr: An information-theoretic MDL-based framework for graph novelty generation using latent space embeddings and finite mixture models, enforcing novelty and reliability constraints to generate data, with theory and experiments showing risk-bounded novelty.
AI.motivation: To generate graph data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency, providing principled novelty with quantifiable risk.
AI.method: Embed data in a latent space; model the latent distribution with finite mixture models; generate novel samples by enforcing novelty (poor explanation by all existing components) and reliability constraints via the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle; derive threshold-based guarantees on misclassification rates; validate on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows misclassification probabilities converge to zero with explicit rates under appropriate thresholds; empirical experiments demonstrate principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk on both synthetic and real graph datasets.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a principled, MDL-based approach to graph novelty generation with provable guarantees and supporting empirical evidence.

title: Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: Vision paper, published in Conference on Games 2026
summary: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

AI.tldr: Reinforcement learning offers potential to create more believable game AI; this paper proposes a framework of requirements for RL in games, discusses deployment practicalities, showcases examples, and outlines bottlenecks and research directions.
AI.motivation: Existing hand-coded NPCs cannot capture complex human-like behavior; ML, especially RL, can learn from interaction or data to produce authentic behavior, enhancing immersion.
AI.method: Proposes a framework of game-specific RL requirements; surveys example games with RL-augmented AI; analyzes practical deployment considerations for player-facing agents; identifies bottlenecks and hard problems.
AI.result: Illustrative examples of RL-enhanced game AI; practical deployment considerations; a set of bottlenecks and open problems to guide future research.
AI.conclusion: Advancing ML-based game AI requires addressing limitations; the proposed framework and identified bottlenecks can accelerate adoption across genres.

title: Autonomous Driving with Priority-Ordered STL Specifications Under Multimodal Uncertainty
summary: Autonomous vehicles must plan trajectories that satisfy a multitude of requirements on safety, passenger comfort, and compliance with traffic rules. However, in safety-critical scenarios, it is not always possible to satisfy all requirements simultaneously, necessitating their prioritization based on importance. At the same time, in these safety-critical scenarios, the uncertainty in trajectory predictions of the surrounding traffic, such as other vehicles and pedestrians, should be explicitly accounted for. In this work, we propose an uncertainty-aware trajectory planning framework that incorporates a predefined lexicographic ordering over Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that stays valid under uncertainty. We implement this formulation with Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control and we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on simulation scenarios, showing that our framework efficiently handles conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware trajectory planning for autonomous vehicles using a lexicographic ordering of STL specifications under uncertainty, implemented with MPPI, demonstrated in simulations to manage conflicting objectives with multi-modal uncertainty.
AI.motivation: In safety-critical driving, prioritizing safety, comfort, and rule compliance is essential, and actor/environment uncertainty must be explicitly accounted for in trajectory planning.
AI.method: Introduce a lexicographic ordering over STL specifications that remains valid under uncertainty; integrate with Model Predictive Path Integral control; operate in a planning framework with multi-modal uncertainty.
AI.result: Simulation results show the framework efficiently handles conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty; demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: A principled uncertainty-aware planning approach that preserves specified priorities under uncertainty and can be implemented with MPPI for real-time planning; potential for broader adoption in autonomous driving with uncertain predictions.

title: Stitching and dimensionality effects on large artificially generated volume datasets
summary: Generating large images via deep learning requires patching input data to accommodate hardware memory limitations, then assembling output patches, a process that can introduce stitching artifacts when neighboring patches do not align at borders. While these artifacts are known to affect segmentation tasks, their impact on generative models for style-transfer remains poorly understood. We investigated three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets. We evaluated both perceptual quality and performance on downstream mitochondria segmentation. Our key findings reveal that: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation performance, (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models on downstream tasks, though the improvement barely justifies the computational cost, and (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Additionally, we demonstrate that ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions can improve low-quality volumes but provides no benefit for high-quality outputs. These results demonstrate that maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful consideration and mitigation of stitching artifacts, and that perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

AI.tldr: Stitching artifacts in patch-based cycleGANs for large biomedical images are not well captured by FID and can impair downstream segmentation; 3D stitching yields marginal gains at high cost, 2D stitching is more stable, and ensemble helps only for low-quality outputs; perceptual metrics alone are insufficient.
AI.motivation: Quantify how stitching artifacts from patch-based generation affect downstream biomedical tasks and evaluate reliable metrics and modeling choices (2D vs 3D patches, stitching strategies, ensembling) on cryo-EM data.
AI.method: Train cycleGANs on cryo-EM datasets with three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D and 3D). Assess perceptual quality (FID) and downstream mitochondria segmentation. Investigate ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions.
AI.result: (1) FID scores fail to detect artifacts that degrade segmentation performance; (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching yield marginal segmentation improvements but at substantial computational cost; (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes; ensembling improves low-quality volumes but not high-quality outputs.
AI.conclusion: Mitigating stitching artifacts is critical for large scientific datasets; perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging; consider cost-benefit when choosing 2D vs 3D and stitching strategies; ensemble methods offer limited gains.

title: Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems
summary: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

AI.tldr: Flow map models define a one-parameter family of denoisers across the distortion-perception frontier, using a lookahead parameter t to interpolate between MMSE ( fidelity ) and perceptual regimes. The approach theoretically recovers the DP frontier for Gaussian targets and shows similar behavior on natural images. A single trained flow map integrated into Plug-and-Play solvers can handle diverse inverse problems, matching or surpassing specialized baselines at both endpoints, validated on CelebA 128 and AFHQ 256 across several tasks.
AI.motivation: The distortion-perception tradeoff in image restoration is a fundamental limitation: optimizing for error yields blur; optimizing for perceptual quality yields less faithful results. Existing methods are confined to a single tradeoff or rely on supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning. A unified, unsupervised or minimally supervised approach that can traverse the DP frontier with a single model is highly desirable.
AI.method: Introduce flow map models (an extension of flow matching for few-step sampling) that learn an average field, yielding a one-parameter family of denoisers parameterized by the lookahead t. The paper analyzes Gaussian targets to show that varying t recovers the optimal DP frontier, and provides empirical evidence on natural images. The method is integrated into Plug-and-Play solvers to general inverse problems, using t to balance perceptual alignment and data consistency. Experiments on CelebA 128×128 and AFHQ 256×256 across multiple linear and nonlinear inverse tasks.
AI.result: For Gaussian targets, varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, similar behavior is observed empirically. A single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff and matches or exceeds specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the approach’s effectiveness across linear and nonlinear inverse problems on CelebA 128 and AFHQ 256.
AI.conclusion: Flow map models offer a simple, unified mechanism to navigate the distortion-perception frontier with a single trained model. The framework provides theoretical justification in the Gaussian case, practical efficacy in natural images, and broad applicability within Plug-and-Play solvers for diverse inverse problems, achieving competitive performance without needing multiple models or extensive hyperparameter tuning.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

AI.tldr: A framework (QMFOL) for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with controllable complexity and a corresponding benchmark (QMFOLBench) of 2880 instances; demonstrates varying model performance with logical complexity and semantic variation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of fine-grained control over logical complexity and semantic diversity in existing deductive reasoning benchmarks, enabling precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in large language and reasoning models.
AI.method: Construct formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns to control reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors; translate to natural language via LLMs; ensure logical consistency via round-trip verification with an external prover; produce 2880 instances across 960 configurations.
AI.result: Evaluation on six LRMs and two LLMs shows performance degradation and higher computational overhead with increasing logical complexity; models perform better on True-labeled tasks than False or Unknown; sensitivity to semantic variation; benchmark is scalable and reliable for evaluating deductive reasoning.
AI.conclusion: QMFOL offers a scalable, controllable framework for deductive reasoning benchmarks, enabling precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities and providing a foundation for future benchmark design and extension.

title: An Infrastructure-less, Control-Independent Solution to Relative Localisation of a Team of Mobile Robots using Ranging Measurements
summary: The ability to localise teams of robots is essential for applications ranging from robotic fleets in unstructured environments to cooperative control and navigation tasks. In such contexts, fixed infrastructure is often unavailable, deployments must be fast and flexible, and system requirements must be minimal. We present a decentralised cooperative localisation algorithm that addresses all these challenges at once. The method is anchor-less, fully decentralised, and, unlike most existing approaches, does not require controlling the robots motion to ensure team observability. It relies only on local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging measurements, and short-range communication, all of which are widely available in practice. The algorithm adopts a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework that maintains the entire set of feasible solutions, ensuring robustness under transient unobservable conditions. Moreover, through information sharing, each agent benefits from the estimates of the entire group, even in partially connected conditions.

AI.tldr: Anchor-less, fully decentralized cooperative localization using a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework with local odometry and sparse inter-agent ranging; robust to transient observability and benefits from shared information in partially connected networks.
AI.motivation: To enable fast, infrastructure-free localization of robot teams in unstructured environments with minimal sensing/communication requirements, while remaining robust under transient observability loss.
AI.method: Decentralised cooperative localisation algorithm that does not require anchor nodes or motion-control to guarantee observability. Uses only local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging, and short-range communication. Employs a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework to maintain the complete set of feasible solutions, enabling robustness, and shares information so each agent benefits from the global estimates even under partial connectivity.
AI.result: Claims robustness under transient unobservable conditions and improved localization through information sharing across agents in partially connected networks, with fully decentralised anchor-less operation and no need to control robot motion for observability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a robust, infrastructure-free solution for multi-robot localisation, combining decentralisation, anchor-less operation, and Bayesian hypothesis maintenance to leverage limited sensing and communication.

title: WeGenBench: A Multidimensional Diagnostic Benchmark towards Text-to-Image Model Optimization
summary: Recent text-to-image generation models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in synthesizing highly realistic images from text inputs alone. Although existing benchmarks can evaluate the generation capabilities of various models to some extent, they struggle to comprehensively and accurately measure performance across multiple dimensions, often failing to reveal the inherent deficiencies of models in specific categories. To address these limitations, we propose WeGenBench, a novel benchmark designed for the comprehensive, multi-perspective evaluation of text-to-image generation capabilities. Our benchmark comprises a total of 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, meticulously balanced between Chinese and English to evaluate bilingual and cross-cultural generation capabilities. Beyond macroscopic scene classification, we annotate each prompt with multi-dimensional tags tailored to the distinct content and challenges of each language, thereby refining the generation tasks into more specific sub-categories. Through a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism leveraging both scene classifications and multi-dimensional tags, WeGenBench can precisely pinpoint model shortcomings in specific generation categories. Furthermore, to measure generation quality more accurately, we design and validate several novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which assess model performance on domain-specific tasks from three core aspects. Crucially, our approach yields both the assessment outcomes and the detailed reasoning trajectories, facilitating a rigorous verification of the accuracy and soundness of the evaluation results. Finally, we conduct systematic benchmarking on current state-of-the-art methods and provide an in-depth analysis of the limitations present in existing models.

AI.tldr: WeGenBench is a bilingual, multi-dimensional benchmark for text-to-image generation with 4,000 prompts across two categories, annotated with language-specific tags; it introduces cross-dimensional evaluation and Vision-Language Model–based metrics with reasoning traces to diagnose model weaknesses and benchmark performance.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks fail to comprehensively measure multi-dimensional and cross-cultural generation capabilities, often masking category-specific deficiencies.
AI.method: Construct 4,000 prompts evenly split between Chinese and English across two categories, annotate prompts with language- and category-specific multi-dimensional tags, apply cross-dimensional evaluation using scene classifications and tags, develop novel VLM-based evaluation metrics across three domain-specific aspects, and provide reasoning trajectories to verify results.
AI.result: Benchmark and metrics are proposed and validated through benchmarking of state-of-the-art T2I models, yielding detailed insights into model limitations and more precise evaluation than prior benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: WeGenBench enables accurate, explainable, cross-linguistic evaluation of text-to-image generation, facilitating targeted model improvements and rigorous verification of results.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

AI.tldr: UARM adds calibrated uncertainty and heteroscedastic reweighting to RLHF reward modeling, mitigating reward hacking by preventing unreliable rewards from dominating GRPO.
AI.motivation: Reward models in RLHF act as point estimators and often cannot signal when predictions are unreliable; GRPO can amplify unreliable rewards, creating vulnerability to reward hacking as policies explore diverse outputs.
AI.method: Introduce Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM) using quantile-based conformal prediction to calibrate reward-model uncertainty and reweight GRPO advantages via heteroscedastic variance decomposition.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF shows improved reward-model calibration, reduced reward hacking, and better downstream alignment compared with standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated uncertainty and variance-aware reweighting in reward models enhance RLHF robustness, mitigating reward hacking and improving alignment quality.

title: Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence
summary: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

AI.tldr: Intelligence is defined as the thermodynamically lawful amplification of rare but admissible futures; recursive self-simulation is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence.
AI.motivation: To provide a universal, quantitative measure of intelligence applicable across physical systems, machines, and humans by tying it to rare-event amplification under domain constraints.
AI.method: Define intelligence via probabilistic lift of rare-valid futures; model world and agent; introduce recursive self-simulation; derive a thermodynamic measure of lift; prove a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement; discuss actuation-limited optimum.
AI.result: High rare-valid lift cannot be achieved without faithful internal simulation of rare-valid futures; with sufficient fidelity and an effective policy, the lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum; the framework yields a universal measure across systems (from passive matter to humans) and even Maxwell-demon-like engines.
AI.conclusion: Recursive self-simulation is necessary and near-sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence within the stated assumptions; establishes a universal, measurable framework for intelligence.

title: CoLI: A Reproducible Platform for Continuum Robot Learning via Monolithic 3D Printing and Isomorphic Teleoperation
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, 1 table, accepted by IROS2026
summary: Continuum robots offer strong potential for manipulation tasks due to their high degrees of freedom, compliant structures, and operational safety. However, their adoption in both research and practical applications has been hindered by reproducibility issues arising from complex fabrication and assembly processes, challenging kinematic modeling, and a lack of intuitive control interfaces. To address these challenges, we present a novel open-source continuum robot design. The platform features a simplified fabrication pipeline enabled by multi-material 3D printing, allowing the arm to be fabricated as a monolithic compliant structure with minimal assembly. Control is achieved through an isomorphic teleoperation interface that establishes a direct actuator-level mapping, eliminating the need for explicit kinematic modeling and providing a singularity-free mapping. Building on this hardware design, the platform further supports imitation-learning-based autonomous control. The proposed system is evaluated through hardware characterization and a set of manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the platform provides a reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot system, accelerating algorithmic development and systematic benchmarking for the continuum robotics community.

AI.tldr: Open-source, monolithic continuum robot design via multi-material 3D printing with an isomorphic teleoperation interface and learning-ready capabilities, validated through hardware characterization and manipulation tasks to enable reproducibility and benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Address reproducibility and usability barriers in continuum robotics arising from complex fabrication, challenging kinematics, and non-intuitive control interfaces.
AI.method: Develop a novel open-source continuum robot built as a monolithic compliant structure fabricated by multi-material 3D printing. Implement an isomorphic teleoperation interface that maps directly at the actuator level, removing explicit kinematic modeling and providing a singularity-free control mapping. Extend the platform to support imitation-learning-based autonomous control.
AI.result: Hardware characterization and manipulation task experiments demonstrate reproducibility and learning-readiness, accelerating algorithmic development and benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: The proposed platform offers a reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot system that lowers barriers to development and benchmarking in the continuum robotics community.

title: Geometry-Preserving in 3D Gaussian Splatting for LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 15 pages (excluding references), 5 figures
summary: Accurate LiDAR-camera calibration is essential for robust multi-modal perception. Targetless approaches avoid manual setup but remain limited by the scarcity of discriminative cross-modal features. Recent methods address this by reconstructing the scene within a differentiable model, enabling extrinsic optimization through dense photometric supervision. Among these, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted as a geometric proxy that bridges LiDAR and camera within a single differentiable framework. However, since 3DGS was originally designed for novel view synthesis, existing methods tend to prioritize rendering quality, causing the proxy geometry to drift from the true LiDAR structure. We propose a framework that preserves the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy by aggregating multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters. We validate our method on public driving datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy.

AI.tldr: A targetless LiDAR-camera calibration framework that preserves metric geometry by using multi-view LiDAR depth supervision and preventing photometric gradients from deforming the Gaussian proxy, improving calibration accuracy on driving datasets.
AI.motivation: In targetless calibration, reliance on cross-modal features is limited and Gaussian proxies (3DGS) drift from true LiDAR geometry; need to maintain metric consistency while leveraging differentiable optimization.
AI.method: Aggregate multiple LiDAR views to derive dense depth supervision for the Gaussian proxy; block photometric gradients from updating Gaussian spatial parameters; optimize extrinsics under dense depth constraints with differentiable rendering for cross-modal consistency.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms existing targetless methods on public driving datasets in calibration accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach preserves the metric geometry of the LiDAR proxy while still benefiting from differentiable supervision, improving targetless calibration performance.

title: Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting
summary:
Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

AI.tldr: GNNs with proximity-graph inputs improve dust source emission forecasting over random-graph GNNs and LSTM.
AI.motivation: Accurately modeling the spatiotemporal dynamics of dust sources requires representing spatial relationships beyond ad hoc or random graph structures.
AI.method: Construct input graphs from proximity graphs (Delaunay, Gabriel, k-NN, Yao) and apply GNN variants (GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT) with message passing; compare against GNNs using random graphs and against LSTM baselines.
AI.result: GNNs utilizing proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and outperform LSTM in forecasting dust source emissions.
AI.conclusion: Proximity-graph representations are effective for robust and accurate dust source forecasting, underscoring the importance of graph construction choices in GNN-based spatiotemporal modeling.

title: A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization
comment: Presented at the 17th Workshop on Optimization and Learning in Multiagent Systems (OptLearnMAS, https://optlearnmas.github.io), co-located with the 25th International Conference on Autonomous Agents and Multiagent Systems (AAMAS 2026)
summary: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: MAMO: a multi-agent RL framework that learns reward weights for constrained optimization, decoupling task execution from objective design to enable autonomous balancing in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Manual tuning of Lagrangian penalty weights is brittle and non-stationary environments change the relative importance of objectives and constraints.
AI.method: Formulates reward-weight selection as a learning problem within a multi-agent RL setup, decoupling task execution from objective design.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and feasibility demonstration; highlights potential for more autonomous and robust RL-based constrained optimization, laying groundwork for future empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: MAMO enables adaptive, autonomous constraint handling by learning weightings; further work should validate empirically and extend to diverse problems.

title: Agentic AutoResearch forSpace Autonomy: An Auditable, LLM-Driven Research Agent for Aerospace Control Problems
summary: Spacecraft guidance, navigation, and control functions are increasingly realized as learned policies distilled from expert solvers. Developing such a policy is itself a research process: an investigator selects an architecture and hyperparameters, runs experiments, and must determine whether an apparent improvement is genuine or merely seed noise. This paper presents AutoResearch, a framework in which a large language model autonomously drives that loop for aerospace control problems, coupled with a credibility layer, built into the loop, that certifies each reported result against the problem's own measured seed noise. The language model serves only as the offline research agent that develops the control policy; the trained policy it produces is then deployed onboard the spacecraft, while the model itself never operates the vehicle. At each iteration the agent reads a plain-language problem description and the run history, proposes a single edit to the training script, executes it, and logs the outcome. No reported result is credited until it passes the same three checks: measured per-problem seed noise, reseeded verification of the best configuration, and leave-one-out pruning of the agent's edits. The same loop is applied, unchanged, to two aerospace control problems: a Clohessy-Wiltshire relative rendezvous and a safety-constrained collision-avoidance docking past a keep-out zone, each calibrated against a known optimal control benchmark. In both, the audited policy clears the measured seed noise by many standard deviations; an undirected search over the same parameters does not. On the docking problem the gap becomes categorical: undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy stays outside the keep-out zone on every seed.

AI.tldr: AutoResearch uses an LLM-driven loop with a credibility layer to autonomously develop and verify aerospace control policies, outperforming undirected searches in two benchmark problems.
AI.motivation: Automate the research cycle for learned spacecraft control policies to improve reproducibility, reliability, and efficiency by mitigating seed noise and certification biases.
AI.method: An offline LLM agent iteratively edits the training script, executes the change, and logs results, with a credibility layer that enforces three checks (per-problem seed noise, reseeded verification of the best config, leave-one-out pruning). The loop is applied to two problems (Clohessy-Wiltshire rendezvous and keep-out-zone docking) and the resulting policy is deployed onboard while the LLM remains offline.
AI.result: The audited policies clear seed noise by many standard deviations; undirected search fails to find viable policies. For docking, undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy remains outside the keep-out zone on every seed.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-driven, credibility-embedded research loop can produce reliable, high-quality policies for aerospace control problems and surpass naive search approaches, suggesting a viable path for automated policy development in space missions.

title: EFIQA: Explainable Fundus Image Quality Assessment via Anatomical Priors
summary: Image quality control is vital for a wide range of downstream applications. Deep learning-based image quality assessment methods typically train classifiers on dataset-specific quality labels, inheriting two limitations: (1) generalization is tied to the labeling criteria of the training set and (2) these methods cannot provide spatial feedback on where the quality is degraded, lacking explainability. In this work, we propose EFIQA, a framework that requires no quality-related supervision and produces spatial quality maps by design. Rather than learning ``what is degradation" from human-annotated labels, EFIQA learns ``what should be there" by leveraging anatomical priors. For fundus photography, we instantiate this as a two-stage approach, by first training an unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions of missing vasculature, and then distilling this prior knowledge into a shallow adapter mapping features of a frozen foundation model to precise quality maps. External-dataset evaluation demonstrates that this label-free approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability compared with supervised methods across benchmarks with different quality criteria, highlighting its potential for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: EFIQA offers a label-free, spatially explainable image quality assessment for fundus photographs. It uses a two-stage approach: unsupervised anomaly detection via masked anatomical inpainting to identify missing vasculature, followed by distilling priors into a shallow adapter that maps frozen foundation-model features to precise quality maps, yielding strong generalization and explainability on external datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome the drawbacks of supervised IQA methods that rely on dataset-specific labels and provide no spatial feedback. By leveraging anatomical priors, EFIQA defines 'what should be there' to assess quality without direct quality annotations.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) train an unsupervised anomaly detector through masked anatomical inpainting to locate regions of missing vasculature; (2) distill prior knowledge into a lightweight adapter that converts features from a frozen foundation model into spatial quality maps.
AI.result: External-dataset evaluation shows the label-free approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability compared with supervised methods across benchmarks with diverse quality criteria.
AI.conclusion: EFIQA provides a practical, explainable IQA framework requiring no quality annotations, delivering localized quality feedback and strong cross-dataset generalization, with potential for real-world application in fundus imaging.

title: When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Binning (AB) is a self-supervised pretext for medical tabular data that uses training-adaptive, feature-wise discretization with a coarse-to-fine curriculum, unifying categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision to improve representations and downstream performance; it yields consistent gains on public datasets and introduces a standardized medical tabular SSL benchmark.
AI.motivation: Solve the lack of reliable labels in clinical tabular data and the limitations of fixed, global discretization in SSL; leverage unlabeled data with a curriculum-based discretization mechanism to improve both value-space concentration and representation-space coherence.
AI.method: Iterative, per-feature discretization guided by plateau detection; representation-aware splits chosen to balance value discretization with representation structure; a heterogeneity-aware objective combines categorical reconstruction for discrete features with ordinal supervision for numeric features; training proceeds with a progressive refinement (coarse-to-fine) curriculum.
AI.result: Experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified protocols show consistent gains for both linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning; a standardized medical tabular SSL benchmark and code release support reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a practical, reproducible SSL approach for medical tabular data, leveraging adaptive binning and curriculum learning; demonstrates robust improvements and provides resources (benchmark and code) to drive progress in the domain.

title: Navigating Unreliable Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: Explicit Knowledge Conflict Resolution for LLM Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across a wide range of language-based tasks by leveraging both extensive parametric knowledge and in-context learning ability, enabling them to incorporate external information provided in the input prompt. However, the integration of external knowledge can introduce conflicts, not only between the model's internal parametric knowledge and the external information, but also among multiple pieces of external contexts. Existing approaches typically assume that either the model or the provided context is reliable, overlooking the possibility that both sources may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source over the other, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework MACR for LLM knowledge conflict resolution that moves beyond the conventional binary choice paradigm and incorporates an explicit conflict-resolution mechanism based on a multi-agent reasoning approach. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive knowledge assessment and retrieval approach that employs a modified semantic entropy measure to quantify an LLM's confidence in its answer to a given query. Based on this confidence estimation, MACR either externalizes the model's internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge when internal knowledge is insufficient, generating basic contexts for subsequent reasoning. Then we introduce an inductive multi-agent reasoning framework with three specialized agents that, respectively, induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts. Empirical results demonstrate that MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, while also providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

AI.tldr: MACR is a novel LLM knowledge-conflict resolution framework that: (i) assesses confidence in the model’s answer with a modified semantic entropy, (ii) externalizes internal knowledge or retrieves external knowledge to form basic contexts, and (iii) uses a three-agent inductive reasoning system to induce rules, analyze conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all contexts, yielding improved performance and interpretable conflict resolutions.
AI.motivation: Current work often assumes either reliable internal knowledge or reliable external context, and frequently avoids conflicts by privileging one source. The presence of conflicts among multiple information sources—internal knowledge, external context, and cross-context inconsistencies—requires explicit, transparent resolution mechanisms.
AI.method: 1) Adaptive knowledge assessment using a modified semantic entropy to estimate the LLM’s confidence for a query. 2) If confident, externalize internal knowledge as textual representations; if not, retrieve relevant external knowledge to create basic contexts. 3) A multi-agent inductive reasoning framework with three specialized agents: a rule-induction agent, a conflict-analysis agent, and a resolution agent. These agents induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts.
AI.result: Empirical results show MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard benchmarks and provides interpretable, explicit conflict resolutions.
AI.conclusion: MACR offers a principled means to go beyond binary source privileging by explicitly resolving conflicts through multi-agent reasoning, improving both accuracy and interpretability in LLMs when handling heterogeneous and potentially conflicting information.

title: LIT-GS: LiDAR-Inertial-Thermal Gaussian Splatting for Illumination-Robust Mapping
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Gaussian Splatting has enabled real-time neural rendering, yet existing LiDAR-inertial-visual (LIV) Gaussian mapping pipelines remain fragile under illumination changes and texture-deficient scenes due to their reliance on RGB photometric cues. We present LIT-GS, a LiDAR-inertial-thermal Gaussian Splatting framework that injects LiDAR-derived plane geometry as an explicit constraint in both pose/structure refinement and Gaussian optimization. Specifically, we exploit LIV visual map points as confidence-aware cross-modal anchors to establish reliable thermal-LiDAR associations, and incorporate weighted LiDAR point-to-plane residuals into bundle adjustment to jointly refine camera poses and 3D points under weak thermal supervision. Building on the refined structure, we further introduce a LiDAR-plane-regularized differentiable splatting objective that constrains rendered 3D points to align with locally observed planes, mitigating surface thickening and structural drift in low-contrast thermal imagery. Experiments on proprietary sequences and public datasets demonstrate that LIT-GS consistently improves geometric accuracy and rendering quality over state-of-the-art LIV-based Gaussian Splatting baselines, particularly in challenging lighting conditions.

AI.tldr: LIT-GS adds LiDAR plane geometry as explicit constraints and uses cross-modal anchors to enhance LIV Gaussian Splatting, improving pose/structure estimation and rendering under challenging lighting.
AI.motivation: RGB photometric cues degrade in texture-poor and low-contrast thermal scenes; robust multi-modal geometric constraints are needed to maintain accuracy.
AI.method: Integrates LiDAR-derived planes into pose/structure refinement via weighted LiDAR point‑to‑plane residuals in bundle adjustment; uses LIV visual map points as confidence-aware anchors for thermal–LiDAR associations; introduces a LiDAR‑plane‑regularized differentiable splatting objective to align rendered points with observed planes.
AI.result: Empirical results on proprietary sequences and public datasets show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art LIV Gaussian Splatting baselines, especially under difficult illumination.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit LiDAR geometry and cross-modal anchors yields more accurate geometry and higher-quality rendering in LiDAR–inertial–thermal Gaussian Splatting, reducing surface thickening and drift in challenging lighting.

title: FrozenDrive: Zero-Shot Text-Guided Driving Scene Generation and Data Augmentation with Parameter-Free Frozen Diffusion Model
summary: Synthetic data for autonomous driving is surging, powered by diffusion models that promise scalable scene generation. Yet key obstacles remain, as enforcing multi-view and temporal consistency often relies on backbone fine-tuning or added layers, which erodes pre-trained knowledge and weakens text alignment. Models also stay close to the training distribution, struggling under adverse weather and unseen configurations, and fidelity favors frequent over rare classes. We address these gaps with FrozenDrive, a controllable generative framework that preserves a pretrained diffusion models knowledge while achieving strong consistency. FrozenDrive conditions on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts, and introduces knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to impose cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass within a parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone. An additional object-focused constraint improves per-object fidelity for rare categories. Without any weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning, our model synthesizes globally coherent multi-view driving scenes from text, particularly under adverse and rare conditions, and surpasses prior baselines. On nuScenes, FrozenDrive augmented data significantly improves AD models performance, especially at night and in rain, demonstrating stronger robustness when trained with our scenario-targeted data.

AI.tldr: FrozenDrive is a parameter-free, knowledge-preserving diffusion-based framework for autonomous driving scene synthesis that achieves cross-view consistency and temporal coherence without fine-tuning, improving robustness under adverse conditions and aiding data augmentation for AD models.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of diffusion-based driving scene generation that rely on backbone fine-tuning or added layers, which erode pre-trained knowledge and degrade text alignment, and often fail under adverse weather or rare classes.
AI.method: Uses a frozen diffusion backbone conditioned on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts, with knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to enforce cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass. Adds an object-focused constraint to improve fidelity for rare categories and avoids weather/scene-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: On nuScenes, FrozenDrive augmented data significantly improves autonomous driving models, especially at night and in rain, and surpasses prior baselines, demonstrating stronger robustness with scenario-targeted data.
AI.conclusion: Efficient, robust synthetic data generation for autonomous driving that preserves pre-trained diffusion knowledge, enables strong cross-view and temporal consistency, and benefits downstream AD models without fine-tuning.

title: Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions
comment: Accepted at PAKDD 2024. Code is available at https://github.com/shiralab/NAM-FS
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

AI.tldr: Adds a feature selection layer to NAM and NBM (GAM-based NN models) to reduce computation, enable two-input NNs for feature interactions, and maintain competitive performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.
AI.motivation: NAM and NBM are interpretable GAM-based models that use neural networks as non-linear shape functions. They suffer from high computational cost when using two-input NNs or high-dimensional data, limiting scalability and interaction modeling.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable feature selection layer with weights updated during training, applied to NAM and NBM. This layer prunes inputs and allows two-input NN interactions while reducing model size and computation. Compare computational cost and predictive performance against vanilla NAM/NBM and state-of-the-art GAMs.
AI.result: The proposed feature-selection NAM/NBM variants are more computationally efficient than their vanilla counterparts, require smaller models, and achieve comparable or better predictive performance, including enabling two-input NN interactions in high-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a learnable feature-selection layer resolves computational bottlenecks, enabling scalable, interpretable GAM-based DNNs with interaction modeling; performance remains competitive with or exceeds existing GAM approaches.

title: Confidence-Aware Automated Assessment of Student-Drawn Scientific Models
summary: Student-generated drawings are widely used in science education to assess learners' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks aligned with the Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS). However, scoring such drawings requires expert human judgment to interpret complex visual representations, making large-scale assessment costly to implement and sustain in classroom settings. In this work, we study automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a vision-based model. We evaluate a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and propose a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions. This confidence signal enables selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review. Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show that the proposed approach improves scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the value of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment.

AI.tldr: Automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a Vision Transformer with confidence-aware scoring enables high-confidence automatic scoring and defers uncertain cases to humans, improving reliability while offering a trade-off between automated coverage and risk.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on expert human raters for large-scale NGSS-aligned assessment tasks involving student drawings; enhance scalability and consistency in interpreting complex visual representations.
AI.method: Employ a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation; develop a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions to enable selective automation.
AI.result: On six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items, the approach improves scoring reliability and demonstrates a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-aware, transformer-based visual scoring is a viable approach for trustworthy and scalable assessment of student-generated drawings in science education.

title: TaCauchy: An Extensible FEM Framework for Vision-Based Tactile Simulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), 2026
summary: Vision-based tactile sensors require high-fidelity simulation for reinforcement learning, yet existing approaches struggle to provide accurate mechanical stress fields within GPU-accelerated robotics platforms. We present TaCauchy, an extensible Finite Element Method (FEM) framework that integrates rigorous physics-based force computation into Isaac Sim. Built on the Unified Incremental Potential Contact (UIPC) solver, TaCauchy directly computes Cauchy stress tensors from hyperelastic constitutive laws and projects them onto contact surfaces to obtain traction forces and pressure distributions, providing mechanical ground truth from first principles rather than empirical estimation. Our framework features automatic mesh generation with geometry-aware adaptive refinement and a modular sensor interface enabling rapid integration of diverse sensors (GelSight Mini, DIGIT, 9DTact) with minimal configuration. Performance benchmarks demonstrate 33.40 FPS for single environments and 555 FPS aggregate throughput across 60 parallel environments, with stress extraction overhead under 1 ms. Physical validation experiments show strong agreement between simulated and real tactile responses across force ranges from 1.2556 N to 4.7332 N, achieving SSIM above 0.93, confirming the framework's capability to provide accurate, physically-grounded force supervision for downstream robotic manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: An extensible FEM-based stress computation framework (TaCauchy) integrated with Isaac Sim to provide physical ground-truth tactile sensing via Cauchy stress projection, achieving high throughput and strong real-world validation.
AI.motivation: To enable high-fidelity, physics-based tactile sensing within GPU-accelerated robotics platforms, addressing the lack of accurate mechanical stress fields from existing simulation approaches and the need for grounded supervision in reinforcement learning.
AI.method: TaCauchy builds on the Unified Incremental Potential Contact (UIPC) solver to directly compute Cauchy stress tensors from hyperelastic constitutive laws, projecting them onto contact surfaces to derive traction forces and pressure distributions. It includes automatic mesh generation with geometry-aware adaptive refinement and a modular sensor interface for rapid integration of GelSight Mini, DIGIT, and 9DTact sensors within Isaac Sim.
AI.result: Achieves 33.40 FPS for a single environment and 555 FPS aggregate throughput across 60 parallel environments; stress extraction overhead under 1 ms. Physical validation shows strong agreement with real tactile responses across forces 1.2556–4.7332 N, with SSIM > 0.93.
AI.conclusion: TaCauchy provides physically-grounded force supervision for tactile-enabled robotic manipulation, combining high-throughput simulation with accurate stress-field predictions and easy sensor integration.

title: Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer: Scalable 3D CT Volume Generation
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/Fredy-Zhang/PRDiT
summary: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to substantial computational demands and optimization difficulties inherent to existing generative models. In this paper, we propose the Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer (PRDiT), a scalable generative framework that synthesizes high-quality 3D medical volumes directly at voxel-level. PRDiT introduces a two-stage training architecture comprising 1) a local denoiser in the form of an MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures efficiently, and 2) a global residual diffusion transformer employing memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across entire volumes. This coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and effectively preserves subtle structures without the limitations of an autoencoder bottleneck. Extensive experiments conducted on the LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, such as HA-GAN, 3D LDM and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD and Wasserstein distance scores.

AI.tldr: PRDiT proposes a two-stage voxel-level 3D CT generator: a local MLP-based patch denoiser plus a global memory-efficient attention diffusion transformer, enabling high-res volumes with better quality and training stability while reducing computational demands. It reports state-of-the-art results on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT against HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D.
AI.motivation: Generate high-resolution, clinically useful 3D medical volumes directly at voxel level without autoencoder bottlenecks, addressing excessive compute, optimization difficulty, and loss of subtle structures in existing generative models.
AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) local denoiser—MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to extract low-frequency content; (2) global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to model/high-frequency residuals across the entire volume; coarse-to-fine modeling; no autoencoder bottleneck; evaluated on LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT.
AI.result: PRDiT achieves significantly lower 3D FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distances than HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D across the datasets, indicating superior perceptual and distributional fidelity of generated CT volumes.
AI.conclusion: PRDiT offers scalable, stable voxel-level 3D generation with improved detail preservation and global coherence. The two-stage design plus memory-efficient attention yields strong performance gains over state-of-the-art baselines, suggesting strong potential for high-resolution synthetic medical volume generation.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention
summary: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

AI.tldr: SEA-PINN integrates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into PINNs to recalibrate neuron importance across layers, yielding stable initialization and reduced variance. It achieves competitive accuracy without Fourier features and significantly boosts performance when combined with TSA-PINN, acting as a lightweight plug-in to enhance nonlinear representation and convergence reliability.
AI.motivation: Enhance physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) by stabilizing initialization, reducing sensitivity to initial conditions, and improving learning of high-frequency or complex dynamics without relying on Fourier features or specialized activations.
AI.method: Introduce SEA-PINN by embedding a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention module to dynamically reweight neurons across layers. Emphasizes stable initialization, avoids Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activations, and can be integrated with TSA-PINN (learnable-frequency high-frequency PINN) to further boost performance.
AI.result: On 17 of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibits negligible variance and markedly reduced initial loss, providing a quasi-deterministic starting point for optimization. It achieves competitive accuracy and shows a ~90% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on a high-frequency case (case 7). When SEA-PINN is integrated into TSA-PINN, performance improves by about 42.49%.
AI.conclusion: SEA-PINN functions as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, strengthens convergence robustness, and improves the overall reliability of physics-informed learning in challenging settings.

title: Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving
summary: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

AI.tldr: Proposes Lagrange, a sparse, open-vocabulary driving framework using Masked Latent Fields and Vision-Language Models to produce a continuous energy field for kinematically constrained planning, addressing efficiency-generalization tradeoffs and OOD risks; validated offline on nuScenes and CODA.
AI.motivation: Need robust generalization to anomalous/open-world driving while maintaining computational efficiency and strict kinematic feasibility. Dense volumetric models are costly and limited in high-level semantics; sparse planners struggle with open-set or OOD events; existing Vision-Language-Action models are ill-suited for continuous control due to autoregressive, discrete generation.
AI.method: Introduce Masked Latent Fields (MLF) that encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic tokens via Vision-Language Models. An intent-driven masked cross-attention module temporally filters irrelevant entities and decodes attended tokens into a continuous energy field over spatial coordinates. Planning is cast as Lagrangian action minimization over this energy field, enforcing vehicle kinematics and collision avoidance without relying on dense reconstructions or closed-set queries.
AI.result: Offline evaluations on nuScenes (standard) and CODA (long-tail) demonstrate that Lagrange achieves robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy, highlighting the promise of the framework for practical deployment.
AI.conclusion: Lagrange presents a viable path toward open-vocabulary, computationally sparse autonomous driving by integrating VLM-based continuous tokens with energy-based, constrained planning. The approach addresses the generalization gap and computation-inefficiency of dense methods while maintaining control feasibility.

title: ARC: Adaptive Robust Joint State and Covariance Estimation
comment: Submitted to information IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L), June 2026. 8 pages, 7 figures, 1 table
summary: Sensor measurements are frequently corrupted by outliers and non-Gaussian noise. These imperfections in the sensor data can cause classical state estimators to generate biased and unreliable state and uncertainty estimates. Robust estimators reject or downweight outliers but do not perform measurement covariance estimation, whereas joint state and covariance estimators assume Gaussian residuals and fixed loss shape parameters. Integrating these two capabilities into a single framework is an opportunity to simultaneously estimate both state and covariance in the presence of outliers. This paper proposes a unified Block-Coordinate Descent framework that combines a norm-aware adaptive robust loss, an Iteratively Reweighted Least-Squares state update, and a Minimum Weighted Covariance Determinant covariance estimator, yielding a self-tuning joint state and covariance estimator. The framework is evaluated in a Monte-Carlo simulation and on real-world ultra-wideband localization experiments in cluttered non-line-of-sight environments. Results show that the proposed estimator consistently recovers the true inlier measurement covariance and matches or exceeds the state estimation accuracy of all baselines, without requiring any manual parameter tuning.

AI.tldr: Unified joint state and covariance estimation under outliers via Block-Coordinate Descent with norm-aware robust loss, IRLS state update, and MWCD covariance estimation; self-tuning without manual parameters; validated in simulation and real UWB experiments.
AI.motivation: Outliers and non-Gaussian noise corrupt sensor data, causing biased state and uncertainty estimates. Robust estimators handle outliers but miss covariance estimation; joint estimators assume Gaussian residuals and fixed loss shapes. An integrated framework aims to jointly estimate state and covariance in the presence of outliers.
AI.method: A Block-Coordinate Descent framework that jointly optimizes: (i) a norm-aware adaptive robust loss, (ii) an Iteratively Reweighted Least-Squares state update, and (iii) a Minimum Weighted Covariance Determinant covariance estimator, yielding a self-tuning joint state and covariance estimator.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation via Monte-Carlo simulations and real-world ultra-wideband localization in cluttered non-line-of-sight environments shows the estimator recovers the true inlier measurement covariance and achieves state estimation accuracy on par with or better than baselines, without manual parameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a robust, self-tuning solution for joint state and covariance estimation in the presence of outliers, demonstrated to recover inlier covariance and maintain high estimation accuracy across simulations and real data.

title: SAM3 Self-Distillation for Fine-Grained GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation
comment: 4th place in ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation Challenge
summary: We describe our 4th-place entry to the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which reached a composite mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set. Our model adapts the image encoder of a recent visual foundation model, Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), with a lightweight decoder. Beyond this, we contribute two techniques and one empirical finding: (i) a self-distillation scheme that re-uses SAM3 itself, prompted with ground-truth boxes, as a teacher on the classes where it outperforms our own model; (ii) an image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation scheme that restores multi-scale inference for a fixed-input-size model by rescaling the image rather than the model input; and (iii) the finding that an aggressive photometric distortion from a winning 2025 GOOSE 2D entry, transplanted onto our pipeline, is its single largest source of improvement.

AI.tldr: 4th-place entry to the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge achieving 69.73% mIoU on the official 1,815-image test set by adapting the Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) encoder with a lightweight decoder, plus two training-time/architecture techniques and one empirical finding: (i) self-distillation using SAM3 as teacher with ground-truth boxes for classes where it outperforms the model; (ii) image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation by rescaling the image (not the model input); and (iii) aggressive photometric distortion from a 2025 winning entry as the largest single-source improvement.
AI.motivation: Leverage a modern visual foundation model to boost fine-grained semantic segmentation performance in a challenge setting; explore mechanisms to transfer and distill knowledge, and to perform robust inference with fixed-input-size models.
