AI.method: Replace a lightweight decoder atop the SAM3 image encoder; implement self-distillation where SAM3 serves as teacher for certain classes using ground-truth boxes; apply image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation by rescaling input images to recover multi-scale inference; adopt aggressive photometric distortion from a prior winning approach.
AI.result: Achieved a composite mean IoU (mIoU) of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set, placing 4th in the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge.
AI.conclusion: A SAM3-based encoder with a lightweight decoder, combined with targeted self-distillation, image-level TT augmentation, and an aggressive photometric distortion strategy, yields competitive performance in fine-grained 2D segmentation and highlights the impact of augmentation and distillation choices on modern foundation-model-based pipelines.

title: Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence
summary: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

AI.tldr: Reverse Fisher divergence provides a markedly nicer optimization landscape for fitting Gaussian mixtures with gradient descent, yielding global convergence guarantees under several settings and separation assumptions, in contrast to the forward objective.
AI.motivation: To address initialization sensitivity and convergence issues in score-matching objectives (forward Fisher divergence) and assess whether reversing the objective improves optimization properties.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze gradient-descent dynamics for Gaussian mixture fitting under the reverse Fisher divergence. Prove global convergence for a single-Gaussian teacher with a fixed-weight, identity-covariance student; extend to teacher-student GMMs with a global random initialization and a nontrivial separation condition; employ a Lyapunov-based analysis to characterize the dynamics.
AI.result: Global convergence from arbitrary initialization in the single-teacher, fixed-weights case; with a GMM teacher, high-probability component-wise convergence to nearest teacher component under a tilde-O(1) separation; conditions for total-variation convergence; the reverse objective yields a more favorable optimization landscape than the forward objective.
AI.conclusion: Reverse Fisher divergence yields improved optimization properties for GD fitting of Gaussian mixtures and provides strong convergence guarantees under realistic assumptions; opens avenues for robust score-matching-based training and future work on broader model classes.

title: Leveraging systems' non-linearity to tackle the scarcity of data in the design of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems
summary: Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) allows for the efficient building of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems (IFDS). On the other hand, DTL methods still heavily rely on large amounts of labelled data. Obtaining such an amount of data can be challenging when dealing with machines or structures faults. This document proposes a novel approach to the design of vibration-based IFDS using DTL in condition of strong data scarcity. A periodic multi-excitation level procedure leveraging intrinsic non-linearities of real-world systems is used to produce images that can be conveniently analysed by pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose faults. A new data visualization method and its augmentation technique are proposed in this paper to tackle the typical lack of data encountered during the design of IFDS. Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A data-scarcity–aware deep transfer learning approach for vibration-based fault diagnosis using a periodic multi-excitation scheme to generate image representations amenable to pre-trained CNNs; introduces a data visualization/augmentation method; validated on a railway pantograph.
AI.motivation: Addresses limited labeled data in industrial fault diagnosis; leverages intrinsic system non-linearities and transfer learning to build effective intelligent fault diagnosis systems (IFDS).
AI.method: Proposes periodic multi-excitation level procedure to synthesize informative image representations from vibration data; utilizes pre-trained CNNs for diagnosis; introduces a novel data visualization and augmentation technique to mitigate data scarcity; experimental validation on railway pantograph.
AI.result: Experimental results show effective support for the proposed method under strong data scarcity; demonstrates feasibility of CNN-based, transfer-learning–driven fault diagnosis from scarce data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed image-based DTL framework with data-visualization augmentation enhances IFDS performance in low-data regimes, with potential applicability to other mechanical systems.

title: Slow Brain, Fast Planner: Latency-Resilient VLM-Augmented Urban Navigation
summary: Learning-based planners for sidewalk navigation can generate diverse candidate trajectories in real time, yet their scoring functions often fail to select the best trajectory in challenging situations, outputting trajectories that make the mobile robot drive onto grass, toward pedestrians, or in the wrong direction, even when better candidates exist in the same set. We call this the trajectory scoring gap: in real-world sidewalk navigation, the gap between an anchor-based planner's top choice and the best possible candidate is substantial, likely due to limited high-level scene understanding capability of the planner. Rather than replacing the planner with an end-to-end Vision-Language-Action model, we propose a VLM-Planner interface that uses a VLM to select a candidate index from the planner's proposal set and then fuse it with the planner's initial output. However, VLMs take 1--3s per query and so cannot directly drive a 5--20Hz control loop. We contribute a training-free, latency-resilient trajectory-level fusion layer that turns a stale VLM selection into real-time planner scoring via geometric similarity with exponential decay. On $\sim$2,000 challenging real-world scenarios (e.g., junctions, pedestrian encounters), VLM selection achieves 30% ADE reduction versus the planner's best selection, while the planner remains competitive in routine situations. In simulation, Score Fusion maintains >80% success rate with delays up to 5s. We demonstrate the full system on a mobile robot navigating challenging campus sidewalks with varied network latency.

AI.tldr: A learning-based sidewalk planner has a trajectory scoring gap between its top anchor and the best candidate. The paper proposes a VLM-Planner interface that uses a Vision-Language Model to select a candidate from the planner's proposals and fuses it with the planner output via a latency-resilient, training-free trajectory-level fusion layer based on geometric similarity with exponential decay. Results show ~30% ADE improvement over planner's best in ~2k challenging real-world scenarios, while routine cases remain competitive; in simulation, >80% success with up to 5s delays; demonstrated on campus sidewalks under network latency.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between the planner's top choice and the best possible candidate due to limited high-level scene understanding in learning-based planners; leverage VLMs without replacing the planner, while contending with VLM latency for real-time control.
AI.method: Introduce a VLM-Planner interface that uses a VLM to pick a candidate index from the planner's proposal set and fuses it with the planner's initial output using a training-free, latency-resilient trajectory fusion layer. The fusion layer leverages geometric similarity with exponential decay to convert stale VLM selections into real-time scoring.
AI.result: Real-world evaluation on ~2,000 challenging scenes (junctions, pedestrian encounters): 30% ADE reduction versus planner's best selection; routine situations: planner remains competitive. Simulation: Score Fusion maintains >80% success with delays up to 5s. Real-world demonstration on campus sidewalks with variable network latency.
AI.conclusion: The latency-resilient fusion layer enables effective VLM-assisted trajectory scoring without replacing the planner, improving decision quality in challenging sidewalk navigation while preserving performance in easy cases; demonstrates practicality in real-world settings with network delays.

title: TriFlow: Generating Artist-Like 3D Mesh Topology via Nearest-Vertex Vector Fields
summary: We present TriFlow, a new generative approach for producing compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions such as signed distance fields. Our key insight is to represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) defined over the surface, where each point encodes its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. We train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize this field, enabling topology generation conditioned on the input geometry. To extract a coherent mesh, we cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and guide a constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization. This yields output meshes that closely match the input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity. Experiments demonstrate that TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, alongside 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.

AI.tldr: TriFlow presents a generative approach for compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology from input geometry by learning a nearest-vertex field (NVF) on the surface via a latent flow-matching model, then extracting topology with clustering and topology-aware constrained QEM mesh simplification; it outperforms prior methods in topology quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Generating topology-controlled meshes that fit input geometry is challenging for existing methods; prior learning-based approaches often sacrifice topology quality or realism. Representing topology as an NVF on the surface enables topology synthesis conditioned on geometry and enables coherent region extraction.
AI.method: Define a nearest-vertex field (NVF) on the surface where each point indicates its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. Train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize the NVF conditioned on input geometry (e.g., signed distance fields). Cluster surface regions guided by the generated NVF to obtain coherent topology; apply constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization to produce a coherent, artist-like topology.
AI.result: TriFlow exhibits strong generalization and considerably improved topology quality relative to state-of-the-art learning-based methods; reports 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: TriFlow can generate meshes that closely conform to input geometry while displaying structured, artist-like connectivity, achieving superior topology quality and computation efficiency compared with existing methods.

title: On the Oracle Complexity of Interpolation-Based Gradient Descent
summary: Recent work on first-order optimizers for empirical risk minimization (ERM) has suggested that smoothness of ERM loss functions in the training data, rather than in the optimization parameters, can be leveraged to improve the oracle complexity of gradient descent (GD) methods. In this paper, we propose an inexact gradient method, piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates the full gradient in each iteration by querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the resulting gradient samples over appropriately sized patches of the data domain. We analyze the oracle complexity of PPI-GD for strongly convex and non-convex loss functions when the data space dimension is bounded by a polylogarithmic function of the number of training samples, and find it to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss function is sufficiently smooth. Furthermore, our analysis extends several techniques from the error analysis of bicubic spline interpolants to the setting of $d$-variate tensor product polynomial interpolants which may be of independent interest in interpolation analysis.

AI.tldr: PPI-GD is an inexact gradient method that uses polynomial interpolation of gradient samples over data-domain patches to approximate the full gradient, yielding improved oracle complexity for ERM under data-domain smoothness. The work provides complexity results for strongly convex and non-convex losses with data dimension polylogarithmic in the number of samples and extends spline interpolation error analysis to d-variate tensor-product polynomials.
AI.motivation: Exploit smoothness of ERM losses in the data domain (rather than only in model parameters) to reduce gradient oracle calls and improve the efficiency of first-order methods for empirical risk minimization.
AI.method: Querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the gradient samples on patches of the data domain; use these interpolants as inexact full-gradient approximations in an iterative gradient method (PPI-GD). Analyze oracle complexity for strongly convex and non-convex losses under a bound where data dimensionality is polylogarithmic in the number of samples.
AI.result: PPI-GD achieves improved oracle complexity relative to several GD variants in key regimes where the loss is sufficiently smooth; results hold for both strongly convex and non-convex losses. The analysis also extends techniques from bicubic spline interpolation error analysis to d-variate tensor-product polynomial interpolants, which may be of independent interest.
AI.conclusion: The proposed interpolation-based gradient scheme provides theoretical efficiency gains for ERM under data-domain smoothness and contributes new interpolation-error analysis for high-dimensional tensor-product polynomial interpolants, with potential broader implications beyond ERM optimization.

title: SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation
summary: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

AI.tldr: Proposes SoftSkill: a frozen-backbone approach that learns a compact continuous context (a length-32 latent prefix) to encode task skills, tuned with next-token prediction, and deployed as latent priors. It outperforms Markdown-based skills and reduces required prompt tokens, with notable gains on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the inefficiency of using long natural-language skill tokens by instead encoding task-specific behavior as compact latent controls that steer a frozen LLM without further fine-tuning.
AI.method: Train a short latent SoftSkill prefix (length 32) on Qwen3.5-4B using next-token prediction with a frozen backbone, and deploy it as a latent behavior prior at inference. Compare to Markdown-based skills (e.g., SkillOpt) and analyze single-round task performance and agentic execution via sparse trajectory imitation.
AI.result: SoftSkill improves accuracy by 8.3 on SearchQA, 42.1 on LiveMath, and 1.3 on DocVQA in the single-round setting. Relative to SkillOpt, gains of 5.2 on SearchQA and 12.5 on LiveMath. It also replaces hundreds to thousands of Markdown tokens with a few virtual tokens, shrinking prompt length while boosting performance.
AI.conclusion: Some skills are better represented as compact latent prompts or priors over a frozen model rather than as long natural-language Markdown. Latent skill priors can yield stronger performance with far fewer prompt tokens, and offer a promising direction for scalable, efficient task specialization of large language models.

title: GroundControl: Anticipating Navigation Failures in Vision-Language Agents via Trajectory-Consistent Uncertainty Estimates
summary: Vision-language navigation agents achieve competitive average success on benchmark tasks, yet failures often arise through predictable trajectory-level breakdowns such as oscillation, stagnation, or inefficient detours. Reliable deployment, therefore, requires uncertainty signals that anticipate emerging failure dynamics during execution rather than reflect only instantaneous action entropy. We introduce \emph{GroundControl}, a trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator defined as statistical deviation from nominal goal-directed distance-to-goal dynamics aggregated over an episode. GroundControl models distance evolution using a constant-velocity Kalman filter and combines normalized innovation statistics with complementary trajectory features capturing progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, and oscillatory behavior. The resulting uncertainty score reflects geometric and temporal inconsistency in navigation behavior rather than local prediction dispersion. To evaluate uncertainty quality independently of task success, we formalize \emph{Selective Risk--Coverage Navigation (SRCN)}, a protocol that measures how effectively an uncertainty score ranks episodes by failure or inefficiency using risk--coverage curves and AURC / E-AURC summaries. Across five EB-Navigation splits ($N=300$ episodes), trajectory-consistent uncertainty achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk, with weighted-average $\mathrm{E\text{-}AURC}_{\mathrm{SR}}=0.0024$ for the GPT-4o model, substantially outperforming entropy-, conformal-, and heuristic baselines. Under SPL-based selective evaluation, GroundControl consistently achieves the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and navigation splits. These results show that modeling deviation from goal-directed dynamics provides an interpretable and robust signal for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents.

AI.tldr: GroundControl proposes a trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator for vision-language navigation, using a constant-velocity Kalman filter to model distance-to-goal dynamics and combining innovation statistics with trajectory features; evaluated via Selective Risk–Coverage Navigation showing near-oracle ranking and strong AURC improvements across models.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty signals for navigation should anticipate future failure dynamics along trajectories rather than only reflect instantaneous action entropy; need trajectory-consistent cues.
AI.method: Model distance-to-goal dynamics with a constant-velocity Kalman filter; compute normalized innovation; fuse with trajectory features (progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, oscillation) to produce GroundControl uncertainty score.
AI.result: In five EB-Navigation splits (N=300), GroundControl achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk (weighted E-AURC_SR = 0.0024 for GPT-4o); outperforms entropy, conformal, heuristic baselines; under SPL-based selective evaluation, achieves lowest AURC/E-AURC across models/splits.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-consistent deviation from goal-directed dynamics yields an interpretable, robust uncertainty signal for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents.

title: SA-VIS: Sparse frame Annotations for training Video Instance Segmentation
summary: Recent online video instance segmentation (VIS) methods have achieved impressive results, thus becoming the preferred approach to segment instances in videos. Despite the resurgence of impressive single image models, the online (or semi-online) VIS approaches outperform single-image models (e.g., based on SAM) by using long sequences of densely annotated frames during training. However,such a training setup of VIS is expensive in the sense of compute as well as dense annotations required. In order to solve these major flaws, we argue that the effective modeling of the instances and their evolution in videos do not require densely annotated frames. To that end, we propose a simple and effective module, called Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) which aggregates low-dimensional features from the image encoder of multiple frames. This simple low-compute module provides tremendous learning capability in using sparse video frame labels for end-to-end training. Combined with a light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries, our Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) significantly improves performance over its baseline. Most interestingly, our simple design that avoids complexities effectively bridges the gap in accuracy between training on sparsely and densely annotated video sequences. This translates to a mere 0.4% drop in performance of SA-VIS when using annotations for only 1/5 of the images in the dataset. Empirically, SA-VIS shows strong improvements over the baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS) and an over 1% improvement in AP on the state-of-the-art in a limited annotations scenario.

AI.tldr: SA-VIS introduces Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) and light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries to train video instance segmentation with sparse annotations, nearly matching dense-label baselines while using only 20% of the frames.
AI.motivation: Reduce compute and annotation burden in online VIS by leveraging temporal information without dense frame labeling; test whether sparse frames can suffice for effective instance modeling.
AI.method: PFP propagates low-dimensional features across multiple frames' encoders; combines with light-weight per-frame Instance Queries; trained end-to-end with sparse frame labels to form Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS).
AI.result: Outperforms the baseline by a significant margin; only ~0.4% performance drop when annotations are available for 1/5 of the images; strong gains on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS); >1% AP improvement in limited-annotation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A simple, low-compute design can bridge the accuracy gap between sparse and dense video-label training, enabling effective VIS with substantially reduced annotation and compute requirements.

title: Matching Markets meet Cumulative Prospect Theory: Towards Optimal and Adversarially Robust Learning
summary: We study a multi-agent multi-armed bandit problem in the competitive setup with two-sided matching markets under a human centric decision making model. To capture human preferences, we use cumulative prospect theory (CPT) that weighs the actions of the agent in a nonlinear fashion using a ($α$-Hölder continuous) weight function. CPT has been widely used in behavioral economics and risk sensitive machine learning to emulate human preferences. We analyze the state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards and obtain a player optimal regret of $\mathcal{O}(K\log T \left(\frac{1}Δ\right)^{2/α})$, where $K$ denotes the number of arms, $T$ is the learning horizon, and $Δ$ represents (suitably defined) players' minimum preference gap. Noticing the dependence on $Δ$ to be sub-optimal, we further improve this regret by judiciously selecting the active set of arms during exploration, which removes the dependence on $K$ in the dominant term and achieves an improved (optimal) regret guarantees in the setting where the number of arms $K$ is significantly larger than the number of players $N$. In addition, we consider adversarial markets where the observed rewards of the agents may be corrupted. We propose and analyze algorithms for robust markets with CPT as risk sensitive measure in both settings where the total corruption budget is known and where it is unknown, and establish logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees in both cases.

AI.tldr: CPT-weighted bandits in two-sided matching: initial regret bounds; improved active-arm exploration; robust/adversarial extensions with known/unknown corruption budgets yielding logarithmic regret.
AI.motivation: Model human-centric decision making in competitive matching with nonlinear CPT risk preferences; study learning performance under CPT distortions and adversarial perturbations.
AI.method: Apply CPT-weighted rewards with an α-Hölder weight; analyze standard learning algorithm; derive player-optimal regret O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}); introduce active-arm exploration to reduce K in dominant term and obtain optimal regret for K ≫ N; extend to adversarial markets; design robust CPT-aware algorithms for known/unknown corruption budgets; prove logarithmic regret.
AI.result: Regret bounds: O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}); active-arm method removes K dependence in dominant term and achieves optimal regret for large K; in adversarial markets with CPT, achieve logarithmic player-optimal regret for both known and unknown budgets.
AI.conclusion: CPT-based learning in two-sided matching is viable; addressing human risk preferences yields scalable regret performance via active exploration and robust adversarial strategies; results bridge behavioral economics and online learning in practical matching markets.

title: Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining
summary: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

AI.tldr: Mining GUI trajectories yields readable skill-like clusters, but readability does not translate into cross-domain policy improvements; current methods yield only marginal gains and may even underperform simple priors.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether interaction data can produce inspectable skill libraries that meaningfully improve downstream policies in GUI tasks.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: segment GUI trajectories, cluster segments into candidate skills, and train a skill-aware policy from resulting annotations; assess readability against known Skill labels and measure cross-domain transfer performance.
AI.result: Clustering yields high-purity clusters (5/8 ≥ 0.95) with InteraSkill Workflows labels; however, transfer gains are modest (GRPO ∼+2 percentage points in skill-step accuracy), BrowseComp+ unchanged, and predictions can be outperformed by simple frequency priors.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory mining exposes interpretable skill structure but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model fail to deliver reliable cross-domain policy improvements; the work is diagnostic regarding the utility of mined skill libraries.

title: Fast Human Attention Prediction for Fixation-guided Active Perception in Autonomous Navigation
comment: Accepted to the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Human visual attention relies on structured scanpaths to efficiently process scenes, yet instilling this behavior into robot autonomy is in its infancy and hindered by the high,computational costs of existing predictive models. To address this, we introduce GazeLNN, a computationally lightweight,scanpath prediction model that leverages Liquid Neural Networks as its recurrent engine and employs MobileNetV3 for feature extraction. Operating auto-regressively, the architecture predicts sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on the current visual stimulus and fixation history. Despite requiring only 0.61 GFLOPs, GazeLNN achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MIT Low Resolution dataset achieving 0.47 ScanMatch score. It outperforms existing recurrent baselines across diverse evaluation metrics, while reducing computational costs by 99.40% and accelerating inference by up to six times. To investigate the role of human attention modeling in robot autonomy and demonstrate the practical utility of this highly efficient architecture, we integrate GazeLNN into an active camera-robot control policy trained via Reinforcement Learning. This integration enables human-fixation-guided perception during autonomous navigation, validated through successful real-world deployments on an aerial robot.

AI.tldr: GazeLNN is a lightweight autoregressive gaze-prediction model using Liquid Neural Networks and MobileNetV3, achieving state-of-the-art performance on MIT Low Resolution data (0.47 ScanMatch) with only 0.61 GFLOPs, 99.40% compute-cost reduction, and up to 6x faster inference, plus real-world validation in autonomous aerial robot navigation.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of human-like scanpath prediction to enable real-time, perception-guided autonomy in robots.
AI.method: Use Liquid Neural Networks as the recurrent engine and MobileNetV3 for feature extraction. Auto-regressively predict sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on current stimulus and fixation history. Integrate the gaze predictor into a reinforcement-learning–trained camera control policy and validate on an aerial robot.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art 0.47 ScanMatch on MIT Low Resolution dataset, outperforms recurrent baselines across metrics, reduces computation by 99.40% and accelerates inference up to 6×. Successful real-world deployment demonstrating human-fixation-guided perception during autonomous navigation.
AI.conclusion: GazeLNN provides a highly efficient, scalable gaze-prediction module that supports perception-guided robotics and validates the practicality of neuro-inspired, lightweight architectures for real-time attention modeling.

title: HEad and neCK TumOR (HECKTOR) 2025: Benchmark of Segmentation, Diagnosis, and Prognosis in Multimodal PET/CT
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables. Overview paper for the HECKTOR 2025 challenge, held as a satellite event at MICCAI 2025. Challenge website: https://hecktor.grand-challenge.org/
summary: Head and neck cancers (HNC) represent a significant global health burden, with accurate tumor delineation being essential for effective radiotherapy planning. The complexity of the oropharyngeal anatomy, combined with the heterogeneous appearance of tumors on imaging, makes manual segmentation time-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. Beyond segmentation, predicting long-term clinical outcomes, such as recurrence-free survival (RFS), and determining human papillomavirus (HPV) status from noninvasive imaging, remain challenging yet clinically valuable goals. The HECKTOR 2025 challenge addresses these needs by establishing a comprehensive benchmark for automated HNC analysis using multimodal PET/CT imaging and electronic health records. Building on previous editions (2020-2022), this challenge features an expanded multi-institutional dataset comprising over 1,100 patients from 10 centers worldwide. Participants were tasked with three complementary objectives: (1) segmenting primary gross tumor volumes (GTVp) and metastatic lymph nodes (GTVn), (2) predicting recurrence-free survival, and (3) classifying HPV status. The challenge attracted 35 registered teams, with 15 final submissions evaluated on a held-out test set. Top-performing algorithms achieved a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.75 for segmentation, a concordance index of 0.66 for survival prediction, and a balanced accuracy of 0.56 for HPV classification. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the submitted methodologies, evaluates their performance across different lesion characteristics, and discusses their implications for clinical translation in automated oncology workflows and decision support systems.

AI.tldr: HECKTOR 2025 establishes a comprehensive benchmark for automated head and neck cancer analysis using multimodal PET/CT and EHR data, with three tasks: GTVp/GTVn segmentation, recurrence-free survival prediction, and HPV status classification; dataset >1,100 patients from 10 centers; top metrics: Dice 0.75, c-index 0.66, balanced accuracy 0.56.
AI.motivation: Address the need for reliable, noninvasive tumor delineation and outcome/patient stratification in HNC while reducing manual segmentation burden and inter-observer variability; extend HPC/AI benchmarks to multimodal imaging and clinical data.
AI.method: A multi-institutional challenge (HECKTOR 2025) using >1,100 patients from 10 centers, with three objectives: segment GTVp and GTVn, predict recurrence-free survival (RFS), and classify HPV status from PET/CT and electronic health records. Evaluation on a held-out test set; 35 registered teams and 15 final submissions. Metrics: Dice for segmentation, concordance index for survival, and balanced accuracy for HPV classification.
AI.result: Top-performing methods achieved Dice ~0.75 for segmentation, c-index ~0.66 for RFS prediction, and balanced accuracy ~0.56 for HPV classification. The paper provides a comprehensive analysis of methodologies, performance across lesion characteristics, and discusses implications for clinical translation in automated oncology workflows and decision-support systems.
AI.conclusion: HECKTOR 2025 advances automated HNC analysis by offering a robust, multicenter benchmark across segmentation, survival prediction, and HPV classification, with insights for translating AI-driven tools into clinical radiotherapy planning and patient management.

title: SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models
summary: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

AI.tldr: SL-S4Wave combines contrastive self-supervised learning with a multiscale global-convolution encoder built on structured state-space models (S4) to model long-sequence, multi-channel physiological waveforms (ECG/EEG). It achieves label-efficient, robust, and cross-domain generalization for arrhythmia and EEG tasks.
AI.motivation: Long-sequence, high-sampling-rate physiological signals (ECG/EEG) are challenging due to long-range dependencies, noise, and limited labeled data. Existing SSL and CNN-based models struggle to capture long-range structure and multichannel peculiarities. S4 architectures excel at long sequences but need adaptation for multichannel waveforms.
AI.method: Propose SL-S4Wave: a self-supervised contrastive framework using a tailored encoder based on structured state-space models with multi-layer global convolution via multiscale subkernels. The encoder captures both local patterns and long-range temporal dynamics in noisy multichannel data. Trains with unlabeled data; evaluates on arrhythmia detection and EEG tasks, with transfer to unseen arrhythmia types.
AI.result: Consistent outperformance of state-of-the-art supervised and SSL baselines in arrhythmia detection with high label efficiency; robust performance on long waveform segments; effective transfer to unseen arrhythmia types; superior performance on EEG tasks, indicating generalizability beyond cardiac data.
AI.conclusion: Integrating S4-based encoders with multiscale convolution in a contrastive SSL framework effectively models long, noisy multichannel physiological signals, yielding strong label-efficient performance and cross-domain generalization across ECG and EEG domains.

title: Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe
summary: FP4 training promises substantial reductions in memory and computation cost for LLM pretraining, yet current FP4 hardware paths and recipes, including NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class systems and AMD MI350-series GPUs, remain centered on E2M1 data elements. In this study, we identify a fundamental limitation of that choice: non-uniform formats such as E2M1 inherently suffer from Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error caused by the geometric asymmetry of their representable bins. We show that this bias accumulates multiplicatively across layers and is amplified by the Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), providing a unified explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes. In contrast, uniform grids (E1M2/INT4) bypass this grid-geometry error and better convert the improved bucket utilization from RHT into higher quantization quality. Based on this finding, we propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit training recipe that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs while restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone. On Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining, UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines, supported by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies. Our results suggest that future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.

AI.tldr: Uniform 4-bit training (UFP4) with uniform E1M2/INT4 grids and RHT improves stability and reduces BF16-relative loss degradation versus E2M1 FP4 baselines; advocates hardware support for uniform 4-bit grids alongside E2M1.
AI.motivation: FP4 reduces memory and compute for LLM pretraining, but current FP4 paths (E2M1) suffer from Shrinkage Bias due to geometric asymmetry of non-uniform grids, causing instability and degraded accuracy.
AI.method: Diagnose Shrinkage Bias caused by non-uniform E2M1 grids and RHT amplification; show multiplicative error accumulation across layers; propose UFP4 using RHT on all three training GEMMs with stochastic rounding limited to dY; evaluate on Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, MoE 124B long-run pretraining; include scaling-law analysis and ablations.
AI.result: UFP4 achieves consistently lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines across the tested scales; results supported by scaling-law analysis and ablations.
AI.conclusion: Future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1 to enable more stable and higher-quality FP4 pretraining.

title: Increasing Resilience of Continuum Robots via Motion Planning Algorithms
summary: This paper presents an experimental study of motion planning for resilient continuum robots. In this study we mainly focused on multi-criteria decision-making, its application for path-planning algorithms, impact on the generated path and execution time. To do this, we used two well-known algorithms for path planning, namely Genetic algorithm and A star algorithm, and modified them by adding the Analytical Hierarchy Process algorithm to evaluate the quality of the paths generated. In our experiment the Analytical Hierarchy Process considers four different criteria, i.e. distance, motors damage, mechanical damage of the robot's arm and accuracy, each considered to contribute to the resilience of a continuum robot. The use of different criteria is necessary to increase the time to maintenance operations of the continuum robot. We conducted the experiments using two different simulated environments of the robot. Although we significantly simplified the robot's model and its environment, we still implemented some of the features of the environment based on the real robot prototype. In particular, one of the environments has single- as well as multi-path points, and other consists of the multi-path points only. The results show that, in contrast to A star, the performance time of Genetic algorithm does not depend on the environment's cardinality. It generates more diverse paths, which increases the robot's resilience.

AI.tldr: A comparative study on motion planning for resilient continuum robots using GA and A* enhanced with AHP across four resilience-focused criteria; GA yields more diverse paths and insensitivity to environment size, while A* performance scales with environment cardinality.
AI.motivation: To improve resilience and maintainability of continuum robots by integrating multi-criteria decision making into path-planning, evaluating how GA and A* perform when guided by AHP under multiple criteria.
AI.method: Two classic path-planning algorithms (Genetic Algorithm and A*) were augmented with Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) to score path quality across four criteria: distance, motor damage, mechanical damage to the arm, and accuracy. Experiments were conducted in two simulated environments derived from a real continuum-robot prototype: one with both single- and multi-path points, and one with multi-path points only. The robot model and environment were simplified but retained key features.
AI.result: GA’s execution time did not depend on the environment’s cardinality and produced more diverse paths, thereby increasing resilience. In contrast, A* performance is affected by the number and complexity of path points; overall, GA demonstrated greater resilience through path diversity, while A* was more sensitive to environment structure.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating AHP for multi-criteria path evaluation combined with GA or A* offers a trade-off between resilience (via path diversity) and computation sensitivity to environment complexity. GA with AHP is favorable for robust motion planning in larger, more complex environment scenarios, whereas A* may be preferable for faster planning in simpler settings.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) models generate realistic likenesses of some individuals when prompted with their names, raising privacy concerns. However, distinguishing whether a generated face is memorized or fabricated currently requires ground-truth photos, access to training data, or white-box access to model internals, limiting applicability. We introduce a fully black-box behavioral probe that distinguishes between these regimes while requiring no reference photos or prior knowledge of training data. To benchmark this task, we present the NAMESAKES dataset of over one thousand names and faces of public figures spanning a wide range of fame levels, along with perturbed, less famous names. Experiments on state-of-the-art T2I models show that our probe substantially predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names, with further insights into differences across model families.

AI.tldr: A black-box behavioral probe can distinguish memorized from fabricated T2I outputs without reference data, using NAMESAKES as a benchmark; it predicts identity memorization across models.
AI.motivation: Mitigate privacy risks from T2I models that can imitate public figures by prompting with names. Existing methods require ground-truth data or white-box access; a fully black-box diagnostic is needed.
AI.method: Develop a fully black-box behavioral probe that infers memorization from prompts/outputs without reference photos or training data. Introduce NAMESAKES, a dataset of 1k+ names and faces of public figures with perturbed variants. Evaluate state-of-the-art T2I models to distinguish memorized vs unrecognized names and compare across model families.
AI.result: The probe effectively predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names; it also reveals systematic differences across model families and fame levels.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical privacy-auditing tool for T2I systems and a benchmarking resource. Encourages broader evaluation of model families and development of safeguards against memorization.

title: Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses
summary:
We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

AI.tldr: Extends post-action, globally budgeted perturbation adversarial bandit optimization from linear to general convex and β-smooth losses; derives regret bounds explicitly dependent on the perturbation budget; shows standard bandit convex optimization results are recovered when perturbations are absent.
AI.motivation: Generalize the adversarial perturbation framework from linear losses to a broader class (convex, β-smooth) and quantify how a global perturbation budget impacts regret.
AI.method: Modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analytical framework to bound the extra regret caused by post-action perturbations under a global budget, for convex β-smooth losses.
AI.result: Establishes expected regret guarantees that explicitly capture the effect of the perturbation budget; reduces to known regret bounds for standard bandit convex optimization with β-smooth losses when perturbations are zero or absent.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates robustness and extensibility of bandit convex optimization to perturbations within a global budget, generalizing prior post-action perturbation results from linear losses to a broader convex setting.

title: Interpretable Sperm Morphology Classification via Attention-Guided Deep Learning
summary: Male infertility is a major cause of couple infertility, often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting their clinical adoption. This study proposes an attention-guided deep learning framework for sperm morphology classification. We combine a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of the sperm head, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Evaluated on the SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets, our model achieves accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948), outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Furthermore, we use Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight features influencing the model's decisions. The results demonstrate that this accurate and transparent framework is a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

AI.tldr: Attention-guided EfficientNet-B0 with CBAM enhances sperm morphology classification; achieves ~90–94% accuracy and high macro F1; provides Grad-CAM++ explanations, outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0.
AI.motivation: Clinical need for accurate and interpretable automated sperm analysis; current DL models often lack transparency, limiting adoption.
AI.method: Pretrained EfficientNet-B0 backbone augmented with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on the sperm head region; trained and evaluated on SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets; comparisons against SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0; Grad-CAM++ used for explanations.
AI.result: Accuracies: 90.2% (SMIDS) and 93.9% (HuSHem); macro F1 scores: 0.913 and 0.948; outperforms the baselines; Grad-CAM++ visualizations highlight features driving decisions.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an accurate and more interpretable framework for automated sperm morphology analysis, with potential for clinical adoption in fertility clinics.

title: Generating Robot Hands from Human Demonstrations
summary: Robot learning has advanced rapidly in learning control, but learning the physical body of a robot remains much more difficult because jointly searching over design and control creates a very large combinatorial problem. Here, we present a data-driven framework for generating robot hands from human demonstrations. Instead of learning a complex controller together with each candidate design, we generate robot hand designs using the same simple control policy used after fabrication: matching fingertip positions through inverse kinematics. Using more than 4 million frames of human fingertip motion from everyday manipulation, our algorithm optimizes tree-structured robot hands to reproduce desired target motions. The framework produced both a 6-degree-of-freedom (DoF) general-purpose hand and lower-DoF task-specific hands with spatial four-bar mimic joints. To accelerate the search over designs, we trained a reinforcement-learning (RL) actor to propose good hand designs and joint angles, reducing search time from hours to minutes. We fabricated the mechanisms directly as one-piece articulated structures with print-in-place joints. In real-world experiments, the 6-DoF hand achieved highly accurate teleoperated fingertip tracking better than available commercial robot hands, whereas the specialized 3-DoF hands reproduced structured human and synthetic trajectories with reduced mechanical complexity. These results showed that large-scale human motion data can be used not only to train robot controllers but also as a reference for optimizing and generating the physical embodiment of robots.

AI.tldr: Data-driven framework to generate robot hands from human demonstrations using a simple IK-based control policy and large-scale motion data, with RL-assisted design search; yields 6-DoF general hand and 3-DoF task-specific hands; enables fabricate-once, print-in-place joints; surpasses commercial hands in teleoperation accuracy and reduces design search time from hours to minutes.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of designing robot bodies by leveraging abundant human motion data to optimize hardware embodiment, decoupling design from control to reduce combinatorial complexity.
AI.method: Optimize tree-structured robot hands to reproduce target fingertip motions using inverse kinematics with a simple control policy; train on over 4 million frames of human fingertip motion; use a reinforcement-learning actor to propose hand designs and joint angles to accelerate search; fabricate mechanisms as one-piece articulated structures with print-in-place joints.
AI.result: The framework produced a 6-DoF general-purpose hand and lower-DoF task-specific hands; the 6-DoF hand achieved highly accurate teleoperated fingertip tracking, outperforming commercial hands; the 3-DoF hands reproduced structured human and synthetic trajectories with reduced mechanical complexity; RL reduced search time from hours to minutes; large-scale human motion data served as reference for both control and embodiment optimization.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale human motion data can guide not only controller training but also the optimization and generation of robotic hardware, enabling efficient, data-driven design of robot hands that balance versatility and simplicity.

title: ARTEMIS: Agent-guided Reliability-aware Temporal Mask Evolution for Imperfectly Supervised Video Polyp Segmentation
summary: Imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation (VPS) aims to learn dense, temporally consistent masks from inexpensive supervision, including weak annotations (points, scribbles) and semi-supervision with few densely labeled frames. This setting is clinically valuable but challenging due to weak contrast, ambiguous boundaries, motion blur, and specular highlights, compounded by sparse pixel-level guidance. While SAM2 can generate dense masks from sparse inputs, direct pseudo-labeling often yields geometry-degraded masks with boundary leakage, underutilizes temporal consistency, and ignores reliability. To address these issues, we propose ARTEMIS, a unified framework for imperfectly supervised VPS driven by agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. ARTEMIS initializes coarse masks from available supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles, while dense labels serve as reliable anchors. A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision, which are propagated bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable or unlabeled frames. Finally, ARTEMIS trains the segmenter using temporal reliability-aware robust learning, incorporating reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss. These components assess mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision instead of discarding difficult samples. Experiments on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble, point, and limited-label settings demonstrate that ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code will be released at https://github.com/wangtong627/ARTEMIS.

AI.tldr: ARTEMIS is a unified framework for imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation that uses agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. It initializes masks with SAM2 and dense labels, uses a debate-and-judge vision-language agent to select reliable temporal anchors, propagates masks bidirectionally, and trains with reliability-aware robust learning to handle weak supervision, achieving state-of-the-art on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble/point/limited-label settings.
AI.motivation: Clinical VPS requires dense, temporally consistent masks but supervision is expensive; existing pseudo-labeling with SAM2 yields degraded geometry, boundary leakage, and poor temporal use. A reliability-aware framework is needed to leverage weak supervision while preserving temporal consistency.
AI.method: Initialize coarse masks from SAM2 (points/scribbles via SAM2, dense labels as anchors). A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision. Propagate anchors bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable/unlabeled frames. Train segmenter with temporal reliability-aware robust learning, including reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss to assess reliability, evolve anchors, transport identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision rather than discarding difficult samples.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 in scribble, point, and limited-label settings; code to be released (GitHub).
AI.conclusion: ARTEMIS effectively leverages reliability-aware temporal evolution and agent-guided anchoring to improve imperfect supervision VPS, balancing precision and robustness under weak supervision.

title: Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures
summary: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

AI.tldr: A universal theorem shows score approximation for diffusion models over any distribution on compact sets with finite upper Minkowski dimension d, using a discrete-mixture approach. A ReLU network approximates the score with complexity exponential in d, not the ambient dimension, explaining robustness to irregular data and supporting diffusion SDE-based generation on non-smooth structures.
AI.motivation: Address restrictive existing complexity bounds that assume Lipschitz densities or smooth manifolds, which fail for real-world data with singularities, sharp boundaries, and clusters. Demonstrate that diffusion models can efficiently approximate scores for irregular, non-smooth data.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete-mixture formulation of the target distribution and apply ReLU network approximation techniques to approximate the score function. Leverage universal approximation results and covering arguments tied to the upper Minkowski dimension d to bound network complexity by a function exponential in d. Integrate with established backward diffusion SDE theory for accuracy in solving the reverse process.
AI.result: Prove a universal score approximation theorem: for any distribution supported on any compact set with upper Minkowski dimension d, a ReLU network can approximate the score with complexity exponential in d. This avoids the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality and aligns with existing diffusion-SDE theory to enable accurate generation on irregular data.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models are theoretically equipped to handle non-smooth, irregular data structures common in real-world perceptual data. The results justify their empirical success and illuminate the role of intrinsic data geometry (dimension d) in determining model complexity, suggesting directions for theory-guided architecture design and dimension-aware training.

title: Context-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of IVF Laboratory Environmental Conditions
summary: IVF pregnancy rates are routinely modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized. We show that this is a missed opportunity. Rather than relying on raw sensor averages, we engineer 55 context-aware temporal features, including rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, and post-stress recovery speed, that capture the dynamics of incubator microenvironments. On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, these features reduce cross-validated prediction error to 1.27%, compared to 3-5% for raw averages. We then train a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model that shares environmental effects across an Asian and a Northern European clinic via partial pooling, while preserving site-specific baselines. On held-out data from the Northern European clinic, the model achieves R2 = 0.86 and a 64% error reduction for the 35-39 age group over a naive baseline, demonstrating that structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal.

AI.tldr: Engineering 55 context-aware temporal features from incubator environmental data improves IVF pregnancy-rate prediction; a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression with cross-site pooling yields transferable signals.
AI.motivation: To leverage high-resolution lab environmental data, which are underutilized in IVF outcome modeling, in order to capture incubator dynamics and improve predictive accuracy and generalizability.
AI.method: Engineered 55 context-aware temporal features (e.g., rolling thermal stability, temperature–humidity adherence, peak stress duration, post-stress recovery) from 61 weeks of Asian clinic data; trained a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression sharing environmental effects across Asian and Northern European sites via partial pooling while preserving site baselines; evaluated with cross-validation and held-out data.
AI.result: Cross-validated prediction error reduced to 1.27% (versus 3–5% with raw averages). On held-out Northern European data, R2 = 0.86 and 64% error reduction for the 35–39 age group over a naive baseline.
AI.conclusion: Structured, high-resolution environmental monitoring provides clinically meaningful, transferable signals for IVF outcome prediction, enabling cross-site generalization and improved predictive performance.

title: MemoryWAM: Efficient World Action Modeling with Persistent Memory
summary: Robust robotic manipulation in the real world requires not only an understanding of the current observation, but also memory and dynamics modeling. World action models (WAMs) possess these capabilities by jointly modeling visual foresight and actions conditioned on both current and historical observations, making them a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation. However, existing WAMs face a fundamental trade-off: methods with efficient inference typically condition only on a bounded window of recent observations and therefore struggle in non-Markovian environments, whereas methods that preserve long histories incur time and space costs that grow substantially with sequence length. To address this challenge, we introduce MemoryWAM, a world action model with efficient persistent memory. MemoryWAM uses a hybrid memory design that combines recent frames, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens that summarize long-range history. A tailored attention mechanism enables retrieval of both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context, supporting memory-dependent decision-making with reduced inference latency and GPU memory usage. Across long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks in both simulation and the real world, MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines while maintaining favorable computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: MemoryWAM proposes a memory-augmented world action model with persistent memory that blends recent frames, event-boundary anchors, and compact gist tokens, enabling efficient long-horizon manipulation with selective attention for short-term detail and long-term summarization.
AI.motivation: Robotic manipulation requires integrating current perception, memory, and dynamics. Existing WAMs struggle to scale memory with sequence length: few-shot or bounded-history models are fast but struggle in non-Markovian environments, while full-history models are accurate but costly. A persistent memory design aims to balance accuracy and efficiency for long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Hybrid memory design combining (i) recent frames for fine-grained recent context, (ii) event-boundary anchor frames to mark meaningful history segments, and (iii) compact gist tokens summarizing longer-range history. A tailored attention mechanism retrieves both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context to inform decisions, enabling memory-dependent inference with reduced latency and GPU memory.
AI.result: In long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks, MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines in both simulation and real-world settings, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency (lower latency and memory usage).
AI.conclusion: Structured persistent memory with selective, attention-driven retrieval enables memory-aware decision making in non-Markovian manipulation tasks, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs than existing WAMs and VLA baselines.

title: Evaluating and Enhancing Negation Comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in various Remote Sensing (RS) tasks. However, their ability to comprehend negation remains underexplored, limiting deployment in real-world applications where models must explicitly identify what is false or absent, e.g., emergency responders need to locate non-flooded routes for evacuation. To comprehensively study this limitation, we introduce RS-Neg, the first benchmark to evaluate negation understanding across region-level to scene-level tasks. Specifically, we design an automated data generation pipeline for RS imagery, using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries, and introduce a dynamic visual focus module for verification. Our evaluation reveals that advanced RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and substantial performance degradation. To close this gap, we propose NeFo, a novel test-time learning method that explicitly incorporates the logical role of negation into the model optimization. Remarkably, using about 5\% unlabeled test samples, NeFo significantly improves the negation understanding of models and shows strong generalization to unseen tasks. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: RS-Neg introduces the first benchmark for negation understanding in Multimodal Large Language Models applied to Remote Sensing, featuring an automated data generation pipeline with LLMs to synthesize negation queries, a dynamic visual focus module for verification, and a test-time learning method NeFo that explicitly embeds negation into model optimization. Results show current RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and performance drop, while NeFo improves negation understanding using ~5% unlabeled test data and generalizes to unseen tasks.
AI.motivation: Negation understanding in remote sensing MLLMs is underexplored yet essential for real-world decision making (e.g., identifying non-flooded routes, absent features). A dedicated benchmark and learning framework are needed to evaluate and improve this capability.
AI.method: An automated RS data generation pipeline using LLMs to craft diverse negation queries across region- to scene-level tasks; a dynamic visual focus module to verify or guide negation reasoning; NeFo, a test-time learning approach that injects the logical role of negation into optimization, leveraging ~5% unlabeled test samples.
AI.result: Advanced RS MLLMs exhibit hallucinations and substantial performance degradation on negation tasks; NeFo significantly improves negation understanding and generalizes to unseen tasks with a small fraction of unlabeled data.
AI.conclusion: RS-Neg addresses a critical gap in evaluating and improving negation understanding for RS MLLMs; NeFo demonstrates a practical, data-efficient path for test-time adaptation. Code and data are planned for release to support replication and broader evaluation.

title: ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Token-level Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT) decouples efficiency incentives from correctness during training using a mode-selection token, enabling adjustable inference-time efficiency without sacrificing long, correct reasoning.
AI.motivation: Long chain-of-thought methods incur high computation; existing efficiency strategies often degrade reasoning due to a sequence-level coupling between efficiency rewards and correctness. A decoupled, token-level approach can preserve reasoning while enabling efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a mode-selection token that toggles fast vs slow reasoning. Apply efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token, decoupling efficiency signals from correctness signals at training. At inference, control the trade-off by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token to traverse the efficiency–performance Pareto frontier.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial inference-cost reductions with preserved or only marginally affected reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks, with the ability to smoothly trade off efficiency and accuracy via the mode-token probability.
AI.conclusion: ADaPT provides a principled, train-time decoupling of efficiency and correctness signals, enabling precise inference-time control over the efficiency–performance trade-off and robust reasoning under computational constraints.

title: What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?
summary: Prior work has shown that in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remains unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations. We study this by mixing benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Across four models, we find that benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. We further show that preference optimization is the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, that demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias, and that models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal. Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

AI.tldr: Benign vs. harmful in-context demonstrations are not interchangeable for triggering harmful compliance. Model behavior depends on content, order, and training method; preference optimization prevents harmful uplift from benign demos; demonstration ordering shows strong recency bias; refusals interact variably with in-context cues across models. This yields a mechanism-level understanding of how demonstration-based jailbreaking works.
AI.motivation: To move beyond showing that demonstrations can jailbreak LMs to understanding how demonstrations are interpreted, and what factors (content, order, training) determine when benign demonstrations influence harmful behavior.
AI.method: Experimentally mix benign (non-harmful request, helpful response) and harmful (harmful request, helpful response) demonstrations across four models; test hypotheses about demonstration composition, training-stage effects (preference optimization), ordering effects (recency bias), and how refusals interact with in-context learning.
AI.result: Benign demonstrations are not interchangeable with harmful ones: they can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. Preference optimization emerges as the key training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harm. Demonstration ordering shows strong recency bias. Models differ in how refusals interact with in-context learning: some align with demonstrated formatting even when refusing, others ignore in-context signals upon refusal.
AI.conclusion: Understanding demonstration-based jailbreaking requires considering what is shown in demonstrations, their order, and the training methodology. Effective defenses should address content selection, ordering effects, and training-time optimization to mitigate harmful compliance.

title: HilDA: Hierarchical Distillation with Diffusion for Advancing Self-Supervised LiDAR Pre-trainin
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Maciej and Jesper contributed equally
summary: Leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation offers a promising solution to the scarcity of annotated data needed to represent the immense geometric and kinematic diversity of real-world autonomous driving (AD). However, current approaches typically treat VFMs as black-box teachers, relying exclusively on frame-wise feature similarity. Consequently, they do not fully exploit the teacher's layer-wise semantic structure and global context, as well as the rich spatiotemporal information inherent in LiDAR sequences. We propose HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that better captures the semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks. HilDA combines hierarchical distillation comprising multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective promoting spatiotemporal consistency. Models pre-trained with HilDA achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform models trained via prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. Code available at: https://maxiuw.github.io/hilda.

AI.tldr: HilDA is a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that uses hierarchical distillation (multi-layer semantic alignment and global context) plus a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to enforce spatiotemporal consistency, leveraging Vision Foundation Models for improved cross-modal distillation. It achieves state-of-the-art results on cross-modal benchmarks and improves 3D detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.
