AI.motivation: Addresses data scarcity and underutilization of Vision Foundation Models in cross-modal distillation. Exploits the layer-wise semantic structure, global scene context, and spatiotemporal information in LiDAR sequences to better learn geometric and semantic representations for autonomous driving.
AI.method: Proposes HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework with hierarchical distillation (multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics) and a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to promote spatiotemporal consistency in LiDAR backbones.
AI.result: Pre-trained HilDA models achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform prior distillation methods on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.
AI.conclusion: HilDA provides an effective, self-supervised path to leverage VFMs for LiDAR pretraining, enabling better semantic and geometric representations and robust spatiotemporal consistency for driving tasks; code is publicly available.

title: Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds
summary: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

AI.tldr: Compositionality in neural networks arises only in a narrow connectivity-depth regime; a mix of sparse connectivity and specific depths yields compositional representations, not in general; the authors propose SP pruning and a depth predictor and provide a theoretical framework.
AI.motivation: Explain why standard gradient optimization yields weak compositionality, identify architectural regimes that foster compositionality, and provide methods to discover and exploit it.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study varying depth and connectivity; introduction of similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity; development of a heuristic depth predictor; theoretical analysis using compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds.
AI.result: Compositionality emerges only within a narrow connectivity and depth regime; depends on which connections remain; gradient descent converges to fractured rather than compositional solutions when regimes are violated; SP recovers compositional connectivity; depth predictor locates where compositionality is likely; theoretical framework explains the phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a path to discover and leverage compositionality, while explaining its occurrence in a restricted regime; suggests focusing on targeted architectures and pruning strategies to achieve compositional generalization.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
summary:
LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench to twelve programming languages by transforming Python tasks into equivalents across languages while preserving contamination controls and evaluation protocol.
AI.motivation: LCB is limited to Python; there is a need for a cross-language, contamination-aware benchmark to assess LLMs on real-world multi-language software engineering tasks.
AI.method: Translate Python LCB tasks into equivalent tasks in eleven additional languages (total twelve), preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol; ensure full compatibility with the original LCB format to auto-track future updates; evaluate 24 LLMs on instruction and reasoning.
AI.result: Evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial multilingual performance disparities; Multi-LCB establishes a rigorous cross-language benchmark and will automatically track future LCB updates.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LCB effectively broadens LCB to a multilingual setting, addressing Python-centric bias and revealing gaps in LLM multilingual code generation capabilities; it provides a robust, contamination-aware framework for cross-language code evaluation.

title: Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long: Exploring Dataset Distillation for Continual Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to maintain model performance under evolving target domains by adapting online without labeled data. However, practical deployments often cannot retain the source dataset due to privacy or licensing constraints, and purely source-free CTTA methods tend to become unstable under long-term distribution shift, suffering from compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce DO-ALL (Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long), a plug-and-play framework that revisits source information in a compact and privacy-conscious form via Dataset Distillation (DD). Before deployment, DO-ALL performs DD to produce a small set of synthetic distilled anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, each target sample is matched with its most semantically aligned anchor, which provides a stable reference for various CTTA via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization. DO-ALL can be seamlessly integrated into existing CTTA algorithms, consistently improving long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark. This demonstrates the potential of leveraging DD to enable stable and continuous adaptation without retaining raw source data. The code is available at https://github.com/blue-531/DOALL.

AI.tldr: DO-ALL distills the source data into a tiny set of anchors for CTTA, enabling stable, long-term adaptation without storing raw data.
AI.motivation: Overcome privacy constraints and instability of source-free CTTA under long-term distribution shift; avoid compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: Compute dataset distillation offline to obtain compact anchors representing the source distribution; during deployment, match each target sample to the most semantically aligned anchor and use it as a stable reference for replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization; plug-and-play with existing CTTA algorithms.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in long-term robustness on CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and CCC benchmark; demonstrates practicality of distilled data as a privacy-preserving source proxy.
AI.conclusion: DO-ALL offers a privacy-conscious, easy-to-integrate solution to enable stable continual test-time adaptation by leveraging distilled source information; potential to extend to other CTTA settings.

title: Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation
summary: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

AI.tldr: Graph-based deep learning applied to map generalization, recasting simplification as node movement and aggregation as link prediction within a unified GNN framework. GraphSAGE performs best for link prediction; node movement prediction remains hard. Aggregation is more complex than simplification, highlighting challenges in capturing higher-order spatial relationships with current DL methods. Limitations include data imbalance and post-processing needs; the work offers methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with DL.
AI.motivation: To assess whether graph neural networks can jointly handle the two core map generalization tasks (simplification and aggregation) in a unified framework and to compare representative GNN architectures (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets.
AI.method: Formulates simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction on a graph. Evaluates GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE on multi-scale building datasets, reporting comparative performance (e.g., link prediction accuracy).
AI.result: GraphSAGE shows strengths in link prediction; node movement prediction remains challenging; aggregation poses greater complexity than simplification; limitations include data imbalance and the need for post-processing.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based deep learning provides valuable insights and directions for automated map generalization, but current models struggle to capture higher-level spatial relationships; future work should address data imbalance and post-processing and explore methodologies to better capture spatial relationships.

title: FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS
summary: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: FlowEdit enables lifelong, memory-based pronunciation correction for frozen flow-matching TTS by storing token-level edits in a Modern Hopfield Network and retrieving them with soft attention, achieving large PIN pronunciation improvements with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Overcome the static behavior of zero-shot flow-matching TTS for out-of-vocabulary proper nouns without retraining, enabling continual, memory-based corrections after deployment.
AI.method: Introduce FlowEdit: produce token-level perturbations in the text embedding space as corrective edits; store corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network acting as content-addressable episodic memory; at inference, retrieve corrections via soft attention with a similarity gate for fuzzy morphological matching.
AI.result: On a curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to zero-shot; preserves general speech quality; corrections complete in ~15 seconds on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: FlowEdit enables life-long adaptation of frozen flow-matching TTS without retraining, delivering substantial pronunciation improvements for proper nouns with minimal inference overhead and simple memory-based retrieval.

title: Evaluation of Image Matching for Art Skills Assessment
summary: While some individuals possess a natural talent for drawing, mastering this skill requires dedicated training and practice. Determining one's skill in the art of drawing requires proper comprehensive assessment. In this paper, we propose a method to measure drawing skill by by matching the hand-drawn image with the original template. Existing techniques often involve complex processes. However, advancements in computer vision allow us to train computers to perform these comparisons at a human-like level, thereby resolving the tedious and overwhelming traditional process. Using computer vision applications, determining image similarity involves identifying the level of similarities in an image with a reference image. We have implemented and analyzed the SIFT feature and Siamese network to measure image similarity. Our results indicate that it is feasible to assess art skill levels. Through feature analysis, we found that SIFT-based key point matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills.

AI.tldr: A computer-vision framework using SIFT keypoints and a Siamese network to quantify drawing skill by comparing student drawings to reference templates; SIFT-based matching proved more effective.
AI.motivation: To provide objective, scalable measurement of drawing skill and reduce manual grading burden by leveraging computer-vision advances.
AI.method: Two approaches are implemented and analyzed: (1) SIFT-based keypoint matching between a hand-drawn image and its template, and (2) a Siamese network trained to assess image similarity to measure drawing quality.
AI.result: The study demonstrates feasibility of rating art skill levels via image similarity, with SIFT-based keypoint matching showing superior effectiveness in detecting drawing skills compared to the Siamese approach.
AI.conclusion: SIFT-based keypoint matching provides a robust indicator of drawing skill, suggesting computer-vision-based assessment can augment or replace tedious manual evaluation.

title: Low-Energy Reduced RISC-V Instruction Subset Processor for Tsetlin Machine Inference at the Edge
comment: 6 pages, 6 Figures, Accepted in IEEE ISVLSI Conference 2026
summary: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a logic-based machine learning approach that relies on simple bitwise operations and finite-state automata, which makes it attractive for edge AI deployments. Recent work has focused on co-processor and accelerator designs based on Tsetlin Machines (TMs). Although these designs achieve high performance, they typically depend on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. In this work, we present a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture and design flow tailored for TM inference. Leveraging the modular structure of RISC-V, we design a reduced instruction subset processor that retains programmability while targeting improved performance and lower energy consumption for TM workloads. Instruction profiling is employed to guide instruction reduction, followed by datapath and control path simplifications tailored to TM inference. Both the baseline RV32IM core and the proposed reduced core are evaluated across multiple datasets and compared with Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), which serve as a hardware-efficient baseline due to their reliance on bitwise operations during inference. Results show that TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 compared to 60.0% for BNN) while reducing execution time by up to 98% across multiple datasets. Furthermore, the proposed design achieves an average $29.7\times$ reduction in energy consumption, demonstrating its effectiveness for programmable and efficient edge AI systems.

AI.tldr: A domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor tailored for Tsetlin Machine inference, delivering large energy savings and speedups with competitive accuracy versus BNN baselines.
AI.motivation: Edge AI demands energy-efficient, programmable accelerators for TM inference. Existing co-processor solutions are tightly coupled and hard to program; a modular, domain-specific RISC-V core offers better flexibility and performance.
AI.method: Design a reduced-instruction-set RV32IM-like core optimized for TM inference using instruction profiling to guide reductions, followed by datapath and control-path simplifications specific to TM workloads. Evaluate both baseline RV32IM and the reduced core on multiple datasets and compare against BNNs as a hardware-efficient baseline.
AI.result: The TM-focused processor achieves high accuracy (up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2, outperforming BNN's 60.0%), reduces execution time by up to 98%, and lowers energy consumption by an average of 29.7x across datasets.
AI.conclusion: A domain-specific, programmable RISC-V TM inference architecture provides substantial energy efficiency and speed advantages while maintaining or improving accuracy, making it well-suited for edge AI deployments.

title: DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs
summary: Neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog combine neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is associational. Counterfactual reasoning additionally requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence. We introduce DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs. Using neural materialization, we reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs), and compute counterfactuals by weighted model counting (WMC) over a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The standard quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities and explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Experiments on MPI3D confirm the transformation against a DeepTwin construction against 12,000 queries, as predicted and a 2.14$\times$ inference speedup from avoiding the Twin's endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands. Code is at https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.

AI.tldr: Proposes DeepSWIP: a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog using neural materialization and SWIPs, enabling exact counterfactuals via weighted model counting; demonstrates speedups and calibration behavior in MPI3D and SUMO HOV; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Enable causal counterfactual reasoning in neurosymbolic systems by providing a formal causal semantics for DeepProbLog and efficient inference.
AI.method: Neural materialization of neural predicates into ProbLog choices, transform to a single-world model via SWIPs, compute counterfactuals with weighted model counting on the transformed program; analyze under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions; compare with DeepTwin; evaluate on MPI3D (12,000 queries) and SUMO HOV; examine calibration and AIPW estimators.
AI.result: DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized factorized conditional model under stated assumptions; yields a 2.14x inference speedup by avoiding the DeepTwin endogenous duplication; active neural probabilities identified and intervention cleaning/calibration-sensitive/rare-evidence instability explained; AIPW estimator reduces first-order bias for population mean and average treatment effect in the SUMO HOV setting.
AI.conclusion: DeepSWIP provides an efficient, exact counterfactual inference mechanism for neurosymbolic systems like DeepProbLog, with practical benefits in speed and bias mitigation; code released.

title: DeepForestVisionV2: Ecology-Driven Taxonomy Expansion for Camera-Trap Monitoring in African Tropical Forests
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026 - Computer Vision for Biodiversity Monitoring and Conservation Workshop
summary: Camera-trap monitoring in African tropical forests increasingly extends beyond closed-canopy interiors to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges. Among available open tools for African forest camera-trap classification, DeepForestVision is the only one providing a matched offline workflow for both photographs and videos, and previous work showed that it outperformed other available baselines on a comparable benchmark. However, it was designed for closed-canopy, ground-level forest interiors and uses a 35-class prediction space that becomes too coarse when deployments encounter arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, or human-associated confounders such as livestock. We present DeepForestVisionV2, an ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal classes plus human, vehicle, and blank) designed to address three recurrent deployment gradients: vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces. DeepForestVisionV2 retains the same offline workflow and is trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Evaluation combines a cross-country cropped-photo validation set, used to assess robustness across sites and camera-trap settings, with three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning the targeted gradients. On the validation set, DeepForestVisionV2 reaches 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, and 0.81 balanced accuracy. On the deployment benchmarks, it preserves or improves baseline accuracy despite its harder classification task, while increasing the number of identified taxa from 22 to 29 in forest-interior videos and from 4 to 9 at riverbanks. In the park-edge use case, it raises accuracy from 0.62 to 0.86 and reduces false alarms from 11 to 0. These results show that DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

AI.tldr: DeepForestVisionV2 expands the original model from 35 to 64 classes to cover arboreal and anthropogenic contexts in African tropical forests, maintaining an offline workflow and improving cross-site robustness and taxa identification across gradients (vertical stratification, openness, anthropogenic interfaces).
AI.motivation: Open, scalable computer-vision tools for camera-trap data in African tropical forests often misclassify arboreal and non-ground taxa due to a coarse 35-class space and closed-canopy assumptions; there is a need for a robust, terrain- and context-aware classifier.
AI.method: Ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 classes (61 animals + human, vehicle, blank); retains offline workflow; trained on 1.54M photos and 243k videos from multi-country African forests; evaluation uses a cross-country cropped-photo validation set plus three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces.
AI.result: Validation: 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, 0.81 balanced accuracy. Deployment benchmarks: taxa identified increase from 22 to 29 in forest interiors and 4 to 9 at riverbanks; park-edge accuracy rises from 0.62 to 0.86 and false alarms drop from 11 to 0.
AI.conclusion: DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility and robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings while expanding taxonomic coverage and maintaining an offline workflow.

title: Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing
summary: Reservoir computing offers a lightweight framework for forecasting dynamical systems but may struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity. Conventional reservoir computing recurrently uses trainable reservoirs with hyperparameter sensitivity, while the next-generation reservoir computing removes recurrence at the cost of rapidly growing feature dimensions. Here, we develop Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), which replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. We rigorously show that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs), preserving the potential expressive capacity of KANs while admitting efficient closed-form training of reservoir computing. At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations. It can also be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation. This work thus establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

AI.tldr: KARC substitutes recurrent reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions per the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, enabling efficient closed-form training while retaining the expressive power of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs); it outperforms traditional reservoir computing at comparable cost and can integrate with diffusion models for text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: Address the limited long-range dependency capture and hyperparameter sensitivity of conventional reservoir computing by replacing recurrent reservoirs with scalable, interpretable basis-function expansions that preserve expressive capacity.
AI.method: Introduce Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC) by replacing reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. Provide rigorous analysis showing KARC is a lightweight instantiation of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) and enables efficient closed-form training. Demonstrate performance at comparable cost and apply to challenging benchmarks (e.g., PDEs), and show integration with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation.
AI.result: KARC achieves superior performance relative to conventional reservoir computing on demanding benchmarks such as partial differential equations, at similar computational cost. It also demonstrates compatibility with diffusion-based generative models for text-to-image tasks.
AI.conclusion: KARC establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, delivering efficient, high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting and expanding RC's applicability to scalable, high-capacity representations.

title: LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents
summary: Policy-adherent tool-calling agents in customer-service domains must maintain task states across turns while calling tools and obeying domain policies. Task states consist of relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed through user interaction and tool calls. In standard agents, task states are not represented separately. Observations, tool returns, and policy instructions are placed in the prompt, leaving agents to reconstruct the relevant states from the prompt each time they decide what to do next. This design makes state management implicit, creating two common failure modes. An agent may retrieve the right facts but later ground its decision in stale, missing, or incorrect information; and a syntactically valid tool call may still violate a domain policy that depends on the current task state. We introduce \textsc{LedgerAgent}, an inference-time method for tool-calling agents that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger and renders the states into the prompt. The ledger is also used to check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls are executed, blocking policy violations. Across four customer-service domains and a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, \textsc{LedgerAgent} improves average pass\textasciicircum{}k over a standard prompt-based tool-calling approach, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

AI.tldr: LedgerAgent maintains an explicit, inferred task-state ledger to ensure state consistency and policy compliance in tool-calling agents; it improves pass@k across domains, especially with stricter consistency.
AI.motivation: Standard prompt-based tool-calling embeds observations, tool outputs, and policies in the prompt, causing implicit state and risks of using stale or policy-violating information.
AI.method: Introduce an inference-time ledger that stores observed task states, renders them into prompts, and checks state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls, blocking violations.
AI.result: Across four customer-service domains and mixed model weights, LedgerAgent improves average pass@k over standard prompt-based tool-calling, with larger gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state ledger improves robustness and policy adherence in tool-calling agents; beneficial across domains and model types.

title: The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups
summary: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery. The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: A first attention mechanism where tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements; attention scores are a closed-form algebra-norm of the relative pose, enabling affine full-frame group coverage without representation-theoretic machinery; demonstrated on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2) with strong data-efficiency and invariance.
AI.motivation: Overcome reliance on vector-token encodings and heavy irrep-based or learned-kernel attention by exploiting intrinsic geometry of Lie groups; achieve invariance/equivariance for a broad class of matrix Lie groups, including non-compact affine groups with scale and shear.
AI.method: Represent tokens as elements g_i in a matrix Lie group G. Compute pairwise scores s_{ij} = -||log(g_i^{-1} g_j)||^2/τ using a chosen logarithm chart; no external action ρ(g); diagonal G-action ensures equivariance; cocycle condition holds automatically; suitable for SE(2), SO(3), Aff(2). Three sequence-completion experiments compare to a learned MLP kernel.
AI.result: The closed-form score matches the learned kernel on invariants and, in SE(2), outperforms it, while using 50–80× fewer score parameters. A vector-token baseline breaks invariance by 5–12 orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Lie-Algebra Attention provides a principled, invariant, and parameter-efficient attention mechanism for matrix Lie groups, extending attention beyond traditional irreps-based or learned-kernel approaches to include full affine transformations.

title: Cinematic Compositing Using Character-Environment-Harmonized Video Generation Models
summary: Cinematic compositing aims to integrate green-screen characters into novel environments while maintaining physical and photometric realism. Previous methods often fail to capture the complex bidirectional interactions between characters and their surroundings, which we characterize as Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization. To address this, we propose an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions, specifically handling the challenges of interactive props. Our approach introduces a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to ensure physically consistent interactions among the character, props, and environment. We further develop an efficient prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering. Finally, a reference-conditioned mechanism enables controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

AI.tldr: Introduces a diffusion-based, end-to-end framework for cinematic compositing that jointly models Character-to-Environment and Environment-to-Character interactions, with tri-mask RGB-D denoising, a data-curation pipeline for relighting pairs, and reference-conditioned controllable environment synthesis and prop replacement, achieving state-of-the-art results in dynamic video compositing.
AI.motivation: Current cinematic compositing methods fail to capture bidirectional C2E and E2C interactions, especially with interactive props, leading to perceptual inconsistencies in lighting, shadows, and material cues. There is a need for a physically grounded, controllable approach that can relight and retexture scenes without costly rendering.
AI.method: Proposes an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions. Key components: tri-mask-guided architecture that segments character, props, and environment; RGB-D joint denoising for consistent geometry and lighting; a prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering; a reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements over existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing, validating realism of interaction and lighting harmonization.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively captures bidirectional interactions and supports controllable environment editing and prop substitution, enabling more realistic and flexible cinematic compositing.

title: Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR): Function-Preserving SVD Compression for LLMs
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Resource-Adaptive Foundation Model Inference (AdaptFM), Seoul, South Korea (non-archival)
summary: We present Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR), an SVD-based LLM compression framework that guides each weight matrix's low-rank approximation with a backward-signal influence metric. Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that integrates influence element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. AIR is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods: alone it exceeds ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further. AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches its quality with ~90% less calibration data, and turns parameter savings into FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.

AI.tldr: AIR is an SVD-based LLM compression framework that uses a backward-signal influence metric to guide low-rank weight approximations via a single closed-form ALS sweep; it is layer-local, orthogonal to end-to-end methods, and can surpass ACIP and LoRA+AIR yields stronger gains—achieving notable perplexity improvements and compute/memory/latency savings at substantial parameter reductions.
AI.motivation: Reduce LLM size and compute while maintaining or improving perplexity, addressing limitations of prior SVD-based approaches that do not account for backward influence or calibration data efficiency.
AI.method: Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR performs a single closed-form alternating least squares sweep that integrates an influence term element-wise with a monotone-descent guarantee. The method is layer-local and can be composed orthogonally with end-to-end methods like LoRA; AIR alone outperforms ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further.
AI.result: AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by more than 18% at ≤60% parameter retention, matches SVD-LLM(W) quality with ~90% less calibration data, and yields gains in FLOPs, peak memory, and per-token latency.
AI.conclusion: AIR provides an effective, calibration-data-efficient, and architecture-friendly low-rank compression framework for LLMs that can operate standalone or with LoRA, delivering meaningful perplexity, data-efficiency, and compute/memory performance gains.

title: How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech
summary: Style-captioned text-to-speech systems use natural language to control voice characteristics, but how individual words influence acoustic output remains unclear. Understanding this is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive TTS. We propose cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to the speech domain for the first time, and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Our method extracts per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps. We analyze 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations comprising 120 style captions conditioning the generation of 30 text transcripts each, revealing how caption tokens shape waveforms. Results show: (1) style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens, confirming global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) attention entropy reaches its minimum at layer 17, co-occurring with the style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage. This is the first study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models

AI.tldr: Cross-attention attribution is applied to speech diffusion models to understand how style-caption tokens control acoustic output, using a DAAM-based method adapted to CapSpeech-TTS to produce per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps, analyzing 3,600 style-caption/text combinations. Key findings: style tokens yield global conditioning, correlate with F0/energy, peak early/deep layers, and minimal attention entropy at layer 17, marking maximal style selectivity. First study of natural-language influence on cross-attention in speech diffusion models.
AI.motivation: To dissect how individual words in style captions influence the acoustic output of style-conditioned TTS systems, enabling better diagnosis of failure modes and finer controllability in expressive TTS.
AI.method: Adapt the DAAM cross-attention attribution framework to the speech diffusion domain. Generate per-token attribution heatmaps across 25 transformer layers and 24 ODE steps in CapSpeech-TTS. Evaluate 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) pairs (120 captions × 30 transcripts) to see how caption tokens shape waveforms.
AI.result: Per-token heatmaps reveal that style tokens contribute to waveform shaping with lower temporal variance than content tokens, indicating global conditioning. Style attention correlates with fundamental frequency (F0) and energy. Conditioning effects peak in early steps and deep layers. Attention entropy reaches a minimum at layer 17, concurrent with the peak of style importance, suggesting maximal network selectivity at this stage.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a first empirical insight into how natural language modulates cross-attention in speech diffusion models, enabling better interpretation and potential improvements in controllability and debugging of style-conditioned TTS.

title: BAFIS: Dataset + Framework to assess occupational Bias and Human Preference in modern Text-to-image Models
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision, WACV 2026
summary: Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to improve productivity and transform the production of creative content. However, existing research indicates that image generation models are significantly influenced by biases. This work investigates the inherent biases and language-induced biases present in text-to-image models within the context of occupation-related image generation, complementing established metrics with human preference feedback. We present a comprehensive evaluation of five current text-to-image models: Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, and FLUX.1-dev , focusing on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. To facilitate this evaluation, we developed the "Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis" (BAFIS), a platform designed to collect human feedback on bias in generated images. Furthermore, we created a dataset comprising 21,140 synthetic images generated using multilingual prompts, which serves as a basis for our analysis. We further place our results within a broader social context by comparing them to official statistics from the German Federal Employment Agency. Our findings reveal systematic biases in text-to-image models, with established evaluation metrics in partial correlation with subjective user ratings. Thus, our research emphasizes the need for including human preferences to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models.

AI.tldr: Text-to-image models exhibit systematic gender and ethnicity biases in occupation-related image generation. The study supplements automatic metrics with human feedback via BAFIS and a multilingual synthetic-image dataset (21,140 images) to evaluate five models (Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, FLUX.1-dev). Results show existing metrics only partially align with human judgments; authors advocate incorporating human preferences to build fairer, more inclusive models and contextualize findings with German employment statistics.
AI.motivation: To uncover and quantify biases in text-to-image generation, particularly gender and ethnicity biases in occupation visuals, and to improve fairness by integrating human feedback and social context.
AI.method: Evaluate five leading T2I models using a dedicated human-feedback platform (BAFIS) to assess bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. Generate a large multilingual synthetic image dataset (21,140 images) and analyze results against official German employment statistics.
AI.result: Identified systematic biases in T2I models. Established metrics show partial correlation with subjective human ratings. A new human-feedback workflow (BAFIS) and a multilingual dataset were created to support bias analysis and fairer model development.
AI.conclusion: Human preferences are essential to developing fairer, more inclusive text‑to‑image models; incorporate human feedback and social-context benchmarks alongside automated metrics to mitigate biases.

title: Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Work in progress; we will continuously update the codebase and arXiv version
summary: This work presents a general framework for training large language models (LLMs) to "Connect the Dots" (CoD), a meta-capability required by long-lifecycle agents: as an LLM-based AI agent gets deployed in an environment, it solves a long sequence of tasks while continuously exploring the environment, learning from its own experiences, and iteratively self-updating its context about the environment, thereby achieving progressively better performance on future tasks conditioned on the updated context. Major components of the CoD framework include: (1) algorithm design and infrastructure for end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) tasks and environments for incentivizing and eliciting the targeted meta-capability in LLMs during training, as well as for faithfully measuring progress during evaluation. We present proof-of-concept implementations of the CoD framework, including a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment, as well as tasks and environments tailored to the targeted meta-capability (rather than domain-specific LLM capabilities or standard task-by-task RL). Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting, and demonstrate the potential for out-of-distribution generalization -- within the training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings -- of the elicited meta-capability. Our investigation of CoD connects several lines of prior works, and opens up new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents. To facilitate further research and applications, we release our implementations at \url{https://github.com/agentscope-ai/Trinity-RFT/tree/research/cod/examples/research_cod}.

AI.tldr: A general framework (CoD) to train LLM-based agents to connect long-horizon experiences by iterative solve-task and context-update cycles via end-to-end RL; shows empirical validation and generalization potential, with open-source code.
AI.motivation: Enable a meta-capability for long-horizon, continual learning in deployed LLM agents by having them learn from ongoing interaction with their environment and updating their internal context over time.
AI.method: End-to-end reinforcement learning with long rollout sequences that interleave solve-task and update-context episodes; a GRPO-style credit assignment algorithm; development of tasks/environments designed to elicit the targeted meta-capability; proof-of-concept implementations; evaluation across domains and Ralph-loop settings; release of code (Trinity-RFT repo).
AI.result: Empirical results validate the feasibility of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting and indicate potential for out-of-distribution generalization within training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings.
AI.conclusion: Connects prior work on long-horizon AI and continual learning, and opens new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents; provides implementations to facilitate further research and applications.

title: Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift
summary: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: MoE calibration under distribution shift: expert calibration suffices for hard routing but not soft routing; introduce adversarial reweighting to improve calibration of routed aggregates, yielding better accuracy-calibration tradeoffs across settings.
AI.motivation: Calibration of predictive uncertainty is crucial for trust. While calibrating individual predictors helps ensembles, the interaction between routing in mixture-of-experts and expert-level calibration under distribution shift is not well understood.
AI.method: The authors perform a theoretical analysis distinguishing hard- versus soft-routed MoEs, showing expert calibration suffices for overall calibration in hard routing but not in soft routing. They introduce adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and evaluate across model families, tasks, and distribution shifts.
AI.result: For hard-routed MoEs, expert calibration suffices to calibrate the overall model under broad distribution shifts; for soft routing, it does not suffice. The proposed adversarial reweighting improves the accuracy–calibration tradeoff on average and on difficult subsets, across model classes, tasks, and shifts.
AI.conclusion: Calibration strategies must account for routing type in MoEs. Expert-level calibration alone is inadequate for soft routing; an adversarially weighted calibration objective yields more reliable calibration and improved accuracy under distribution shift across diverse settings.

title: SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

AI.tldr: SPOT-E: a plug-and-play test-time method that improves evidence readout in vision-language models by shaping answer uncertainty with low-entropy anchors and instance-wise spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight GRPO-based tuning; shows consistent gains across benchmarks and better robustness to visual corruptions.
AI.motivation: Evidence-intensive VLM tasks suffer from fragile readout of decisive visual cues; naive entropy minimization can conflate genuine evidence use with shortcut shortcuts. A mechanism is needed to verify and guide the model to use actual evidence.
AI.method: Introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective. SPOT-E produces question-conditioned spotlights at test time, optimized per instance with lightweight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). It is plug-and-play and does not require retraining the base VLM.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple benchmarks and VLM families shows consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware, test-time interventions can enhance evidence readout in VLMs with minimal retraining, offering a generalizable and model-agnostic approach to better grounding in vision-language tasks.

title: StreamKL: Fast and Memory-Efficient KL Divergence for Boosting Attention Distillation
summary: Attention distillation, which trains one attention distribution to match another by minimizing their Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, is widely used in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLM training. However, existing approaches materialize both attention distributions before computing the KL reduction, incurring $O(N_QN_K)$ memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths. We present StreamKL, the first fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that eliminates this quadratic materialization. StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for the coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. For the backward pass, StreamKL recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. We further design and implement efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations. Experiments show StreamKL delivers up to $43\times$ and $14\times$ speedups over baseline methods in the forward and backward passes, respectively. Most importantly, StreamKL reduces the extra HBM footprint of attention distillation from $O(N_QN_K)$ to $O(1)$, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: StreamKL is a fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that avoids quadratic materialization by streaming query-key tiles through on-chip memory, enabling a single-pass forward computation and tile-by-tile backward recomputation. It delivers large speedups and reduces memory footprint, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Attention distillation requires forming full query-key distributions to compute KL divergence, which scales quadratically with context length and incurs heavy memory/IO costs, hindering scalability for long contexts.
AI.method: Introduce an online, coupled KL reduction formulation. A single forward pass streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM for the KL computation. The backward pass recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. Implement specialized GPU kernels with optimizations.
AI.result: Up to 43x speedup in the forward pass and 14x in the backward pass over baselines. Reduces extra HBM footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1), enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: StreamKL provides a practical, scalable solution to attention KL distillation by eliminating quadratic intermediates and memory bottlenecks, expanding feasibility of long-context attention distillation on standard GPUs.

title: Human Universal Grasping
summary: Humans can grasp objects effortlessly, whereas multi-fingered robots are far from this level of generality. We argue that the most natural source of robot grasping data is from humans, who pick up thousands of objects every day. We present HUG, a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any user-specified object in a single RGB-D image captured from a stereo camera. Using smart glasses, we first collect 1M-HUGs, an egocentric dataset of human grasps spanning 1M frames (27.8 hrs) and 6,707 object instances across 41 buildings. Next, to model the distribution of natural human grasps, our novel flow-matching model fuses RGB and depth observations to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Predicted grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands, enabling zero-shot grasping in everyday scenes. To standardize evaluation, we build a new simulated benchmark, HUG-Bench, of 90 unseen objects from five geometric categories and various sizes, with metric-scale 3D meshes. We evaluate HUG in the real world on the 30-object test set of HUG-Bench across multiple stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments. HUG outperforms the state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on our challenging object set. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo are released on our website: https://grasping.io/

AI.tldr: HUG introduces a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps from a single RGB-D image, using a large egocentric dataset to enable zero-shot robot grasping; a new HUG-Bench benchmark standardizes evaluation and the approach achieves significant improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between human grasp versatility and robotic grasping by learning from rich human grasp data and enabling retargeting to various robot hands; provide a standardized evaluation framework for grasping.
AI.method: Collect 1M-HUGs: an egocentric RGB-D grasp dataset from smart glasses (1M frames, 6,707 objects, 41 buildings). Develop a flow-matching model that fuses RGB and depth to predict grasps parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Retarget grasps to robot hands for zero-shot grasping. Create HUG-Bench, a simulated 3D-mesh benchmark with 90 unseen objects across five geometric categories. Evaluate on real-world 30-object test set across multiple stereo cameras and robots.
AI.result: HUG improves over state-of-the-art grasping baselines by 23% and 34% on the challenging object set in HUG-Bench. Public release of code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and interactive demo for reproducibility and broader adoption.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that leveraging large-scale human grasp data with a flow-based grasp predictor enables robust, generalizable, zero-shot grasping across diverse objects and scenes, and provides a standardized benchmark to accelerate progress in the field.

title: Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification for Weakly Supervised Histopathology Segmentation
comment: Accepted to MICCAI 2026. This is the pre-review submitted version, not the camera-ready version. The final authenticated version will be available in the MICCAI 2026 proceedings
summary: Existing weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) methods in computational pathology rely on a multi-stage paradigm: class activation map (CAM) generation, offline pseudo-mask refinement, and fully supervised retraining. While established, this decoupled approach presents fundamental limitations. The multi-stage process not only incurs high computational training costs but also suffers from error propagation: local texture biases in shallow CNN layers generate false-positive artifacts that subsequent refinement steps often fail to correct. To address these persistent challenges through a simple yet highly effective approach, we propose the Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) framework. Rather than passively refining CAMs post-hoc, our method proactively purifies intermediate feature representations during the forward pass. We introduce a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that utilizes deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers. This mechanism generates high-fidelity activation maps directly within a single training loop. Experiments on the LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets demonstrate that SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods. Furthermore, SSHR reduces training duration by 2 to 5 times. This efficiency minimizes computational overhead and accelerates clinical translation for large-scale histopathology workflows. The code is available at: https://github.com/trongduc-nguyen/SSHR

AI.tldr: Proposes Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) to replace multi-stage WSSS in computational pathology by rectifying shallow features during forward pass via a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) using global context, yielding high-fidelity activation maps in one pass, with reduced training time and improved accuracy on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS; code released.
AI.motivation: Current WSSS in computational pathology rely on decoupled multi-stage pipelines (CAM generation, pseudo-mask refinement, and retraining). This causes high computational costs and error propagation from local texture biases in shallow layers, motivating a single-stage, more robust approach.
AI.method: Introduce SSHR with HFRM that leverages deep global semantic context to filter local anomalies in shallow layers during the forward pass. The approach purifies intermediate features in a single training loop, generating high-quality activation maps without a separate refinement stage.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets shows SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods and achieves 2–5x faster training times, indicating both improved performance and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SSHR provides an effective, efficient single-stage alternative to traditional multi-stage WSSS in histopathology, facilitating faster clinical translation for large-scale workflows; code is publicly available.

title: VIMPO: Value-Implicit Policy Optimization for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a central tool for improving the reasoning ability of large language models, but current methods face a trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment. Group-relative methods such as GRPO avoid training a critic, but typically assign a trajectory-level advantage to every token. Actor-critic methods provide denser learning signals, but require a learned value function with its own training instability. We introduce VIMPO, a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized reinforcement learning. For autoregressive generation, the resulting value recurrence can be written in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This gives a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without training a critic. The same derivation also yields a critic-free actor advantage, allowing VIMPO to separate reward incorporation through the value loss from policy improvement through a PPO-style actor update. On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with especially larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO, suggesting that policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training.

AI.tldr: VIMPO is a critic-free policy optimization method deriving a policy-implied value function from KL-regularized RL, enabling a simple value loss and a critic-free actor advantage. It improves over GRPO on math benchmarks and remains robust to noisy rewards.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off between simple, critic-free training (group-relative methods) and dense credit assignment (actor-critic methods) by deriving a value function directly from optimality conditions, eliminating the need to train a separate critic while providing better credit assignment in verifiable-reward RL scenarios.
AI.method: Derive a value recurrence for autoregressive generation from KL-regularized RL optimality. Express the value function in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and terminal conditions. Form a simple value loss without a learned critic. Also derive a critic-free actor advantage to enable a PPO-style update, separating reward incorporation from policy improvement.
AI.result: On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO outperforms GRPO on MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with larger gains on competition-style tasks. It remains robust under noisy rewards, indicating finer credit assignment without sacrificing the simplicity of critic-free training.
AI.conclusion: Policy-implied value optimization via KL-regularized RL provides effective credit assignment in verifiable-reward settings while avoiding critic training. VIMPO combines a simple value loss with a PPO-style actor update, yielding practical gains over critic-free and critic-based baselines.

title: U$^2$Mamba: A Two-level Nested U-structure Mamba for Salient Object Detection
summary: Mamba-based models have emerged as a promising alternative for salient object detection (SOD), offering significant advantages in modeling long sequences. However, existing models often fail to explore contextual information and the depth of the entire architecture. This paper introduces U$^2$Mamba, a powerful and innovative U-structured network for salient object detection. We propose multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) that enhance the model depth to improve local feature extraction capabilities. Our newly developed nested U-structure, incorporating MMUBs, enables the network to integrate various receptive fields from shallow and deep layers, thereby collecting richer contextual information and longer-range data without being constrained by resolution. Instead of using the traditional deep supervision scheme and top-level supervised training, we propose a hierarchical training supervision method where the loss is computed at each level during the training process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that U$^2$Mamba achieves highly competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods. The source code is available at \url{https://github.com/JL021/U2Mamba}.

AI.tldr: Introduces U2Mamba, a U-structured SOD network using multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) within a nested U-structure, with hierarchical level-wise supervision to enhance context aggregation and depth, achieving competitive results without traditional deep supervision.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations in modeling long-range dependencies and contextual information in SOD by increasing network depth and integrating multi-scale receptive fields across shallow and deep layers, without being constrained by resolution.
AI.method: Propose MMUBs as multiscale Mamba U-blocks and nest them into a nested U-structure. Enable integration of various receptive fields from shallow and deep layers. Replace traditional deep supervision with a hierarchical training scheme that computes loss at multiple levels during training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that U2Mamba achieves highly competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods for SOD. Code is available at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: The combination of MMUBs, nested U-structure, and hierarchical supervision enhances contextual information capture and long-range dependencies in SOD, offering a strong competitive alternative to existing methods.

title: Self-Adaptive Scale Handling for Forecasting Time Series with Scale Heterogeneity
comment: This is the full version of the paper accepted by the IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP). The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF
summary: Current time series forecasting (TSF) research predominantly focuses on scale-homogeneous data, where different time series share similar numerical magnitude ranges. However, in real-world industrial scenarios such as financial product sales, different time series often differ by orders of magnitude (scale heterogeneity). Since these series share similar temporal patterns, joint modeling is desirable for better data utilization, yet existing scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling). This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module that learns adaptive scale factors tailored to each input, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC), which calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor, avoiding over-calibration. Experiments on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay show that AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance. The code and dataset are available at the link https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF.

AI.tldr: Introduces a plug-in self-adaptive scale-handling (AS) module for scale-heterogeneous time series forecasting that calibrates and selectively applies scale factors to preserve semantics and reduce errors, improving performance across models on real-world fund sales data.
AI.motivation: Address scale heterogeneity across time series in industrial TSF where different series span different magnitude ranges, while leveraging shared temporal patterns. Existing global/window scaling can harm semantic discriminability or introduce inverse-scaling errors.
AI.method: AS comprises two components: Scale Calibrating (SC) which learns to calibrate prior mean scale factors via neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS) which decides whether calibration should be applied or the original factor retained, mitigating over-calibration. It is designed as a plug-in module for popular TSF models and trained end-to-end with them.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world fund sales datasets (Ant Fortune and Alipay) shows consistent performance gains when AS is integrated into various TSF models. The work also provides code and data availability, indicating good reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: AS provides an effective, plug-in approach to handle scale heterogeneity in TSF by adaptively calibrating and selecting scale factors, preserving semantic discriminability, reducing inverse-scaling errors, and improving forecasting accuracy across models on real industrial data.

title: CMDS-AD: Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Decoupling for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: Few-shot anomaly detection remains challenging due to limited training data. Multi-modal anomaly detection (MAD) offers a viable solution, leveraging 3D geometric cues to enrich 2D RGB representations and compensate for this scarcity. However, existing MAD methods apply spatially uniform feature processing, conflating stable macroscopic structures with high-frequency localized defect signals, exacerbating cross-modal misalignment and inflating false-positive rates. To overcome this, we present CMDS-AD, a Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Anomaly Detection framework. A LoRA-guided diffusion model generates diverse RGB samples to mitigate extreme data scarcity. For 3D normal augmentation, we employ a pre-trained diffusion model as a normal estimator. Crucially, this estimator inherently acts as a non-linear low-pass filter, directly extracting low-frequency normal representations from RGB inputs. This establishes an auxiliary estimated stream of purely low-frequency information, anchoring robust structural templates and assisting the uncompressed real stream, containing coupled high- and low-frequency components, to precisely isolate micro-defects. A Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper adaptively aligns cross-modal semantics, while a multiplicative scoring mechanism filters modality-specific noise. Under the extreme 1-shot setting, CMDS-AD achieves absolute performance gains of 5.7% (I-AUROC) and 2.0% (AUPRO) on MVTec 3D-AD, alongside 7.7% and 5.6% improvements on EyeCandies, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: CMDS-AD is a cross-modal dual-stream framework for 1-shot anomaly detection that uses diffusion-based RGB augmentation and a low-frequency normal-estimation stream to align modalities and isolate micro-defects; it achieves state-of-the-art results on MVTec 3D-AD and EyeCandies.
AI.motivation: Few-shot anomaly detection suffers from data scarcity and high false positives. Multi-modal anomaly detection using 3D cues helps, but existing methods apply spatially uniform feature processing, causing cross-modal misalignment and conflating macroscopic structures with high-frequency defect signals. There is a need for adaptive cross-modal alignment and robust structural templates.
AI.method: CMDS-AD comprises two streams: a real RGB stream that carries coupled high- and low-frequency information and an auxiliary low-frequency stream produced by a pre-trained diffusion model acting as a normal estimator (non-linear low-pass filter). Additional components include a LoRA-guided diffusion model to generate diverse RGB samples, a Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper for adaptive cross-modal semantic alignment, and a multiplicative scoring mechanism to filter modality-specific noise.
AI.result: In the 1-shot setting, CMDS-AD yields absolute performance gains of 5.7 percentage points in I-AUROC and 2.0 percentage points in AUPRO on MVTec 3D-AD, and gains of 7.7 percentage points and 5.6 percentage points on EyeCandies, achieving new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that separating a low-frequency normal stream and aligning cross-modal semantics with a dual-stream architecture effectively mitigates cross-modal misalignment and improves micro-defect isolation under extreme data scarcity, establishing SOTA on the evaluated benchmarks.

title: Hierarchical Control in Multi-Agent Games: LLM-based Planning and RL Execution
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong performance in sequential decision-making, yet scaling to complex multi-agent environments remains challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the difficulty of learning coordinated strategies. We propose a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model (LLM) acts as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. We evaluate this hybrid system in a competitive 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and \emph{``Flat''} RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition) baselines. The LLM+RL system achieves task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4\% vs 51.5\% win rate, $p=0.103$) while both significantly outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study ($n=15$) reveals that 60\% of participants perceive LLM+RL agents as the most human-like ($p=0.027$), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, achieving competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

AI.tldr: An LLM-guided centralized planner selects among pretrained RL skills to coordinate a team of agents, combining high-level reasoning with low-level RL execution, achieving competitive performance and higher perceived believability versus baselines in a 2v2 task.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in scaling RL to complex multi-agent environments—sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the need for coordinated strategies—by leveraging pretrained LLMs to orchestrate specialized RL skills rather than end-to-end training or hand-crafted rules.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model serves as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. Evaluation in a 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior trees (BT) and flat RL baselines; includes a user study assessing perceived believability.
AI.result: The LLM+RL system achieves 46.4% win rate versus BT (51.5%, p=0.103), statistically equivalent to BT and both outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study (n=15) shows 60% of participants perceived the LLM+RL agents as most human-like (p=0.027). These results indicate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, enabling competitive coordination and heightened perceived believability without manual rule engineering.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained LLMs can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills to achieve competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

title: CUPID: Reconstructing UV Texture Maps for Interpretable Person-of-Interest Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfakes targeting a high-profile individual, known as Person-of-Interest (POI), are a threat to modern democracies and societies.
