Current POI deepfake detection methods still struggle to combine robustness to post-processing, efficiency and interpretability, focal aspects of modern deepfake detectors. In this paper we propose CUPID, a POI video deepfake detector that combines UV texture maps, a facial appearance representation derived from 3D face reconstructions, with the representation learning capabilities of the Masked Autoencoder (MAE). Our method does not require any deepfake videos in its training phase. Moreover, it does not even require to include a specific POI in the training set: the combination of UV texture maps extracted from real video frames and the MAE context-guided reconstruction yields a latent space that captures rich and discriminative facial features also for identities unseen during training. In the testing phase, the embeddings extracted from a query video depicting the POI can be matched against pristine reference videos to assess the video authenticity. Furthermore, operating in the UV space naturally provides an additional layer of interpretability. Specifically, we can extract decoded residual maps that highlight which facial regions of a test video deviate most from the identity representation of the corresponding POI. Experiments on four deepfake datasets show that CUPID outperforms current state of the art on most datasets and achieves the best overall robustness against strong downscaling and compression, providing also substantially faster inference. Our experimental code will be released at https://github.com/polimi-ispl/CUPID.

AI.tldr: CUPID uses UV texture maps and MAE-based reconstruction to detect POI deepfakes without training on deepfakes or specific POIs, generalizing to unseen identities, offering interpretability via residual maps and robust, fast inference.
AI.motivation: Address weaknesses in existing POI deepfake detectors: robustness to post-processing, efficiency, and interpretability; enable training-free generalization to unseen POIs.
AI.method: Fuse UV texture maps from real frames with a context-guided Masked Autoencoder (MAE) representation; train without deepfake videos and without including the POI in training; at test time, embed query POI video and compare to pristine references; UV space enables interpretability via decoded residual maps showing deviations from the identity representation.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art on most of four deepfake datasets, with best robustness to strong downscaling/compression and substantially faster inference; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: CUPID provides robust, identity-generalizable POI deepfake detection with interpretable UV-space residuals and efficient inference, addressing key gaps in current detectors.

title: Adaptive Distance-Aware Trunk Deep Operator Learning for Long-Span Roadway Bridges
summary: Long-span roadway bridges exhibit highly localized structural responses under vehicular loading, making repeated FE analysis computationally expensive for applications such as influence surface generation and structural digital twins. Existing SciML approaches struggle to accurately capture these localized responses. To address this challenge, this study proposes an adaptive-trunk DeepONet for localized structural response prediction in large-scale bridge systems. The framework dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain using a KNN strategy, allowing the network to focus on structural influence zones. The trunk network is further enhanced using distance-aware features that encode the geometric relationship between the load and structural nodes. A physics-based full-field reconstruction is incorporated through a stiffness-informed Schur complement formulation, enabling predictions at adaptive nodes to be extended to the entire structural domain. To enable scalable training, response data are generated using a reduced-order equivalent shell model that preserves the dominant global behavior while significantly reducing computational cost. The proposed framework is validated on both a benchmark bridge model and the real-world Mussafah Bridge. Results show that the method achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors below 5%, while reducing the total response evaluation time (including full-field reconstruction) by approximately 60x; excluding the post-processing reconstruction step, the AD-DeepONet inference is up to four orders of magnitude faster than FEM. In addition, the framework enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading configurations, demonstrating strong potential for large-scale bridge analysis and digital twin applications.

AI.tldr: An adaptive-trunk DeepONet with a KNN-derived, load-dependent learning domain and distance-aware features, combined with a stiffness-informed Schur complement for full-field reconstruction, and trained on a reduced-order shell model to predict localized responses in large-scale bridge systems with FEM-level accuracy and massive computational speedups.
AI.motivation: Long-span bridges exhibit highly localized responses under vehicular loading, making repeated FE analyses for influence surfaces and digital twins computationally prohibitive. Existing SciML approaches struggle to capture these localizations efficiently.
AI.method: Adaptive-trunk DeepONet architecture that (i) constructs a load-dependent learning domain via a KNN strategy to focus on influence zones, (ii) augments the trunk with distance-aware features encoding load-node geometry, (iii) uses a stiffness-informed Schur complement for full-field reconstruction from adaptive-nodes, and (iv) employs a reduced-order equivalent shell model to generate training data while preserving global behavior.
AI.result: FEM-level accuracy with relative errors below 5%, total response evaluation time reduced by ~60x when including full-field reconstruction; excluding post-processing, AD-DeepONet inference is up to four orders of magnitude faster than FEM. Validated on a benchmark bridge model and the Mussafah Bridge; enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, accurate, and fast solution for large-scale bridge analysis and digital-twin applications, effectively capturing localized structural responses and enabling rapid, data-driven predictions for influence analysis.

title: GEN-Guard: Correcting Generalization Failures for Deployable Federated Surgical AI
summary: Federated Learning (FL) in surgical video AI enables collaborative model training without sharing sensitive data. However, standard evaluation practices - selecting the "best" global model based only on validation data from participating hospitals - can lead to suboptimal deployment choices. We identify this critical failure mode as performance leakage, where the selected model overfits internal federation data and fails to generalize to unseen institutions. We propose GEN-Guard, a practical post-hoc framework to detect and correct generalization failures in federated surgical AI. It integrates Generalization Detection via Client-Blocked Evaluation (CBE), which validates performance on isolated client distributions to prevent performance leakage, and Generalization Correction through Disagreement-Aware Distillation (DAD), which learns adaptive feature-level corrections for cross-institutional robustness. Both components operate after standard FL convergence while providing robust support for zero-shot adaptation to unseen environments. We first quantify the severity of performance leakage, observing Model Selection Failures (MSFs) exceeding 80% under standard evaluation. GEN-Guard is evaluated on two multi-center clinical challenges: surgical phase recognition in laparoscopic cholecystectomy and polyp segmentation in colonoscopy. Across both datasets, GEN-Guard consistently corrects these failures, improving in-federation F1 scores by up to 2 points, unseen-institution performance by up to 3 points, and worst-case institutional performance by 3-9 points. Performance leakage represents a systematic and previously under-recognized risk in federated surgical AI. GEN-Guard provides a practical solution for detecting and correcting such failures. By improving cross-institutional robustness and zero-shot generalization, it strengthens the reliability of FL for real-world surgical deployment.

AI.tldr: GEN-Guard is a post-hoc framework to detect and correct generalization failures in federated surgical AI, addressing performance leakage caused by model selection on federation data. It adds a Client-Blocked Evaluation (CBE) to validate across isolated client distributions and a Disagreement-Aware Distillation (DAD) for adaptive feature-level corrections, improving cross-institutional robustness and zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Standard FL evaluation tends to select the best-performing global model using validation data from participating hospitals, which can overfit to federation data and fail on unseen institutions (performance leakage). There is a need for post-hoc mechanisms to detect this leakage and restore generalization to unseen environments.
AI.method: Post-convergence: (1) Generalization Detection via Client-Blocked Evaluation (CBE) tests model performance on isolated client distributions to identify leakage; (2) Generalization Correction through Disagreement-Aware Distillation (DAD) learns adaptive feature-level corrections by leveraging disagreement signals to improve cross-institution robustness. Validation on two clinical tasks (surgical phase recognition in laparoscopic cholecystectomy; polyp segmentation in colonoscopy).
AI.result: GEN-Guard consistently mitigates performance leakage across tasks, improving in-federation F1 by up to 2 points, unseen-institution performance by up to 3 points, and worst-case institutional performance by 3–9 points. Model Selection Failures (MSFs) under standard evaluation exceed 80% in their setting.
AI.conclusion: Performance leakage is a substantial risk in federated surgical AI. GEN-Guard offers a practical, post-hoc solution to detect and correct such failures, enhancing cross-institutional robustness and zero-shot generalization for real-world deployment.

title: Exploring the potential of AlphaEarth and TESSERA embeddings for Fine-scale Local Climate Zone Mapping: A case study across five cities in Switzerland
summary: Understanding urban spatial morphology is critical for climate modeling, risk assessment, and sustainable urban design, and Local Climate Zone (LCZ) mapping provides the basic framework for this. However, many cities still use coarse ~100-m resolution LCZ records, which are unsuitable for fine-scale urban research. In this study, precomputed embeddings from TESSERA (Feng et al., 2025) and AlphaEarth (Brown et al., 2025) are compared to traditional Sentinel-1/2 (S1S2) composites in five Swiss cities to see if they can upscale coarse LCZ maps to 10-m resolution using an attention-based U-Net. Three experiments assess multi-city transferability, the impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness to year-to-year phenology changes. We find that all datasets achieve strong performance with test data Intersection-over-Union (IoU) ranging from 0.59-0.69 and 0.77-0.82 in the first two experiments. TESSERA consistently outperforms both S1S2 and AlphaEarth across both settings As expected, we find that the transfer of embedding-based models from one year to another remains an open challenge. Overall, however, our results demonstrate the promising potential of embeddings derived from EO foundation models to reduce time consuming preprocessing, respectively, manual feature engineering tasks and to guide a universal deep learning-based LCZ mapping workflow. When combined with a simple location-aware attention U-Net architecture, the embeddings enhance regional transferability and scalability, supporting the development of comprehensive and reproducible fine-scale LCZ maps for global urban climate applications Improving reference data quality remains the strongest lever for further accuracy gains.

AI.tldr: Embedding-based upscaling of LCZ maps to 10 m using attention U-Net with TESSERA, AlphaEarth, and S1S2; TESSERA performs best; year-to-year transfer remains challenging; high-quality reference data yields larger gains; promising for scalable, reproducible LCZ mapping.
AI.motivation: Enable fine-scale urban climate mapping by leveraging EO foundation-model embeddings to upscale coarse LCZ maps, reducing preprocessing and feature engineering while improving transferability.
AI.method: Take precomputed embeddings from TESSERA, AlphaEarth, and S1S2 composites as inputs to an attention-based U-Net to upscale 100 m LCZ maps to 10 m in five Swiss cities; evaluate with Intersection-over-Union (IoU) across three experiments: multi-city transferability, impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness; compare datasets.
AI.result: IoU ranges: 0.59-0.69 and 0.77-0.82 in the first two experiments; TESSERA consistently outperforms S1S2 and AlphaEarth across settings; year-to-year transfer remains an open challenge; embedding-based models enhance regional transferability and scalability; improving reference data quality is the strongest lever for accuracy gains.
AI.conclusion: EO foundation-model embeddings, when paired with a location-aware attention U-Net, show strong potential for universal, scalable, fine-scale LCZ mapping; focus should be on improving reference data quality; temporal generalization remains a bottleneck.

title: Through the PRISM: Preference Representation in Intermediate States of Video Diffusion Models
summary: Evaluating video generation with clean, pixel-based reward models disconnects evaluation from the noisy diffusion process and incurs massive VAE decoding costs. In this paper, we challenge this paradigm by asking a fundamental question: Can a powerful video generator inherently discriminate preferences directly from noisy latents? To answer this, we introduce \textbf{PRISM} (\textbf{P}reference \textbf{R}epresentation in \textbf{I}ntermediate \textbf{S}tates of Diffusion \textbf{M}odels). PRISM employs a lightweight Query-based Aggregation head with a frozen video diffusion backbone to decode preference signals from noisy latents. Surprisingly, PRISM not only achieves SOTA preference accuracy but also unlocks strong noise-robustness, which enables early-stage Best-of-$N$ sampling. This allows for filtering suboptimal candidates at the very beginning of denoising, drastically reducing computation while boosting video quality. We also reveal a strong positive correlation between a backbone's generative performance and its inherent evaluative power, enabling self-improving video backbones.

AI.tldr: PRISM decodes user preferences from noisy latent representations in a frozen video diffusion backbone via a lightweight query-based aggregation head, achieving state-of-the-art preference accuracy, noise robustness, and enabling early Best-of-N sampling to prune candidates and reduce compute; also reveals a link between a backbone's generative quality and evaluative power.
AI.motivation: To move beyond pixel-based reward evaluation, which is costly and tied to diffusion noise, by testing whether a diffusion backbone's latent space can inherently capture user preferences and enable self-improving backbones.
AI.method: We freeze a video diffusion backbone and attach a lightweight Query-based Aggregation head that decodes preference signals directly from noisy latents; we demonstrate robustness to noise and show that Best-of-N can be applied early during denoising, reducing computation.
AI.result: PRISM achieves state-of-the-art preference accuracy, exhibits strong noise robustness, enables early-stage Best-of-N sampling, reducing computation while improving video quality, and reveals a positive correlation between a backbone's generative performance and its evaluative power.
AI.conclusion: Backbone generative quality is predictive of its evaluative capability; PRISM enables latent-based preference evaluation and paves the way for self-improving video backbones.

title: Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis using a Multimodal Approach with 3D MRI and PET
comment: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine (BIBM)
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an irreversible neurodegenerative disorder and a leading cause of death worldwide. Early diagnosis plays an important part especially at the Mild Cognitive Impairment stage, where timely intervention can help slow its progression before it advances to AD. Neuroimaging data, like Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scans, can help detect brain changes early by providing structural and functional brain changes related to the disease. Yet, many multimodal models still fuse MRI and PET with static concatenation and apply identical computation to all subjects, which limits robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and can waste computation. To address these limitations, we present the first study of combining 3D convolutional feature extractors with three fusion strategies - concatenation, Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU), and gated self-attention - and a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier that performs input-adaptive routing, activating only the most informative experts per case. Finally, we utilize Grad-CAM to visualize disease-related regions, ensuring model interpretability. Experiments are performed across three binary classification tasks (NC vs. MCI, MCI vs. AD, and NC vs. AD). Results show that GMU achieves accuracies of 80.46 % (NC vs. MCI) and 95.47 % (NC vs. AD), while gated self-attention attains 82.08 % on MCI vs. AD. Ablations show that removing the MoE consistently degrades accuracy across all tasks. These findings underscore the value of input-adaptive, multimodal modeling for AD diagnosis by leveraging the complementary nature of MRI and PET.

AI.tldr: Proposes input-adaptive, multimodal fusion of MRI and PET for Alzheimer's disease using 3D CNN features, comparing concatenation, GMU, and gated self-attention, plus a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts classifier; demonstrates improved accuracy and interpretability with Grad-CAM.
AI.motivation: Address robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and computational waste in static, uniform multimodal fusion; exploit complementary information from MRI and PET for earlier and more reliable AD diagnosis.
AI.method: Use 3D convolutional feature extractors for MRI and PET; evaluate three fusion strategies (concatenation, GMU, gated self-attention) followed by a sparsely gated MoE classifier with input-adaptive routing; employ Grad-CAM for visualization to ensure interpretability.
AI.result: On three binary tasks (NC vs MCI, MCI vs AD, NC vs AD): GMU achieves 80.46% (NC vs MCI) and 95.47% (NC vs AD); gated self-attention achieves 82.08% (MCI vs AD). Ablations show that removing the MoE degrades accuracy across all tasks; Grad-CAM highlights disease-relevant brain regions.
AI.conclusion: Input-adaptive, multimodal modeling of MRI and PET improves AD diagnostic performance and interpretability; the MoE component is crucial for robust, task-specific routing across subjects.

title: Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration for Frozen Pose-Flow Video Anomaly Detection
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 7 tables. Code available at https://github.com/iNing10/RPC
summary: Pose-flow video anomaly detectors are attractive for one-class surveillance because they provide likelihood-based rankings for tracked skeleton windows. However, a single likelihood score may hide multimodal normal behavior and be sensitive to pose-observation noise. We study a frozen-detector setting in which the pose-flow backbone, cached skeleton tracks, and evaluation pipeline are fixed. Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC) is a post-hoc score calibration method for this setting. It adds a standardized nearest-prototype deviation in the frozen latent space to the standardized flow score, and uses keypoint confidence only to gate this added geometric evidence. Thus, RPC preserves the original density signal while correcting the ranking with empirical normal-mode structure under pose reliability. Across two frozen pose-flow backbones and four datasets, RPC improves frame-level AUROC in all eight backbone-dataset pairs, with gains ranging from 0.34 to 4.49 percentage points and averaging 2.03 points. Ablation and reliability analyses show that prototype deviation is the main corrective signal, while reliability gating is most useful when pose observations are less trustworthy. These results suggest that lightweight post-hoc calibration can strengthen cached pose-flow systems when retraining or reproducing the full pose pipeline is impractical.

AI.tldr: RPC: a post-hoc calibration for frozen pose-flow anomaly detectors using standardized prototype deviation in latent space, gated by keypoint reliability, improving frame AUROC without retraining.
AI.motivation: One-class surveillance with pose-flow detectors can misrank normal multimodal behavior and be sensitive to pose noise; a calibration method is needed when the pipeline is fixed or retraining is impractical.
AI.method: Introduce Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC). Compute standardized nearest-prototype deviation in the frozen latent space and add it to the standardized flow score. Gate this geometric evidence using keypoint confidence to avoid unreliable evidence.
AI.result: RPC improves frame-level AUROC across 2 backbones and 4 datasets (8 backbone-dataset pairs), with gains 0.34–4.49 percentage points, average 2.03.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, post-hoc calibration strengthens cached pose-flow systems when retraining the full pose pipeline is impractical; prototype deviation is the main corrective signal, while reliability gating is beneficial when pose observations are unreliable.

title: Comparative Study of Neural Surrogate Architectures for Autoregressive Prediction of Internal Battery States
summary: The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model resolves internal electrochemical states in lithium-ion batteries with high fidelity. However, the numerical solution of its governing equations is computationally prohibitive for real-time deployment, limiting scalability from individual cells to pack and fleet-scale applications. While machine learning surrogates can substantially reduce inference latency through GPU acceleration, most existing approaches learn solution approximations tied to specific operating conditions rather than learning generalizable state-evolution dynamics. This work presents a systematic comparison of four neural network architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) formulated as autoregressive state-transition operators that predict full DFN internal states across a wide range of operating conditions. To ensure a controlled architectural comparison, all models are trained under a unified framework using multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning, isolating the impact of spatial inductive bias. Results demonstrate that the U-Net's multi-scale feature hierarchy achieves a mean final-step nRMSE of 3% averaged across all internal state variables after 300-step autoregressive rollouts, while providing a 5.38x speed-up over the numerical solver. These findings highlight spatial inductive bias as a critical determinant of surrogate performance, advancing the development of surrogates for internal state observability for next-generation battery management systems and digital twins.

AI.tldr: U-Net with a multi-scale feature hierarchy serves as the most effective autoregressive surrogate for the DFN model, delivering ~3% final-step nRMSE after 300-step rollouts and 5.38x faster-than-solver speed, highlighting spatial inductive bias as key for generalizable state-evolution surrogates.
AI.motivation: The DFN model provides high-fidelity internal electrochemical states but is too computationally expensive for real-time deployment; there is a need for generalizable surrogates that capture state-evolution dynamics across diverse operating conditions; the study also aims to isolate architectural impact by controlling training setup.
AI.method: Four neural architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) are configured as autoregressive state-transition operators. Models are trained under a unified framework with multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning to compare spatial inductive biases, evaluating performance across a wide range of operating conditions.
AI.result: U-Net achieves a mean final-step normalized RMSE of ~3% across all internal state variables after 300-step rollouts, with a 5.38× speed-up relative to the DFN solver; other architectures (MLP, ResNet, FNO) show comparatively worse performance in this setup.
AI.conclusion: Spatial inductive bias, embodied by the U-Net’s multi-scale hierarchy, is a critical determinant of surrogate performance for DFN-state surrogacy. This advances practical surrogates for internal state observability in battery management systems and digital twins.

title: Geometry-Aware Superpixel Graph Transformer with Metadata for Skin Lesion Classification
summary: Automated skin cancer classification from dermoscopic images remains challenging due to heterogeneous lesion structure, strong intra-class variability, and subtle visual differences between benign and malignant cases. Existing CNN/ViT pipelines typically rely on global or patch-level features and often combine patient metadata via late fusion, which limits spatially grounded multimodal reasoning. We present a novel region-based graph learning framework that explicitly models lesions as graphs of spatially coherent superpixel regions represented as frozen CNN features. To capture fine-grained lesion arrangements, we encode inter-regional geometry as edge attributes and introduce a dedicated metadata context node connected to all regions, providing structured integration of demographic/clinical variables within the same relational space. Node representations are updated using our edge-aware graph transformer followed by attention-driven propagation, and a final graph-level embedding for benign-malignant classification. Experiments on four public benchmarks demonstrate that explicit region-level relational modeling and graph-native multimodal fusion yield consistent gains over the state-of-the-art. Consequently, we establish a new graph-centric perspective in which CNN features are modeled as relational nodes and improved through contextual integration, yielding more expressive and robust classifications.

AI.tldr: Region-based graph learning for dermoscopic lesion classification using superpixel-level nodes with edge attributes and a metadata context node, processed by an edge-aware graph transformer for benign/malignant prediction; shows gains on four public benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CNN/ViT pipelines struggle with spatially grounded multimodal reasoning when relying on global/patch features and late fusion of metadata; anatomy of lesions and demographic/clinical context merit explicit relational modeling.
AI.method: Build graphs from lesion superpixels as frozen CNN feature nodes; encode inter-regional geometry as edge attributes; introduce a metadata context node connected to all regions; update node representations with an edge-aware graph transformer and attention-driven propagation; produce a graph-level embedding for benign/malignant classification.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four public benchmarks shows consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, validating a graph-centric framework that integrates CNN features and structured multimodal context.
AI.conclusion: A graph-centric perspective where CNN features are treated as relational nodes and enhanced via contextual fusion yields more expressive and robust lesion classifications, highlighting the value of explicit region-level relational modeling in dermoscopic analysis.

title: PaAno+: Multiscale Encoding and Cross-Variable Attention for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time-series anomaly detection has significant practical value for industrial and medical monitoring, as well as other critical domains. Current Transformer- and large-model-based detection approaches incur excessive computational overhead, while existing lightweight alternatives are constrained by insufficient feature extraction and inadequate modeling of dependencies across multivariate variables. To mitigate the above drawbacks, this study develops a lightweight, efficient anomaly detection model, dubbed PaAno, within the patch-oriented representation learning paradigm. In the encoder module, a multiscale feature-extraction backbone is constructed using convolutional kernels with differentiated receptive fields to capture hierarchical temporal characteristics; subsequent cross-scale adaptive attention aggregation, combined with residual connection optimization, further stabilizes feature representation learning. A cross-variable fusion attention module is embedded to explicitly characterize inter-variable correlations, empowering the model to identify anomalous patterns amid intricate operational conditions. Moreover, a novel pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting is customized to uncover intrinsic structural properties of time series, and triplet loss is leveraged to optimize the patch embedding space for enhanced feature discrimination. Extensive experiments on the TSB-AD benchmark demonstrate that the proposed PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks, yielding significant performance gains across evaluation metrics, including VUS-PR, relative to the original PaAno. Leveraging a compact network design, the presented model achieves favorable computational efficiency, enabling deployment on resource-limited terminals for real-time anomaly inference.

AI.tldr: PaAno is a lightweight, patch-based time-series anomaly detector that uses multiscale CNNs, cross-scale adaptive attention, cross-variable fusion, a temporal patch-window sorting pretext task, and triplet loss to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on TSB-AD with efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the high computational overhead of Transformer/large-model detectors and the insufficient feature extraction and inter-variable dependency modeling of lightweight methods.
AI.method: Patch-oriented representation learning with an encoder that uses a multiscale convolutional backbone (differentiated receptive fields) for hierarchical temporal features, cross-scale adaptive attention with residual connections, cross-variable fusion attention to capture inter-variable correlations, plus a novel pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting and triplet loss to optimize patch embeddings for discrimination.
AI.result: On the TSB-AD benchmark, PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks, with notable gains in metrics such as VUS-PR relative to the original PaAno, and maintains favorable computational efficiency suitable for real-time inference on resource-limited terminals.
AI.conclusion: PaAno delivers accurate, efficient anomaly detection by combining patch-based representation learning, multiscale temporal feature extraction, cross-variable attention, and auxiliary self-supervised objectives to enhance embedding discrimination and capture inter-variable dependencies.

title: FlowBender: Feedback-Aware Training for Self-Correcting Conditional Flows
summary: Conditional diffusion and flow models routinely fail to satisfy the very constraints that define their task. For instance, a depth-conditioned model often produces images whose re-extracted depth disagrees with the input, even though the forward operator--the depth predictor defining the constraint--is available during both training and inference. Existing approaches generally fall into two categories: supervised models that treat the conditioning signal as a static cue and ignore alignment information at inference, and guidance-based methods that consult it through hand-tuned linear updates, typically trading fidelity to the condition against the plausibility of the generated sample. We argue that the fundamental gap in both paradigms is that the model is never trained to utilize its own alignment error. We introduce FlowBender, a closed-loop framework that treats this error as a first-class input, training the network to learn a correction policy conditioned on inference-time feedback. At each step, an unguided look-ahead pass estimates the clean signal, a task-specific deviation is computed via the forward operator, and a refinement pass consumes this signal to produce a corrected velocity. We propose several variants of FlowBender, including a gradient-based formulation for differentiable operators and a zero-order variant for non-differentiable settings such as JPEG compression. For efficient sampling, we introduce a prior-step shortcut that enables closed-loop correction at a minimal additional computational cost. Across image-to-image translation, restoration, and 3D mesh texturing, FlowBender consistently outperforms standard supervised baselines, alignment-loss-augmented training, and state-of-the-art inference-time guidance, improving fidelity and plausibility simultaneously rather than trading them against each other. Project page: https://flow-bender.github.io/

AI.tldr: FlowBender introduces a closed-loop correction framework for conditional diffusion and flow models that uses inference-time alignment error as a learnable input, enabling iterative refinement to improve fidelity and plausibility without sacrificing either.
AI.motivation: Current conditional generative models struggle to satisfy constraints defined by forward operators (e.g., depth consistency). Existing training paradigms either ignore alignment at inference or rely on heuristic guidance, failing to utilize alignment error during generation. There is a need for a method that explicitly uses alignment feedback to correct outputs.
AI.method: FlowBender trains a network to accept an alignment error (difference between the forward operator's output on the generated signal and the conditioning constraint) and produce corrected velocity updates in a closed-loop sampling process. It uses an unguided look-ahead pass to estimate the clean signal, computes a task-specific deviation via the forward operator, and then refines with a correction pass. Variants include a gradient-based formulation for differentiable operators and a zero-order variant for non-differentiable operators (e.g., JPEG compression). A prior-step shortcut is introduced to enable efficient closed-loop correction with minimal overhead.
AI.result: FlowBender consistently outperforms standard supervised baselines, alignment-loss-augmented training, and state-of-the-art inference-time guidance across tasks such as image-to-image translation, restoration, and 3D mesh texturing, improving fidelity and plausibility simultaneously rather than trading one for the other.
AI.conclusion: A closed-loop, alignment-error-conditioned approach can bridge the fidelity-plausibility trade-off in conditional diffusion/flow models, enabling more faithful adherence to constraints without sacrificing perceptual plausibility, demonstrated across multiple imaging tasks.

title: What Makes Effective Supervision in Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Information-Theoretic Analysis
summary: Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) internalizes reasoning within continuous hidden states, offering a promising alternative to verbose discrete reasoning traces. However, robust latent reasoning remains difficult because outcome supervision provides weak learning signals and leaves latent trajectories prone to semantic drift. In this work, we analyze Latent CoT from an information-theoretic perspective and identify this failure as a dual collapse: gradient attenuation along the optimization path and representational drift in the latent space. We further decompose process supervision into two complementary dimensions: Trajectory Supervision, which injects dense stepwise reasoning signals, and Space Supervision, which preserves the semantic structure of the latent manifold. Our analysis shows that rigid geometric compression can collapse the reasoning space, whereas generative reconstruction provides a more flexible semantic anchor that better preserves information capacity. To measure these effects, we introduce the Unified Latent Probe (ULP), which quantifies the mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps. Experiments reveal a clear Information-Performance Binding: reasoning accuracy depends on the information fidelity preserved in the latent chain. These findings provide a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and suggest shifting from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Supervision-in-Latent-CoT}{this repository}.

AI.tldr: A principled, information-theoretic analysis of Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reveals a dual collapse (gradient attenuation and semantic drift) under weak supervision. The work separates supervision into Trajectory (dense stepwise signals) and Space (semantic structure) components, arguing geometric compression hurts information capacity while generative reconstruction anchors semantics. It introduces Unified Latent Probe (ULP) to measure mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps, finding an information–performance binding. The authors advocate shifting from geometric imitation to mutual information maximization, and provide code.
AI.motivation: Robust latent reasoning in CoT is challenged by weak outcome supervision which causes gradient attenuation and representational drift; a principled framework is needed to preserve information in latent reasoning.
AI.method: Information-theoretic analysis of latent CoT; decompose process supervision into Trajectory and Space dimensions; propose Unified Latent Probe (ULP) to quantify MI between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps; compare rigid geometric compression with generative reconstruction; conduct experiments to assess information fidelity and reasoning accuracy.
AI.result: Identify dual collapse: gradient attenuation along optimization path and representational drift in latent space; demonstrate that rigid geometric compression collapses the reasoning space whereas generative reconstruction better preserves information Capacity; ULP measures MI and correlates with reasoning performance (information–performance binding).
AI.conclusion: Suggest shifting supervision from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization in latent CoT; provide a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and share code.

title: Spectral Query-Key Product Weight Steering for Training-Free VLM Hallucination Mitigation
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often generate fluent but visually unsupported descriptions, especially by mentioning objects absent from the image. We propose QK Product Steering, a data-free, training-free, and zero-inference-cost weight edit for reducing object hallucination. The method directly edits the per-head query-key product, the operator that produces pre-softmax attention logits, by suppressing a small number of dominant singular modes in selected middle layers. The edited product is then mapped back to the query weights through a closed-form query-only update while keeping shared key weights fixed, making the edit compatible with grouped-query attention. We further decompose the QK product into symmetric and antisymmetric components to distinguish mutual content-similarity patterns from directional attention patterns. Across three GQA-based VLMs, QK Product Steering achieves an average relative CHAIR$_s$ reduction of $4.0\%$, while matched random-mode controls show negligible change. Interpretability ablations show that the hallucination signal is specific to dominant QK modes and is primarily localized to the symmetric mutual-attention channel. Overall, QK Product Steering offers a simple alternative to decoding-time mitigation, requiring no additional data, fine-tuning, or inference-time overhead while largely preserving general multimodal capability.

AI.tldr: Data-free, training-free weight editing (QK Product Steering) reduces object hallucination in vision-language models by suppressing dominant QK modes in middle layers, with a closed-form query-only update; achieves ~4% relative CHAIR_s reduction across three GQA-based VLMs and is interpretable via symmetric/antisymmetric QK decomposition.
AI.motivation: VLMs often hallucinate non-existent objects; existing mitigation often requires data, fine-tuning, or decoding-time changes. A data-free, cost-free method would address hallucinations without compromising capability.
AI.method: Edit the per-head QK product (pre-softmax attention logits) by suppressing a small number of dominant singular modes in selected middle layers. Map the edited product back to the query weights through a closed-form query-only update, keeping key weights fixed. Ensure compatibility with grouped-query attention. Decompose QK product into symmetric and antisymmetric components to separate content-similarity from directional attention patterns.
AI.result: Across three GQA-based VLMs, achieved an average relative CHAIR_s reduction of 4.0%; matched random-mode controls show negligible change; hallucination signal localized primarily to the symmetric mutual-attention channel; interpretability ablations support this. No data, fine-tuning, or inference-time overhead; largely preserves multimodal capability.
AI.conclusion: A simple, data-free, training-free weight-editing approach (QK Product Steering) effectively reduces object hallucination with minimal impact on general multimodal performance, and its interpretability is supported by decomposition into symmetric/antisymmetric QK components.

title: Sensorimotor World Models: Perception for Action via Inverse Dynamics
summary: Perception for action suggests that representations of the world should be shaped not by visual fidelity alone, but by their relevance for actions. At the same time, latent JEPA-style world models advocate learning compact predictive states from high-dimensional observations to facilitate the prediction of future states, but end-to-end training of these models is nontrivial because representations may collapse if our only goal is to construct a latent state that is easy to predict. We introduce a sensorimotor world model (SMWM): a latent world model trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization. This single regularizer addresses both issues: it prevents representation collapse and induces action-aligned representations. By forcing latent states to preserve information about the action underlying a transition, it biases the model toward the controllable degrees of freedom of the environment while discarding uncontrollable distractors. This yields stable latent world models trained from offline, reward-free trajectories, without frozen encoders, exponential moving averages, or complex latent regularizers. Empirically, SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and enables competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

AI.tldr: A sensorimotor world model (SMWM) uses inverse dynamics regularization to prevent latent collapse and align representations with controllable actions, enabling offline, reward-free training and competitive planning with compact, interpretable latent spaces.
AI.motivation: Address two problems in latent world models: (1) representation collapse when optimizing solely for predictability, and (2) the lack of action-aligned, controllable latent factors. The approach aims to learn useful, compact latent states from high-dimensional observations without requiring online training or complex regularizers.
AI.method: Train a JEPA-style latent world model end-to-end with a single inverse dynamics regularizer. The regularizer forces latent states to preserve information about the action that caused a transition, biasing the model toward controllable DOF while discarding distractors. No frozen encoders, no exponential moving averages, and no complex latent regularizers. Training uses offline, reward-free trajectories.
AI.result: SMWM yields compact, interpretable latent spaces and achieves competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.
AI.conclusion: Inverse dynamics regularization provides a simple, effective mechanism to prevent representation collapse and produce action-aligned latent representations, enabling stable, offline training of latent world models suitable for planning in controllable environments.

title: InfantFace: Detecting infant faces in neonatal clinical environments
comment: 32 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables; supplementary information included
summary: Reliable localisation of the neonatal face is the first step for several video-camera based non-contact assessments such as pain and distress related facial expression analysis, pain scoring, cardiorespiratory signal extraction and cessation of breathing alerts. However, major challenges persist in neonatal clinical environments. Cluttered backgrounds, illumination changes and poor lighting conditions can reduce the accuracy of face detection models. Clinical interventions, monitoring equipment and, in some cases, medical devices can obstruct the face, making visual assessment difficult. We propose a one-stage YOLOv11m-based model tailored for face detection of infants in neonatal clinical environments. We combined multiple publicly available datasets (VGGFace2, CelebA, FDDB, WIDER FACE) to train and evaluate our proposed model. We then fine-tuned our model on a neonatal research dataset involving 228 videos from 114 recording sessions of 113 independent infants. Before fine-tuning, our model achieved an AP50 of 0.87, surpassing the performance of three state-of-the-art general face detectors. Performance improved further to an AP50 of 0.96 after clinical-domain adaptation. Evaluating face detection performance across different datasets remains a challenge due to the lack of publicly available neonatal datasets. Prioritising the creation of such datasets, while upholding appropriate privacy safeguards and ethical standards in their creation and use, would greatly support further progress in this field.

AI.tldr: One-stage YOLOv11m-based neonatal face detector trained on mixed public datasets and neonatal videos; achieves AP50 0.87 pre-domain adaptation and 0.96 post-adaptation; highlights dataset scarcity and need for ethical neonatal datasets.
AI.motivation: Reliable neonatal face localization enables non-contact assessments (pain, distress, scoring, cardiorespiratory signal extraction) in cluttered clinical environments where occlusions and lighting vary.
AI.method: One-stage YOLOv11m detector; pretraining on public face datasets (VGGFace2, CelebA, FDDB, WIDER FACE); fine-tuning on neonatal data: 228 videos from 114 recording sessions of 113 infants.
AI.result: AP50 of 0.87 before fine-tuning, higher than three state-of-the-art general face detectors; AP50 of 0.96 after clinical-domain adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Neonatal-face datasets are scarce; creating public neonatal datasets with privacy and ethical safeguards would greatly aid progress in robust, domain-specific face detection for neonatal care.

title: Quantile of Means: A Bonus-Free Ensemble Method for Minimax Optimal Reinforcement Learning
summary: Optimal Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms typically rely on carefully constructed count-based uncertainty estimates to drive exploration. Although theoretically sound, such estimates are hard to compute in practical settings and therefore offer limited insight for designing exploration heuristics. Meanwhile, ensembling has emerged as a practical approach, but remains without theoretical justification. Building on a recent ensemble-based method for Multi-Armed Bandits, we propose a quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Our simple count-free approach achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

AI.tldr: A quantile-based, count-free ensemble method for finite-horizon MDPs is proposed, yielding variance-dependent regret bounds and providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between practical ensemble exploration methods and theoretical guarantees, replacing brittle count-based uncertainty with scalable, count-free estimates for RL exploration.
AI.method: Extend ensemble-based exploration from Multi-Armed Bandits to finite-horizon MDPs using a quantile-based aggregation of ensemble estimates, enabling a count-free uncertainty signal that supports variance-dependent regret analysis.
AI.result: Derives optimal variance-dependent regret bounds for the proposed count-free, quantile-based ensemble method, establishing a theoretical justification for ensemble-based exploration in RL.
AI.conclusion: Count-free, quantile-based ensembles can provide theoretical guarantees for exploration in RL and offer a practical, scalable alternative to traditional count-based uncertainty estimates.

title: PCFootprint: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Vectorized Building Footprint Extraction from Aerial LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: Building footprint extraction is a fundamental task in photogrammetry, remote sensing, and computer vision. Recent image-based methods have achieved remarkable progress in extracting vectorized footprints from high-resolution optical imagery. However, optical imagery inherently susceptible to occlusions, perspective distortions, and residual relief displacement, yielding incomplete or misaligned footprint extraction. Furthermore, the lack of explicit elevation information limits its direct applicability to Level of Detail building modeling. In this paper, we present PCFootprint, the first large-scale public dataset for footprint extraction from airborne laser scanning point clouds. PCFootprint comprises \num{33000} tiles derived from the Estonian Land and Spatial Development Board, covering diverse urban and rural landscapes. Each tile spans \qtyproduct{128 x 128}{\m} with systematically aligned vectorized footprints aligned to point clouds. The dataset includes a \num{3000} tiles cross-domain test set for evaluating generalization across geographic regions. We establish comprehensive benchmarks by evaluating mainstream methods. Experimental results reveal significant challenges including high intra-class variance, data imbalance, and noise across complex geospatial environments. We believe PCFootprint will advance future research in building modeling, urban scene understanding, and geospatial analysis. The PCFootprint dataset is publicly available at \url{https://huggingface.co/datasets/Haoyuan-Shen/PCFootprint}.

AI.tldr: A large-scale public LiDAR-based dataset PCFootprint for building footprint extraction, with 33k 128x128 m tiles and a 3k-tile cross-domain test set, aligning vectorized footprints to airborne laser scanning point clouds; benchmarks reveal challenges like intra-class variance, class imbalance, and noise, enabling improved 3D-aware footprint modeling.
AI.motivation: Limitations of optical imagery for accurate footprints due to occlusions, perspective distortion, and relief displacement, combined with lack of explicit elevation information hindering Level-of-Detail building modeling; a LiDAR-grounded dataset is needed for robust footprint extraction and 3D urban analysis.
AI.method: Curates PCFootprint from Estonian LiDAR data, producing 33k tiles, each 128x128 m, with vectorized footprints systematically aligned to point clouds; includes a 3k-tile cross-domain test set; conducts benchmarks with mainstream methods to identify challenges and establish baselines.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial intra-class variance, data imbalance, and noise across geospatial contexts; establishes baselines for various methods on this dataset; indicates room for improvement in cross-domain generalization and LiDAR-based footprint extraction.
AI.conclusion: PCFootprint is publicly available and anticipated to drive advances in building modeling, urban scene understanding, and geospatial analysis by providing a large, elevation-grounded footprint dataset and standardized benchmarks.

title: When Calibration Fails the Vulnerable Hospital: Federated Conformal Risk Control via Risk-Curve Shrinkage
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to the DeCaF Workshop at MICCAI 2026
summary: Conformal risk control (CRC) provides distribution-free guarantees on segmentation quality by calibrating a prediction-set threshold on held-out data. In federated deployments, the standard approach pools calibration scores across sites into a single threshold. We provide the first quantification, on real multi-institutional brain tumor data (FeTS-2022, 1,251 subjects, 20 institutions), showing that this naive pooled CRC protects the average hospital but violates coverage at 40% of individual institutions, with the worst site exceeding the target false-negative rate by 7.8 percentage points. The naive alternative, per-site local CRC, largely restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by 83x, rendering them clinically useless. We propose a shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol: each site transmits only its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized threshold per site. A single hyperparameter n0 smoothly trades worst-case coverage for prediction-set efficiency; leave-one-site-out sensitivity analysis identifies n0=19, achieving 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. We further show that direct Lagrangian optimization of coverage budgets fails, concentrating risk on vulnerable hospitals, and that the finite-sample correction term is essential: removing it triples violations. The marginal CRC guarantee is preserved by construction under the stated site-mixture assumption; per-site coverage is validated across four targets with three seeds. No patient-level images, masks, or per-volume scores leave any site.

AI.tldr: Federated conformal risk control with a shrinkage-based threshold improves per-site coverage without inflating prediction sets; achieves near-nominal coverage across institutions using a server-side shrinkage method with site G scalars and a hyperparameter n0; avoids worst-site risk concentration and outperforms naive pooling or local CRC.
AI.motivation: Mitigate coverage inequities in federated CRC: pooling calibrations protects average but can strongly violate individual institutions; per-site CRC inflates predictions; aim for data-efficient, data-ethical federated calibration with preserved marginal guarantees.
AI.method: Sites transmit only their empirical risk curves (G scalars) to a central server, which computes shrinkage-regularized thresholds per site. A single hyperparameter n0 trades worst-case coverage against prediction-set efficiency. Leave-one-site-out analysis guides n0 (n0=19 in the study). The approach is contrasted with direct Lagrangian optimization and with a finite-sample correction term.
AI.result: Empirical study on FeTS-2022 (1,251 subjects, 20 institutions): pooled CRC protects average but violates 40% of sites; worst site misses the target by 7.8 percentage points. Local CRC restores per-site coverage but inflates sets by ~83x. The proposed shrinkage-based federated CRC with n0=19 yields 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. Finite-sample correction is essential; removing it triples violations. Marginal CRC guarantee preserved under site mixture; per-site coverage validated across four targets with three seeds; no patient-level data leaves site.
AI.conclusion: Shrinkage-based federated CRC preserves the marginal guarantee while improving per-site coverage without exposing raw data; avoids risk concentration seen with Lagrangian optimization and offsets the inefficiency of per-site CRC. The method relies on site-wise risk curves and a tunable hyperparameter to balance coverage and efficiency.

title: Scalable Training of Spatially Grounded 2D Vision-Language Models for Radiology
comment: Accepted for MICCAI 2026. First two authors: equal contribution. Last two authors: equal supervision
summary: We study how to train visually grounded vision-language models (VLMs) for radiology without manual spatial annotations. We introduce RefRad2D, a large-scale bilingual (German/English) dataset of 1.2M CT and MR image-text pairs derived from clinical practice, with task-specific VQA and spatial grounding subsets generated automatically via LLM-based curation and automated segmentation. Trained on this data, our model RadGrounder jointly performs report generation, visual question answering, and spatial grounding via bounding-box detection or segmentation. On external VQA benchmarks (Slake, VQA-RAD), RadGrounder achieves competitive results with specialized medical VLMs. Adding our clinical data to the training mixture improves open-ended VQA over fine-tuning on the downstream datasets alone, showing the transferability of our dataset. Crucially, adding grounding supervision does not degrade language quality, enabling spatially verifiable outputs at no cost to VQA performance.

AI.tldr: A large-scale bilingual radiology dataset (RefRad2D) with 1.2M CT/MR image-text pairs is introduced, plus automatically generated VQA and spatial-grounding subsets using LLM curation and automated segmentation. A model, RadGrounder, jointly performs report generation, visual question answering (VQA), and spatial grounding. It achieves competitive results on external VQA benchmarks, and incorporating the data improves open-ended VQA without hurting language quality or grounding performance.
AI.motivation: Enable training visually grounded vision-language models for radiology without manual spatial annotations, leveraging multilingual clinical data to improve VQA and grounding capabilities.
AI.method: Assemble RefRad2D from clinical practice as a 1.2M image-text corpus in German/English. Use LLM-based curation to create task-specific VQA subsets and automated segmentation for grounding. Train RadGrounder to perform report generation, VQA, and spatial grounding (bounding boxes or segmentation) in a unified model.
AI.result: RadGrounder yields competitive performance on external VQA benchmarks (Slake, VQA-RAD) relative to specialized medical VLMs. Adding the clinical data to the training mix improves open-ended VQA beyond fine-tuning alone. Grounding supervision does not degrade language quality, enabling spatially verifiable outputs without sacrificing VQA performance.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables scalable, end-to-end radiology VLM training with grounding capabilities, with demonstrated transferability and no penalty to language/QA performance when grounding signals are included.

title: Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning for Implicit Earth Embeddings via Location Tying
summary: Spatial prediction tasks are often limited by a lack of high-quality labelled ground-truth observations. To overcome this challenge, self-supervised pre-training is a possible solution, with contrastive learning dominant for location encoders. Those approaches usually align geographic coordinates with just one additional modality. We propose two multimodal contrastive learning architectures: Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying (MELT) and Sequential Alternating Location Training (SALT). These architectures expand this framework beyond two modalities by utilising unpaired geospatial data. Both methods are technically viable and match the performance of the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks. However, increasing the number of modalities does not consistently improve performance, suggesting that the chosen location encoder is the main limitation - the contrastive objective reaches its peak early, regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume. MELT provides more stable training than SALT and presents a stronger foundation for future scaling.

AI.tldr: Multimodal contrastive pre-training with MELT and SALT extends beyond two modalities and matches SATCLIP across tasks, but adding modalities often yields diminishing returns; MELT offers more stable training and a better path for scaling.
AI.motivation: Lack of high-quality ground-truth data for spatial prediction; self-supervised pre-training as a solution; prior approaches mainly linked coordinates to a single extra modality; need to leverage unpaired geospatial data and more modalities.
AI.method: Introduce two architectures: MELT (Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying) and SALT (Sequential Alternating Location Training). Both extend contrastive learning to multiple modalities beyond two, using unpaired geospatial data to learn aligned representations. MELT ties location information across modalities; SALT alternates training steps to align modalities sequentially. Evaluate on four downstream tasks against the SATCLIP baseline.
AI.result: Both MELT and SALT are technically viable and achieve performance on par with SATCLIP across four downstream tasks. Increasing modality count does not consistently improve performance; the location encoder is the main bottleneck, and the contrastive objective saturates early regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume. MELT yields more stable training and a stronger foundation for scaling than SALT.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal extension via MELT and SALT is feasible and competitive, but gains plateau due to encoder limitations. Focus should shift to improving the location encoder and objective design; MELT is the preferred base for future scaling.

title: How Fragile Are Training-Free AI-Generated Image Detectors? A Controlled Audit of Score Direction, Preprocessing, and Compression
summary: Training-free detectors of AI-generated images promise generator-agnostic deployment without classifier training, yet their reported numbers are rarely compared under a single controlled protocol. We audit two representative training-free scores -- an autoencoder-reconstruction score (AEROBLADE-style) and a noise-perturbation feature-similarity score (RIGID-style) -- plus a naive feature-kNN control, on a common 1,500-image GenImage-derived benchmark spanning seven generators and JPEG compression at quality 70 and 50. The audit yields three cautionary findings. (i) Implementation details masquerade as method differences: replacing the LPIPS backbone (AlexNet -> VGG-16) changes overall AUROC by +0.085, and switching between resize-to-512 and native-resolution preprocessing flips per-generator conclusions by up to 0.38 AUROC. (ii) Score direction is not a property of the method but of its hyperparameters: the RIGID-style score is inverted (AUROC < 0.5) on SD1.5 and Wukong at noise level sigma=0.05, recovers to >0.5 for every generator at sigma=0.01, and collapses to 0.15 at sigma=0.3. (iii) Dataset format bias inflates robustness claims: without unified re-encoding, AUROC under JPEG-50 exceeds the clean condition for the AlexNet-backbone reconstruction score; after bias correction the residual anomaly localizes to a single generator (BigGAN). The audited scores have complementary per-generator failure sets, but naive z-score fusion does not beat the best single score, indicating that exploiting complementarity requires direction-aware combination.

AI.tldr: Three major caveats in training-free detectors for AI-generated images: (1) results are highly sensitive to implementation details; (2) score direction depends on hyperparameters, causing per-generator flips; (3) dataset-encoding bias inflates robustness claims; naive fusion by z-scores underperforms the best single score, indicating the need for direction-aware combination to exploit complementarity.
AI.motivation: Assess the reliability and cross-generator robustness of training-free detectors under a unified, controlled protocol to reveal hidden biases and reliability limits.
AI.method: Reproduce two representative training-free scores (AEROBLADE-style autoencoder reconstruction; RIGID-style noise-perturbation feature similarity) plus a naive feature-kNN control, on a common 1,500-image GenImage-derived benchmark spanning seven generators and JPEG quality 70 and 50. Examine backbone choices (AlexNet, VGG-16) and preprocessing (resize-to-512 vs native resolution) to evaluate protocol sensitivity.
AI.result: Three cautionary findings: (i) Implementation details masquerade as method differences; changing LPIPS backbone from AlexNet to VGG-16 shifts AUROC by ~0.085 and resize strategy can flip per-generator conclusions by up to 0.38 AUROC. (ii) Score direction is hyperparameter-driven; RIGID-style AUROC inverts (<0.5) on SD1.5 and Wukong at sigma=0.05, recovers (>0.5) at sigma=0.01, and collapses to 0.15 at sigma=0.3. (iii) Dataset-encoding bias inflates robustness claims; without unified re-encoding, AUROC under JPEG-50 exceeds the clean condition for AlexNet-based reconstruction, but bias-corrected residuals localize anomaly to a single generator (BigGAN). The scores have complementary per-generator failure sets, but naive z-score fusion does not beat the best single score, indicating the need for direction-aware combination to exploit complementarity.
AI.conclusion: The audit uncovers substantial reliability and reproducibility issues in training-free detectors. The results stress the need for standardized evaluation protocols, reporting of hyperparameters and preprocessing details, and principled, direction-aware score fusion to harness complementarity across detectors.

title: Predicting gestational age at birth in the context of preterm birth from multi-modal fetal MRI
comment: Accepted for publication at the Journal of Machine Learning for Biomedical Imaging (MELBA) https://melba-journal.org/2026:013
summary: Preterm birth is associated with significant mortality and a risk for lifelong morbidity. The complex multifactorial aetiology hampers accurate prediction and thus optimal care. A pipeline consisting of bespoke machine learning methods for data imputation, feature selection, and regression models to predict gestational age (GA) at birth was developed and evaluated from comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control cases and 93 preterm birth cases. The GA at birth predictions were classified into term and preterm categories and their accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were reported. An ablation study was performed to further validate the design of the pipeline. Performance was evaluated using stratified 10-fold cross-validation. The pipeline achieves an R2 score of 0.13 and a mean absolute error of 2.74 weeks. It also achieves a 0.77 accuracy, 0.59 sensitivity, and 0.82 specificity across folds. The predominant features selected by the pipeline include cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2* values. The confluence of fast, motion-robust and multi-modal fetal MRI techniques and machine learning prediction allowed the prediction of the gestation at birth. This information is essential for any pregnancy. To the best of our knowledge, preterm birth had only been addressed as a classification problem in the literature. Therefore, this work provides a proof of concept. Future work will increase the cohort size to allow for finer stratification within the preterm birth cohort. Our code is available at https://github.com/dfajardorojas/ml-for-preterm-birth-.

AI.tldr: ML pipeline for predicting gestational age at birth from multi-modal fetal MRI; modest regression performance (R2 0.13, MAE 2.74 weeks) and classification performance (accuracy 0.77, sensitivity 0.59, specificity 0.82); key features include cervical length and placental T2* statistics; first proof-of-concept addressing GA at birth as a regression problem; future work to expand cohort.
AI.motivation: Preterm birth is a leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity; prediction remains challenging due to multifactorial etiology. Current literature often frames GA-related risk as a classification task; this work aims to predict gestational age at birth (a continuous outcome) using ML on multi-modal fetal MRI to inform care.
AI.method: A pipeline combining data imputation, feature selection, and regression models applied to multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control and 93 preterm cases. Performance evaluated with stratified 10-fold cross-validation and an ablation study.
AI.result: R2 = 0.13; mean absolute error (MAE) = 2.74 weeks. Classification: accuracy = 0.77, sensitivity = 0.59, specificity = 0.82 across folds. Predominant features: cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2* values. Code available at GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of predicting gestational age at birth from fetal MRI using machine learning; presented as a proof-of-concept for regression-based GA prediction. Future work should enlarge the cohort for finer stratification within the preterm group.

title: FreeStyle: Free Control of Style-Content Dual-Reference Generation from Community LoRA Mining
summary: Style-content dual-reference generation aims to synthesize an image that preserves the structure and semantics of a content reference while adopting the style of a separate style reference.Despite recent progress, this setting remains challenging because models must balance content fidelity, style alignment, and instruction following avoiding semantic leakage from the style reference.A key bottleneck is the lack of large-scale triplet data with clean content-style separation and broad long-tail style coverage.In this work, we propose FreeStyle, a scalable dual-reference generation framework based on community LoRA mining.We treat community LoRAs as compositional anchors for style and content, and design a rigorous generation and filtering pipeline to construct large-scale Style-Reference and Content-Reference triplets across multiple base models.To address content leakage, we adopt a two-stage curriculum with stage-specific disentanglement mechanisms: an attention-level enrichment constraint that suppresses style-reference leakage in the style-transfer stage, and a frequency-aware RoPE modulation strategy that targets positional-correspondence-based leakage in the harder dual-reference stage.We also introduce a benchmark covering both style-reference and dual-reference generation, with evaluations on style similarity, content preservation, aesthetics, instruction following, and leakage rejection. The benchmark incorporates a style-invariant Content Alignment Score (CAS) and introduces a calibrated VLM-based Rejection Score for evaluating generation reliability and leakage suppression.Extensive experiments show that our model achieves a strong balance among style alignment, content preservation, and leakage suppression.

AI.tldr: FreeStyle proposes a scalable dual-reference generation framework using community LoRA mining to synthesize content-preserving, style-adopting images while mitigating style leakage; it introduces a large-scale triplet data creation pipeline and evaluation benchmark with leakage-aware metrics.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of generating images that preserve content while adopting target styles, requiring large-scale triplet data with clean content-style separation and broad long-tail style coverage, and minimizing semantic leakage from style references.
AI.method: Leverage community LoRAs as compositional anchors for style and content; build a generation and filtering pipeline to create Style-Reference and Content-Reference triplets across multiple base models. Implement a two-stage curriculum with (1) attention-level enrichment to suppress style leakage in style transfer, and (2) frequency-aware RoPE modulation to reduce positional leakage in dual-reference generation.
AI.result: Demonstrates a strong balance among style alignment, content preservation, and leakage suppression across extensive experiments; introduces a Content Alignment Score (CAS) and a calibrated VLM-based Rejection Score to evaluate generation reliability and leakage; reports improved metrics over baselines.
AI.conclusion: FreeStyle offers a scalable solution for dual-reference generation with effective leakage mitigation and broad style coverage, supported by a dedicated benchmark for evaluating style fidelity, content preservation, and leakage rejection.

title: Computational Methods and Challenges in Cell-Free DNA Analysis for Multi-Cancer Early Detection
summary: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is a promising avenue for non-invasive multicancer early detection (MCED), in that, it can enable multiple cancer detection simultaneously from a single blood draw, with particular sensitivity to cancers that currently lack established screening programs. Here we review the computational methods developed between 2022 and 2025 for cfDNA-based MCED. We focus on how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted and analyzed to detect cancer at early stages. We first briefly outline the biological basis of cfDNA signals, then review classical statistical and machine learning approaches alongside deep learning frameworks including autoencoder-based models. For each method we discuss biological interpretability, validation strategy, and readiness for clinical integration. Furthermore, we categorize the current challenges into technical, computational, and methodological while outlining open problems in the field. This review shows that multimodal ensemble approaches have the strongest promise for clinical integration and the highest readiness. However, for better assessment of future work and side-by-side comparison, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting results will be crucial.

AI.tldr: A survey of computational cfDNA-based multicancer early detection (MCED) methods (2022–2025) focusing on fragmentomics and epigenetic features, covering classical statistics, ML, and deep learning (notably autoencoders). It assesses interpretability, validation, and clinical readiness, categorizes technical/computational/methodological challenges, and concludes that multimodal ensemble approaches hold the most promise but require standardized evaluation/reporting for fair comparisons.
AI.motivation: To synthesize current computational approaches for cfDNA-based MCED, highlighting how fragmentomics and epigenetic signals are extracted, analyzed, and integrated to enable early, non-invasive cancer detection across multiple cancer types.
AI.method: A narrative/systematic review of recent literature (2022–2025) on cfDNA-based MCED. It discusses signal biology (fragmentomics and epigenetics), surveys methodological approaches (classical statistics, ML, deep learning including autoencoders), and evaluates biological interpretability, validation strategies, and clinical readiness. It then categorizes challenges (technical, computational, methodological) and argues for standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting, with a conclusion that multimodal ensembles are most promising for clinical translation.
AI.result: Synthesis that multimodal ensemble methods currently exhibit the strongest clinical promise and readiness for MCED with cfDNA, but progress requires standardized evaluation protocols and reporting for robust cross-study comparisons; identifies ongoing challenges across technical, computational, and methodological dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal ensemble approaches offer the best path toward clinical-ready cfDNA MCED, but standardization of evaluation and reporting is essential to enable fair, cross-study comparisons and accelerate translation.

title: S-Agent: Spatial Tool-Use Elicits Reasoning for Spatial Intelligence
summary: Real-world spatial intelligence requires reasoning over a continuous and evolving 3D world, yet existing VLMs and tool-augmented agents largely remain tied to static, stateless inference from isolated visual observations. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{S-Agent}}, a spatial tool-use agentic paradigm for understanding and reasoning over continuous multi-view images and videos. By formulating spatial reasoning as spatio-temporal evidence accumulation rather than isolated frame-level prediction, \textsc{S-Agent} reshapes spatial perception into scene-centric understanding beyond frame-centric recognition. Specifically, \textsc{S-Agent} casts the VLM as a semantic planner that decides what evidence is needed, while a hierarchy of spatial tools and experts grounds objects in 2D, lifts them into 3D geometric evidence, and aggregates this evidence into high-level spatial knowledge (\textit{e.g.}, counting, measurement, orientation, and relative position). Additionally, a temporal memory mechanism, including Scene Memory for maintaining the evolving scene state and Agent Memory for accumulating reasoning context, enables evidence integration across frames and reasoning steps. Comprehensive experiments on multi-view and video spatial reasoning benchmarks show that \textsc{S-Agent} consistently improves both open-source and closed-source VLMs in a training-free manner. Beyond inference-time augmentation, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on \textsc{S-Agent}-generated spatial trajectories \textsc{S-300K} yields \textsc{S-Agent-8B}, a compact spatial agent that significantly surpasses similar-scale baselines (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) and performs comparably to advanced closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3).

AI.tldr: S-Agent introduces a spatial tool-use agent that reframes spatial reasoning as spatio-temporal evidence accumulation for continuous multi-view and video inputs, using memory modules and a tool hierarchy to achieve scene-centric understanding and superior performance without training, with S-300K enabling an 8B spatial agent.
AI.motivation: To address real-world spatial reasoning needs where worlds are continuous and evolving, current VLMs are frame-based and stateless, and tool-augmentation is underexplored for sustained spatial inference.
AI.method: Formulate spatial reasoning as spatio-temporal evidence accumulation. Use a VLM as semantic planner to select needed evidence. Employ a hierarchy of spatial tools/experts to ground 2D observations, lift to 3D, and aggregate into high-level spatial knowledge. Incorporate Scene Memory for evolving scene state and Agent Memory for reasoning context. Evaluate in training-free settings; perform supervised fine-tuning on S-Agent-generated spatial trajectories to obtain S-300K, yielding S-Agent-8B.
AI.result: S-Agent consistently improves both open-source and closed-source VLMs in a training-free manner on multi-view and video spatial reasoning benchmarks. S-300K fine-tuning produces S-Agent-8B, which surpasses similar-scale baselines (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B) and is competitive with advanced closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3).
AI.conclusion: Casting spatial reasoning as scene-centric, evidence-accumulating inference with memory and a hierarchy of tools enables robust, scalable spatial understanding that improves existing VLMs and yields competitive compact agents through S-300K fine-tuning.
