AI.conclusion: Padding-based defense provides a practical, deployable CPS solution for FDIA resilience, compatible with existing DNNs and requiring no core-model changes; future work could explore theoretical guarantees, broader CPS benchmarks, and resilience against adaptive attackers.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

AI.tldr: This work investigates removing explicit timestep embeddings from diffusion models, showing that the global minimizer may be achieved without temporal conditioning and that time-agnostic models can match or exceed conditioned models on CelebA and CIFAR-10 in metrics like FID, precision, and recall, suggesting implicit noise-scale inference is possible under certain assumptions.
AI.motivation: Question the entrenched reliance on explicit temporal signals in diffusion models and explore whether such conditioning is necessary for optimal denoising and generative quality; provide a theoretical framework for when conditioning may be redundant.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the diffusion training objective under conditions where timestep conditioning is removed, along with empirical ablation studies using U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures on CelebA and CIFAR-10 to assess performance (FID, precision, recall) and to examine the model's ability to infer noise scales from corrupted inputs.
AI.result: Time-agnostic diffusion models maintain high structural fidelity and can even surpass conditioned counterparts on key metrics (FID, precision, recall); the models appear robust to completely removing timestep embeddings and may implicitly infer noise scales from input statistics under certain assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The traditional reliance on explicit timestep conditioning in diffusion models is challenged; results indicate potential for more efficient, time-agnostic architectures that rely on intrinsic cues in corrupted data to infer noise levels, prompting a rethink of diffusion model design and training.

title: Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations
summary: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

AI.tldr: Neural surrogates (DeepGaLA) for differential equations provide uncertainty-aware forward-model predictions to enable scalable Bayesian inference for inverse problems; compared to Gaussian processes, they offer similar accuracy with better scaling in parameter dimension and can incorporate DE constraints; validated via delayed-acceptance MCMC diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational burden of Bayesian inverse problems (likelihood evaluation with complex forward models) and mitigate overconfidence when data are limited by delivering calibrated uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: Train a neural-network surrogate for DE solvers that yields uncertainty-aware predictions. Use it within Bayesian inference to approximate the forward model; diagnose surrogate fidelity with a short delayed-acceptance MCMC run; compare performance against Gaussian-process surrogates; allow incorporation of differential-equation constraints, including nonlinear ones.
AI.result: Forward-model approximations achieve accuracy comparable to GP surrogates; efficiency improves as parameter dimension grows; surrogate maintains uncertainty to prevent overconfident inferences; performance holds when incorporating DE constraints.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable, reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems, matching GP accuracy while scaling better with dimensionality and accommodating DE constraints.

title: Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

AI.tldr: A controlled toy-world framework reveals that representation overlap (superposition) interacts with sparsity and strength to shape forgetting in continual learning, challenging the notion that more overlap always worsens retention.
AI.motivation: To disentangle forgetting mechanisms in continual learning by creating a tractable, synthetic environment where latent features, sparsity, and task overlap can be precisely controlled and observed, enabling falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools.
AI.method: A generator-separator synthetic pipeline defines ground-truth latent features, constructs tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, measures representation strength and directional overlap (superposition), and models retention dynamics by fitting sparse dynamical systems (SINDy) using exposure history. A task-level analysis via effective rank assesses capacity allocation across tasks.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Superposition grows over time with transient dips at task boundaries, indicating boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift; (2) Higher sparsity increases superposition but does not necessarily cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap; (3) Task-level effective rank increases with sparsity, suggesting broader capacity usage under sparse regimes.
AI.conclusion: The study refines the common belief that overlap directly drives forgetting, showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. The toy framework yields falsifiable hypotheses and practical diagnostics for continual learning.

title: Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: Equal advising: Daria Pugacheva and Fedor Ratnikov. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI and PDEs
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

AI.tldr: A two-stage evolutionary algorithm for hyperparameter tuning in PINNs, using fast low-fidelity screening followed by full training; achieves lower mean error than standard training under fixed compute on Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz.
AI.motivation: PINNs suffer from unstable convergence and high sensitivity to architecture/optimization hyperparameters due to the non-convex, multi-term physics-informed loss. Outer-loop hyperparameter optimization is noisy and difficult with traditional methods.
AI.method: Stage 1: perform low-fidelity, truncated-epoch training runs to rapidly screen candidate hyperparameter configurations with a black-box outer loop. Stage 2: fully train the most promising candidates using standard gradient-based optimizers to refine solutions.
AI.result: The two-stage approach consistently outperforms standard PINN training, achieving significantly lower mean error under constrained computational budgets across Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz problems.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage evolutionary search improves PINN training robustness and accuracy under fixed budgets by effectively exploring and exploiting hyperparameter configurations; the method shows promise for other PDEs but may rely on correlation between low-fidelity and high-fidelity performance and incurs overhead from evolutionary search.

title: Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness
summary: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

AI.tldr: Extends a bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to ensure representation of intersectional subgroups, trading small residual bias for data efficiency; formulates an integer linear program to optimize mitigation strategies and analyzes the data-modification cost (price of fairness) under fairness tolerances; evaluated on public datasets with maintained accuracy and improved subgroup representation.
AI.motivation: Address discriminatory outcomes and degraded performance for individuals at intersection of sensitive attributes by lacking principled, intersectional bias measures and suffering from under-representation in training data; seek data-efficient fairness with regulatory and governance relevance.
AI.method: Enhance an existing bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to enforce sufficient subgroup representation (including intersectional groups); allow small approximation errors in bias to gain data efficiency; cast bias mitigation as an integer linear program optimizing over mitigation strategies; quantify the price of fairness as a function of fairness tolerance.
AI.result: Demonstrates that bias mitigation via the proposed framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers and that coverage constraints are essential for preserving downstream ML performance; shows data-efficiency trade-offs and provides metrics on data modification costs.
AI.conclusion: Coverage-driven, data-efficient bias mitigation enables reliable intersectional fairness while maintaining performance, with a formal decision-theoretic view (price of fairness) to guide trade-offs for regulatory compliance and data governance.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
summary: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

AI.tldr: ASYS is a prior-guided symbolic search framework that evolves interpretable symbolic PDE models by combining structure search with gradient optimization, yielding analytical representations including previously unknown forms.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between mathematical understanding and data-driven approaches, enabling recovery of known analytical forms and discovery of new analytical approximations to guide PDE analysis.
AI.method: Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS): a prior-informed agent translates PDE theory, constraints, and search experience into differentiable symbolic programs; symbolic structure is evolved (evolutionary search) while continuous parameters are optimized by gradient descent; results are interpretable symbolic forms.
AI.result: On five problems across bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS returns interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter Keller-Segel contraction law; it also recovers known analytical forms when available and constructs analytical approximations otherwise.
AI.conclusion: ASYS demonstrates a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions beyond handcrafted analysis, mesh-based numerics, and neural networks, potentially guiding mathematicians with interpretable analytical forms.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
summary: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

AI.tldr: 4-bit KV-cache with rotation and codebook quantization, plus vLLM FP8 KV caching anchors, yields large latency and throughput gains for long-context multi-turn agent workloads via UltraQuant on AMD GPUs.
AI.motivation: Context-heavy agents impose unusual pressure on KV caches due to long-prefix reuse and concurrency limits; 4-bit KV caching aims to reduce memory/compute while maintaining quality in this regime.
AI.method: Frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads; apply TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as quality anchors; adopt asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants; implement serving optimizations on AMD GPUs (optimized decode-attention kernels, UltraQuant FP4 path with FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, native MFMA on CDNA4).
AI.result: UltraQuant reduces P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds and 2.3x across all rounds; output throughput increases by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.
AI.conclusion: 4-bit KV caching with the described design choices and GPU optimizations delivers robust performance improvements for cache-pressured, long-context, multi-turn agents, demonstrating practical viability of the UltraQuant approach.

title: Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution
summary: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

AI.tldr: MAA introduces cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation for batch-style trace distillation, using differential signals and EMA to identify stable operations while reducing token usage.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation that prevents distinguishing stably effective operations from accidental hits; formalize the requirements as alignability and comparability.
AI.method: MAA constructs differential signals to enable cross-batch comparability, accumulates signed evidence per operation via exponential moving average (EMA), and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging; implemented as a post-processing architecture.
AI.result: Outperforms existing batch-level distillation baselines in 14 of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models; matches or exceeds online alternatives in most settings; reduces optimization-phase token consumption by ~75%.
AI.conclusion: MAA provides an effective cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism for batch-style trace distillation, yielding strong empirical gains and substantial efficiency improvements.

title: Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: When large language models serve as evaluators in multi-agent systems, their systematic evaluation biases propagate through the agent network. We introduce Contagion Networks, a formal framework for measuring how evaluator biases spread across interacting LLM agents. In a controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three distinct evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based), we measure the Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3 and find that evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]), even within the same underlying model. We identify three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N), and demonstrate that homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients observed in prior work (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. We show that increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%, providing an actionable mitigation strategy. We release the open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

AI.tldr: Contagion Networks quantify evaluator-bias spread in multi-agent LLMs; in a 3-agent DeepSeek-chat setup, evaluator biases propagate (gamma ~0.16–0.35); contagion regimes link to spectral radius; cross-model contagion stronger than homogeneous; increasing evaluator committee size to k=3 cuts contagion by 72.4%; open-source framework released.
AI.motivation: Understand and mitigate bias contagion in LLM-based agent ecosystems by formalizing contagion and providing actionable mitigation guidance.
AI.method: Controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based); compute Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3; analyze propagation regimes via spectral radius rho(Gamma_N); compare homogeneous-model vs cross-model contagion; test effect of evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3; release open-source framework.
AI.result: Evaluator biases propagate across agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]); three propagation regimes governed by spectral radius rho(Gamma_N); homogeneous-model agents exhibit contagion coefficients 3–5x weaker than cross-model (MM-EPC gamma ≈ 0.85–1.3), placing them in a suppression regime; increasing committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%; open-source Contagion Network framework released.
AI.conclusion: Provides a formal framework to measure and mitigate evaluator-bias contagion in LLM-agent ecosystems; actionable mitigation via larger evaluator committees; spectral-radius–based regime taxonomy; limitations include scale and generalizability across tasks/models; future work to broaden bias profiles and tasks.

title: Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving
summary: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

AI.tldr: Graph-bound execution-state capsules enable low-latency, on-device LLM serving by snapshotting/restoring the entire execution boundary, not just KV caches; FlashRT runs plans on static buffers for sub-millisecond GPU state operations; yields substantial TTFT speedups and complements KV caches.
AI.motivation: Address the latency challenge in on-device, interactive LLM agents with frequent branching by moving beyond token-addressed KV fragments to complete execution-state reuse.
AI.method: Introduce execution-state capsules and a white-box FlashRT backend that executes captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers. Support snapshot/restore/fork/rollback of full state including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP, and metadata. Validate on RTX 5090, Jetson AGX Thor, and DGX Spark; compare against KV-only baseline.
AI.result: Byte-exact restoration at stored state; token-identical decoding under greedy decoding; sub-millisecond GPU snapshot/restore; TTFT speedup grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k; correctness holds across hardware; not a replacement for KV caches, but a complementary latency-first reuse mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Capsules enable a complete-execution-state reuse paradigm for low-latency on-device LLM serving, providing a complementary, latency-focused alternative to KV-based reuse with provable correctness under defined boundaries.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
summary: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

AI.tldr: A multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that represents prior information as in-context prefixes and uses a transformer to adapt predictions across families of priors, achieving oracle-level accuracy with much faster inference and robustness to distribution shifts on spatiotemporal data.
AI.motivation: Bayesian predictive inference is principled but often intractable to compute exactly; existing amortized approaches for prior-data fitting are limited by the training priors and struggle to adapt to new priors, reducing robustness under distribution shift. There's a need for an amortized method that explicitly incorporates priors and adapts at test time.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-task in-context learning framework where prior information is encoded as a prefix of in-context datasets. A Transformer is trained on sequences of prior and target tasks to learn cross-prior adaptation, enabling predictions across families of priors (hierarchical Bayesian setting). Evaluation includes increasing difficulty such as out-of-meta-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures, plus a real-world spatiotemporal temperature benchmark.
AI.result: Matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster; demonstrates robustness to distribution shifts and improved scalability. Code provided at the cited repository.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a robust, fast amortized approach to hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that can adapt to varied priors at test time, showing strong performance across challenging tasks and real-world data.

title: Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.

AI.tldr: A fine-grained privacy notion, privacy via predictability, measures leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker’s ability to predict sensitive information given outputs, under a compromised data model. It is generally incomparable to differential privacy (DP), but in a worst-case regime it implies mutual-information DP. The paper frames a GMM-based analysis for asymptotic predictability with stationary, ergodic, mixing compromised data and develops a predictability-calibrated ERM perturbation, positioning predictability as a complement to DP.
AI.motivation: To capture realistic attacker knowledge and data-generation processes, enabling fine-grained privacy control beyond worst-case DP and aligning privacy guarantees with specific sensitive information and adversarial models.
AI.method: Define predictability as the incremental predictive power for sensitive attributes after observing the algorithm’s output, conditioned on compromised data. Develop a general framework using the generalized method of moments (GMM) to analyze asymptotic predictability under stationary, ergodic, mixing data-generating processes. Establish relationships to DP and derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation for empirical risk minimization (ERM).
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) DP and predictability are generally incomparable; (2) in the extreme regime where all but one individual is compromised and all binary queries are sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP; (3) a GMM-based asymptotic analysis enables design of calibrated perturbations for ERM; (4) the framework is complementary to DP and can be used alongside it for fine-grained privacy control.
AI.conclusion: Predictability offers a fine-grained privacy metric tailored to specific attacker models and sensitive information. When combined with DP, it provides a richer toolkit for privacy-preserving learning, enabling calibrated perturbations informed by realistic data-generation and attack knowledge.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
summary:
A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

AI.tldr: Deterministic multicalibration achieves minimax-optimal luff? Actually luff? Correction: The paper proves a deterministic algorithm attains minimax-optimal inal sample complexity ive^{-3} for rac{multicalibration}, previously only randomized predictors achieved this rate; extends to deterministic outcome indistinguishability and to omnipredictors/panpredictors with optimal rates.
AI.motivation: Ensure multicalibration is achievable deterministically with optimal sample complexity, and extend determinism to outcome indistinguishability (OI) as well as omnipredictors/panpredictors, addressing open questions about the necessity of randomness and the existence of deterministic predictors with optimal rates.
AI.method: Construct a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor; generalize the approach to obtain optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability for finite/finitely covered test collections; derive implications for deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors.
AI.result: A deterministic algorithm attains the minimax-optimal �75^{-3} sample complexity for 8 multi-calibration, previously only achieved by randomized predictors; extends to deterministic predictors achieving outcome indistinguishability for test collections; yields deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.
AI.conclusion: Randomization is not necessary for achieving minimax-optimal sample complexity in multicalibration; the framework extends to outcome indistinguishability and to deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors, solving related open questions.

title: How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?
comment: 20 main text pages and 6 pages of references and appendices
summary: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

AI.tldr: Decomposing transparency into variable and algorithmic components shows DiffusionGemma’s apparent opacity is largely mitigated by an interpretable bottleneck, but algorithmic transparency remains harder; diffusion-specific interpretability phenomena emerge; monitorability is comparable to Gemma 4.
AI.motivation: Address the intuition that latent-space computation in diffusion models may reduce reasoning transparency. Propose a two-component framework (variable vs algorithmic transparency) to rigorously evaluate model interpretability and debugging potential in diffusion-based LLMs.
AI.method: 1) Define two components of transparency. 2) Quantify 'opaque serial depth' between interpretable states. 3) Introduce an interpretable token bottleneck to track information flow across denoising steps without harming performance. 4) Treat intermediate states as interpretable to reassess variable transparency. 5) Conduct interpretability case studies to identify diffusion-specific phenomena. 6) Evaluate monitorability by downstream task usefulness.
AI.result: Variable transparency: initially poor due to long serial depth, but reduced to 1.1x of Gemma 4 when using the bottleneck and treating intermediate states as interpretable, with no loss in downstream performance. Algorithmic transparency: inherently harder in diffusion models due to all token predictions potentially changing at each denoising step; preliminary diffusion-specific phenomena observed (non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, intermediate-context reasoning). Monitorability: DiffusionGemma shows comparable monitorability to Gemma 4.
AI.conclusion: A two-component framework clarifies diffusion-model transparency: latent-space computation does not preclude interpretable, digitized state tracking when bottlenecks are used; algorithmic transparency remains a tougher challenge requiring further study and targeted interpretability tools. Early evidence of diffusion-specific interpretability phenomena suggests unique reasoning dynamics in diffusion models. Monitorability aligns with autoregressive baselines, indicating practical usefulness for downstream tasks.
